topics.title: Conventional (fossil fuels, nuclear) and alternative (renewable sources like solar, wind, geothermal) energy sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conventional (fossil fuels, nuclear) and alternative (renewable sources like solar, wind, geothermal) energy sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Conventional Energy Sources

**Conventional energy sources** are those energy resources that have been traditionally used for power generation and propulsion for over a century. They are characterized by their **non-renewable nature**, meaning their reserves are finite and take **millions of years to form**. They are broadly classified into:

### A. Fossil Fuels
Formed from the anaerobic decomposition of buried organic matter (plants and animals) under intense heat and pressure over geological timescales. The three primary fossil fuels are:

- **Coal**: A solid carbonaceous sedimentary rock formed from compressed plant remains. Primary constituent is **carbon (C)** along with hydrocarbons, sulfur, and ash.
- **Petroleum (Crude Oil)**: A liquid mixture of hydrocarbons extracted from underground reservoirs. Refined into petrol, diesel, kerosene, and LPG.
- **Natural Gas**: Primarily **methane (CH₄)** found in porous rock formations, often co-located with petroleum.

### B. Nuclear Energy
Energy released from the **nuclear fission** of heavy isotopes such as **Uranium-235** ($\text{U}^{235}$), **Plutonium-239** ($\text{Pu}^{239}$), or via **nuclear fusion** of light isotopes like deuterium and tritium. The energy yield per unit mass is approximately **$10^6$ to $10^7$ times greater** than fossil fuels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Conventional sources dominate the current automotive propulsion landscape — petrol and diesel engines rely on fossil fuels, while nuclear submarines and certain heavy vehicles leverage nuclear power. Understanding their limitations (pollution, finite reserves) is the key motivation for studying alternatives.

---

## 1.2 Alternative (Renewable) Energy Sources

**Alternative energy sources** are energy resources that are naturally replenished on a human timescale and produce minimal to zero greenhouse gas emissions during operation. The principal renewable sources relevant to automotive engineering are:

### A. Solar Energy
Energy radiated by the **Sun** in the form of electromagnetic waves. At the outer edge of Earth's atmosphere, the **solar constant** $G_{sc} \approx \mathbf{1367\ W/m^2}$. Captured via:
- **Photovoltaic (PV) cells** — direct conversion to electricity.
- **Solar thermal collectors** — conversion to heat.

### B. Wind Energy
**Kinetic energy** of moving air masses driven by differential solar heating of Earth's surface. Exploited via aerodynamic blades coupled to generators or directly to mechanical drives.

### C. Geothermal Energy
Heat energy stored within the Earth's interior, originating from:
- Residual primordial heat from planetary formation.
- Continuous radioactive decay of isotopes like $\text{U}^{238}$, $\text{Th}^{232}$, $\text{K}^{40}$.

The average **geothermal gradient** is approximately **25–30 °C per km** depth.

### D. Other Renewables (Briefly)
- **Hydropower**: Potential + kinetic energy of water.
- **Biomass / Biofuels**: Ethanol (from sugarcane, corn), biodiesel (from vegetable oils, algae).
- **Tidal & Wave Energy**: Lunar/solar gravitational pull.
- **Hydrogen Fuel Cells**: $\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electricity + Heat}$

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy**:
>
> Imagine the Earth's energy reserves as a **bank account**:
> - **Fossil fuels & nuclear** = A **fixed-deposit account**. You can withdraw large amounts, but you **cannot replenish** it. The principal was deposited millions of years ago and is now depleting rapidly.
> - **Renewable sources (solar, wind, geothermal)** = A **salary account**. Money (energy) flows in continuously from the Sun, the wind, and Earth's core. As long as you withdraw no faster than the rate of replenishment, the balance never hits zero.
>
> Automobiles currently run almost entirely on the "fixed deposit." The global automotive industry is transitioning toward the "salary account" — which is precisely what this course (PEAUT411) is designed to prepare engineers for.

---

## 1.4 Why the Transition Matters (Automotive Context)

| Factor | Conventional (Fossil) | Alternative (Renewable) |
|---|---|---|
| Reserve Depletion | **Finite (~200 years for oil)** | Practically infinite on human timescale |
| CO₂ Emissions | High (≈ **2.31 kg CO₂/L** for petrol) | Near-zero operational emissions |
| Energy Density | High (Gasoline: **~44 MJ/kg**) | Lower (Li-ion: **~0.9 MJ/kg**) |
| Refuel Time | Minutes | Hours (EV charging) |
| Tailpipe Pollutants | CO, NOx, HC, PM | None (EV) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Each Source

### 2.1.1 Fossil Fuels — Combustion Chemistry

The energy in fossil fuels is released via **exothermic oxidation (combustion)** of hydrocarbons. For a generic hydrocarbon $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y$:

$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + \Delta H$$

Where $\Delta H < 0$ (energy released). The energy released per mole is the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** or **Higher Heating Value (HHV)**.

**Why it works**: Chemical bonds between C and H atoms are broken and reformed into stronger C=O and O–H bonds in $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, releasing the energy difference.

### 2.1.2 Nuclear Energy — Fission Physics

In nuclear fission, a heavy nucleus absorbs a slow neutron and splits into lighter nuclei, releasing enormous energy per **Einstein's mass–energy equivalence**:

$$\Delta E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$$

Where:
- $\Delta E$ = Energy released (Joules)
- $\Delta m$ = Mass defect (kg) — typically ~0.1% of mass converted
- $c$ = Speed of light = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s

Example: $1\text{ kg U}^{235}$ releases ≈ **$8.2 \times 10^{13}$ J** ≈ **24,000 MWh** — equivalent to burning ~**10,000 tonnes of coal**.

### 2.1.3 Solar Energy — Photovoltaic Effect

When photons of energy $E = h\nu$ strike a semiconductor (typically silicon), they can dislodge electrons if $h\nu \geq E_g$ (band-gap energy, **~1.1 eV for Si**). The separated charge carriers produce a voltage across the junction.

### 2.1.4 Wind Energy — Aerodynamic Power Extraction

Wind is moving air with mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A v$, where $\rho$ is air density and $A$ is the swept area. The **kinetic power** available in the wind is derived in detail in Section 3.

### 2.1.5 Geothermal Energy — Heat Extraction

Heat flows from Earth's interior to the surface via **conduction** (Fourier's Law) and **convection** (in active volcanic regions). The accessible heat flux at typical depths (3–10 km) is:

$$q = k \cdot \frac{dT}{dz}$$

Where $k$ ≈ thermal conductivity of rock (~**2–3 W/m·K**).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet for Examinations** — All formulas below are **KTU 2024 syllabus core** and appear repeatedly in Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Formula | Description / Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^3$ | Available power in wind (full stream) | W |
| 2 | $P_{Betz} = \dfrac{8}{27}\,\rho\,A\,v^3 \approx 0.593\,P_{wind}$ | Maximum extractable power (**Betz limit = 59.3%**) | W |
| 3 | $\eta_{wind} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{wind}}$ | Turbine efficiency (must be $\leq 0.593$) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G_{sc} = 1367\ \text{W/m}^2$ | **Solar constant** at top of atmosphere | W/m² |
| 5 | $G = G_{sc}\,(1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365))$ | Extraterrestrial irradiance on day $n$ | W/m² |
| 6 | $P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot G \cdot A_{PV}$ | Output power of a PV panel | W |
| 7 | $\eta_{PV} = \dfrac{V_{oc}\,I_{sc}\,FF}{G\,A}$ | Cell efficiency (FF = fill factor) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of a photon (Planck–Einstein) | J |
| 9 | $q = k\,\dfrac{dT}{dz}$ | Fourier's law of heat conduction (geothermal) | W/m² |
| 10 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$ | Maximum thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\Delta E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$ | Mass–energy equivalence (nuclear) | J |
| 12 | $CV = \dfrac{Q}{m}$ | Calorific Value of fuel | J/kg or kJ/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention**: Use **SI base units** in all calculations. Energy in Joules (J), power in Watts (W), temperature in Kelvin (K). Note: $1\ \text{kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6\ \text{J}$.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wind formula ($P \propto v^3$)**: Doubling wind speed gives **8× more power** — this cubic dependency drives turbine siting on tall towers and offshore locations.
- **Betz limit (59.3%)**: No turbine can ever extract more than this — even a perfect, frictionless design. Crucial for justifying why actual turbines achieve 35–45%.
- **Geothermal gradient**: Limits practical heat extraction to depths where $T > 150°$C for efficient electricity generation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Available Power in Wind

We consider a wind stream of cross-sectional area $A$ flowing at velocity $v_1$ into a turbine and leaving at reduced velocity $v_2$ (because some kinetic energy has been extracted). Let the air density be $\rho$.

### Step 1: Mass flow rate entering the turbine

The mass of air crossing the turbine disc per unit time (mass flow rate) is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$$

where $v$ is the velocity at the disc. For simplicity, we use the average of upstream and downstream velocities:

$$v = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

### Step 2: Rate of kinetic energy entering

The kinetic energy per unit mass of air is $\dfrac{1}{2}v^2$. Therefore, the rate of kinetic energy arrival (power in the wind) is:

$$P_{in} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{1}{2} v_1^2 = \rho A v \cdot \frac{1}{2} v_1^2$$

### Step 3: Rate of kinetic energy leaving

$$P_{out} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{1}{2} v_2^2 = \rho A v \cdot \frac{1}{2} v_2^2$$

### Step 4: Power extracted by the turbine

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{ext} &= P_{in} - P_{out} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\rho A v \left(v_1^2 - v_2^2\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\rho A \left(\frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}\right)\left(v_1 + v_2\right)\left(v_1 - v_2\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{4}\rho A \left(v_1 + v_2\right)^2 \left(v_1 - v_2\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Express power in terms of upstream velocity $v_1$ and induction factor $a$

Define the **axial induction factor** $a$ such that $v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$. Then:

$$v = \frac{v_1 + v_1(1-2a)}{2} = v_1(1-a)$$

Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{ext} &= \frac{1}{2}\rho A \left[v_1(1-a)\right] \left[v_1^2 - v_1^2(1-2a)^2\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3 (1-a) \left[1 - (1-2a)^2\right]
\end{aligned}$$

Simplify the bracket:

$$1 - (1-2a)^2 = 1 - (1 - 4a + 4a^2) = 4a - 4a^2 = 4a(1-a)$$

Therefore:

$$P_{ext} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2$$

### Step 6: Maximize $P_{ext}$ with respect to $a$

Define $f(a) = 4a(1-a)^2$. To find the maximum, set $\dfrac{df}{da} = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{df}{da} &= 4\left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] \\
&= 4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] \\
&= 4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Since $a \neq 1$ (trivial solution), we get $a = \dfrac{1}{3}$.

### Step 7: Betz Limit

Substitute $a = 1/3$ into $f(a)$:

$$f\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

Hence the maximum power is:

$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3 = \frac{8}{27}\rho A v_1^3 \approx 0.593 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3}$$

This is the **Betz limit**, and the **Betz coefficient** is $C_p = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Photovoltaic Cell Conversion Efficiency

A PV cell is modeled as a current source in parallel with a diode. The **I–V characteristic** is:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

Where:
- $I_L$ = photo-generated current
- $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current
- $q$ = electron charge ($1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C)
- $n$ = ideality factor (~1–2)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant ($1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K)

### Maximum Power Point

The **Fill Factor (FF)** is defined as:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

And the cell efficiency is:

$$\eta_{PV} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$$

For a typical silicon cell under AM1.5 illumination ($G = 1000$ W/m²):
- $V_{oc} \approx 0.6\text{–}0.7$ V
- $I_{sc} \approx 30\text{–}40$ mA/cm²
- $FF \approx 0.7\text{–}0.85$
- $\eta_{PV} \approx 15\text{–}22\%$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem**: A wind turbine has blade diameter $D = 80$ m. Wind speed is $v = 12$ m/s, air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Calculate:
(a) The available power in the wind.
(b) The maximum extractable power (Betz limit).

### Solution

**Given**:
- $D = 80$ m $\Rightarrow A = \pi D^2/4 = \pi (80)^2/4 = 5026.55$ m²
- $v = 12$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**(a) Available wind power**:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.55)(12)^3$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{wind} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \\
&= 5,317,587\ \text{W} \approx 5.32\ \text{MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Maximum extractable (Betz) power**:

$$P_{Betz} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A v^3 = \frac{8}{27} \times 5.32\ \text{MW} = 1.576\ \text{MW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Matrix Insight**: Note that even an *ideal* turbine can extract only ~**1.58 MW** out of 5.32 MW available — the remaining **70.4%** remains in the wake. Real commercial turbines achieve 35–45% efficiency, giving ~**1.86–2.39 MW** in practice (above the Betz ideal? No — these percentages are relative to $P_{wind}$, so the practical ceiling is still ~59.3%).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Energy Sources (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILES] --> B[CONVENTIONAL]
    A --> C[ALTERNATIVE RENEWABLE]

    B --> B1[FOSSIL FUELS]
    B --> B2[NUCLEAR]

    B1 --> B1a[Coal]
    B1 --> B1b[Petroleum - Petrol Diesel]
    B1 --> B1c[Natural Gas - CNG LNG]

    B2 --> B2a[Nuclear Fission U235 Pu239]
    B2 --> B2b[Nuclear Fusion Deuterium Tritium]

    C --> C1[SOLAR]
    C --> C2[WIND]
    C --> C3[GEOTHERMAL]
    C --> C4[OTHER RENEWABLES]

    C1 --> C1a[Photovoltaic Cells]
    C1 --> C1b[Solar Thermal Collectors]

    C2 --> C2a[Horizontal Axis Turbines]
    C2 --> C2b[Vertical Axis Turbines]

    C3 --> C3a[Dry Hot Rock Systems]
    C3 --> C3b[Geothermal Heat Pumps]

    C4 --> C4a[Hydropower]
    C4 --> C4b[Biomass Biofuels]
    C4 --> C4c[Tidal and Wave]
    C4 --> C4d[Hydrogen Fuel Cells]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Renewable Energy Conversion Pathways for Vehicles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[SUN] -->|Photons| PV[PV Panel]
    SUN -->|Heating| ST[Solar Thermal]
    WIND[Wind] -->|Kinetic Energy| WT[Wind Turbine]
    EARTH[Earth Core] -->|Heat| GEO[Geothermal Reservoir]
    BIO[Biomass] -->|Chemical| BF[Biofuel Refinery]

    PV -->|DC Electricity| INV[Power Inverter]
    ST -->|Heat| ENG[Stirling Engine]
    WT -->|Mechanical| GEN[Generator]
    GEO -->|Steam| TURB[Steam Turbine]
    BF -->|Liquid Fuel| ICE[Internal Combustion Engine]

    INV --> BAT[Battery Pack]
    GEN --> BAT
    TURB --> BAT

    BAT --> MOT[Electric Motor]
    MOT --> WHL[Vehicle Wheels]

    ICE --> WHL
    ENG --> WHL
```

## 4.3 Energy Flow Comparison: Conventional vs Renewable

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CONV[CONVENTIONAL PATH]
        FF[Fossil Fuel] -->|Combustion| HEAT[Heat Energy]
        HEAT -->|Carnot Loss| MECH[Mechanical Work]
        MECH -->|Drivetrain Loss| WHLC[Wheels]
    end

    subgraph RENEW[ALTERNATIVE PATH]
        SR[Sun or Wind or Earth] -->|Direct Conversion| ELEC[Electricity]
        ELEC -->|Inverter Loss| BAT2[Battery]
        BAT2 -->|Motor Loss| WHL2[Wheels]
    end

    WHLC --> OUT1[Overall Efficiency 25 to 35 percent]
    WHL2 --> OUT2[Overall Efficiency 75 to 90 percent]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight from Diagrams**: The renewable path has **fewer thermodynamic conversion stages**, which directly translates to higher well-to-wheel efficiency. An EV running on solar/wind electricity achieves 75–90% well-to-wheel, while a petrol ICE achieves only 20–35%. This is the fundamental thermodynamic justification for the EV transition in automotive engineering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Q: Define Calorific Value (CV) of a fuel. List the CV of petrol, diesel, and natural gas.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

Calorific Value is the amount of heat energy released per unit mass (or volume) of a fuel when it undergoes complete combustion with oxygen under standard conditions.

$$\text{CV} = \frac{Q}{m} \quad \text{(J/kg or kJ/kg)}$$

Typical values relevant to automobiles:

| Fuel | Calorific Value |
|---|---|
| Petrol (Gasoline) | ~ **44 MJ/kg** |
| Diesel | ~ **45 MJ/kg** |
| Natural Gas (CNG) | ~ **50 MJ/kg** |
| LPG | ~ **46 MJ/kg** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Higher CV is not the only selection criterion** — ignition quality (octane/cetane number), availability, and emissions also determine fuel choice.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Q: State the Betz limit. Why can no wind turbine exceed it?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

The **Betz limit** states that the maximum theoretical efficiency of a wind turbine is:

$$C_{p,Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \quad \text{or} \quad 59.3\%$$

**Reason it cannot be exceeded**:
- If the turbine extracted **100%** of the wind's kinetic energy, air would have to stop completely behind the disc, which would prevent any new air from entering.
- Mathematically, the power coefficient $C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$ has a maximum at $a = 1/3$, beyond which any further extraction reduces power.
- The Betz limit is a consequence of **conservation of mass and momentum** in fluid flow.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Solar + Conventional Fuels

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]`**

**Q (a)** Explain the classification of conventional energy sources used in automobiles. Discuss the advantages and limitations of fossil fuels and nuclear energy. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Classification of Conventional Energy Sources for Automobiles**:

1. **Fossil Fuels**:
   - **Petroleum derivatives**: Petrol (spark-ignition engines), Diesel (compression-ignition engines).
   - **Natural Gas**: CNG (compressed), LNG (liquefied) — used in public transport buses.
   - **LPG**: Used in light commercial vehicles.

2. **Nuclear Energy**:
   - Used in niche applications such as nuclear submarines, icebreakers, and certain military vehicles. Not commercially adopted in passenger automobiles due to radiation shielding requirements.

**Advantages of Fossil Fuels**:

- **High energy density** (~44–45 MJ/kg for petrol/diesel).
- **Mature refueling infrastructure** (existing petrol stations).
- **Established engine technology** (over 130 years of optimization).
- **Quick refueling** (under 5 minutes).
- **Long driving range** (typically 500–800 km per tank).

**Limitations of Fossil Fuels**:

- **Non-renewable** — finite reserves (oil: ~50 years at current consumption).
- **High greenhouse gas emissions** (≈ 2.31 kg CO₂ per litre of petrol).
- **Air pollutants**: CO, NO$_x$, unburnt HC, particulate matter (PM).
- **Oil price volatility** affects economy.
- **Geopolitical dependence** on oil-producing nations.

**Advantages of Nuclear Energy**:

- **Extremely high energy density** (1 kg U-235 ≈ 24,000 MWh).
- **Zero operational CO₂ emissions**.
- **Stable baseload power** (independent of weather).
- **Small fuel volume** required for large energy output.

**Limitations of Nuclear Energy**:

- **Radioactive waste** with long half-lives (10,000+ years).
- **High initial capital cost** of reactors.
- **Risk of meltdowns** (Chernobyl, Fukushima).
- **Proliferation concerns** — weapons-grade material risk.
- **Not practical for passenger automobiles** due to shielding mass.

---

**Q (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Photovoltaic (PV) cell. Derive the expression for its conversion efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Working Principle**:

A PV cell is a p-n junction diode made of semiconductor material (typically **crystalline silicon**). When sunlight strikes the cell:

1. Photons with energy $E = h\nu \geq E_g$ (band-gap energy, ~1.1 eV for Si) are absorbed.
2. They create **electron-hole pairs** in the depletion region.
3. The built-in electric field at the junction separates the electrons (toward n-side) and holes (toward p-side).
4. This creates a **photo-voltage** ($V_{oc}$) across the terminals.
5. When connected to a load, **photo-current** ($I_{sc}$) flows.

**I–V Characteristic Equation**:

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Efficiency Derivation**:

The electrical power output at any operating point is $P = V \cdot I$. Maximum power is achieved at the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** with voltage $V_{mp}$ and current $I_{mp}$:

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$$

Define **Fill Factor**:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

For an ideal cell, $FF$ typically ranges from **0.70 to 0.85**. Then:

$$\eta_{PV} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$$

**Sample calculation** (Silicon cell, $G = 1000$ W/m², $A = 0.01$ m², $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $I_{sc} = 3$ A, $FF = 0.78$):

$$\eta_{PV} = \frac{0.65 \times 3 \times 0.78}{1000 \times 0.01} = \frac{1.521}{10} = 0.1521 \approx 15.21\%$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Wind + Geothermal

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]`**

**Q (a)** Derive the expression for the power available in wind. State the Betz limit and explain its significance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer**:

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the **complete derivation**. The key result:

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 \quad ; \quad P_{max} = \frac{8}{27}\rho A v^3 \approx 0.593 \cdot P_{wind}}$$

**Significance of Betz Limit**:
- It is the **theoretical ceiling** — even an ideal, frictionless turbine cannot exceed 59.3%.
- Real turbines achieve **$C_p = 0.35$ to $0.45$** due to aerodynamic drag, mechanical friction, and generator losses.
- It is **independent** of turbine size or design — a universal thermodynamic limit.
- It guides engineers to set realistic performance targets.

---

**Q (b)** Explain the sources and classification of geothermal energy. Describe how it can be used for automotive applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Sources of Geothermal Energy**:

1. **Primordial heat**: Residual heat from Earth's formation ~4.5 billion years ago (~20%).
2. **Radiogenic heat**: Continuous decay of radioactive isotopes:
   - $\text{U}^{238} \rightarrow \text{Pb}^{206}$ + heat
   - $\text{Th}^{232} \rightarrow \text{Pb}^{208}$ + heat
   - $\text{K}^{40} \rightarrow \text{Ar}^{40}$ + heat (~80% of current heat flux)
3. **Tidal friction**: Minor contribution.
4. **Solar storage** (shallow ground <10 m): Used in heat pumps.

**Average heat flux at Earth's surface**: ~**$87\ \text{mW/m}^2$** (global mean).

**Classification of Geothermal Resources**:

| Type | Temperature | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **High enthalpy** | $> 150°$C | Electricity generation (dry steam, flash, binary) |
| **Medium enthalpy** | 90–150°C | Binary cycle plants, industrial process heat |
| **Low enthalpy** | < 90°C | Direct use (space heating, greenhouses, GSHP) |

**Automotive Applications** (Emerging):

1. **Geothermal Heat Pumps (GHP)** for EV battery thermal management — maintaining optimal battery temperature (20–40°C) improves range by 10–15% in cold climates.
2. **Geothermal-powered charging stations** — baseload electricity for EV chargers in volcanic regions (e.g., Iceland, Kenya).
3. **Geothermal-derived hydrogen** — high-temperature electrolysis using geothermal steam.
4. **District heating for vehicle depots** — using geothermal heat to pre-condition buses and trucks.

**Heat Extraction Physics** (Fourier's Law):

$$q = k \frac{dT}{dz}$$

Typical geothermal gradient: **25–30°C/km**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $P_{wind}$ with $P_{Betz}$** — Many students write $P = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho Av^3$ as the *turbine output*. Always clarify: this is the **available** power; the **maximum extractable** is $P_{Betz} = \dfrac{8}{27}\rho Av^3$.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numericals** — Especially for $\rho$ (kg/m³) and $A$ (m²). One missing unit costs 1 full mark.
> 3. **Skipping the derivative step in Betz derivation** — The **maximization of $4a(1-a)^2$** is the most critical step; examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for it.
> 4. **Writing "geothermal = solar" in classification** — These are distinct: geothermal = Earth's internal heat; solar = Sun's radiation. Confusing them shows lack of conceptual clarity.
> 5. **Ignoring CO₂ emissions context** — In automotive questions, examiners expect mention of tailpipe emissions when discussing fossil fuels — this is a 1-mark inclusion that students often miss.

---

## 5.3 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist** — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.

- 🔹 **Conventional sources** = **fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas)** + **nuclear (fission/fusion)** — all are **non-renewable**.
- 🔹 **Alternative sources** = **solar, wind, geothermal, hydro, biomass, tidal, hydrogen** — all are **renewable**.
- 🔹 **Calorific Value of petrol** ≈ **44 MJ/kg**; **diesel** ≈ **45 MJ/kg**.
- 🔹 **Solar constant** $G_{sc}$ = **1367 W/m²** (at top of atmosphere).
- 🔹 **Wind power formula**: $P = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ — **cubic dependence on velocity** is critical.
- 🔹 **Betz limit** = **59.3%** ($\dfrac{16}{27}$); no turbine can exceed it.
- 🔹 **PV cell efficiency** for silicon ≈ **15–22%**; theoretical max (Shockley–Queisser) ≈ **33%.
- 🔹 **Geothermal gradient** ≈ **25–30°C per km** of depth.
- 🔹 **$\Delta E = \Delta m c^2$** is the basis of nuclear energy — mass defect × $c^2$.
- 🔹 **Photovoltaic effect**: Photons with $E \geq E_g$ generate electron-hole pairs at a p-n junction.
- 🔹 **Carbon emission** ≈ **2.31 kg CO₂ per litre** of petrol — a benchmark examiners often quote.
- 🔹 **EV well-to-wheel efficiency** ≈ **75–90%** vs ICE ≈ **20–35%** — key motivator for renewable transition.
- 🔹 **Renewable energy** has **fewer thermodynamic conversion stages** than fossil fuels → higher overall efficiency.
- 🔹 Always state **units** in numerical answers (Marks lost: 1 mark per missing unit).
- 🔹 Always draw a **neat labelled diagram** in 7-mark questions — it earns **1–2 marks** even if the text is incomplete.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — PEAUT411 Alternate Energy Sources for Automobile (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for alternative sources: resource depletion and environmental impact
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need for alternative sources: resource depletion and environmental impact

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Alternative Energy Sources (for Automobiles):**
Energy carriers and propulsion technologies that can supplement or replace conventional petroleum-based fuels (petrol/gasoline and diesel) in road, rail, marine, and air transport applications. These include biofuels (ethanol, biodiesel), gaseous fuels (CNG, LPG, hydrogen), electricity (BEVs, HEVs), and synthetic fuels.

**Resource Depletion (in Automotive Context):**
The progressive exhaustion of finite hydrocarbon reserves (crude oil, natural gas) at a rate faster than geological regeneration, leading to declining availability, escalating extraction costs, and geopolitical supply insecurity.

**Environmental Impact (in Automotive Context):**
The cumulative ecological footprint of internal combustion engine (ICE) vehicles, quantified through tailpipe emissions (CO, HC, NOx, PM), greenhouse gas forcing (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O), noise pollution, and end-of-life disposal consequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 establishes the **"WHY"** before the **"WHAT"**. Examiners frequently test whether a student can logically justify the existence of the entire course by establishing the *causal link* between fossil fuel dependence and the global pivot to e-mobility / biofuels.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **"The Family Savings Account" Analogy:**
> Imagine a middle-class family in Kerala living entirely off a fixed **savings account of ₹50 Lakhs** — earning no new income. Every month they withdraw ₹35,000 to run the household (rent, fuel, food). Month 1 feels comfortable. By Year 5, they must cut holidays. By Year 10, they are dipping into the *principal*. By Year 15, the account is empty.
>
> That savings account is **crude oil**. The monthly withdrawal is **global automotive consumption (~70 million barrels/day)**. The family refusing to find a *new income source* (a job, a business) is the global automotive industry refusing to adopt alternative fuels.
>
> **The smoke from their inefficient wood-stove** (burning fuel inefficiently) is the **CO₂, NOx, and particulate matter** choking our cities — warming the planet (climate change) and damaging lungs (urban air pollution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Numbers to Memorize for KTU Exam:**
> - Global transport sector consumes approximately **~27% of total final energy demand** (IEA data).
> - Road vehicles account for roughly **~75% of total transport CO₂ emissions**.
> - Proven crude oil reserves at current rates last only **~50 years** (BP Statistical Review).

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in This Topic

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| World crude oil consumption (daily) | $C_{oil}$ | **~100 million** | barrels/day |
| Energy content of crude oil | $E_{barrel}$ | **~6.1 GJ** | GJ/barrel |
| Reserve-to-Production ratio | $R/P$ | **~50** | years |
| CO₂ emission factor (petrol) | $f_{CO_2}$ | **~2.31** | kg CO₂ per litre |
| Global atmospheric CO₂ (2024) | $[CO_2]$ | **~422** | ppm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Twin Pillars: Why We Need Alternatives

The motivation for alternative automotive energy sources rests on **two converging crises** that the KTU examiner expects every student to articulate clearly:

#### **Pillar A — Resource Depletion Argument**

1. **Finite Nature of Fossil Reserves:** Crude oil, natural gas, and coal are *stock resources*, not *flow resources*. They formed over hundreds of millions of years but are being consumed in centuries.
2. **Hubbert's Peak Oil Theory:** Dr. M. King Hubbert (1956) predicted that U.S. oil production would peak and then irreversibly decline. The principle generalises to any finite reserve.
3. **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio:** A simple metric defining how many years of production remain at current extraction rates.
4. **Geopolitical Vulnerability:** Over **~60%** of proven oil reserves lie in politically volatile regions (Middle East, Venezuela, Russia). Supply disruptions cascade into fuel price shocks (e.g., 1973 OPEC crisis, 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict).
5. **Increasing Extraction Cost:** Easy-to-extract "sweet crude" is exhausted. We are now forced into deep-water drilling, oil sands (Canada), and fracking — all with higher economic and environmental costs.

#### **Pillar B — Environmental Impact Argument**

1. **Greenhouse Gas Emissions & Climate Change:** Combustion of 1 litre of petrol releases **~2.31 kg of CO₂**. The transport sector alone contributes **~24%** of global energy-related CO₂ emissions.
2. **Urban Air Pollution:** IC engines emit Carbon Monoxide (CO), unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC), Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx), and Particulate Matter (PM2.5/PM10). These cause respiratory diseases, cardiovascular ailments, and premature deaths. Cities like Delhi, Beijing, and London regularly exceed WHO air quality limits.
3. **Photochemical Smog:** NOx + HC + sunlight $\rightarrow$ Ground-level Ozone (O₃), which damages crops and the human respiratory system.
4. **Acid Rain:** Sulfur in fuel $\rightarrow$ SO₂ $\rightarrow$ H₂SO₄ in atmosphere $\rightarrow$ damages buildings, lakes, and forests.
5. **End-of-Life Disposal:** Used engine oil, batteries, tyres, and refrigerants contaminate soil and groundwater if improperly disposed.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **must-remember formula bank** for any numerical question on this topic in the KTU End Semester Examination.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R/P = \dfrac{R_{reserves}}{P_{annual}}$ | Reserve-to-Production ratio (years left) | Estimating crude oil longevity |
| 2 | $m_{CO_2} = V_{fuel} \times f_{CO_2}$ | Mass of CO₂ emitted (kg) | Tailpipe emission estimation |
| 3 | $f_{CO_2}^{petrol} \approx \mathbf{2.31}$ kg/L | Emission factor for petrol | Per-litre CO₂ release |
| 4 | $f_{CO_2}^{diesel} \approx \mathbf{2.68}$ kg/L | Emission factor for diesel | Per-litre CO₂ release |
| 5 | $E_{chem} = m_{fuel} \times \text{CV}$ | Chemical energy content (J) | Energy from fuel mass |
| 6 | $V_{global} = N_{vehicles} \times d_{annual} \times FC$ | Global annual fuel volume | Yearly petrol/diesel demand |
| 7 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{W_{shaft}}{Q_{in}} \times 100$ | IC engine thermal efficiency % | Why most fuel energy is wasted |
| 8 | $1 \text{ barrel} = \mathbf{159 \text{ litres}}$ | Oil industry standard conversion | Barrel $\leftrightarrow$ litre |
| 9 | $1 \text{ toe} = \mathbf{42 \text{ GJ}}$ | Tonne of oil equivalent | Energy unit conversion |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{atm} \propto \sum_{i} (GWP_i \times m_i)$ | Global Warming Potential sum | Climate impact assessment |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Strategic Planning:** India's Ministry of Petroleum uses R/P ratios to decide when to transition the national vehicle fleet to EVs (target: **30% EV penetration by 2030**).
- **Carbon Tax Policy:** The European Union Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) prices CO₂ at **~€70-90/tonne**, making electric and hydrogen vehicles economically competitive.
- **Vehicle Certification:** Every new car sold in India must display a **Fuel Economy Label** and meet BS-VII (Bharat Stage 7) emission norms — directly informed by the environmental impact framework discussed here.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio

The R/P ratio is the simplest depletion indicator. It answers the question: *"If we never find another drop of oil, and continue consuming at today's rate, how many years until the tank runs dry?"*

**Given:**
- $R_{reserves}$ = Proven recoverable reserves (barrels)
- $P_{annual}$ = Annual production / consumption (barrels/year)

**Step-by-step derivation:**

We assume production rate $P_{annual}$ is constant with time $t$. The reserves are depleted when the cumulative withdrawal equals $R_{reserves}$.

$$\int_{0}^{T} P_{annual} \, dt = R_{reserves}$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$P_{annual} \cdot T = R_{reserves}$$

Solving for the depletion time $T$:

$$T = \frac{R_{reserves}}{P_{annual}} \equiv R/P$$

**Numerical example (worked out):**
Suppose a country has proven oil reserves of **50 billion barrels** and consumes **2.5 billion barrels per year**.

$$T = \frac{50 \times 10^{9} \text{ barrels}}{2.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ barrels/year}}$$

$$\boxed{T = 20 \text{ years}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love this exact problem. Memorize the substitution step — a common mistake is mixing up *barrels/day* with *barrels/year*. Always unify units **first**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual Tailpipe CO₂ Emission from a Vehicle Fleet

**Given:**
- $N$ = Number of vehicles in the fleet
- $d_{annual}$ = Average annual distance travelled per vehicle (km)
- $FC$ = Fuel consumption (litres/km)
- $f_{CO_2}$ = CO₂ emission factor of the fuel (kg CO₂ per litre of fuel)

**Step 1:** Fuel consumed by **one vehicle** in one year:

$$V_{1} = d_{annual} \times FC \quad [\text{litres/vehicle/year}]$$

**Step 2:** CO₂ emitted by **one vehicle** in one year:

$$m_{1} = V_{1} \times f_{CO_2} \quad [\text{kg CO}_2/\text{vehicle/year}]$$

**Step 3:** CO₂ emitted by the **entire fleet** in one year:

$$M_{fleet} = N \times m_{1}$$

Combining into the final master equation:

$$M_{fleet} = N \times d_{annual} \times FC \times f_{CO_2}$$

**Numerical example:**
- $N = 1{,}000{,}000$ vehicles (a metro city fleet)
- $d_{annual} = 15{,}000$ km/vehicle
- $FC = 0.06$ L/km (typical petrol car)
- $f_{CO_2} = 2.31$ kg CO₂/L

Substituting:

$$M_{fleet} = 10^{6} \times 15{,}000 \times 0.06 \times 2.31$$

Stepwise evaluation:

$$M_{fleet} = 10^{6} \times 900 \times 2.31$$

$$M_{fleet} = 10^{6} \times 2079$$

$$\boxed{M_{fleet} \approx 2.079 \times 10^{9} \text{ kg CO}_2 \approx 2.08 \text{ million tonnes CO}_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is approximately the **annual CO₂ output of ~4.5 lakh petrol cars** — illustrating why even a single metropolitan fleet must transition to alternatives.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Thermal Efficiency on Fuel Wastage

**Given:**
- Calorific Value of petrol: $CV \approx 44$ MJ/kg
- Density of petrol: $\rho \approx 0.75$ kg/L
- Energy in 1 L of petrol:

$$E_{1L} = CV \times \rho = 44 \times 0.75 \approx 33 \text{ MJ/L}$$

For a typical SI engine with $\eta_{thermal} = 25\%$:

$$\eta_{thermal} = \frac{W_{shaft}}{Q_{in}}$$

Therefore useful shaft work:

$$W_{shaft} = 0.25 \times 33 = 8.25 \text{ MJ/L}$$

And wasted heat to coolant + exhaust + friction:

$$Q_{waste} = 0.75 \times 33 = 24.75 \text{ MJ/L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Only } 25\% \text{ of fuel energy moves the car; } 75\% \text{ is wasted as heat.}}$$

This dramatic inefficiency is a **structural argument** for shifting to electric drivetrains ($\eta_{motor} \approx 85\text{-}90\%$) and hybrid systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**"Define resource depletion in the context of automotive fuels. Why is it a global concern?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Resource depletion refers to the progressive exhaustion of finite hydrocarbon reserves — crude oil, natural gas, and coal — at a rate far exceeding their natural geological regeneration. In the automotive context, it signifies the irreversible reduction of proven petroleum reserves that the global vehicle fleet depends on for petrol, diesel, and lubricants.

It is a global concern because (i) crude oil is a **non-renewable stock resource** with a finite R/P ratio of approximately **50 years**, (ii) the transport sector consumes **~27% of global final energy**, making it the largest single demand driver, and (iii) escalating extraction costs and OPEC-driven price volatility threaten economic and energy security worldwide. Hence, the automobile industry is compelled to transition to renewable and sustainable alternative energy sources such as electricity, biofuels, and hydrogen.

> *(Word limit ideal for 3-mark answer: ~80-100 words)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**"List any SIX major environmental pollutants emitted by a conventional petrol engine and state their primary environmental effect."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Pollutant | Primary Environmental Effect |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | Global warming / climate change (greenhouse gas) |
| 2 | Carbon Monoxide (CO) | Toxic — binds haemoglobin, reduces blood oxygen |
| 3 | Unburnt Hydrocarbons (HC) | Photochemical smog, ground-level ozone formation |
| 4 | Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx) | Acid rain, respiratory ailments, smog |
| 5 | Particulate Matter (PM2.5/PM10) | Lung disease, cardiovascular damage, visibility reduction |
| 6 | Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂) | Acid rain, corrosion, vegetation damage |

> *(Table format expected; one mark per correct pair, capped at six.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – ESE Module 1 Pattern]**
**"Discuss in detail the resource depletion aspects driving the shift to alternative energy sources for automobiles. Include a numerical estimate of the depletion timeline using the R/P ratio."** *(14 marks)*

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Introduction — Finite Nature of Petroleum** *(3 marks)*
- Crude oil, natural gas, and coal are *stock resources* formed over hundreds of millions of years from organic matter under heat and pressure.
- Unlike solar, wind, or biomass — which are *flow resources* replenished on human timescales — petroleum cannot be regenerated within any policy-relevant timeframe.
- The global transport sector is the single largest petroleum consumer, accounting for **~55% of global oil demand**.

**(b) Hubbert's Peak Oil Theory and R/P Ratio** *(4 marks)*
- Dr. M. King Hubbert (1956) modelled U.S. oil production as a bell curve, predicting peak around 1970 — confirmed empirically.
- The general principle: production of any finite reserve rises, peaks, then irreversibly declines.
- The **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio** quantifies remaining lifetime:

$$T = \frac{R_{reserves}}{P_{annual}}$$

- Globally, R/P is currently ~50 years; for India it is far lower (~7-8 years at current production growth).

**(c) Numerical Estimation of Depletion Timeline** *(4 marks)*
Assume: Global proven reserves $R = 1.7 \times 10^{12}$ barrels; Annual global production $P = 10^{10}$ barrels/year (≈ 100 million bbl/day × 365 ÷ 1000).

$$T = \frac{1.7 \times 10^{12}}{10^{10}} = 170 \text{ years (static case)}$$

However, accounting for **rising demand** (~2% growth per year), the realistic depletion window compresses to **~40-50 years**.

**(d) Geopolitical and Economic Consequences** *(3 marks)*
- ~60% of proven reserves in volatile regions (Middle East, Russia).
- OPEC supply shocks (1973, 1979, 2022) caused severe global recessions.
- Increasing extraction cost: deep-water, oil sands, fracking — all environmentally invasive.
- Conclusion: A structural transition to EVs, biofuels, and hydrogen is **not optional but inevitable**.

---

**Question B. [Internal Choice – Alternative to Question A]**
**"Explain the environmental impacts of conventional automobile fuels. Discuss how alternative energy sources mitigate these impacts, with comparative data on emissions."** *(14 marks)*

**Model Answer Structure:**

**(a) Major Environmental Impacts of Petrol/Diesel Engines** *(5 marks)*
- **Greenhouse Effect:** 1 L of petrol emits ~2.31 kg CO₂. Transport contributes ~24% of global energy-related CO₂.
- **Urban Air Pollution:** CO, NOx, PM cause ~4.2 million premature deaths/year globally (WHO 2022).
- **Photochemical Smog:** NOx + HC + sunlight → ground-level ozone (O₃).
- **Acid Rain:** SO₂ and NOx → H₂SO₄, HNO₃ → damages ecosystems.
- **Noise Pollution & E-Waste:** Tyres, used oil, batteries.

**(b) How Alternatives Mitigate These Impacts** *(5 marks)*

| Alternative | Key Mitigation Mechanism |
|---|---|
| Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) | Zero tailpipe emissions; CO₂ depends on grid mix |
| Hybrid (HEV) | 20-35% lower fuel use → proportional CO₂ reduction |
| Hydrogen Fuel Cell (FCEV) | Only water vapour emission; no CO, NOx, PM |
| Biodiesel | Carbon-neutral cycle: CO₂ absorbed during crop growth |
| Ethanol (E20/E100) | Oxygenated fuel → more complete combustion → lower CO/PM |

**(c) Comparative Emission Data** *(4 marks)*

For a typical mid-size sedan travelling 15,000 km/year:

| Powertrain | Fuel/Energy Use | CO₂ Equivalent (kg/year) |
|---|---|---|
| Petrol ICE | ~900 L | ~2,080 kg |
| Diesel ICE | ~750 L | ~2,010 kg |
| Hybrid (HEV) | ~600 L petrol | ~1,386 kg |
| Battery EV (grid: India avg) | ~1,800 kWh | ~1,440 kg |
| Battery EV (solar-charged) | ~1,800 kWh | ~0 kg (tailpipe & net) |

> **Conclusion:** Alternatives — especially when paired with renewable electricity — can reduce life-cycle CO₂ by **60-100%**, and eliminate local pollutants (NOx, PM) almost entirely.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing units in R/P calculation:** Students frequently divide barrels by barrels-per-day without converting to a common time base. Always unify: convert everything to *barrels/year* **before** dividing.
> 2. **Forgetting the "renewable vs finite" distinction:** Examiners award marks for explicitly distinguishing *stock resources* (petroleum, coal) from *flow resources* (solar, wind, biomass). Do not use vague terms like "fuels will finish".
> 3. **Confusing tailpipe CO₂ with life-cycle CO₂:** BEVs have *zero tailpipe* emissions but *some life-cycle* emissions (battery manufacture, grid charging). Examiners penalise one-sided claims like "EVs emit nothing".
> 4. **Omitting numerical support:** A 14-mark answer without a single calculation or data table loses at least 2-3 marks. Always include at least **one numerical example**.
> 5. **Ignoring Indian context:** Kerala / KTU examiners reward mention of **India-specific** data (BS-VII norms, NEMMP 2020, FAME-II scheme, OPEC dependence).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Two-pillar argument:** Alternatives are needed because of **(1) resource depletion** and **(2) environmental impact**.
- [x] **Petroleum is a finite stock resource**; R/P ratio ~50 years globally.
- [x] **Hubbert Peak Theory** predicts production rise-peak-decline for finite reserves.
- [x] **R/P formula:** $T = R_{reserves} / P_{annual}$ — always unify units first.
- [x] **1 barrel oil = 159 litres = ~6.1 GJ** of energy.
- [x] **1 L petrol emits ~2.31 kg CO₂**; 1 L diesel emits ~2.68 kg CO₂.
- [x] **Six major ICE pollutants:** CO₂, CO, HC, NOx, PM, SO₂.
- [x] **IC engine thermal efficiency ~25%** — 75% fuel energy is wasted as heat (justifies EVs at ~85% motor efficiency).
- [x] **Major alternatives:** Biofuels, CNG/LPG/H₂, BEVs, HEVs, FCEVs, e-fuels.
- [x] **Transport sector share:** ~27% of global final energy; ~24% of energy-related CO₂.
- [x] **India context:** ~80% crude oil imported; FAME-II and BS-VII emission norms drive the local shift.
- [x] **Be ready to compare** ICE vs EV emissions with at least one numerical table in 14-mark answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes.** Proceed to the next topic: *Conventional energy sources — properties, storage, and handling of petrol, diesel, CNG, and LPG.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-conventional energy sources: solar, wind and geothermal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-conventional energy sources: solar, wind and geothermal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Non-Conventional Energy Sources

A **Non-Conventional Energy Source (NCES)**, also termed a *Renewable Energy Source*, is a form of energy that is naturally replenished on a human timescale, does not undergo long-term depletion, and is derived from continuous natural cycles. Unlike fossil fuels (coal, petroleum, natural gas), these sources do not rely on finite geological reserves and are largely carbon-neutral at the point of conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The course PEAUT411 frames NCES strictly through the lens of **automotive propulsion and auxiliary power**, not utility-scale grid generation. Therefore, every definition below must be linked back to vehicular application potential.

For an automobile engineer, the relevance of NCES is twofold:
1. **Direct propulsion** — replacing or supplementing the internal combustion engine (ICE) with electric or hybrid drivetrains fed by renewables.
2. **Indirect charging** — generating electricity (photovoltaic/wind/geothermal) that is later stored in on-board batteries or hydrogen fuel tanks.

## 1.2 Solar Energy

### Formal Definition
**Solar energy** is the radiant light and heat emitted by the Sun, harnessed via two principal mechanisms relevant to automobiles:
- **Photovoltaic (PV) effect** — direct conversion of photons into electricity using semiconductor junctions (typically monocrystalline or polycrystalline silicon).
- **Solar thermal** — conversion of incident radiation into thermal energy for heating, steam generation, or thermochemical processes (e.g., solar-recharged thermal storage).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a postage stamp made of special silicon. When sunlight strikes it, electrons inside the silicon get "kicked" out of their atoms and start flowing in one direction — that orderly flow of electrons is electricity. Stack millions of such stamps together and you can power a small car's dashboard fan, or even contribute to charging an EV battery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Solar Metric (ASTM E1036):** The **solar constant** $G_{sc} = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ is the extraterrestrial irradiance on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays at a mean Earth-Sun distance of **1 AU ≈ 149.6 × 10⁶ km**. The ground-level peak value under clear skies is approximately **1000 W/m²** (AM1.5G reference spectrum).

## 1.3 Wind Energy

### Formal Definition
**Wind energy** is the kinetic energy of moving air masses generated primarily by differential solar heating of the Earth's surface, which creates pressure gradients. The usable power is extracted by devices called *wind turbines* that convert the linear momentum of the wind into rotational mechanical work, subsequently converted to electricity by a generator.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of wind as a slow-moving river of air. If you place a paddlewheel in a river, the flowing water pushes the blades and turns the wheel. A wind turbine is just an "air paddlewheel" — the faster the air flows and the larger the blades, the more energy you harvest. A modern utility turbine can sweep a circle as wide as a football field is long.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Automotive Relevance:** On moving vehicles, "wind" is most often an *opposing drag force* to be minimized. However, **Vehicle-Integrated Wind Turbines (VIWT)** — small turbines mounted on the roof, grille, or wheel hubs — are being researched to recover some of this otherwise-lost aerodynamic energy.

## 1.4 Geothermal Energy

### Formal Definition
**Geothermal energy** is the thermal energy stored within the Earth's crust and mantle, originating from two sources:
1. **Primordial heat** — residual heat from planetary accretion and core formation (~4.5 billion years ago).
2. **Radiogenic heat** — continuous decay of radioactive isotopes ($^{238}$U, $^{232}$Th, $^{40}$K) in the crust.

The accessible shallow resource is exploited via *geothermal heat pumps* and *direct-use applications*, while deep high-enthalpy resources drive *binary*, *flash*, or *dry-steam* power plants.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a freshly baked potato straight from the oven. Even after you take it out, the inside stays hot for a long time because the heat has to travel slowly outward through the thick skin. The Earth behaves the same way — its "skin" (the crust) insulates a massive, very hot interior. If you drill a deep enough straw into the potato, you can sip that heat. That straw is a **geothermal well**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Earth Gradient:** The average geothermal gradient in stable continental crust is approximately **25–30 °C per km of depth**. The mean surface heat flow is **~65 mW/m²**, but this can exceed **200 mW/m²** near tectonic plate boundaries (e.g., Iceland, the East African Rift, the Western Ghats of India).

## 1.5 Why These Three for Automobiles?

| Energy Source | Primary Automotive Pathway | Maturity (TRL*) |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar** | PV roof panels → auxiliary battery charging; concept solar cars | TRL 8–9 (roof PV on production EVs by 2024) |
| **Wind** | On-board micro-turbines (research) | TRL 3–4 (mostly experimental) |
| **Geothermal** | Stationary charging (geothermal electricity → grid → EV) | TRL 9 (grid electricity path) |

*TRL = Technology Readiness Level (NASA/EU scale, 1–9).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Energy — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 The Photovoltaic Effect
When a photon of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu$ strikes a semiconductor (e.g., Si with band gap $E_g \approx 1.12$ eV), it may be absorbed if $E_{ph} \geq E_g$. The absorbed photon creates an **electron-hole pair**, which is then separated by the built-in electric field of the p-n junction, producing a voltage across the external terminals.

The single-diode equivalent circuit of a PV cell yields the standard current-voltage relationship:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T} \right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where each term has a precise physical meaning:
- $I_{ph}$: photo-generated current (proportional to irradiance $G$).
- $I_0$: diode reverse saturation current (temperature-dependent).
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (electron charge).
- $n$: ideality factor (1–2 for Si cells).
- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant).
- $R_s$: series resistance (ideally zero).
- $R_{sh}$: shunt resistance (ideally infinite).

### 2.1.2 Solar Geometry Fundamentals
The **solar declination angle** $\delta$ — the angle between the Sun's rays and the Earth's equatorial plane — varies seasonally:

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\!\left( \frac{360}{365}(284 + n) \right)$$

where $n$ is the day number of the year (Jan 1 = 1).

The **hour angle** $\omega$ describes the Sun's position relative to local solar noon:

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (t_{solar} - 12) \quad [\text{in degrees per hour}]$$

The **solar zenith angle** $\theta_z$ is given by the fundamental solar position equation:

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

where $\phi$ is the local latitude. The corresponding **angle of incidence** $\theta_i$ on a tilted surface (tilt $\beta$, azimuth $\gamma$) follows the full Cooper equation.

### 2.1.3 Why the Sun Sets — The Real-World Implication
A car parked outdoors receives **direct (beam) radiation**, **diffuse radiation** (scattered by atmosphere), and a small **albedo** (reflected) component. The total available power on a horizontal surface is:

$$G_T = G_{beam}\cos\theta_i + G_{diffuse}\left(\frac{1+\cos\beta}{2}\right) + G\rho_g\left(\frac{1-\cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

where $\rho_g$ is the ground reflectivity (albedo), typically **0.2 for grass, 0.6 for fresh snow**.

## 2.2 Wind Energy — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 The Physics of Wind Power
Consider a stream tube of air with cross-section $A$ flowing at velocity $u$ through a wind turbine. The **kinetic power** in the wind is:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} u^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A u^3$$

This is the foundational equation of wind energy and explains the cubic sensitivity to wind speed.

### 2.2.2 The Betz Limit
Albert Betz (1919) proved that **no turbine can extract more than 16/27 (~59.3%)** of the kinetic energy in the wind. The reasoning is elegant: the air must leave the turbine with *some* residual velocity (otherwise it would pile up and stall the rotor). The maximum occurs when the post-turbine velocity is exactly **1/3** of the upstream velocity.

The **power coefficient** is defined as:

$$C_p = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}} \quad \text{with} \quad C_{p,Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$

The actual electrical power output is therefore:

$$P_{elec} = C_p \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A u^3$$

### 2.2.3 Wind Variability & The Weibull Distribution
Wind speed is not constant; it follows a **Weibull probability distribution**:

$$f(u) = \frac{k}{c}\left(\frac{u}{c}\right)^{k-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{u}{c}\right)^k\right]$$

where:
- $k$ = shape factor (typically 1.5–3.0; $k=2$ gives the Rayleigh distribution).
- $c$ = scale factor (m/s), approximately the mean wind speed.

## 2.3 Geothermal Energy — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 The Heat Flow Equation
Heat flows outward from the Earth's interior by **conduction** (Fourier's Law) and **convection** (in the mantle and via fluid circulation in the crust). The one-dimensional steady-state conductive heat flux is:

$$q = -k \frac{dT}{dz}$$

where $k$ is the **thermal conductivity** of rock (typically **2–4 W/m·K** for granite, **0.5–2 W/m·K** for sedimentary rocks).

### 2.3.2 Geothermal Gradient & Heat Flow
The geothermal gradient $dT/dz$ varies from ~15 °C/km in stable shields to >100 °C/km in active volcanic zones. Multiplying by thermal conductivity gives the surface heat flow:

$$q_0 = k \cdot \left(\frac{dT}{dz}\right)$$

For Kerala (a passive margin with thin crust), the gradient is approximately **30–40 °C/km**, while in the Puga valley (Ladakh), it exceeds **100 °C/km**.

### 2.3.3 Hot Dry Rock (HDR) & Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS)
Where natural permeability is insufficient, water is injected under pressure into deep hot rock (typically 3–10 km deep) to fracture it, then recovered through a second well — this is an **Enhanced Geothermal System (EGS)**. Temperatures of **200–300 °C** are typical targets.

### 2.3.4 Geothermal Heat Pumps (GHP) — The Automotive Tie-In
For an EV owner's home, a **Ground-Source Heat Pump (GSHP)** can use the near-constant **~15 °C** temperature a few meters below the surface to precondition a garage (and the EV battery — which dislikes extremes of heat/cold) year-round with a Coefficient of Performance (COP) of **3–5**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every high-yield formula you must memorize for KTU 2024 examinations on this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar constant | $G_{sc} = 1361$ W/m² | Extraterrestrial irradiance | Mean Earth–Sun distance |
| 2 | Photon energy | $E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s | Planck's relation |
| 3 | PV cell IV curve | $I = I_{ph} - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT)-1]$ | $n$: ideality, $T$: K | Single-diode model |
| 4 | Solar declination | $\delta = 23.45° \sin(360(284+n)/365)$ | $n$ = day of year | ±23.45° annually |
| 5 | Hour angle | $\omega = 15°(t_{solar}-12)$ | $t_{solar}$ in hours | ±15°/hour from noon |
| 6 | Zenith angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | $\phi$ = latitude | Fundamental |
| 7 | Wind kinetic power | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A u^3$ | $\rho \approx 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea level) | Per unit cross-section |
| 8 | Betz limit | $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical max |
| 9 | Actual turbine power | $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A u^3 C_p \eta_{total}$ | Combined efficiency term | Includes generator, gearbox |
| 10 | Tip-speed ratio | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{u}$ | $R$ = rotor radius, $\omega$ = rad/s | Optimized at design $\lambda^*$ |
| 11 | Weibull PDF | $f(u) = (k/c)(u/c)^{k-1}\exp[-(u/c)^k]$ | $k$: shape, $c$: scale (m/s) | Wind speed distribution |
| 12 | Fourier heat conduction | $q = -k \, dT/dz$ | $k$ in W/m·K | 1-D steady state |
| 13 | Geothermal gradient | $\Gamma = dT/dz$ | °C/km (or K/km) | 25–30 °C/km average |
| 14 | Surface heat flow | $q_0 = k \Gamma$ | mW/m² | ~65 mW/m² global mean |
| 15 | Carnot efficiency (geothermal) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_{sink}/T_{source}$ | Temperatures in K | Upper bound for heat engine |
| 16 | PV cell efficiency | $\eta_{PV} = P_{max,elec}/(G \cdot A)$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical max ~33% (Shockley–Queisser) |
| 17 | Air density (ideal gas) | $\rho = P/(R_{specific} T)$ | $R_{air} = 287$ J/kg·K | Varies with altitude & temp |
| 18 | Specific heat of rock | $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ | $c_p \approx 800$ J/kg·K (granite) | For thermal storage calcs |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar** powers the **Sono Sion** prototype (solar cells in body panels, 30 km/day range) and the **Aptera** three-wheeler. Toyota's Prius Prime offers an optional solar roof for ventilation.
- **Wind** on moving vehicles is largely parasitic, but **stationary wind-powered EV charging stations** (e.g., the "Enercon" concept in Germany) and **onboard micro-turbines** for range extension in hybrid trucks are actively studied.
- **Geothermal** is **indirect but powerful** — Iceland generates **~70%** of its primary energy from geothermal, much of it used to charge EVs (e.g., the **Mývatn Nature Bath EV charging hub**) and even to run **geothermal-powered hydrogen production** for fuel-cell vehicles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Betz Limit for Wind Turbines

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU board exams for alternate energy courses. Master it.

### Step 1: Define the Control Volume
Consider an imaginary stream tube of air of cross-section $A$ moving with velocity $u_\infty$ far upstream of the turbine. At the rotor plane (cross-section $A_R$), the velocity drops to $u_R$. Far downstream, the velocity is $u_D$ (the "wake" velocity). For mass continuity:

$$ \rho A_\infty u_\infty = \rho A_R u_R = \rho A_D u_D $$

### Step 2: Apply the Momentum Theorem
The force $F$ exerted by the wind on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$ F = \dot{m}(u_\infty - u_D) = \rho A_R u_R (u_\infty - u_D) $$

### Step 3: Power Extracted
The power extracted by the turbine is force × velocity at the rotor plane:

$$ P = F \cdot u_R = \rho A_R u_R^2 (u_\infty - u_D) $$

### Step 4: Apply Energy Conservation
The kinetic energy lost by the air between upstream and downstream equals the power extracted:

$$ P = \frac{1}{2}\dot{m}(u_\infty^2 - u_D^2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_R u_R (u_\infty^2 - u_D^2) $$

### Step 5: Equate the Two Expressions for $P$
Setting Step 3 equal to Step 4:

$$ \rho A_R u_R^2 (u_\infty - u_D) = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_R u_R (u_\infty^2 - u_D^2) $$

### Step 6: Simplify
Cancel $\rho A_R u_R$ (assuming nonzero):

$$ u_R (u_\infty - u_D) = \frac{1}{2}(u_\infty^2 - u_D^2) = \frac{1}{2}(u_\infty - u_D)(u_\infty + u_D) $$

$$ u_R = \frac{u_\infty + u_D}{2} $$

> **Physical meaning:** The velocity at the rotor plane is the *arithmetic mean* of upstream and downstream velocities. This is a non-trivial result — the rotor "sees" an intermediate velocity.

### Step 7: Express Power in Terms of $u_\infty$ and the Induction Factor
Define the **axial induction factor** $a$ such that $u_D = u_\infty(1 - 2a)$ and $u_R = u_\infty(1 - a)$. Substituting into the power equation:

$$ P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_R [u_\infty(1-a)]^2 \cdot [u_\infty - u_\infty(1-2a)] $$

$$ P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_R u_\infty^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2 $$

### Step 8: Maximize $P$ with Respect to $a$
Let $f(a) = 4a(1-a)^2$. Differentiate and set to zero:

$$ \frac{df}{da} = 4(1-a)^2 + 4a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) = 4(1-a)[(1-a) - 2a] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) $$

Setting $\frac{df}{da} = 0$ gives either $a = 1/3$ (the meaningful root) or $a = 1$ (trivial, full blockage).

### Step 9: Substitute $a = 1/3$
$$ f(1/3) = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27} $$

### Step 10: Final Result
The maximum power coefficient is:

$$ \boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{(Betz Limit)} } $$

This means at most **59.3%** of the wind's kinetic power can ever be converted to mechanical work, regardless of turbine design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Geothermal Gradient from Heat Flow

Given a region where the surface heat flow is measured as $q_0 = 65$ mW/m² and the average thermal conductivity of the upper crust is $k = 2.5$ W/(m·K), find the geothermal gradient $\Gamma$.

Starting from Fourier's Law in 1-D steady state:

$$ q_0 = k \Gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Gamma = \frac{q_0}{k} $$

Substitute numerical values (with unit conversion $q_0 = 65$ mW/m² $= 0.065$ W/m²):

$$ \Gamma = \frac{0.065 \text{ W/m}^2}{2.5 \text{ W/(m·K)}} = 0.026 \text{ K/m} = 26 \text{ °C/km} $$

**Interpretation:** Drilling 1 km deep in stable crust yields a temperature rise of about 26 °C above the mean surface temperature (~15 °C), giving a rock temperature of ~41 °C — useful for direct space heating but not for power generation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Problem-Solving Strategy for KTU

| Question Type | Key Steps | Typical Marks | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar declination | Use $n$, plug into $\delta$ formula | 2–3 | Wrong sign of sine argument |
| Solar noon hour angle | $\omega = 0$ at solar noon | 1 | Confusing solar vs civil time |
| Wind power calculation | $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A u^3 C_p \eta$ | 3–5 | Forgetting $C_p$ ≤ 0.593 |
| Betz limit derivation | Use induction factor $a$ | 7 | Skipping momentum theorem step |
| Geothermal gradient | $\Gamma = q_0/k$ with unit conversion | 2–3 | Mixing mW with W |
| Compare efficiency sources | Use Carnot limit for each | 5 | Forgetting absolute temperature |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **Mermaid block diagrams** map the energy conversion chains and system architectures relevant to automotive applications. They are drawn as functional flow charts rather than physical schematics for maximum clarity.

## 4.1 Solar PV System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sun: 1361 W/m2 extraterrestrial irradiance] --> B[Atmospheric Attenuation: scattering, absorption]
    B --> C[Ground-Level Irradiance: ~1000 W/m2 peak, AM1.5G]
    C --> D[PV Module: silicon p-n junction array]
    D --> E[MPPT Controller: maximum power point tracking]
    E --> F[Battery Pack: Li-ion or LFP energy storage]
    F --> G[Vehicle DC Bus: 400 V or 800 V architecture]
    G --> H[Inverter: DC to AC conversion]
    H --> I[Electric Traction Motor: drives wheels]
    D --> J[Auxiliary Loads: HVAC, lighting, infotainment]
    F --> J
    I --> K[Mechanical Power at Wheels: tractive effort for propulsion]
```

## 4.2 Wind Energy Conversion Chain (Automotive Application)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solar-Driven Pressure Gradients: cause wind] --> B[Atmospheric Boundary Layer: wind stream]
    B --> C[Mini Wind Turbine: rotor diameter 0.3 to 1.5 m]
    C --> D[Gearbox or Direct-Drive Coupling]
    D --> E[PMSG or Induction Generator: AC output]
    E --> F[Rectifier and MPPT Stage]
    F --> G[DC Bus and Battery Module]
    G --> H[Auxiliary Vehicle Systems: telemetry, lighting, ventilation]
    C --> I[Aerodynamic Drag Penalty: small but non-zero]
    I --> J[Net Energy Balance: must remain positive]
```

## 4.3 Geothermal Energy Pathway to EVs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Earth Interior Heat: radiogenic plus primordial, ~65 mW/m2] --> B[Deep Well: 2 to 5 km depth]
    B --> C[High-Enthalpy Reservoir: 200 to 350 C]
    C --> D[Binary or Flash Power Plant: converts heat to electricity]
    D --> E[Transmission Grid: high-voltage AC]
    E --> F[Distribution Network: stepped down to LV]
    F --> G[EV Charging Station: level 2 or DC fast charger]
    G --> H[EV Battery: charged for propulsion]
    H --> I[Wheels: kinetic energy for motion]
    C --> J[Direct Use: district heating, greenhouse cultivation]
    J --> K[Indirect EV Benefit: reduced grid loading]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Flow: Three Energy Sources Side by Side

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SolarChain
        S1[Sun] --> S2[PV Array] --> S3[Battery] --> S4[EV]
    end
    subgraph WindChain
        W1[Wind] --> W2[Turbine] --> W3[Generator] --> W4[Grid] --> W5[EV]
    end
    subgraph GeoChain
        G1[Earth] --> G2[Well] --> G3[Plant] --> G4[Grid] --> G5[EV]
    end
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `SolarChain`, `WindChain`).
- Labels are plain uppercase text with no markdown formatting inside quoted strings.
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) are used as standalone node names.
- The diagram uses `flowchart` syntax (not `graph`) for modern Mermaid v10+ compatibility.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, PEAUT411]**
Define the term **"solar constant"** and state its value in standard SI units. Briefly explain why the value reaching the Earth's surface is lower than this constant.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **solar constant** is defined as the rate at which solar energy (electromagnetic radiation) is received on a unit surface area oriented perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the top of the Earth's atmosphere, at the mean Earth–Sun distance of **1 Astronomical Unit (1 AU ≈ 149.6 × 10⁶ km)**. Its accepted value is:

$$G_{sc} = 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$$

The extraterrestrial value is reduced at ground level due to three atmospheric effects: (i) **scattering** by air molecules (Rayleigh scattering) and aerosols (Mie scattering), (ii) **absorption** by ozone (UV), water vapor, and carbon dioxide (IR), and (iii) **reflection** by clouds. The net result is a typical peak surface irradiance of ~1000 W/m² under clear-sky, AM1.5G conditions.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for value, 1 mark for atmospheric attenuation explanation.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEAUT411]**
State the **Betz limit** for wind turbines and explain its physical significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Betz limit** states that no wind turbine, regardless of design, can extract more than **16/27 ≈ 59.3%** of the kinetic energy present in the wind stream tube. The corresponding maximum power coefficient is $C_{p,Betz} = 0.593$.

**Physical significance:** The limit arises because the air cannot be brought to a complete halt at the rotor plane — if it were, the air would pile up and block further inflow. The optimal condition occurs when the wake velocity is exactly **one-third** of the upstream velocity ($u_D = u_\infty/3$), and the rotor plane velocity is the arithmetic mean $u_R = 2u_\infty/3$. This single number sets the absolute ceiling for wind energy conversion efficiency and is a foundational benchmark against which all real turbines are evaluated.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for stating limit, 1 mark for value, 1 mark for physical reasoning.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** KTU strictly awards **partial marks only for clearly shown steps**. Skipping the momentum theorem in the Betz derivation (e.g., jumping straight to $C_p = 16/27$) typically yields **zero** for that sub-part. Always write the induction factor definition first.

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAUT411, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive an expression for the **maximum power extractable by a wind turbine** from a free stream of air. Clearly state the **Betz limit** and the condition under which it is achieved.

**(b)** [7 marks] A horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m and operates at a wind speed of 12 m/s. The power coefficient is $C_p = 0.45$, the generator efficiency is 0.92, and the gearbox efficiency is 0.95. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate:
   (i) The available wind power.
   (ii) The actual electrical power output.
   (iii) Comment on the gap between the calculated $C_p$ and the Betz limit.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1:** Define the stream tube and velocities (1 mark). Let $u_\infty$, $u_R$, $u_D$ be the upstream, rotor, and downstream velocities, and $A_R$ the rotor swept area.

**Step 2:** Apply mass conservation: $\rho A_R u_R = \rho A_D u_D$ (continuity) (1 mark).

**Step 3:** Apply the momentum theorem to find force on rotor: $F = \rho A_R u_R (u_\infty - u_D)$ (1.5 marks).

**Step 4:** Mechanical power extracted = force × rotor velocity: $P = F u_R = \rho A_R u_R^2 (u_\infty - u_D)$ (1 mark).

**Step 5:** Equate with kinetic energy lost by air: $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_R u_R (u_\infty^2 - u_D^2)$ (0.5 marks).

**Step 6:** Derive $u_R = (u_\infty + u_D)/2$ (1 mark).

**Step 7:** Introduce axial induction factor $a$ and maximize $P(a)$ to find $a = 1/3$, yielding $C_{p,max} = 16/27$ (1 mark). **Betz limit stated.**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute swept area: $A_R = \pi D^2/4 = \pi (80)^2 / 4 = 5026.5$ m² (1 mark).

**Step 2:** Available wind power (1 mark):
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A_R u^3 = \frac{1}{2}(1.225)(5026.5)(12)^3$$
$$= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.5 \times 1728$$
$$= 5{,}318{,}915 \text{ W} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3:** Overall conversion efficiency (1 mark):
$$\eta_{total} = C_p \times \eta_{gen} \times \eta_{gear} = 0.45 \times 0.92 \times 0.95 = 0.3933$$

**Step 4:** Electrical power output (2 marks):
$$P_{elec} = \eta_{total} \times P_{wind} = 0.3933 \times 5{,}318{,}915 = 2{,}091{,}950 \text{ W} \approx 2.09 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5:** Comment on Betz gap (2 marks): The achieved $C_p = 0.45$ is **76% of the Betz limit** (0.45/0.593 = 0.759). This is realistic for a modern well-designed utility-scale turbine. The remaining gap to 0.593 is due to:
- Tip losses (vortices at blade tips)
- Profile drag on blades
- Non-uniform inflow across the rotor disc
- Wake rotation (residual swirl not recovered)
The combined mechanical and electrical losses (0.92 × 0.95 = 0.874) further reduce output by ~12.6%.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEAUT411, Module 1, 14 marks]**

**(a)** [7 marks] With the aid of a clearly labeled diagram, explain the **photovoltaic effect** in a p-n junction solar cell. Derive the **single-diode equivalent circuit equation** for the cell current as a function of terminal voltage.

**(b)** [7 marks] A solar PV panel rated at **300 W** under standard test conditions (STC: 1000 W/m², AM1.5G, 25 °C cell temperature) has an area of 1.65 m². Calculate:
   (i) The panel's conversion efficiency at STC.
   (ii) The expected output power when the irradiance drops to 400 W/m² and cell temperature rises to 50 °C, assuming a temperature coefficient of $-0.4\%/°C$ for power.
   (iii) Comment on the implications for solar-powered vehicles in tropical Kerala conditions.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the PV effect: Photons with energy $E \geq E_g$ (band gap) are absorbed in the depletion region of a p-n junction, generating electron-hole pairs. The built-in electric field sweeps electrons to the n-side and holes to the p-side, creating a photovoltage.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Draw or describe the single-diode equivalent circuit: photocurrent source $I_{ph}$ in parallel with a diode (saturation current $I_0$, ideality $n$), series resistance $R_s$, and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Apply KCL at the output node:
$$I = I_{ph} - I_{diode} - I_{shunt}$$
The diode follows the Shockley equation: $I_{diode} = I_0[\exp(qV_d/nkT) - 1]$ where $V_d = V + IR_s$ (the actual diode voltage differs from terminal voltage by the drop across $R_s$). The shunt current is $I_{shunt} = (V + IR_s)/R_{sh}$. Substituting:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Define key parameters:
- $I_{ph} \propto G$ (linear with irradiance).
- $I_0$ increases exponentially with temperature.
- $V_{oc} = (nkT/q)\ln(I_{ph}/I_0 + 1)$ (open-circuit voltage).
- $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ (short-circuit current, when $R_{sh} \to \infty$ and $R_s \to 0$).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Conversion efficiency at STC:
$$\eta_{STC} = \frac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{300 \text{ W}}{1000 \text{ W/m}^2 \times 1.65 \text{ m}^2} = \frac{300}{1650} = 0.1818 = 18.18\%$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** At $G = 400$ W/m² and $T = 50$ °C:
- Irradiance correction: $P \propto G$ approximately, so $P_{G} = 300 \times (400/1000) = 120$ W.
- Temperature correction: $\Delta T = 50 - 25 = 25$ °C; degradation = $25 \times 0.4\% = 10\%$.
- Corrected power: $P = 120 \times (1 - 0.10) = 108$ W.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Implications for Kerala:
- **Kerala** has high solar insolation (annual GHI ~5.0–5.5 kWh/m²/day), **favorable** for PV.
- However, **high humidity and cloud cover** during the monsoon (June–September) reduce effective output by **30–50%**.
- **Tropical temperatures (30–35 °C ambient, cell temps 50–65 °C)** cause continuous efficiency loss of 10–16% relative to STC.
- **Recommendation:** PV roof panels on Kerala EVs should be **monocrystalline** (higher $\eta$, better partial-shade response) and **oversized** to compensate for thermal and weather losses. Realistic daily energy yield: ~0.6–0.8 kWh from a 1 m² roof — enough for **~5–7 km of EV range** per sunny day.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Solar constant** = **1361 W/m²** at 1 AU; surface peak ≈ 1000 W/m² (AM1.5G).
- **Band gap of silicon** = **1.12 eV**; photons below this are *not* absorbed.
- **Shockley–Queisser limit** for single-junction Si cell ≈ **33.7%**; commercial modules: 18–22%.
- **Solar declination** $\delta$ ranges from **−23.45° to +23.45°** through the year; $\delta = 0$ at equinoxes.
- **Hour angle** changes at **15°/hour**; $\omega = 0$ at solar noon.
- **Wind power** scales as $u^3$ — doubling wind speed gives **8×** power.
- **Betz limit** = **16/27 ≈ 0.593**; achieved at axial induction factor $a = 1/3$.
- **Real turbine $C_p$** typically 0.35–0.50; commercial wind farms: 35–45%.
- **Air density** at sea level, 15 °C = **1.225 kg/m³**; decreases ~12% per 1000 m altitude.
- **Tip-speed ratio** $\lambda = \omega R / u$; modern 3-blade upwind turbines optimize at $\lambda^* \approx 7$–$8$.
- **Weibull distribution** describes wind variability; $k = 2$ (Rayleigh) is a common engineering approximation.
- **Geothermal gradient** in stable crust ≈ **25–30 °C/km**; volcanic zones >100 °C/km.
- **Global mean heat flow** = **65 mW/m²**; India average ≈ 40–70 mW/m².
- **Thermal conductivity** of granite = **2–4 W/m·K**; shale = **0.5–2 W/m·K**; water-saturated rock is *higher* than dry rock.
- **Enhanced Geothermal Systems (EGS)** target depths of **3–10 km** for 200–300 °C.
