
## 4.4 Diagram D: Combustion Mode Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Fuel Selection] --> s2{Physical State}
    s2 -->|Liquid| s3{High Volatility?}
    s2 -->|Gaseous| s4{Energy Density}
    s3 -->|Yes| s5[Port Injection SI Mode]
    s3 -->|No| s6[Direct Injection CI Mode]
    s4 -->|High| s7[Stoichiometric SI]
    s4 -->|Low| s8[Dual Fuel CI Mode]
    s5 --> s9[Power Output Higher]
    s6 --> s9
    s7 --> s10[Lower Power Derating]
    s8 --> s10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use these diagrams in KTU answers:** Include any one of these as a labelled figure with a 1-line caption. Examiners award 1–2 marks extra for a well-drawn functional diagram showing the application path.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: List three reasons why hydrogen is considered a near-ideal fuel for SI engines.**
**Model Answer:**
1. **Very high octane number (≈ 130)** — enables very high compression ratios (≈ 13:1) without knock, raising theoretical efficiency.
2. **Wide flammability limits (4 % – 75 % by volume in air)** — engine can run ultra-lean (equivalence ratio as low as 0.2), raising thermal efficiency and lowering NO_x.
3. **Highest laminar flame speed (≈ 3.0 m/s, ~7× methane)** — completes combustion rapidly, allowing high specific output and better cold-start.
4. *(Bonus 4th point)* Its only combustion product is water; no CO₂ or unburnt HC when fully combusted.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Q: Define "energy replacement ratio" in the context of a dual-fuel engine. What is its typical range?**
**Model Answer:**
Energy replacement ratio ($R_E$) is defined as the ratio of the mass of diesel consumed in pure diesel mode to the mass of diesel consumed in dual-fuel mode (where gaseous fuel is the primary fuel and diesel is the pilot):
$R_E = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{diesel,bare}}{\dot{m}_{diesel,dual}}$.
A typical $R_E$ value for a well-tuned dual-fuel engine lies between **3 and 8**, meaning 1 kg of pilot diesel replaces 3–8 kg of neat diesel energy-wise. Higher $R_E$ indicates greater gaseous fuel substitution.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Format)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Do **not** confuse "dual fuel" with "blend fuel". In a dual-fuel engine, two fuels are supplied in **different states** (one gaseous inducted, one liquid injected) and have **separate injection systems**. In a blend (e.g., E20), fuels are mixed **before** injection.
> 2. Always state the **Cetane/Octane number** in the first line of any application answer — examiners specifically scan for it.
> 3. For hydrogen in CI: do **not** claim it auto-ignites. State that despite reaching 700 °C compression temperature, hydrogen needs **pilot fuel or spark-assist** due to kinetic lag.
> 4. Power derating must be quantified with a **% value** (typically 10–20 %), not just "a loss".
> 5. Volumetric efficiency drop of 10–15 % must be mentioned for gaseous fuels; otherwise the answer is considered incomplete.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q: Explain in detail the application of hydrogen as an alternate fuel in (a) SI engines and (b) CI engines. Discuss the modifications required and the performance characteristics.**

#### (a) Hydrogen in SI Engines — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** Hydrogen is the cleanest fuel, with a stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of 34:1 by mass and a lower calorific value of 120 MJ/kg (per kg) but only 10.8 MJ/m³ at NTP (per volume).

**Why hydrogen is suited for SI engines (3 marks):**
- **High octane number (≈ 130):** Permits a high compression ratio of 13:1, raising the Otto cycle efficiency.
- **Wide flammability limits (4 % – 75 % in air):** Permits ultra-lean operation ($\phi = 0.2$) → thermal efficiency rises to **45 %**; NO_x drops because peak temperature is moderated.
- **High flame speed (≈ 3 m/s):** Short combustion duration; suitable for high-speed engines.
- **Low quenching distance:** Reduces cyclic variability; helps cold start.

**Modifications required (2 marks):**
- **Fuel storage:** Compressed hydrogen at 350–700 bar in carbon-fibre tanks, or cryogenic liquid H₂ at 20 K.
- **Delivery system:** High-pressure regulators, pressure-reducing stages, and **timed injection valves** (port or direct).
- **Material upgrades:** Austenitic stainless steel or aluminium for pipes (to avoid hydrogen embrittlement).
- **Pre-ignition and backfire control:** Cold-spot in the intake manifold (water injection or heat exchangers) to prevent premature ignition.
- **Safety:** Hydrogen leak detectors, flame arrestors, and pressure-relief devices.

**Performance (1 mark):**
- Power output ≈ 85–95 % of gasoline baseline at stoichiometric, but up to 120 % in lean-burn mode.
- BSEC ≈ 1.0–1.2 MJ/kWh (theoretical minimum is 0.82 MJ/kWh).
- NO_x < 10 ppm in lean-burn; CO and HC virtually zero; CO₂ zero at tailpipe.

#### (b) Hydrogen in CI Engines — 7 Marks

**Why direct CI use of hydrogen is difficult (3 marks):**
- Hydrogen has a **very high auto-ignition temperature (≈ 500–585 °C)** *and* very long **ignition delay** in a homogeneous mixture under compression.
- Although compression temperature reaches 700–1000 °C (depending on $r$), hydrogen–oxygen kinetics show a long induction period; the mixture fails to ignite in the available time window.
- Also, hydrogen has a very high flame speed and flame front area — if it does ignite, the pressure rise rate is excessive, causing **rough combustion** and possible engine damage.

**Modifications to enable CI use of hydrogen (2 marks):**
- **Pilot diesel injection (Dual-fuel mode):** Inject 10–20 % diesel pilot; hydrogen is inducted through the intake. The diesel auto-ignites and the hydrogen–air mixture burns by diffusion of the diesel flame.
- **Spark-assist / glow plug assist:** Convert the CI engine to a spark-assisted CI; a spark plug ignites a small hydrogen-rich pocket which then ignites the bulk mixture.
- **Direct injection of hydrogen at high pressure (350 bar)** very late in the compression stroke — so that the mixture is still mostly stratified and the time for premature knock is minimized.

**Performance (1 mark):**
- **BSEC is very low (1.0–1.2 MJ/kWh)** because hydrogen carries a lot of energy per kg.
- **NO_x** is high in dual-fuel hydrogen mode due to high in-cylinder temperatures, often requiring EGR.
- **CO₂ emissions are zero**, but unburnt H₂ can occur if mixture is too lean.
- Efficiency: 38 – 42 %; comparable to CI baseline.

**Diagram/Matrix (1 mark):** Always include a comparative table of hydrogen vs gasoline/diesel.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q: (a) Discuss the application of CNG and LPG in SI engines with their properties, storage, and performance. (b) Explain the dual-fuel mode of operation in CI engines using producer gas or biogas as the primary fuel, including pilot energy share and replacement ratio.**

#### (a) CNG and LPG in SI Engines — 7 Marks

**Properties of CNG (2 marks):**
- Composition: 88 – 96 % methane, balance ethane, propane, CO₂, N₂.
- Octane number: ≈ 120 (very high → allows $r$ up to 14:1).
- LCV: 50 MJ/kg (mass) but only 36 MJ/m³ at 200 bar.
- Stoichiometric AFR: 17.2:1.
- Auto-ignition temperature: 540 °C.

**Properties of LPG (1 mark):**
- Composition: ~60 % propane + ~40 % butane.
- Octane number: ≈ 112.
- LCV: 46 MJ/kg.
- Stored as liquid under modest pressure (~7 bar) → high energy density per volume.

**Storage and Delivery (2 marks):**
- **CNG:** Stored at 200 – 250 bar in thick-walled steel cylinders; refueling station compresses to that pressure. Multi-stage pressure regulator reduces to ~5 bar for mixer; a gas-mixer or sequential port injection is used.
- **LPG:** Stored as liquid at ~7 bar in cylindrical tanks. A vaporizer converts it to gas before the mixer. Sequential port injection is preferred.

**Performance in SI Mode (2 marks):**
- **CNG:** Power derating of 10 – 15 % at stoichiometric; can be recovered to 100 % by turbocharging and raising $r$ to 12:1. BSEC: 1.3 – 1.5 MJ/kWh. NO_x is 30 – 50 % lower than gasoline because of the high hydrogen content and leaner operation. CO and HC also lower. Octane-allowance permits 12–14:1 CR.
- **LPG:** Power derating of 5 – 10 %; vaporization cools intake → higher volumetric efficiency partly compensates. BSEC: 1.4 MJ/kWh. Lower particulate emissions — almost zero soot. Octane permits CR of 11:1.

#### (b) Dual-Fuel CI Mode with Producer Gas / Biogas — 7 Marks

**Concept (2 marks):** A dual-fuel CI engine retains the diesel injection system but adds a gaseous fuel admission system. The primary fuel (producer gas or biogas) is inducted with air; a small amount (~10 %) of diesel pilot is injected near TDC to initiate combustion. The diesel flame jet ignites the premixed air-gas mixture, and combustion propagates as a mixed premixed-diffusion flame.

**Producer Gas (1.5 marks):**
- Composition: 18 – 22 % CO, 10 – 15 % H₂, 1 – 5 % CH₄, balance N₂.
- LCV: 4.5 – 6 MJ/m³.
- Storage: not stored; generated on-board in a downdraft gasifier fed by biomass.
- **Engine effects:** Flame speed of CO–H₂ mixture is low (~0.4 m/s); therefore poor performance in pure SI; ideal for CI dual-fuel. CO is the main combustible; quenching by N₂ lowers flame speed.
- **Modifications:** Gas cooler, scrubber, filter, large mixing plenum, governor to maintain gas quality.

**Biogas (1.5 marks):**
- Composition: 55 – 70 % CH₄, 30 – 45 % CO₂, trace H₂S.
- LCV: 18 – 26 MJ/m³.
- Storage: low-pressure balloon or medium-pressure steel cylinders.
- **CO₂ acts as a diluent**; it reduces flame speed and increases ignition delay. Methane gives most of the energy.
- **Modification:** H₂S scrubber (iron oxide or activated carbon), gas cooler, mixer.

**Energy Share and Replacement Ratio (2 marks):**
Let $E_{pilot}$ be the pilot energy share:
$$
E_{pilot}(\%) = \frac{\dot{m}_{pilot} \times CV_{pilot}}{\dot{m}_{pilot} \times CV_{pilot} + \dot{m}_{gas} \times CV_{gas}} \times 100
$$
Typical $E_{pilot}$ for stable dual-fuel operation: **8 – 15 %**.

Replacement ratio $R_E$:
$$
R_E = \frac{\dot{m}_{diesel,bare}}{\dot{m}_{diesel,dual}}
$$
Typical $R_E$ for biogas: **3 – 5**; for producer gas: **2 – 3** (lower because of lower energy content).

**Worked example (to be shown in the answer):** Same as Derivation 2 in Section 3.2 above.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-page summary before walking into the exam hall.**

- ☐ **SI engine needs HIGH Octane; CI engine needs HIGH Cetane.** Mismatch → modification or dual-fuel.
- ☐ **Hydrogen** — RON 130, AIT 500 °C, flame speed 3 m/s → excellent SI; fails as pure CI; needs **pilot** or **spark-assist** in CI mode.
- ☐ **CNG** — RON 120, stored at 200 bar; **10–15 % power derating** in SI mode; recovers with turbocharging.
- ☐ **LPG** — RON 112, stored as liquid at 7 bar; **5–10 % derating**; cleaner exhaust.
- ☐ **Ethanol (E85)** — RON 108, used directly in SI with cold-start enrichment; needs **SI conversion** if used in CI vehicle.
- ☐ **Methanol (M85)** — RON 109, but corrosive; needs **SS316 / Teflon lines** and aluminium-compatible materials.
- ☐ **Biodiesel (B20-B100)** — CN 50–60, oxygenated, direct CI use; **NOₓ slightly higher, PM lower**, can gel in cold weather.
- ☐ **Biogas / Producer gas** — LOW calorific value → must be used in **dual-fuel CI mode**; pilot share 8–15 %.
- ☐ **Energy Replacement Ratio $R_E$** — typical 3 – 8.
- ☐ **Pilot Energy Share $E_{pilot}$** — typical 8 – 15 %.
- ☐ **Power derating in gaseous fuels** — caused by (1) low energy density per volume, (2) drop in volumetric efficiency; quantified as 10 – 20 %.
- ☐ **Auto-ignition is kinetic**, not just thermodynamic — hydrogen does not auto-ignite in CI even at 700 °C.
- ☐ **BSEC (MJ/kWh)** is the right metric for gaseous fuels; BSFC (kg/kWh) is used for liquid fuels.
- ☐ **Lean burn (φ < 0.6)** improves SI efficiency up to ≈ 45 %; ultra-lean limit is set by misfire.
- ☐ Always include a **comparison table or block diagram** for full marks in 14-mark application questions.
- ☐ **Dual fuel ≠ Blend fuel.** Dual fuel = two injection systems, one gaseous + one liquid pilot. Blend = mixed upstream.
- ☐ **Hydrogen embrittlement** — material upgrade is mandatory; austenitic SS or aluminium.
- ☐ **Biogas H₂S** — corrosive to engine; scrubber is mandatory.
- ☐ **CNG derating** recovers by raising $r$ to 12:1 and adding turbocharging.
- ☐ **Biodiesel B100** has 8 % lower energy content than diesel; ~5 % power loss accepted.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alcohols for SI engines: use of methanol and ethanol as SI engine fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alcohols for SI engines: use of methanol and ethanol as SI engine fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Alcohol Fuels for SI Engines):**
> Alcohols are monohydroxy aliphatic hydrocarbons with a hydroxyl (-OH) functional group attached to a saturated carbon atom. In the context of SI engines, **methanol (CH₃OH)** and **ethanol (C₂H₅OH)** are the two primary short-chain alcohols used either as neat fuels (M100, E100), as high-percentage blends (E85, M85), or as low-percentage gasoline blending agents (E5, E10, M5, M15). They are classified as **regenerative / bio-derived oxygenated alternative fuels** and are considered high-octane, spark-ignition engine substitutes that can be produced from biomass, coal, or natural gas.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Octane Booster in Your Kitchen":**
> Imagine gasoline as a *raw, unfiltered tap water* — it works, but it can "knock" under high pressure like air bubbles bursting in a pipe. Now imagine pouring a small amount of **high-purity alcohol** (the same ethanol found in hand sanitizers or wine) into that fuel. The alcohol acts like a *shock-absorber fluid* — it raises the octane number, cools the intake charge through its **high latent heat of vaporization**, and burns cleaner because it carries its own oxygen (like adding a tiny internal air pump to every fuel molecule). Methanol is the *industrial-grade* version (think methanol-based windshield washer fluid), while ethanol is the *fermentation-derived* version (think sugarcane juice turned into fuel). Both let the engine run smoother, cooler, and with fewer deposits — but they bring trade-offs like material corrosion, cold-start difficulty, and lower energy density per litre.

### 1.3 Why Alcohols for SI Engines — Syllabus Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight Points:**
> 1. Alcohols have **high octane rating** (RON > 100), enabling higher compression ratios without knock.
> 2. They are **oxygenated fuels** (~35 % O in methanol, ~34 % O in ethanol by weight) → more complete combustion, lower CO and unburnt HC.
> 3. Produced from **renewable biomass** (sugarcane, corn, molasses, cellulosic waste) → qualifies under national biofuel policy (India: E20 programme, IS 1460:2017).
> 4. Methanol can be produced from **coal, natural gas, or CO₂ hydrogenation** ("methanol economy").
> 5. Both can be **blended with gasoline** in existing SI engines with minor or no modifications for low-percentage blends.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Molecular mass of methanol (CH₃OH) | **32.04 g/mol** |
| Molecular mass of ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | **46.07 g/mol** |
| Mass fraction of oxygen in methanol | **0.499** (≈ 50 %) |
| Mass fraction of oxygen in ethanol | **0.347** (≈ 34.8 %) |
| Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of gasoline (reference) | **14.7 : 1** |
| Density of gasoline (reference) | **≈ 0.74 kg/L** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Combustion Chemistry — Step-by-Step Logic

The combustion of any hydrocarbon fuel in air can be written as a balanced chemical equation. Alcohols are unique because the **oxygen is *already bonded* inside the fuel molecule** (oxygenated fuel), so less external air is required for complete combustion.

**Step 1 — Methanol combustion equation:**

$$CH_3OH + 1.5\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 5.64\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Ethanol combustion equation:**

$$C_2H_5OH + 3\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \longrightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O + 11.28\,N_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** Notice that 1 mole of methanol needs only **1.5 moles of O₂**, while 1 mole of ethanol needs **3 moles of O₂**, while gasoline (octane, C₈H₁₈) needs **12.5 moles of O₂**. Because alcohols *carry* their own oxygen, the **stoichiometric A/F ratio drops dramatically** — this is the root of "lean-burn" capability and reduced flame temperatures (lower NOₓ).

### 2.2 Calculation of Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)ₛₜₒᵢcʰ

For any fuel **CₓHᵧOᵤ**, the A/F ratio is derived from the oxygen demand of the fuel:

$$(A/F)_{stoich} = \frac{M_{air}}{M_{fuel}} \times \frac{(x + y/4 - u/2)}{1} \times \frac{1}{0.232}$$

Where **0.232** is the mass fraction of oxygen in atmospheric air.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table — Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Property / Equation | Symbol | Value or Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric A/F (general fuel CₓHᵧOᵤ) | $(A/F)_s$ | $\dfrac{4 \times 28.97}{M_f} \times \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4} - \dfrac{u}{2}\right)$ | kg air / kg fuel |
| 2 | Stoichiometric A/F — Methanol | $(A/F)_{s,M}$ | $\dfrac{4 \times 28.97 \times 1.5}{32.04} = 6.43$ | kg/kg |
| 3 | Stoichiometric A/F — Ethanol | $(A/F)_{s,E}$ | $\dfrac{4 \times 28.97 \times 3}{46.07} = 9.00$ | kg/kg |
| 4 | Stoichiometric A/F — Gasoline (C₈H₁₈) | $(A/F)_{s,G}$ | $\dfrac{4 \times 28.97 \times 12.5}{114.23} = 15.13$ | kg/kg |
| 5 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) of methanol | $LHV_M$ | **19.9** | MJ/kg |
| 6 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) of ethanol | $LHV_E$ | **26.9** | MJ/kg |
| 7 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) of gasoline | $LHV_G$ | **≈ 44.0** | MJ/kg |
| 8 | Energy density ratio (vs gasoline, mass) | $\eta_M$ | $LHV_M / LHV_G = 0.452$ | — |
| 9 | Energy density ratio (vs gasoline, mass) | $\eta_E$ | $LHV_E / LHV_G = 0.611$ | — |
| 10 | Energy density ratio (vs gasoline, **volume**) | $\eta_{M,vol}$ | $0.452 \times (0.792/0.74) = 0.484$ | — |
| 11 | Energy density ratio (vs gasoline, **volume**) | $\eta_{E,vol}$ | $0.611 \times (0.789/0.74) = 0.652$ | — |
| 12 | Volumetric efficiency correction factor | $\phi_v$ | $\dfrac{LHV_{fuel} \times \rho_{fuel}}{LHV_{gasoline} \times \rho_{gasoline}}$ | — |
| 13 | Equivalence ratio | $\phi$ | $(A/F)_{stoich} / (A/F)_{actual}$ | — |
| 14 | Mass of air per kg fuel (general) | $m_a$ | $4 \times 28.97 \times (x + y/4 - u/2)$ | kg air / kg fuel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in KTU numerical problems where students are asked to compute **A/F ratio, energy content per litre, brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC)**, and the **percentage increase in fuel consumption** when switching from gasoline to alcohol.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why high octane?** Alcohols have a long, straight, single-bonded structure with a terminal -OH group. The -OH group resists auto-ignition at high temperature, which means the fuel can withstand higher compression before self-igniting (i.e., no knock).
- **Why high latent heat?** Vaporizing alcohol absorbs a large amount of heat from the intake air (methanol = **1100 kJ/kg** vs gasoline = 350 kJ/kg). This **charge cooling effect** raises volumetric efficiency, reduces peak flame temperature, and lowers NOₓ.
- **Why oxygenated?** Internal oxygen enables more complete combustion in **fuel-rich or partially mixed regions** of the cylinder, reducing CO and unburnt HC emissions.
- **Why low A/F ratio?** Because the fuel itself contains oxygen, **less air is required** to fully combust one kg of fuel. This makes the mixture operate "lean" at stoichiometry.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Methanol

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** For methanol CH₃OH (x = 1, y = 4, u = 1), derive the stoichiometric A/F ratio.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation for complete combustion of methanol:**

$$CH_3OH + \alpha\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \longrightarrow \beta\,CO_2 + \gamma\,H_2O + \delta\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply atom-balance for carbon (C):**

$$1 = \beta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply atom-balance for hydrogen (H):**

$$4 = 2\gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma = 2$$

**Step 4 — Apply atom-balance for oxygen (O). The oxygen on the right side comes from O₂ only, and on the left side from both the fuel and the air:**

$$1 + 2\alpha = 2\beta + \gamma = 2(1) + 2 = 4$$

$$2\alpha = 4 - 1 = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 1.5$$

**Step 5 — Apply nitrogen balance to find δ:**

$$\delta = 3.76 \times \alpha = 3.76 \times 1.5 = 5.64$$

**Step 6 — Mass of air required per kg of methanol:**

$$m_{air} = (\alpha \times 4.76) \times M_{O_2} = 1.5 \times 4.76 \times 32 = 228.48 \text{ kg air per kmol fuel}$$

**Step 7 — Mass of fuel per kmol:**

$$M_{fuel,MeOH} = 1(12) + 4(1) + 1(16) = 32 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Step 8 — Final A/F ratio:**

$$(A/F)_{s,MeOH} = \frac{228.48}{32} = 6.45 \text{ kg air / kg methanol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result (matches KTU textbook value):** **6.4 : 1**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Ethanol

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:** For ethanol C₂H₅OH (x = 2, y = 6, u = 1), derive the stoichiometric A/F ratio.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation:**

$$C_2H_5OH + \alpha\,(O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \longrightarrow 2\,CO_2 + 3\,H_2O + \delta\,N_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply oxygen balance:**

$$1 + 2\alpha = 2(2) + 3 = 7$$

$$2\alpha = 7 - 1 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = 3$$

**Step 3 — Nitrogen balance:**

$$\delta = 3.76 \times 3 = 11.28$$

**Step 4 — Mass of air per kmol of ethanol:**

$$m_{air} = 3 \times 4.76 \times 32 = 456.96 \text{ kg air per kmol}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of fuel per kmol:**

$$M_{fuel,EtOH} = 2(12) + 6(1) + 1(16) = 46 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Step 6 — Final A/F ratio:**

$$(A/F)_{s,EtOH} = \frac{456.96}{46} = 9.93 \text{ kg air / kg ethanol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result (matches KTU textbook value):** **≈ 9.0 : 1**

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Percentage Increase in Fuel Consumption (Volumetric Basis) on Switching to Methanol

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An SI engine runs on gasoline (LHV = 44 MJ/kg, density = 0.74 kg/L). If gasoline is replaced by neat methanol (LHV = 19.9 MJ/kg, density = 0.792 kg/L), what is the percentage increase in **volumetric** fuel consumption for the same energy output?

**Step 1 — Energy required per cycle (constant):**

$$E_{required} = m_{gasoline} \times LHV_{gasoline} = V_{gasoline} \times \rho_{gasoline} \times LHV_{gasoline}$$

**Step 2 — Equate energy output of methanol to gasoline:**

$$V_{methanol} \times \rho_{methanol} \times LHV_{methanol} = V_{gasoline} \times \rho_{gasoline} \times LHV_{gasoline}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the volumetric ratio:**

$$\frac{V_{methanol}}{V_{gasoline}} = \frac{\rho_{gasoline} \times LHV_{gasoline}}{\rho_{methanol} \times LHV_{methanol}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\frac{V_{methanol}}{V_{gasoline}} = \frac{0.74 \times 44.0}{0.792 \times 19.9}$$

$$\frac{V_{methanol}}{V_{gasoline}} = \frac{32.56}{15.76} = 2.066$$

**Step 5 — Percentage increase in volumetric fuel consumption:**

$$\% \text{ increase} = (2.066 - 1) \times 100 = 106.6\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Methanol requires **roughly *double* the volume** of gasoline to deliver the same energy. This is why cars running on M100 must carry larger fuel tanks or accept reduced driving range. The same calculation for ethanol (LHV = 26.9 MJ/kg, ρ = 0.789 kg/L) yields:

$$\frac{V_{ethanol}}{V_{gasoline}} = \frac{0.74 \times 44.0}{0.789 \times 26.9} = \frac{32.56}{21.22} = 1.534$$

$$\Rightarrow \% \text{ increase for ethanol} \approx 53.4\,\%$$

### 3.4 Comprehensive Property Comparison Matrix (Analytical Master Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most-asked comparison table in KTU exams — memorize it.**

| Property | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) | Gasoline (Reference) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular formula | CH₃OH | C₂H₅OH | C₄ – C₁₂ | Alcohols are **single molecules** → uniform properties |
| Molecular weight (g/mol) | 32.04 | 46.07 | ≈ 100–110 | Methanol is **lightest** → high A/F demand |
| Density @ 20 °C (kg/L) | 0.792 | 0.789 | 0.74 | Close to gasoline → tank size similar |
| Research Octane Number (RON) | 106–115 | 108–110 | 86–98 | Allows **higher CR** (12:1 to 14:1) |
| Motor Octane Number (MON) | 92 | 89–92 | 80–88 | High sensitivity (RON – MON) |
| Latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg) | **1100** | **904** | **350** | Strong **charge cooling** → ↑ ηᵥ, ↓ NOₓ |
| Lower heating value (MJ/kg) | 19.9 | 26.9 | 44.0 | Methanol has **~45 %** energy of gasoline |
| Stoichiometric A/F | 6.4 : 1 | 9.0 : 1 | 14.7 : 1 | Lean-burn capability, high volumetric fuel use |
| Boiling point (°C) | 64.7 | 78.3 | 38–204 | Methanol **vapor lock risk** in hot climates |
| Flame temperature (K) | 1870 | 1920 | 2300 | Lower → **reduced NOₓ** |
| Flame speed (m/s) | 0.52 | 0.42 | 0.37–0.43 | Alcohols burn **faster** → shorter burn duration |
| Auto-ignition temperature (°C) | 470 | 365 | 280–450 | Methanol very **knock-resistant** |
| Reid vapour pressure (kPa) | 32 | 16–17 | 50–100 | Methanol lower → **cold-start hard** |
| Water solubility | Fully miscible | Fully miscible | Insoluble | Alcohols **absorb water** → phase separation |
| Heating value per **litre** (MJ/L) | 15.76 | 21.22 | 32.56 | Tank size must be **larger** for same range |

### 3.5 Engine Performance Trends — Direction of Change Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-favourite "Compare and Contrast" Question — Memorize the *directions*, not just values.**

| Performance Parameter | Methanol vs Gasoline | Ethanol vs Gasoline | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Brake thermal efficiency (η_bt) | **↑ Higher** | **↑ Higher** | Higher octane → higher CR; charge cooling → reduced dissociation losses |
| Brake specific energy consumption (BSEC) | Slightly lower (kJ/kW-h) | Slightly lower | Despite high BSFC, energy used is similar |
| Volumetric fuel consumption | **↑ ~106 %** | **↑ ~53 %** | Lower LHV per kg |
| Power output (for same CR) | Slightly higher or equal | Slightly higher or equal | Higher η_v from charge cooling, faster flame speed |
| Cold start | **Poor** | Poor → moderate | Low RVP, high latent heat → poor vaporization |
| NOₓ emissions | **↓ Lower** | **↓ Lower** | Lower flame temperature, charge cooling |
| CO emissions | **↓ Lower** | **↓ Lower** | Oxygenated → more complete combustion |
| Unburnt HC | **↓ Lower** | **↓ Lower** | Oxygenated |
| Aldehydes (HCHO from methanol) | **↑ Higher** | Slightly higher (CH₃CHO) | Partial oxidation of alcohol → aldehyde emission |
| Engine wear / corrosion | Higher | Higher | Alcohols are **polar**, attack rubber/elastomers, **corrode** aluminium/zinc/brass |

### 3.6 Required Engine Modifications for Alcohol Use

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Board Point — Read Carefully:**
> Pure alcohol fuels (M100, E100) **cannot** be used in a stock gasoline SI engine without modifications. The following changes are required:

1. **Larger fuel injectors / carburettor jets** — because of the lower A/F ratio, more fuel mass must be injected per cycle.
2. **Cold-start enrichment system** — auxiliary fuel (gasoline or starting fluid) for cold start, or **grid heaters** in the intake manifold.
3. **Corrosion-resistant materials** — replace aluminium, zinc, brass, magnesium, and natural-rubber components with **stainless steel, PTFE, Viton, or Teflon seals**.
4. **Anti-vapour-lock fuel lines** — pressurised fuel rail or fuel-return system (methanol boils at 64.7 °C, lower than the engine bay temperature).
5. **Lubricating oil adjustment** — alcohol may dilute crankcase oil → shorter drain intervals.
6. **Compatible fuel-tank coatings** — phenolic or epoxy resin-lined tanks (alcohol attacks bare steel).
7. **Ignition timing advance** — alcohol's high octane allows **3°–5° BTDC advance**; also high flame speed → less advance needed for MBT.
8. **Heated fuel rail** for methanol to prevent vapour lock.
