## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A carries 3 marks each, expects a precise 3–4 sentence answer covering definition, value, and significance.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**

**(a) List any three advantages and two disadvantages of using methanol as a fuel in SI engines. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **High octane number (RON 106–115)** allows higher compression ratios and improved thermal efficiency.
2. **High latent heat of vaporisation (1100 kJ/kg)** provides strong charge cooling, increasing volumetric efficiency and reducing NOₓ emissions.
3. **Oxygenated nature** ensures more complete combustion, reducing CO and unburnt HC emissions; also, methanol can be produced from **coal, natural gas, or biomass**, diversifying the energy base.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Low heating value (19.9 MJ/kg)** — roughly half that of gasoline, requiring about double the volume of fuel for the same energy output.
2. **Toxicity of vapours and formaldehyde emissions**; methanol is poisonous if ingested and produces formaldehyde (HCHO) on partial oxidation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(b) Why is ethanol considered a renewable fuel for SI engines? Mention any two of its properties that affect engine performance. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Ethanol is considered a **renewable fuel** because it is produced by the **fermentation of sugars** obtained from agricultural feedstocks such as sugarcane, corn, molasses, or cellulosic biomass. The CO₂ released during combustion of ethanol is **re-absorbed** by the next crop grown for its production, making the cycle **carbon-neutral** in the long term.

Two engine-performance-affecting properties:

1. **High octane rating (RON ≈ 108–110)** — enables higher compression ratios without knock, improving brake thermal efficiency.
2. **High latent heat of vaporisation (904 kJ/kg)** — cools the intake charge, increasing volumetric efficiency and reducing peak flame temperature, which lowers NOₓ formation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** 14-mark question with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student chooses **one of two alternatives**. Both alternatives are given below for practice.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Methanol Focus

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Q1]**

**A (a) Explain in detail the production methods of methanol and discuss its key physico-chemical properties relevant to SI engine operation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Production of methanol:**

1. **From natural gas (most common route):** Natural gas (mostly CH₄) is first **steam-reformed** with water at 800–900 °C over a nickel catalyst to produce **synthesis gas (syngas)** — a mixture of CO, CO₂, and H₂:

$$CH_4 + H_2O \longrightarrow CO + 3H_2$$

2. **Syngas is then converted to methanol** in a catalytic reactor at 200–300 °C and 50–100 atm over a Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst:

$$CO + 2H_2 \longrightarrow CH_3OH$$

3. **From coal:** Coal is **gasified** with oxygen and steam to produce syngas, which is then catalytically converted to methanol — the **CTL (coal-to-liquid)** route.
4. **From biomass:** Biomass is **gasified** to syngas, which is then converted to methanol — the **BTL (biomass-to-liquid)** route.
5. **From CO₂ hydrogenation (future route):** Renewable H₂ + captured CO₂ → methanol; this is the **"methanol economy"** concept.

**Key physico-chemical properties relevant to SI engines:**

| Property | Value | Engine Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight | 32.04 g/mol | Lightest alcohol → low A/F ratio (6.4:1) |
| Density | 0.792 kg/L | Slightly higher than gasoline → minor tank change |
| RON | 106–115 | Allows CR up to 14:1 without knock |
| LHV | 19.9 MJ/kg | About 45 % of gasoline → ~106 % more fuel by volume |
| Latent heat | 1100 kJ/kg | Excellent charge cooling → ↑ η_v, ↓ NOₓ |
| Boiling point | 64.7 °C | Risk of vapour lock in summer → pressurised fuel rail needed |
| Stoich A/F | 6.4:1 | Lean mixture → low flame temperature (≈ 1870 K) |
| Flame speed | 0.52 m/s | Faster than gasoline → shorter combustion duration |
| Water miscibility | Fully miscible | Absorbs water → phase separation, corrosion risk |

**Concluding remark:** Methanol's high octane and charge-cooling effect give **better thermal efficiency** than gasoline, but its low energy density and material compatibility issues require **engine hardware modifications** (larger injectors, corrosion-resistant seals, cold-start aids).

---

**A (b) With the help of neat sketches, describe the engine modifications required to run a gasoline SI engine on neat methanol (M100). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The following modifications are mandatory for M100 operation:

1. **Larger fuel injectors / carburettor main jets:** Because methanol requires ~2.3× the **mass** of fuel per cycle compared with gasoline (lower A/F ratio of 6.4:1 vs 14.7:1), the injector flow rate must be increased, or the duty cycle must be lengthened.

2. **Pressurised and heated fuel rail:** Methanol's low boiling point (64.7 °C) makes it prone to **vapour lock** in hot engine bays. A pressurised (3 bar) fuel rail with an in-line fuel cooler/heater maintains liquid-phase delivery.

3. **Auxiliary cold-start system:** Methanol's high latent heat (1100 kJ/kg) and low Reid vapour pressure (32 kPa) make cold starting very difficult. A small **gasoline or diethyl-ether auxiliary tank** is used for the first few seconds of cranking; alternatively, a **glow plug or grid heater** vaporises a portion of the methanol.

4. **Corrosion-resistant material substitution:**
   - **Seals, O-rings, hoses:** Replace natural rubber and Buna-N with **Viton, PTFE (Teflon), or FKM**.
   - **Fuel pump, carburettor bowls:** Use **stainless steel or phenolic-resin-lined** components (avoid bare aluminium, zinc, magnesium, brass).
   - **Fuel tank:** Use **epoxy-lined steel or cross-linked polyethylene** tanks.

5. **Ignition timing advance:** Methanol's high octane and fast flame speed allow **3°–5° BTDC advance** over gasoline's MBT timing, raising brake thermal efficiency.

6. **Larger fuel tank or extended range module:** To compensate for the ~106 % higher volumetric consumption, the fuel tank is enlarged by ~30–50 %, or the vehicle range is accepted as lower.

7. **Crankcase oil change interval reduced:** Methanol can **blow-by past piston rings** and dilute the lubricating oil, so oil change intervals are halved.

8. **Catalytic converter with aldehyde stage:** A conventional three-way catalyst must be supplemented with an **aldehyde-oxidation catalyst** to handle formaldehyde (HCHO) emissions characteristic of methanol combustion.

9. **Sketch (verbal description for board answer):** Draw a labelled block diagram of the fuel system showing: fuel tank → fuel pump → fuel filter → heated & pressurised fuel rail → enlarged injector → combustion chamber, with an auxiliary cold-start gasoline line and an EGR/grid heater.

**Concluding remark:** M100 operation is feasible and well-studied (e.g., FFVs in Brazil, California); however, for low-blend (M5–M15) operation, **no modifications are required** other than a fuel-system flush, which is why India and other countries prefer the blend route.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Ethanol Focus

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Q2]**

**B (a) Discuss ethanol as a fuel for SI engines. Compare its properties with gasoline and explain the effect of ethanol blending on engine performance and emissions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Ethanol as an SI engine fuel:**

Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) is a **clear, colourless, water-miscible alcohol** produced by the **fermentation of sugars** from sugarcane, corn, sugar beet, molasses, or by the **hydrolysis of cellulose** from agricultural waste. It has been used as a **motor fuel** in Brazil (since 1975, ProÁlcool programme) and is now blended in petrol worldwide (E5, E10, E20 in India; E85 flex-fuel vehicles in the USA).

**Comparison of ethanol with gasoline:**

| Property | Ethanol | Gasoline | Engine Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| RON | 108–110 | 86–98 | Higher octane → ↑ CR possible |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 26.9 | 44.0 | ~61 % of gasoline on mass basis |
| Stoich A/F | 9.0 | 14.7 | Lean operation |
| Latent heat (kJ/kg) | 904 | 350 | Strong charge cooling |
| Boiling point (°C) | 78.3 | 38–204 (range) | Ethanol single boiling point → uniform vaporisation |
| Density (kg/L) | 0.789 | 0.74 | Slightly denser |
| Water miscibility | Fully miscible | Negligible | Risk of phase separation with water contamination |
| Reid vapour pressure | 16–17 kPa | 50–100 kPa | Slightly lower → mild cold-start issue |
| Flame speed (m/s) | 0.42 | 0.37–0.43 | Similar |
| Heat of vaporisation effect | Strong | Mild | ↑ η_v, ↓ NOₓ |

**Effect of ethanol blending on performance:**

- **Power output:** With E10–E20 blends, BSFC increases by ~3–7 % and brake power is largely unchanged because the higher octane permits small CR increases.
- **Thermal efficiency:** Increases marginally (1–3 %) due to higher octane and charge cooling.
- **Volumetric fuel consumption:** Rises with ethanol fraction (E100 needs ~53 % more volume than gasoline).
- **Cold start:** Slightly degraded below 5 °C for E20; E85 requires cold-start enrichment.

**Effect of ethanol blending on emissions:**

- **CO emissions:** Reduced by 15–30 % due to oxygenated combustion.
- **Unburnt HC:** Reduced by 5–10 %.
- **NOₓ:** Reduced by 5–15 % due to lower flame temperature (charge cooling).
- **Aldehydes (CH₃CHO):** Slightly increased.
- **CO₂ well-to-wheel:** Lower (renewable) compared with gasoline.

**Concluding remark:** Ethanol is a **clean-burning, renewable, high-octane** SI engine fuel. Blends up to E20 require **no engine modifications**; E85 needs material upgrades and a cold-start system. India's **E20 (petrol with 20 % ethanol)** target under the Ethanol Blended Petrol Programme is a major policy push.

---

**B (b) A four-cylinder, four-stroke SI engine rated at 2000 cm³ operates on gasoline with a stoichiometric A/F ratio of 14.7. Calculate: (i) the mass of air inducted per cycle per cylinder, and (ii) the new A/F ratio and percentage change in fuel consumption if the gasoline is replaced by methanol. Assume the volumetric efficiency is 0.85, ambient conditions are 1 bar and 30 °C, and the calorific value of gasoline is 44 MJ/kg. Take the calorific value of methanol as 19.9 MJ/kg. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given data:**
- Total displacement volume $V_d = 2000 \text{ cm}^3 = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- Number of cylinders $n = 4$
- Stoichiometric A/F of gasoline $(A/F)_s = 14.7$
- Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.85$
- Ambient pressure $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Ambient temperature $T_1 = 30\,°C = 303 \text{ K}$
- LHV of gasoline = 44 MJ/kg; LHV of methanol = 19.9 MJ/kg
- Density of air at 1 bar, 30 °C: $\rho_{air} = \dfrac{P_1}{R_{air} \cdot T_1} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^5}{287 \times 303} = 1.149 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Part (i) — Mass of air inducted per cycle per cylinder:**

**Step 1 — Displacement volume per cylinder:**

$$V_{d,cyl} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Actual volume of air inducted per cylinder per cycle (using $\eta_v$):**

$$V_{air,actual} = \eta_v \times V_{d,cyl} = 0.85 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-4} = 4.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Mass of air inducted per cylinder per cycle:**

$$m_{air} = \rho_{air} \times V_{air,actual} = 1.149 \times 4.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$m_{air} = 4.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} = 0.488 \text{ g/cycle/cylinder}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result (i):** Mass of air inducted per cycle per cylinder ≈ **4.88 × 10⁻⁴ kg (0.488 g)**

---

**Part (ii) — New A/F ratio and percentage change in fuel consumption:**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric A/F of methanol:**

Using the standard formula (already derived in Section 3.1):

$$(A/F)_{s,MeOH} = 6.4 \text{ (kg air per kg fuel)}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of fuel per cycle (gasoline, original):**

$$m_{f,gasoline} = \frac{m_{air}}{(A/F)_s} = \frac{4.88 \times 10^{-4}}{14.7} = 3.32 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle/cylinder}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of methanol per cycle (replacement, stoichiometric operation):**

$$m_{f,methanol} = \frac{m_{air}}{(A/F)_{s,MeOH}} = \frac{4.88 \times 10^{-4}}{6.4} = 7.625 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle/cylinder}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage change in **mass** fuel consumption:**

$$\% \text{ change}_{mass} = \frac{m_{f,methanol} - m_{f,gasoline}}{m_{f,gasoline}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ change}_{mass} = \left(\frac{7.625 \times 10^{-5}}{3.32 \times 10^{-5}} - 1\right) \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ change}_{mass} = (2.296 - 1) \times 100 = 129.6\,\%$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric fuel consumption comparison (using densities):**

Volume of fuel per cycle: $V_f = m_f / \rho_f$

$$V_{f,gasoline} = \frac{3.32 \times 10^{-5}}{0.74} = 4.486 \times 10^{-5} \text{ L/cycle/cylinder}$$

$$V_{f,methanol} = \frac{7.625 \times 10^{-5}}{0.792} = 9.628 \times 10^{-5} \text{ L/cycle/cylinder}$$

$$\% \text{ change}_{volume} = \left(\frac{9.628 \times 10^{-5}}{4.486 \times 10^{-5}} - 1\right) \times 100 = 114.6\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result (ii):**
> - New A/F ratio (methanol) = **6.4 : 1**
> - % increase in **mass-based** fuel consumption ≈ **129.6 %**
> - % increase in **volumetric** fuel consumption ≈ **114.6 %**

**Concluding remark:** Switching from gasoline to methanol at the same intake-air mass more than **doubles** the mass-based fuel consumption because of the lower stoichiometric A/F ratio and lower heating value of methanol. The driver will see a corresponding **~115 % increase in litres per 100 km**, requiring a larger fuel tank or reduced driving range.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing mass-based and volume-based fuel-consumption increases.** Many students quote "methanol needs double the fuel" without specifying whether it is *mass* or *volume* based. The numbers are different (~129 % by mass, ~106 % by volume). Always state the basis.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the oxygen already present in alcohol.** When writing the combustion equation, students often balance O₂ demand as if it were a pure hydrocarbon. This leads to a wrong A/F ratio (e.g., 9.4 instead of 6.4 for methanol). Apply the formula $(A/F)_s = 4 \times 28.97 \times (x + y/4 - u/2) / M_f$ and reduce for the existing oxygen in the molecule.
>
> 3. **Mixing up RON and MON.** KTU questions sometimes specify "octane number" — the safe answer is **RON** (research octane number). If the question says "sensitivity," then state RON – MON.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the unit of molecular weight in A/F derivations.** Use kg/kmol, not g/mol, when dividing by molecular weight in kg/kg equations. A common error: writing $228.48 / 32.04 \text{ g/mol}$ without unit conversion.
>
> 5. **Stating "ethanol is non-polluting."** This is incorrect. Ethanol combustion produces **acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO)** and NOₓ (reduced but not zero). Always use the word "**cleaner**" not "**clean**."
>
> 6. **Skipping the cold-start system discussion** when asked about M100/E100 modifications. KTU examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for cold-start issues, which is a frequently-tested sub-point.
>
> 7. **Not justifying modifications with numerical values.** Simply listing "larger injectors" is a 1-mark answer. The full-mark answer is: "larger injectors because A/F drops from 14.7 to 6.4 (i.e., 2.3× the fuel mass per cycle)."

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Board Revision):**
>
> ✅ Methanol: CH₃OH, MW 32.04, A/F 6.4, LHV 19.9 MJ/kg, RON 106–115, LH 1100 kJ/kg.
> ✅ Ethanol: C₂H₅OH, MW 46.07, A/F 9.0, LHV 26.9 MJ/kg, RON 108–110, LH 904 kJ/kg.
> ✅ Gasoline reference: A/F 14.7, LHV 44 MJ/kg, RON 86–98, LH 350 kJ/kg.
> ✅ Combustion equations: methanol needs 1.5 mol O₂; ethanol needs 3 mol O₂; octane needs 12.5 mol O₂.
> ✅ High latent heat → strong **charge cooling** → ↑ η_v, ↓ peak flame temperature, ↓ NOₓ.
> ✅ Oxygenated → ↓ CO, ↓ unburnt HC; ↑ aldehydes (formaldehyde from methanol, acetaldehyde from ethanol).
> ✅ Methanol → **double** the volumetric fuel consumption (~106 % ↑).
> ✅ Ethanol → ~53 % ↑ volumetric fuel consumption.
> ✅ Production: methanol from **syngas** (natural gas, coal, biomass, CO₂+H₂); ethanol from **fermentation** of sugars.
> ✅ Required engine mods for M100/E100: **larger injectors, heated/pressurised fuel rail, cold-start aid, corrosion-resistant materials (Viton/PTFE/SS), advanced ignition timing, aldehyde catalyst.**
> ✅ Low-percentage blends (E5–E20, M5–M15) need **no modifications** — these are the policy-preferred route (India E20, Brazil E27).
> ✅ Cold start is **poor** for neat alcohol → auxiliary gasoline tank or grid heater is mandatory.
> ✅ Vapour-lock risk for **methanol** (BP 64.7 °C) → pressurised fuel rail required.
> ✅ Phase-separation risk with water → alcohol-tolerant fuel systems and tank draining protocols.
> ✅ **Methane-to-methanol** reaction: $CO + 2H_2 \xrightarrow{Cu/ZnO/Al_2O_3,\, 250°C,\, 50 \text{ atm}} CH_3OH$.
> ✅ KTU numerical: always state the **stoichiometric** A/F and the corresponding **% change in fuel consumption** — these two answers carry 5–7 of the 14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing processes and comparison of properties with gasoline
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Manufacturing processes and comparison of properties with gasoline

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Alternate Fuels** for automotive applications are defined as combustible energy carriers, other than conventional gasoline and diesel, which can be derived from renewable biomass feedstocks, natural gas reserves, or industrial by-products through well-defined chemical conversion pathways. The **manufacturing process** encompasses a sequence of physical, thermochemical, or biochemical unit operations that transform raw feedstock into a fuel whose physico-chemical properties meet the **automotive-grade specifications** required for safe storage, distribution, and spark-ignition (SI) or compression-ignition (CI) engine operation.

The **comparison of properties with gasoline** involves a rigorous benchmarking of parameters such as calorific value, octane rating, volatility (Reid Vapour Pressure), stoichiometric air-fuel ratio, latent heat of vaporization, flash point, and kinematic viscosity to determine suitability as a direct or blended fuel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must be conversant with the manufacturing route of at least **five major alternate fuels** — *Ethanol, Methanol, Biodiesel, CNG, LPG, and Hydrogen* — and must be able to tabulate their properties against the reference gasoline baseline.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of gasoline as the **"reference currency"** of the automobile world — a universally accepted energy coin. Alternate fuels are like **"foreign currencies"** that need to be minted (manufactured), validated (purified), and exchange-rated (benchmarked) before they can be used in the same fuel pump.

For example:
- **Ethanol** is like brewing a strong, distilled alcoholic beverage from sugarcane — the same yeast that ferments wine is used, but in industrial stainless-steel reactors.
- **Biodiesel** is essentially the "transformation" of vegetable oil (which is too thick to flow in a fuel injector) into a thinner fuel through a chemical handshake with methanol, mediated by a catalyst — much like turning solid butter into pourable oil using heat.
- **Hydrogen** is split from water using electricity, analogous to separating the two yolks of a boiled egg — energy is needed to "crack" the molecule, but clean fuel is released upon recombination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Density of gasoline ≈ **720–780 kg/m³**
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV) of gasoline ≈ **44 MJ/kg**
> - Stoichiometric A/F ratio of gasoline ≈ **14.7:1** (by mass)
> - Octane number of gasoline ≈ **RON 91–95**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Manufacturing Routes — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) — Biochemical Pathway

1. **Feedstock Preparation:** Sugar-rich (sugarcane, molasses) or starch-rich (corn, cassava) biomass is crushed/milled.
2. **Saccharification:** Starch is hydrolysed to glucose using enzymes (amylase, glucoamylase) at **60–90 °C**.
3. **Fermentation:** Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) converts glucose to ethanol and CO₂ under anaerobic conditions at **30–35 °C**:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase}} 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$$

4. **Distillation:** The fermented "beer" (≈ 8–12% ethanol) is heated in distillation columns. Ethanol-water azeotrope limits purity to **95.6 vol%**.
5. **Dehydration:** Azeotrope is broken using molecular sieves (3 Å zeolite) to produce **anhydrous ethanol (99.5%+)** — grade E100 suitable for blending.
6. **Denaturing (optional):** For industrial/automotive use, **2–5% gasoline** is added to make it unpotable.

### 2.1.2 Methanol (CH₃OH) — Thermochemical Pathway

Produced predominantly via catalytic synthesis from **syngas** (a mixture of CO and H₂):

$$2\,\text{H}_2 + \text{CO} \xrightarrow{\text{Cu/ZnO/Al}_2\text{O}_3,\ 220\text{–}300\,°\text{C},\ 50\text{–}100\,\text{bar}} \text{CH}_3\text{OH}$$

The syngas itself is generated by **steam reforming of natural gas**:

$$\text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2$$

This is the dominant commercial route. An emerging green route uses **CO₂ hydrogenation** (captured CO₂ + green H₂).

### 2.1.3 Biodiesel (FAME — Fatty Acid Methyl Esters) — Transesterification

Vegetable oil (triglyceride) reacts with methanol in the presence of a **base catalyst** (NaOH or KOH) to yield biodiesel + glycerol:

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{NaOH}]{60\,°\text{C}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

**Why this reaction?** Triglycerides are viscous (≈ 40 mm²/s at 40 °C) and cause injector coking. Transesterification reduces viscosity to ≈ **4–6 mm²/s**, close to diesel.

### 2.1.4 Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)

1. **Extraction:** Natural gas (≈ 85–95% methane) is extracted from underground reservoirs.
2. **Processing:** Removal of H₂S, CO₂, water, and heavier hydrocarbons (NGL recovery) in amine absorption and glycol dehydration units.
3. **Compression:** Pressurized to **20–25 MPa** for vehicle storage at **ambient temperature**.
4. **Distribution:** Transported via pipelines or as **LNG (–162 °C)** for long distances.

### 2.1.5 Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)

Recovered as a by-product during **crude oil refining** (from catalytic cracking and hydrocracking units) and also extracted from natural gas processing. Composed primarily of **propane (C₃H₈) and butane (C₄H₁₀)**. Stored as a liquid under moderate pressure (≈ **0.7–0.9 MPa** at ambient temperature).

### 2.1.6 Hydrogen (H₂) — The "Universal Fuel"

- **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR):** Dominant commercial route; CH₄ + 2H₂O → CO₂ + 4H₂
- **Electrolysis:** 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂ (green H₂ if powered by renewables)
- **Partial Oxidation, Biomass Gasification, Photo-electrochemical** (emerging routes)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.2.1 Energy & Combustion Relations

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Mass-based A/F ratio | $(A/F)_m = \dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$ | dimensionless | Gasoline stoichiometric = 14.7 |
| Mol-based A/F ratio | $(A/F)_{mol} = \dfrac{n_{\text{air}}}{n_{\text{fuel}}}$ | dimensionless | Use for gaseous fuels |
| Energy density (volumetric) | $E_v = \rho \times \text{LHV}$ | MJ/L | Critical for range estimation |
| Calorific equivalence ratio | $\phi_{eq} = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{\text{fuel}}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{gasoline}}} \times \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fuel}}}{\rho_{\text{gasoline}}}$ | dimensionless | Used for blend-volume calculations |
| Brake specific fuel consumption | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better |

### 2.2.2 Octane & Cetane Relationships

| Parameter | Definition | Value (Gasoline) |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Octane Number (RON) | Knock resistance of SI fuels | 91–95 |
| Motor Octane Number (MON) | Knock resistance under high-speed conditions | 82–85 |
| Cetane Number (CN) | Ignition delay for CI fuels | 5–20 (gasoline ignites poorly in CI) |

### 2.2.3 Property Comparison Matrix (High-Yield)

| Property | Gasoline | Ethanol (E100) | Methanol | Biodiesel | CNG | LPG | Hydrogen |
|----------|----------|----------------|----------|-----------|-----|-----|----------|
| Formula | C₄–C₁₂ | C₂H₅OH | CH₃OH | FAME (C₁₇–C₁₉) | CH₄ (≈ 95%) | C₃H₈ + C₄H₁₀ | H₂ |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | **44** | 26.9 | 19.9 | 37.2 | 50.0 | 46.0 | 120.0 |
| Density (kg/m³) | 750 | 789 | 792 | 880 | 0.72 (kg/m³ gas) | 510 (liq) | 71 (liq at –253°C) |
| RON | 95 | **108** | 106–112 | — | 120 | 96–111 | 130+ |
| Stoich. A/F | **14.7** | 9.0 | 6.4 | 12.5 | 17.2 | 15.6 | 34.3 |
| Latent heat (kJ/kg) | 305 | 904 | 1100 | ≈ 300 | 509 | 426 | 445 |
| Flash point (°C) | –43 | **13** | 11 | 130–170 | –188 | –60 | < –253 |
| Auto-ignition (°C) | 257 | 420 | 470 | ≈ 300 | 540 | 450 | 500 |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In automotive practice, the *volumetric energy density* dictates the **vehicle range** per tank, while the *stoichiometric A/F ratio* dictates **carburettor/injector calibration** when switching fuels. A high latent heat of vaporization (like ethanol's 904 kJ/kg) cools the intake charge, increasing volumetric efficiency and knocking resistance — this is why ethanol is a favoured SI fuel.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flex-Fuel Vehicles (FFVs)** in Brazil and the US run on E85 (85% ethanol + 15% gasoline) — the high RON (108) of ethanol enables higher compression ratios (up to 13:1) for thermal efficiency gains of 8–10%.
- **CNG retrofits** in Delhi NCR reduce PM emissions by 70% and NOx by 35% versus BS-IV diesel — the high hydrogen-to-carbon ratio of methane yields a cleaner combustion.
- **Biodiesel (B20)** is approved under ASTM D7467 for blending; its high cetane number improves CI cold-start and reduces soot.
- **Hydrogen FCVs (e.g., Toyota Mirai)** use 700-bar Type IV tanks with carbon-fibre overwrap — the extremely low density of gaseous H₂ (≈ 0.09 kg/m³ at NTP) is the central engineering challenge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air Requirement of Methanol

Combustion equation for methanol:

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + a\,\text{O}_2 + 3.76\,a\,\text{N}_2 \rightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.76\,a\,\text{N}_2$$

**Step 1:** Balance Carbon. 1 C atom on left → $b = 1$ CO₂ molecule.

**Step 2:** Balance Hydrogen. 4 H atoms on left → $c = 2$ H₂O molecules.

**Step 3:** Balance Oxygen. Left has 1 (from methanol) + $2a$ (from O₂) = 4 oxygen atoms. Right has $1 \times 2 + 2 \times 1 = 4$ oxygen atoms. Thus:

$$1 + 2a = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 1.5\,\text{mol O}_2$$

**Step 4:** Air required per mol of methanol: $n_{\text{air}} = 4.76 \times a = 4.76 \times 1.5 = 7.14\,\text{mol}$

**Step 5:** Mass-based stoichiometric A/F ratio:

$$(A/F)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{air}} \times M_{\text{air}}}{M_{\text{fuel}}} = \dfrac{7.14 \times 28.97}{32.04} = 6.46$$

This matches the **accepted value of 6.4** in standard tables — ✓ verification.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calorific Equivalence for Ethanol–Gasoline Blend (E10)

**Given:** E10 blend = 10% ethanol (by volume) + 90% gasoline.

**Step 1:** Volumetric energy density of each component.

$$E_{v,\text{eth}} = \rho_{\text{eth}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{eth}} = 789 \times 26.9 = 21{,}224\,\text{kJ/L}$$

$$E_{v,\text{gas}} = 750 \times 44{,}000 = 33{,}000\,\text{kJ/L}$$

**Step 2:** Energy density of E10 blend (linear volumetric mixing — no significant volume change on blending for this order of accuracy).

$$E_{v,\text{E10}} = 0.10 \times 21{,}224 + 0.90 \times 33{,}000 = 2122.4 + 29{,}700 = 31{,}822\,\text{kJ/L}$$

**Step 3:** Percentage energy drop versus pure gasoline.

$$\Delta E\% = \dfrac{33{,}000 - 31{,}822}{33{,}000} \times 100 = 3.57\%$$

**Result:** E10 retains ≈ 96.4% of the energy of pure gasoline. The fuel economy penalty on a vehicle running E10 is therefore **~3–4%** — an important KTU numerical.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Fuel Consumption Correction for CNG

**Given:** An engine produces 50 kW brake power on gasoline, BSFC = 0.30 kg/kWh.

**Step 1:** Mass flow rate of gasoline.

$$\dot{m}_{\text{gas}} = \text{BSFC} \times P_b = 0.30 \times 50 = 15\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 2:** Volume flow rate of gasoline.

$$\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{gas}}}{\rho_{\text{gas}}} = \dfrac{15}{750} = 0.02\,\text{m}^3/\text{h} = 20\,\text{L/h}$$

**Step 3:** On switching to CNG, BSFC (mass-based) increases due to lower calorific value per kg. But for CNG:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{CNG}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{gas}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{gas}}}{\eta_{\text{CNG}} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{CNG}}}$$

Assuming thermal efficiency ratio ≈ 1.0:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{CNG}} = 15 \times \dfrac{44}{50} = 13.2\,\text{kg/h}$$

**Step 4:** Volume of CNG at standard conditions (gas density 0.72 kg/m³):

$$\dot{V}_{\text{CNG, STP}} = \dfrac{13.2}{0.72} = 18.33\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

This is why CNG tanks are large — the **gas occupies ~917× more volume** than an equivalent-mass gasoline charge.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Property

| Engineering Decision | Key Property Used | Why It Matters |
|----------------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Range prediction | Volumetric energy density | Tank volume is fixed |
| Cold-start behaviour | Flash point + RVP | Low flash fuels vaporize easily |
| Knock resistance | RON/MON | Higher RON permits higher CR |
| Material compatibility | Sulphur content, water solubility | LPG/H₂ require special alloys |
| Storage pressure | Critical temperature | CNG vs LNG selection |
| CI applicability | Cetane number | CN < 40 → not suitable for CI |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Ethanol Manufacturing Route (Moliohydration + Fermentation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sugarcane or Molasses Feedstock] --> A2[Crushing and Juice Extraction]
    A2 --> A3[Sugar-Rich Syrup]
    A3 --> A4[Fermentation Reactor: 30 to 35 degC, 48 to 72 h, Yeast]
    A4 --> A5[Fermented Beer: 8 to 12 percent Ethanol]
    A5 --> A6[Distillation Column]
    A6 --> A7[Ethanol-Water Azeotrope: 95.6 percent]
    A7 --> A8[Molecular Sieve Dehydration Unit]
    A8 --> A9[Anhydrous Ethanol: 99.5 to 99.9 percent]
    A9 --> A10[Denaturant Addition: 2 to 5 percent Gasoline]
    A10 --> A11[Automotive Grade Ethanol E100 Storage]
    A5 --> A12[Spent Wash: By-Product to Biogas Plant]
    A12 --> A4
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Biodiesel Transesterification Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Vegetable Oil Triglyceride Feed] --> B2[Pre-treatment: Degumming and Deacidification]
    B2 --> B3[Reactor: 60 degC, 1 atm, NaOH Catalyst]
    M1[Methanol Feed] --> B3
    B3 --> B4[Transesterification: 3 FAME plus Glycerol]
    B4 --> B5[Decanter: Gravity Separation]
    B5 --> B6[Biodiesel FAME Layer]
    B5 --> B7[Crude Glycerol Layer]
    B6 --> B8[Water Wash Column]
    B8 --> B9[Drying under Vacuum]
    B9 --> B10[Filtered Biodiesel Product B100]
    B7 --> B11[Glycerol Purification: Pharma or Soap Grade]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparative Property Map of Fuels vs Gasoline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Gasoline Baseline: LHV 44 MJ per kg, RON 95, A by F 14.7]
    C1 --> C2[Ethanol: LHV 26.9 MJ per kg, RON 108, A by F 9.0]
    C1 --> C3[Methanol: LHV 19.9 MJ per kg, RON 110, A by F 6.4]
    C1 --> C4[Biodiesel: LHV 37.2 MJ per kg, CN 50, A by F 12.5]
    C1 --> C5[CNG: LHV 50 MJ per kg, RON 120, A by F 17.2]
    C1 --> C6[LPG: LHV 46 MJ per kg, RON 100, A by F 15.6]
    C1 --> C7[Hydrogen: LHV 120 MJ per kg, RON 130, A by F 34.3]
    C2 --> D1[Higher Octane enables Higher CR]
    C5 --> D2[Cleaner Combustion due to H to C Ratio]
    C4 --> D3[Renewable and Biodegradable]
    C7 --> D4[Zero Carbon Emissions, FCV or H2 ICE]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture — Fuel Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: New Vehicle Powertrain Design] --> S1{Engine Type}
    S1 -->|SI Engine| S2{RON requirement above 95}
    S1 -->|CI Engine| S3{Feedstock Available}
    S2 -->|Yes| S4[Select Ethanol E85 or Methanol M85]
    S2 -->|No| S5[Use Gasoline E10 or E20]
    S3 -->|Vegetable Oil| S6[Use Biodiesel B20 to B100]
    S3 -->|Natural Gas| S7[Use CNG or LNG]
    S3 -->|Refinery Off Gas| S8[Use LPG Autogas]
    S3 -->|Renewable Electricity| S9[Use Green Hydrogen in FCV]
    S4 --> S10[Engine Calibration: Stoich A by F and Injection Timing]
    S5 --> S10
    S6 --> S11[Engine Calibration: Cetane and Viscosity]
    S7 --> S12[Engine Calibration: High Pressure Regulator and Timing Advance]
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S13[Fuel Cell Stack and Power Electronics Tuning]
    S10 --> S14[Emission Compliance Check]
    S11 --> S14
    S12 --> S14
    S13 --> S14
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** Each block represents a *process unit* (a real physical plant component) or a *design decision node* (an engineering choice). The arrows denote material flows or logical transitions. This abstraction allows students to reproduce a complete fuel-value chain in a single KTU exam answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the four main manufacturing routes for producing ethanol from biomass and identify which step limits the maximum concentration achievable in simple distillation.**

**Model Answer:**
The four main routes are: (i) fermentation of sugars, (ii) fermentation of starch (after saccharification), (iii) fermentation of cellulose (after hydrolysis), and (iv) ethylene hydration (synthetic route). **The ethanol-water azeotrope at 95.6% (by volume)** limits simple distillation. To obtain anhydrous ethanol (≥ 99.5%), molecular sieve dehydration or azeotropic distillation with benzene/cyclohexane is required.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Compare the octane number, calorific value, and stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of methanol with gasoline. State one advantage and one disadvantage of methanol as an automotive fuel.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Gasoline | Methanol |
|----------|----------|----------|
| RON | 91–95 | 106–112 |
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 44 | 19.9 |
| Stoich. A/F | 14.7 | 6.4 |

**Advantage:** Higher RON permits higher compression ratio → better thermal efficiency.  
**Disadvantage:** Lower calorific value → nearly **2.2× larger fuel mass** needed for the same energy output; also corrosive to some metals and hygroscopic.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a process flow diagram, describe the **transesterification process** for manufacturing biodiesel from vegetable oil. State the role of the catalyst and explain why biodiesel viscosity is much lower than that of the parent oil. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Tabulate the following properties of **gasoline, ethanol, methanol, biodiesel, and CNG**: Lower Heating Value, Octane Rating (or Cetane where applicable), Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass basis), Flash Point, and Auto-ignition Temperature. Comment on the suitability of each fuel for SI engines. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Transesterification Process:**

1. **Feed preparation:** Refined vegetable oil is pre-heated to **60 °C** to reduce viscosity.
2. **Mixing of reactants:** Methanol (6:1 molar ratio to oil) is mixed with **NaOH (1% w/w of oil)** to form sodium methoxide (the active catalyst).
3. **Reaction:** The mixture is held in a stirred reactor at **60–65 °C, atmospheric pressure, for 1–2 hours**:

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{NaOH}]{60\,°\text{C}} 3\,\text{R-COO-CH}_3 + \text{Glycerol}$$

4. **Phase separation:** The mixture is transferred to a decanter where **biodiesel (FAME) floats** on top of the dense **glycerol layer** (due to density difference, ≈ 880 vs 1260 kg/m³).
5. **Purification:** Biodiesel is water-washed to remove soap and residual methanol, then dried under vacuum.
6. **Glycerol recovery:** The by-product is purified to pharmaceutical grade (extra revenue stream).

**Role of catalyst:** NaOH deprotonates methanol to form methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻), which is a strong nucleophile that attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride. Without catalyst, the reaction rate is impractically slow.

**Why viscosity drops:** The triglyceride molecule is a **triester of glycerol** (MW ≈ 880 g/mol) — a large molecule with long-chain fatty acids entangled together. Transesterification cleaves it into **three smaller FAME molecules** (MW ≈ 290 g/mol each). The shorter, more branched FAME molecules slip past each other easily, reducing kinematic viscosity from ≈ 40 mm²/s to ≈ **4–6 mm²/s** — comparable to diesel.

#### Model Solution to (b):

| Property | Gasoline | Ethanol | Methanol | Biodiesel (FAME) | CNG |
|----------|----------|---------|----------|------------------|-----|
| LHV (MJ/kg) | 44 | 26.9 | 19.9 | 37.2 | 50.0 |
| RON (SI) | 95 | 108 | 110 | N/A (CN ≈ 50) | 120 |
| Stoich. A/F | 14.7 | 9.0 | 6.4 | 12.5 | 17.2 |
| Flash point (°C) | –43 | 13 | 11 | 130–170 | –188 |
| Auto-ignition (°C) | 257 | 420 | 470 | ≈ 300 | 540 |

**Suitability for SI engines:**
- **Gasoline:** Reference baseline — fully suitable.
- **Ethanol:** Excellent — high RON enables higher CR; needs fuel-system modification (corrosion-resistant materials).
- **Methanol:** Excellent — very high RON, but corrosive and toxic vapour; requires SS fuel lines.
- **Biodiesel:** Unsuitable directly in SI engines (poor volatility, high flash point); only usable in CI engines or as a small percentage additive.
- **CNG:** Excellent — gaseous fuel, high RON, requires pressurized tank and gas mixer/injector.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the **steam reforming process** for manufacturing hydrogen. Write the balanced chemical equations. List two methods of on-board hydrogen storage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flex-fuel vehicle runs on **E20 (20% ethanol + 80% gasoline by volume)**. The vehicle delivers a brake power of 60 kW. The brake specific energy consumption (BSEC) of the engine on pure gasoline is 9.5 MJ/kWh. Assuming the engine BSEC remains the same on E20, calculate the **mass flow rate and volume flow rate** of the fuel blend. (Densities: gasoline = 750 kg/m³, ethanol = 789 kg/m³; LHV: gasoline = 44 MJ/kg, ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg.) **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Steam Reforming of Methane (SMR):**

$$ \text{(i) Reforming:} \quad \text{CH}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{700\text{–}850\,°\text{C},\ \text{Ni cat.}} \text{CO} + 3\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +206\,\text{kJ/mol} $$

$$ \text{(ii) Water-Gas Shift:} \quad \text{CO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{200\text{–}450\,°\text{C}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = -41\,\text{kJ/mol} $$

$$ \text{(iii) Overall:} \quad \text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{H}_2 \quad \Delta H = +165\,\text{kJ/mol} $$

The CO₂ produced is captured (e.g., amine scrubbing) for "blue hydrogen" classification. When the source H₂O/heat is renewable, this becomes "green hydrogen" (in the strict sense, "green" is reserved for electrolytic routes, but emerging taxonomies accept bio-SMR).

**On-board H₂ storage methods:**
1. **700-bar compressed gaseous hydrogen (CGH₂)** in Type IV tanks — carbon-fibre overwrap on polymer liner.
2. **Liquid hydrogen (LH₂)** cryogenic storage at –253 °C — high energy density but boil-off losses.
3. **Metal hydrides** (e.g., LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂) — chemical absorption; lower pressure but heavy.
4. **Carbon nanotubes / MOFs** — adsorption-based, still under R&D.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Energy required per hour at the engine.

$$ \dot{E} = \text{BSEC} \times P_b = 9.5 \times 60 = 570\,\text{MJ/h} $$

**Step 2:** Volumetric LHV of E20 blend.

$$ \text{LHV}_{\text{E20}} = 0.20 \times 26{,}900 + 0.80 \times 44{,}000 = 5380 + 35{,}200 = 40{,}580\,\text{kJ/L} $$

**Step 3:** Density of E20 blend (assuming no volume change on mixing — a standard engineering approximation).

$$ \rho_{\text{E20}} = 0.20 \times 789 + 0.80 \times 750 = 157.8 + 600 = 757.8\,\text{kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 4:** Mass-based LHV of E20.

$$ \text{LHV}_{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{40{,}580}{0.7578} = 53{,}550\,\text{kJ/kg} \approx 53.55\,\text{MJ/kg} $$

**Step 5:** Mass flow rate of fuel.

$$ \dot{m}_f = \dfrac{\dot{E}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{mass}}} = \dfrac{570}{53.55} = 10.64\,\text{kg/h} $$

**Step 6:** Volume flow rate.

$$ \dot{V}_f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{\rho_{\text{E20}}} = \dfrac{10.64}{757.8} = 0.01404\,\text{m}^3/\text{h} = 14.04\,\text{L/h} $$

**Answer:** Mass flow rate ≈ **10.64 kg/h**; Volume flow rate ≈ **14.04 L/h**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing RON and MON** — they are not interchangeable. Always specify which octane number is being quoted.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mass-based LHV to volumetric** before computing fuel-tank range problems — this is the single most common 2-mark loss in KTU numericals.
> 3. **Writing the transesterification reaction unbalanced** — students frequently forget the stoichiometric coefficient of 3 for methanol and FAME. The correct equation has **3 CH₃OH** and produces **3 FAME + 1 glycerol**.
> 4. **Mixing up CNG and LNG storage conditions** — CNG = 200–250 bar at ambient T; LNG = cryogenic at –162 °C. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Quoting calorific value as HHV** instead of LHV — for automotive work, **LHV is the standard** because exhaust water remains in vapour form.
> 6. **Ignoring the 95.6% ethanol-water azeotrope** — students often claim distillation can yield 100% ethanol, which is physically impossible without dehydration.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ethanol** is produced by **fermentation** of sugars/starch, capped at 95.6% by azeotrope, then **molecular-sieve dehydrated** for E100.
- **Methanol** is produced **synthetically** from syngas using a **Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst at 220–300 °C and 50–100 bar**.
- **Biodiesel** is produced by **transesterification** of triglycerides with methanol using a **base catalyst (NaOH/KOH)**; viscosity drops from ~40 to ~5 mm²/s.
- **CNG** is natural gas (≈ 95% CH₄) compressed to **20–25 MPa**; **LNG** is the cryogenic (–162 °C) liquid form.
- **LPG** is a by-product of **refining** (catalytic cracking) and **natural gas processing**; stored as a liquid at ~0.8 MPa.
- **Hydrogen** is dominantly produced by **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)**, with electrolysis as the green alternative.
- **Octane Number** is a measure of knock resistance in **SI engines**; **Cetane Number** is its counterpart for **CI engines**.
- **Gasoline baseline values** to memorize: LHV = **44 MJ/kg**, A/F = **14.7**, RON = **95**.
- **Ethanol advantage:** Highest practical RON (108) → enables high CR and efficiency.
- **Biodiesel limitation:** High flash point (130–170 °C) → unsuitable for direct SI use.
- **CNG advantage:** High H/C ratio → cleanest hydrocarbon combustion, particulate-free.
- **Hydrogen challenge:** Very low density → storage is the bottleneck (700 bar tanks or cryo-liquid).
- **Energy density comparison** (volumetric, MJ/L): Gasoline 33 > E100 21 > Biodiesel 33 > LNG 22 > LH₂ 8.5 → explains why **hydrogen tanks are huge**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine performance with alcohols: impact on engine performance and emissions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Engine performance with alcohols: impact on engine performance and emissions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Alcohol fuels** in the automotive context refer to low-carbon, oxygenated liquid fuels—primarily **methanol (CH₃OH)** and **ethanol (C₂H₅OH)**—derived from biomass, agricultural residues, or synthesis gas. They are utilized either as **neat fuels (M100, E100)**, as **high-blend components (E85, M85)** with gasoline, or as **low-percentage blendstocks (E10, E20)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition**: Alcohol fuels are oxygenated hydrocarbon fuels where one or more hydrogen atoms of the parent alkane are replaced by a **hydroxyl (-OH) functional group**. The presence of this -OH group fundamentally alters combustion chemistry, energy density, latent heat of vaporization, and emission profile compared to conventional petroleum fuels.

### 1.2 Key Physical & Chemical Identity

| Property | Methanol (CH₃OH) | Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | CH₃OH | C₂H₅OH |
| Molar Mass | **32.04 kg/kmol** | **46.07 kg/kmol** |
| Oxygen Content (by mass) | **49.9 %** | **34.7 %** |
| Octane Number (RON) | **106–115** | **100–110** |
| Self-Ignition Temperature | **463 °C** | **363 °C** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | **1100 kJ/kg** | **904 kJ/kg** |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Oxygenated Fuel Cocktail"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students**: Imagine your conventional gasoline as a tightly packed suitcase full of hydrocarbon chains (C and H only). Now imagine an alcohol molecule as the same suitcase, but with a small oxygen "keychain" attached (the -OH group).
>
> 🔹 During combustion, this oxygen "rides along" inside the fuel itself. So even if your engine's air supply is slightly lean or the mixing is imperfect, the fuel brings its **own oxidizer**.
>
> 🔹 This is **why** alcohols burn cleaner (less soot, lower CO) than gasoline—the oxygen is already embedded in the molecule. However, the "keychain" also makes the molecule **bulkier and heavier per unit of energy**, which is why alcohol-powered cars consume more fuel per kilometre.

### 1.4 Why Alcohols Are Considered Alternate Fuels

- **Renewable**: Both can be produced from biomass (sugarcane, corn, switchgrass, algae).
- **Oxygenated**: Internal oxygen reduces particulate emissions.
- **High Octane**: Allows higher compression ratios → higher thermal efficiency.
- **Lower Flame Temperature**: Reduces **NOx formation**.
- **Domestic Production Potential**: Reduces crude oil import dependency—a critical strategic concern for India's Ethanol Blending Programme (E20 target by 2025).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Module 2 expects students to quantitatively compare performance and emissions of alcohol-fuelled engines against baseline gasoline/diesel operation. Mastery of stoichiometric A/F ratio, calorific value impact on indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP), and emission trade-offs is essential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

### 🔹 Step 1: Stoichiometric Combustion Chemistry

**Methanol combustion:**

$$\text{CH}_3\text{OH} + 1.5\, \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\, \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Ethanol combustion:**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\, \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\, \text{CO}_2 + 3\, \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> **Why it matters**: The stoichiometric oxygen demand governs the **air-fuel ratio**, which directly influences flame temperature, heat release rate, and emissions.

### 🔹 Step 2: Performance Impact Pathway

The chain of influence on engine performance is:

```
Alcohol Properties  →  Air-Fuel Ratio  →  Volumetric Efficiency  →
Combustion Efficiency  →  Brake Power & Thermal Efficiency
```

### 🔹 Step 3: Emission Impact Pathway

```
Oxygen in Fuel  +  Lower Flame Temp  →  ↓ CO, ↓ HC, ↓ NOx, ↓ PM
High Latent Heat  →  Charge Cooling  →  ↓ NOx but ↑ Unburned HC
Aldehyde Formation  →  ↑ HCHO, ↑ CH₃CHO (regulated pollutants)
```

### 🔹 Step 4: Why Alcohols Lower Energy Density per Volume

Since alcohol molecules contain **chemically bound oxygen**, a portion of the molecular mass is non-combustible. For the same **volume** of fuel drawn into the cylinder, alcohol releases less energy than gasoline.

$$\boxed{\rho_{\text{alcohol}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{alcohol}} < \rho_{\text{gasoline}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{gasoline}}}$$

This results in **higher volumetric fuel consumption** for alcohol-fuelled engines (typically 1.6× for M100, 1.4× for E100 vs. gasoline).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric A/F — Methanol | $\text{AFR}_{s,\text{MeOH}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 32 \times 4.31}{32}$ | **6.45** | kg air/kg fuel |
| 2 | Stoichiometric A/F — Ethanol | $\text{AFR}_{s,\text{EtOH}} = \dfrac{3 \times 32 \times 4.31}{46}$ | **9.0** | kg air/kg fuel |
| 3 | Stoichiometric A/F — Gasoline | $\text{AFR}_{s,\text{gas}} \approx 14.7$ | 14.7 | kg air/kg fuel |
| 4 | Lower Heating Value (LHV) — Methanol | — | **19.9** | MJ/kg |
| 5 | LHV — Ethanol | — | **26.9** | MJ/kg |
| 6 | LHV — Gasoline | — | **43–44** | MJ/kg |
| 7 | Energy per kg of air (stoich. charge) | $Q_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{\text{LHV}}{\text{AFR}_s}$ | MeOH: 3.08, EtOH: 2.99, Gas: 2.99 | MJ/kg air |
| 8 | Brake Specific Energy Consumption | $\text{BSEC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}{\dot{W}_b}$ | — | kJ/kWh |
| 9 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{it}} = \dfrac{W_i}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | — | — |
| 10 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{bt}} = \dfrac{W_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}}$ | — | — |
| 11 | Energy Density Ratio (volumetric) | $\rho_r = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{al}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{al}}}{\rho_{\text{gas}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{gas}}}$ | MeOH ≈ 0.48, EtOH ≈ 0.66 | — |
| 12 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{actual}}}{\rho_a \cdot V_d \cdot N/2}$ | 75–90 % | — |
| 13 | Air-Fuel Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(\text{A/F})_{\text{actual}}}{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich}}}$ | <1 lean, >1 rich | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU**: Although methanol and ethanol have **much lower stoichiometric A/F ratios** than gasoline, the **energy per kg of stoichiometric air** is nearly identical (≈3 MJ/kg air). This is why an engine's **volumetric air capacity** remains the limiting factor, and BSFC (brake specific fuel consumption) on an *energy-equivalent* basis is comparable.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flex-Fuel Vehicles (FFV)**: Modern FFVs use ethanol blends (E85 in USA, E100 in Brazil) with electronic control units (ECU) that adapt injection timing and duration based on fuel composition sensed via an oxygen/fuel sensor.
- **India's E20 Programme**: Ethanol 20 % blend mandate for petrol vehicles from 2025 onwards, projected to reduce **CO₂ emissions by ~3 million tonnes/year** and **petroleum import by ~1 billion USD/year**.
- **Racing Applications**: Methanol's high latent heat and high octane enable **high-boost turbocharging** without knock—used in IndyCar, drag racing, and Formula 1 historically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Ethanol (C₂H₅OH)

**Starting Combustion Reaction:**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\, \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\, \text{CO}_2 + 3\, \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step 1: Balance the Equation (Verification)

Left side atom count:
- Carbon (C): 2 ✓
- Hydrogen (H): 6 ✓
- Oxygen (O): 1 (from ethanol) + 6 (from O₂) = 7

Right side atom count:
- C: 2 ✓
- H: 6 ✓
- O: 4 (from CO₂) + 3 (from H₂O) = 7 ✓

**Equation is balanced.**

### Step 2: Identify Molecular Masses

| Species | Molar Mass (kg/kmol) |
|---|---|
| C₂H₅OH | $2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46$ |
| O₂ | $32$ |
| CO₂ | $44$ |
| H₂O | $18$ |

### Step 3: Compute Mass of Oxygen Required per kg of Fuel

From the balanced equation, **3 kmol of O₂** are required per **1 kmol of ethanol**.

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 \text{ per kmol of ethanol} = 3 \times 32 = 96 \text{ kg O}_2$$

$$\text{Mass of O}_2 \text{ per kg of ethanol} = \frac{96}{46} = 2.087 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

### Step 4: Convert Oxygen Mass to Air Mass

Atmospheric air contains **23.2 % oxygen by mass** (the remainder being mostly N₂ and trace gases).

$$\text{Mass of air per kg fuel} = \frac{\text{Mass of O}_2}{0.232}$$

$$\text{Mass of air per kg fuel} = \frac{2.087}{0.232} = 8.996 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

### Step 5: Express Final Stoichiometric A/F Ratio

$$\boxed{(\text{A/F})_{\text{stoich, EtOH}} \approx 9.0}$$

### Step 6: Generalised Formula for Any Hydrocarbon-Oxygenate CₓHᵧOᵤ

For a generic fuel **CₓHᵧOᵤ**, stoichiometric oxygen requirement:

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ needed (kmol)} = x + \frac{y}{4} - \frac{u}{2}$$

The general stoichiometric A/F ratio becomes:

$$(\text{A/F})_s = \frac{(x + y/4 - u/2) \times 32 \times (100/23.2)}{12x + y + 16u}$$

> **Conversion Logic**: The numerator scales oxygen requirement × molecular weight × inverse oxygen mass fraction; the denominator gives the molecular weight of the fuel.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Engine Performance with Alcohols vs. Gasoline

| Performance / Emission Parameter | Gasoline (Baseline) | M100 (Neat Methanol) | E100 (Neat Ethanol) | E85 | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | 25–30 % | **30–36 %** ↑ | **30–35 %** ↑ | 28–33 % | Higher compression ratio possible; higher latent heat |
| Brake Power Output | Baseline | **~95 %** (drop) | **~98 %** (≈same) | ~98 % | Lower LHV partly offset by higher η_thermal |
| Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC, mass basis) | 250 g/kWh | **~410 g/kWh** ↑ | **~360 g/kWh** ↑ | ~330 g/kWh | Lower LHV requires more mass of fuel |
| BSFC (energy-equivalent) | Baseline | ≈ Same | ≈ Same | ≈ Same | Same air-charge energy limit |
| Volumetric Fuel Consumption | Baseline | **~1.6×** ↑ | **~1.4×** ↑ | ~1.3× | Lower energy density per litre |
| Cold Start | Easy | **Difficult** ↓ | **Difficult** ↓ | Moderate | High latent heat + low vapour pressure |
| CO Emissions | Baseline | **↓ 50–80 %** | **↓ 40–60 %** | ↓ 30–50 % | Oxygenated fuel → better oxidation |
| HC Emissions | Baseline | **↓ 50–70 %** (unburned fuel); but **↑ Aldehydes** | **↓ 40–60 %** | ↓ 30–50 % | Leaner combustion; some fuel escapes in quench zone |
| NOx Emissions | Baseline | **↓ 20–60 %** | **↓ 15–40 %** | ↓ 10–30 % | Lower adiabatic flame temperature |
| CO₂ Emissions (well-to-wheel) | Baseline | **↓ 40–60 %** (if biomass-derived) | **↓ 50–80 %** | ↓ 40–70 % | Biogenic carbon cycle neutrality |
| Aldehyde Emissions (HCHO) | Negligible | **↑↑ Significantly** | **↑ Moderately** | ↑ Slight | Incomplete oxidation of -OH group |
| Engine Wear / Lubricity | Normal | **Higher wear** (methanol washes oil film) | Higher wear | Slight wear | Alcohols are poor lubricants; corrosion issues |
| Material Compatibility | Normal | Attacks **aluminium, zinc, certain elastomers** | Less aggressive | Generally OK | Polar -OH group causes corrosion |

## 3.3 Derivation: Why Higher Latent Heat Improves Thermal Efficiency

Otto cycle thermal efficiency depends on compression ratio:

$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

Higher octane of alcohol → allows **higher compression ratio (r)** without knock. Additionally, the high **latent heat of vaporization** cools the intake charge:

$$T_{\text{after evaporation}} = T_{\text{manifold}} - \frac{\dot{m}_f \cdot h_{\text{fg}}}{\dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a}}$$

Cooler charge → **higher density** → more mass of air inducted per cycle → higher **indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP)**.

$$\text{IMEP} = \frac{W_i}{V_d}$$

Combined effect: alcohol engines achieve **3–8 % higher thermal efficiency** than baseline gasoline engines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: When asked "Why does an alcohol-fuelled engine show higher brake thermal efficiency despite lower calorific value?" — answer in two parts: (1) higher octane allows higher compression ratio; (2) high latent heat cools the charge, increasing volumetric efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Alcohol-Fuelled Spark Ignition Engine System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the operational flow and feedback loops in a flex-fuel (E85-capable) spark ignition engine. All node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FuelTank: AlcoholBlend M100 or E100] --> B1[FuelPump]
    B1 --> C1[FuelRailPressureRegulator]
    C1 --> D1[FuelInjector: PortInjection]
    D1 --> E1[IntakeManifold: ChargeCooling occurs here]
    E1 --> F1[IntakeValve: Mixture enters cylinder]
    F1 --> G1[CombustionChamber: SparkIgnition]
    G1 --> H1[PistonPowerStroke]
    H1 --> I1[Crankshaft: MechanicalPower]
    I1 --> J1[TransmissionToWheels]

    G1 --> K1[ExhaustPort]
    K1 --> L1[CatalyticConverter: CO HC NOx reduction]
    L1 --> M1[LambdaOxygenSensor: AFR feedback]
    M1 --> N1[EngineControlUnit ECU]
    N1 --> C1
    N1 --> D1

    G1 --> O1[EmissionOutput: CO2 H2O plus traces of HCHO CH3CHO NOx]

    subgraph FeedbackLoop
        M1
        N1
        C1
        D1
    end
```

### Diagram Interpretation Notes (for KTU students)

- **Charge Cooling Block (E1)**: The high latent heat of vaporization of alcohol absorbs heat from the intake manifold walls, dropping charge temperature by **15–25 °C** compared to gasoline.
- **ECU Feedback Loop (N1 → C1, D1)**: Modern flex-fuel ECUs use a **fuel composition sensor** (often in the fuel rail) to detect ethanol percentage via measurement of **dielectric constant** or **refractive index** of the fuel. The ECU then adjusts injection pulse width and ignition timing.
- **Emission Output Block (O1)**: Note the characteristic aldehydes (formaldehyde HCHO and acetaldehyde CH₃CHO) — alcohols' signature unregulated-to-regulated emission trade-off.

## 4.2 Cause-Effect Mermaid: Performance & Emission Trade-offs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[High Octane Number] --> P2[Higher Compression Ratio]
    P2 --> P3[Higher Thermal Efficiency]
    P2 --> P4[Higher Brake Power]

    P5[High Latent Heat] --> P6[Charge Cooling]
    P6 --> P7[Higher Volumetric Efficiency]
    P6 --> P8[Lower Flame Temperature]

    P9[Oxygenated Fuel] --> P10[Lower CO Emission]
    P9 --> P11[Lower HC Emission]

    P8 --> P12[Lower NOx Emission]

    P13[Low Energy Density per Volume] --> P14[Higher Volumetric Fuel Consumption]

    P15[Polar -OH Group] --> P16[Higher Aldehyde Emission]
    P15 --> P17[Material Corrosion Issue]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram for KTU answers**: Each "Performance Benefit" branch (P1–P8) culminates in measurable engine improvements, while the trade-off branches (P13–P17) explicitly highlight engineering challenges. Use this structure in your 14-mark answers to demonstrate systematic thinking.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: List any three advantages and two disadvantages of using ethanol as a fuel for spark ignition engines compared to gasoline.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Higher octane number** (RON ≈ 100–110 vs. gasoline's 88–95), permitting higher compression ratios and thus higher thermal efficiency.
2. **Renewable and domestically producible** from sugarcane, corn, and lignocellulosic biomass — reduces crude oil import dependency.
3. **Oxygenated fuel** (34.7 % oxygen by mass) significantly reduces CO and unburned HC emissions due to in-fuel oxygen availability.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Lower calorific value** (≈ 26.9 MJ/kg vs. gasoline's 43 MJ/kg) — higher fuel consumption per km.
2. **Material compatibility issues** — corrodes aluminium, attacks certain rubber/elastomer seals; requires upgraded fuel system components.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is methanol considered a suitable alternative fuel despite its lower calorific value? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

Methanol is considered a suitable alternative fuel because, despite a lower LHV (19.9 MJ/kg) than gasoline, it offers several compensating advantages:
1. **Very high octane number (RON 106–115)** allows higher compression ratios (12:1 to 14:1), improving thermal efficiency by 5–10 %.
2. **High latent heat of vaporization (1100 kJ/kg)** provides significant charge cooling, increasing volumetric efficiency.
3. **Oxygen content of 49.9 %** leads to cleaner combustion with substantially lower CO, HC, and NOx emissions.
4. It can be **produced from coal, natural gas, or biomass** (via synthesis gas), offering fuel security.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: A four-stroke spark ignition engine is fuelled with neat ethanol (C₂H₅OH). Discuss in detail the impact on (a) engine performance and (b) emission characteristics compared to baseline gasoline operation.**

**(a) Engine Performance (7 Marks)**

The impact of neat ethanol on engine performance parameters is analysed as follows:

**1. Thermal Efficiency:** Ethanol's higher octane number (RON ≈ 100–110) permits operation at **higher compression ratios (10:1 to 12:1)** without knock, compared to gasoline's typical 8:1 to 10:1. From Otto cycle equation:

$$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$

An increase in compression ratio from 9:1 to 11:1 raises thermal efficiency by approximately **5–8 %**.

**2. Volumetric Efficiency:** Ethanol's high latent heat of vaporization (904 kJ/kg vs. gasoline's ≈ 305 kJ/kg) substantially cools the intake charge, increasing its density and thus the **mass of air inducted per cycle**. Volumetric efficiency may rise by 3–6 %.

**3. Brake Power Output:** The lower stoichiometric A/F ratio (9.0 vs. 14.7) means each kg of ethanol displaces more air; hence, for the same displacement, the **maximum achievable brake power drops by ~2–5 %** unless the engine is optimized (e.g., higher compression, advanced timing).

**4. Brake Specific Fuel Consumption:** BSFC on mass basis increases (~360 g/kWh vs. 250 g/kWh for gasoline) due to lower LHV. However, on an **energy-equivalent basis** (kJ/kWh), BSFC is comparable.

**5. Cold Start:** Ethanol's high latent heat and low vapour pressure make cold starting **difficult**, requiring auxiliary systems such as heated injectors, grid heaters, or volatile starting fuels (typically 15–20 % gasoline blend).

**(b) Emission Characteristics (7 Marks)**

**1. Carbon Monoxide (CO):** Ethanol is **oxygenated** (34.7 % O by mass), enabling more complete oxidation even in fuel-rich regions. CO emissions drop by **40–60 %** compared to gasoline.

**2. Unburned Hydrocarbons (HC):** Reduced by **40–60 %** due to leaner effective combustion and oxygen availability. However, **increased aldehyde emissions** (especially acetaldehyde CH₃CHO) offset some of this HC reduction.

**3. Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx):** Reduced by **15–40 %** because the lower adiabatic flame temperature (due to charge cooling and lower energy density) suppresses thermal NO formation via the Zeldovich mechanism:

$$\text{O} + \text{N}_2 \longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{N}$$

**4. Carbon Dioxide (CO₂):** Tailpipe CO₂ reduces by ~12–15 % per kg of fuel, but **well-to-wheel CO₂ reduction can reach 50–80 %** if ethanol is biomass-derived (closed biogenic carbon cycle).

**5. Aldehydes (HCHO, CH₃CHO):** Significantly **higher** for ethanol fuels due to partial oxidation of the -OH group. These are **toxic and potentially carcinogenic**, requiring upgraded catalytic converters (e.g., special washcoat formulations).

**6. Particulate Matter:** Substantially lower than gasoline (essentially negligible for neat ethanol in SI engines).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q: Explain with suitable diagrams the major modifications required in a conventional SI engine to operate efficiently on methanol (M100). Discuss the resulting performance and emission trade-offs.**

**Major Engine Modifications for M100 Operation (7 Marks)**

**1. Fuel System Upgrades:**
- Stainless steel or fluoropolymer-lined fuel lines (methanol attacks aluminium, zinc, magnesium, and natural rubber).
- Methanol-compatible elastomer seals (FKM/Viton or PTFE-based).
- Larger diameter fuel injectors (≈ 2× flow capacity) to compensate for ~2× higher stoichiometric mass of fuel required.

**2. Cold Start System:**
- Auxiliary **glow plug** or **grid heater** in the intake manifold.
- Injected **pilot quantity of gasoline** during cold start (dual-fuel system).
- Coolant-heated fuel rail to improve vapour formation.

**3. Ignition Timing Advance:**
- Methanol's high octane and faster flame speed permit **5–10° crank angle advance** over gasoline baseline for optimum phasing of peak pressure.

**4. Compression Ratio Increase:**
- Increase from 9:1 to **12:1 to 14:1** to exploit methanol's high RON (106–115) and improve thermal efficiency.

**5. Lubrication System:**
- Use of **alcohol-resistant motor oil** with higher additive package to compensate for oil-film dilution by methanol ingress past piston rings.

**Performance & Emission Trade-offs (7 Marks)**

| Aspect | Methanol (M100) | vs. Gasoline |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal Efficiency | **30–36 %** | ↑ |
| Brake Power | **90–95 %** of baseline | ↓ Slight |
| Volumetric Consumption | **~1.6×** higher | ↑↑ |
| Cold Start | Requires assist system | ↓ Poor |
| CO | **↓ 50–80 %** | ↓↓ |
| HC | **↓ 50–70 %** | ↓↓ |
| NOx | **↓ 20–60 %** | ↓ |
| HCHO (Formaldehyde) | **↑↑** Significantly | ↑↑ |

**Key Trade-off Summary:** Methanol delivers **higher efficiency and lower regulated emissions** but requires **substantial capital investment** in fuel-system and cold-start modifications, has **higher operational cost** (due to greater fuel volume consumption), and produces **significant unregulated aldehyde emissions**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse LHV with HHV** when quoting calorific values in answers. The KTU convention uses **LHV (Lower Heating Value)** for engine calculations since exhaust water leaves as vapour.
> 2. Many students write "alcohol engines have lower thermal efficiency" — this is **WRONG**. Alcohol engines have **higher thermal efficiency** due to higher compression ratio, despite lower calorific value.
> 3. Always state **both mass-based AND volume-based** effects when discussing fuel consumption.
> 4. For aldehyde emissions, specify the actual compounds (**HCHO and CH₃CHO**), not vague "toxic emissions".
> 5. In diagrams, label nodes clearly and **draw the ECU feedback loop** when discussing flex-fuel systems — KTU examiners award marks for schematic completeness.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Alcohol Fuels**

### 📌 Core Knowledge Points
- ☐ Methanol: CH₃OH, M = 32, RON = 106–115, LHV = 19.9 MJ/kg, O₂ content = 49.9 %
- ☐ Ethanol: C₂H₅OH, M = 46, RON = 100–110, LHV = 26.9 MJ/kg, O₂ content = 34.7 %
- ☐ Stoichiometric A/F: Methanol ≈ 6.45, Ethanol ≈ 9.0, Gasoline ≈ 14.7
- ☐ Energy per kg of stoichiometric air ≈ 3 MJ/kg for **all** these fuels (this is why energy-equivalent BSFC is comparable)

### 📌 Performance Effects
- ☐ **Higher thermal efficiency** due to higher octane → higher compression ratio
- ☐ **Higher volumetric efficiency** due to high latent heat charge cooling
- ☐ **Lower brake power** (slightly) due to lower energy density per volume
- ☐ **Higher volumetric fuel consumption** (~1.4× ethanol, ~1.6× methanol)
- ☐ **Difficult cold start** (high latent heat + low vapour pressure)

### 📌 Emission Effects
- ☐ ↓ CO (40–80 % reduction)
- ☐ ↓ HC (40–70 % reduction)
- ☐ ↓ NOx (15–60 % reduction)
- ☐ ↑ Aldehydes (HCHO, CH₃CHO) — **unregulated but toxic**
- ☐ ↓ CO₂ (well-to-wheel, especially biomass-derived)

### 📌 Engineering Modifications Required
- ☐ Alcohol-resistant materials (SS, FKM seals, PTFE)
- ☐ Larger injectors (~2× flow)
- ☐ Cold-start assist (glow plug, grid heater, pilot gasoline)
- ☐ Higher compression ratio (12:1 to 14:1 for methanol)
- ☐ Flex-fuel sensor and ECU reprogramming capability

### 📌 Numerical Constants to Memorize
- ☐ Air contains 23.2 % oxygen by mass
- ☐ Molecular weight of air ≈ 28.97 kg/kmol
- ☐ Stoichiometric air-to-fuel ratio general formula for CₓHᵧOᵤ: $(\text{A/F})_s = \dfrac{32(x + y/4 - u/2) \times (100/23.2)}{12x + y + 16u}$

### 📌 Real-World Context (for context-rich KTU answers)
- ☐ India: **E20 blending mandate** (2025) — ethanol 20 % in petrol
- ☐ Brazil: **E100 (neat ethanol)** widespread since 1970s (ProÁlcool programme)
- ☐ USA: **E85** (85 % ethanol + 15 % gasoline) for flex-fuel vehicles
- ☐ Europe: **E5, E10** standard blends; exploring E20/E30
