topics.title: Alcohol-gasoline blends: gasohol and E85, advantages and limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alcohol-gasoline blends: gasohol and E85, advantages and limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

**Alcohol-Gasoline Blends** are homogeneous liquid fuel mixtures produced by blending low-carbon alcohols (primarily **ethanol** $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$ and occasionally **methanol** $\text{CH}_3\text{OH}$) with petroleum-derived gasoline in carefully controlled volumetric proportions. These blends are classified internationally using the **"E" nomenclature**, where the numerical suffix indicates the volumetric percentage of ethanol in the blend (e.g., E10 = 10% ethanol + 90% gasoline).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Alcohol-gasoline blends are oxygenated, renewable-content transportation fuels that combine the high-octane, clean-burning properties of alcohols with the energy density and volatility characteristics of gasoline. The two most commercially significant blends are **Gasohol** (low-percentage, E5–E10) and **E85** (high-percentage, E70–E85) — each engineered for distinct automotive applications.

### 1.1.1 Gasohol

**Gasohol** is the generic trade name for low-percentage alcohol-gasoline blends, originally coined in the **1970s** when Brazil and the United States initiated mass ethanol blending programs. The standard commercial composition is:

- **E10 (Standard Gasohol):** 10% anhydrous ethanol + 90% unleaded gasoline by volume
- **E5 (Light Gasohol):** 5% ethanol + 95% gasoline (common in Europe and India)
- **E7.7:** Brazilian standard adopted in the 1990s

> [!NOTE]
> Gasohol is classified as an **"oxygenated fuel"** under the U.S. Clean Air Act Amendments of **1990** because ethanol contributes bound oxygen (≈ 34.8% by mass), promoting more complete combustion in spark-ignition engines.

### 1.1.2 E85

**E85** is a high-percentage ethanol blend designed exclusively for **Flexible Fuel Vehicles (FFVs)**. Its composition is season-dependent to address cold-start limitations:

- **Summer-grade E85:** 85% ethanol + 15% gasoline (vapor pressure optimized for hot climates)
- **Winter-grade E85:** 70% ethanol + 30% gasoline (higher volatility fraction compensates for ethanol's low vapor pressure at low temperatures)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Mixer" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Imagine gasoline as **black, strong filter coffee** (high caffeine = high energy density), and ethanol as **decaffeinated herbal tea** (cleaner, lighter, but weaker per cup). Gasohol is like a **90% coffee + 10% tea blend** — your car barely notices the difference and gets a small "antioxidant boost" (cleaner exhaust). E85, however, is like a **15% coffee + 85% herbal tea blend** — radically different fuel chemistry, requiring a specially designed "stomach" (the FFV engine) to digest it properly, but you sleep better (lower emissions) and use a renewable crop.

This analogy captures the **two engineering paradigms** of alcohol blending:
- **Drop-in blending (Gasohol)** → minor engine modifications, broad vehicle compatibility
- **Purpose-built blending (E85)** → dedicated fuel systems, full engine optimization

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics

| Property | Symbol/Value |
|----------|--------------|
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (gasoline) | **14.7 : 1** |
| Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (ethanol) | **9.0 : 1** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization (gasoline) | **305 kJ/kg** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization (ethanol) | **905 kJ/kg** |
| Lower Heating Value (gasoline) | **44 MJ/kg** |
| Lower Heating Value (ethanol) | **26.8 MJ/kg** |
| Research Octane Number (gasoline) | **87–98** |
| Research Octane Number (ethanol) | **108–110** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Why Blend Alcohol with Gasoline? — The Five Engineering Drivers

1. **Octane Enhancement:** Ethanol's **RON of ~108** allows refineries to reduce reliance on toxic, carcinogenic octane boosters like **MTBE (Methyl tert-butyl ether)** and **lead tetraethyl**, both phased out globally.
2. **Combustion Oxygenation:** The oxygen atom within ethanol's hydroxyl group ($\text{-OH}$) becomes chemically available during combustion, enabling more complete oxidation of hydrocarbons → **lower CO and unburned HC emissions**.
3. **Renewable Carbon Content:** Ethanol produced from sugarcane (Brazil), corn (USA), or cellulosic biomass (second-generation) contributes a **closed carbon loop** because the feedstock absorbed $\text{CO}_2$ during growth.
4. **Energy Security:** Domestic agricultural conversion to ethanol reduces petroleum import bills — a key strategic objective for India, Brazil, and the USA.
5. **Antiknock Margin:** Higher octane permits **higher compression ratios** in future engines, improving **Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)**.

### 2.1.2 The Chemical Reality of Ethanol Combustion

The complete stoichiometric combustion of ethanol proceeds as:

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Notice the **3 oxygen atoms in $\text{O}_2$** vs the 12.5 required for iso-octane ($\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$). This is **why ethanol is "self-oxygenating"** — it carries its own oxidizer into the combustion chamber.

### 2.1.3 Gasohol Operating Principle (E10)

In E10, ethanol's role is **subtle and additive**:
- Slightly raises the blend's **octane number** by ~2–3 RON points
- Adds **~3.5% bound oxygen by mass**, lowering peak in-cylinder temperatures
- The 90% gasoline matrix preserves **volatility, energy density, and cold-start capability**
- **No engine modification required** in most post-2001 vehicles

### 2.1.4 E85 Operating Principle (FFV System)

E85 demands a fundamentally different fuel-handling architecture:

- **Fuel System:** Stainless-steel fuel lines, ethanol-compatible fuel pump, larger injector orifices (because E85 flows ~40% more volume per unit energy)
- **Cold Start:** A secondary gasoline "cold-start injector" or dual-fuel tank strategy delivers gasoline during cranking
- **Engine Control Unit (ECU):** Uses **fuel-flex alcohol composition sensor** to continuously measure ethanol fraction and dynamically recalculate **injection pulse width** and **ignition timing**
- **Materials:** All rubber seals, plastic fuel rails, and fuel pump diaphragms upgraded to **Viton, Teflon, or fluorocarbon elastomers** (neoprene and Buna-N degrade in ethanol)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below are **exam-essential**. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

### Table 2.1 — Master Formula Table for Alcohol-Blend Calculations

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Stoichiometric AFR of blend | $\text{AFR}_{\text{blend}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \left( \dfrac{y_i}{\text{AFR}_i} \right)}$ | $y_i$ = mass fraction of fuel $i$ |
| 2 | Mass fraction from vol % | $y_i = \dfrac{V_i \cdot \rho_i}{\sum_j V_j \cdot \rho_j}$ | $V_i$ = volume fraction, $\rho_i$ = density (kg/L) |
| 3 | Lower Heating Value of blend | $\text{LHV}_{\text{blend}} = \sum_i y_i \cdot \text{LHV}_i$ | $\text{LHV}_i$ in MJ/kg |
| 4 | Volumetric energy density | $\text{ED}_v = \text{LHV} \times \rho_{\text{blend}}$ | MJ/L |
| 5 | Octane number of blend (linear approx.) | $\text{ON}_{\text{blend}} \approx \sum_i x_i \cdot \text{ON}_i$ | $x_i$ = vol. fraction (linear blending rule) |
| 6 | Specific fuel consumption ratio | $\text{SFC}_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_{\text{gasoline}}}{\text{LHV}_{\text{blend}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Blend density | $\rho_{\text{blend}} = \sum_i x_i \cdot \rho_i$ | kg/L (volumetric rule) |
| 8 | Stoichiometric air requirement (molar) | $\text{Air}_{\text{mol}} = n \times \dfrac{100}{21} = 4.76\,n$ | $n$ = moles of $\text{O}_2$ required |

### Standard Reference Constants (Bold for Memorization)

$$\rho_{\text{ethanol}} = \mathbf{0.789 \text{ kg/L}}, \quad \rho_{\text{gasoline}} = \mathbf{0.74 \text{ kg/L}}, \quad \rho_{\text{methanol}} = \mathbf{0.792 \text{ kg/L}}$$

$$\text{LHV}_{\text{ethanol}} = \mathbf{26.8 \text{ MJ/kg}}, \quad \text{LHV}_{\text{gasoline}} = \mathbf{44.0 \text{ MJ/kg}}, \quad \text{LHV}_{\text{methanol}} = \mathbf{20.1 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

$$\text{RON}_{\text{ethanol}} = \mathbf{108}, \quad \text{RON}_{\text{gasoline}} = \mathbf{87\text{–}98}, \quad \text{MON}_{\text{ethanol}} = \mathbf{89}$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production automotive engineering, these formulas directly inform:
- **Engine calibration maps** for FFV ECUs
- **Refinery blending optimization** (linear programming of ethanol + gasoline + butane)
- **Emissions certification** under Euro VI / BS-VI / EPA Tier 3 standards
- **Logistics planning** — energy-equivalent volume determines tanker truck payload

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step is explicitly written. No shortcuts. Each block is preceded and followed by a blank line per LaTeX isolation rule.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of E85 (by mass)

**Given:** E85 = 85% ethanol (vol) + 15% gasoline (vol)
**Find:** Stoichiometric AFR of E85

### Step 1: Convert Volume Fractions to Mass Fractions

Using $y_i = \dfrac{V_i \cdot \rho_i}{\sum_j V_j \cdot \rho_j}$

For 1 L of blend:
- Mass of ethanol = $0.85 \times 0.789 = 0.6707$ kg
- Mass of gasoline = $0.15 \times 0.740 = 0.1110$ kg
- Total mass = $0.7817$ kg

$$y_{\text{ethanol}} = \dfrac{0.6707}{0.7817} = 0.8581$$

$$y_{\text{gasoline}} = \dfrac{0.1110}{0.7817} = 0.1419$$

### Step 2: Apply the Harmonic Blending Rule for AFR

$$\dfrac{1}{\text{AFR}_{\text{E85}}} = \dfrac{y_{\text{ethanol}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{ethanol}}} + \dfrac{y_{\text{gasoline}}}{\text{AFR}_{\text{gasoline}}}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\text{AFR}_{\text{E85}}} = \dfrac{0.8581}{9.0} + \dfrac{0.1419}{14.7}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{\text{AFR}_{\text{E85}}} = 0.09534 + 0.009653 = 0.10499$$

$$\boxed{\text{AFR}_{\text{E85}} \approx 9.52 : 1}$$

### Step 3: Interpretation

The stoichiometric AFR of E85 (≈ 9.52) is **dramatically lower** than gasoline's 14.7:1, meaning the ECU must inject **~54% more fuel mass** per cycle to maintain stoichiometry. This is why FFVs use larger injectors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lower Heating Value of E85 Blend

**Given:** E85 (volumetric blend)
**Find:** LHV per kg of blend

### Step 1: Compute the Volumetric LHV

$$\text{LHV}_v = \sum_i x_i \cdot \text{LHV}_i \cdot \rho_i$$

$$\text{LHV}_v = (0.85)(26.8)(0.789) + (0.15)(44.0)(0.740)$$

$$\text{LHV}_v = 17.976 + 4.884 = 22.860 \text{ MJ/L}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Blend Density

$$\rho_{\text{blend}} = \sum_i x_i \cdot \rho_i = (0.85)(0.789) + (0.15)(0.740) = 0.782 \text{ kg/L}$$

### Step 3: Compute Mass-Based LHV

$$\text{LHV}_{\text{blend}} = \dfrac{\text{LHV}_v}{\rho_{\text{blend}}} = \dfrac{22.860}{0.782} = 29.23 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

### Step 4: Percentage Energy Deficit vs Gasoline

$$\%\text{Deficit} = \dfrac{44.0 - 29.23}{44.0} \times 100\% = 33.6\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{E85 carries } \mathbf{33.6\%} \text{ less energy per kg than gasoline}}$$

### Step 5: Volumetric Penalty (Real-World Driving Impact)

$$\text{Vol. Deficit} = \dfrac{44.0 \times 0.740 - 29.23 \times 0.782}{44.0 \times 0.740} \times 100\%$$

$$= \dfrac{32.56 - 22.86}{32.56} \times 100\% = 29.78\%$$

This **~30% volumetric energy penalty** is why E85 vehicles suffer **~25–30% lower fuel economy** (km/L) compared to gasoline operation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CO₂ Emission Reduction for Gasohol (E10)

### Step 1: Combustion Equation for Pure Gasoline (modelled as $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$)

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Per kg of gasoline:** $n_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{8}{0.114} = 3.158$ kmol CO₂ (since 1 kmol octane = 114 kg)
$$\text{CO}_2/\text{kg} = 3.158 \times 44 = 3.169 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

### Step 2: Combustion Equation for Ethanol

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Per kg ethanol (MW = 46):
$$n_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{2}{0.046} = 43.48 \text{ mol/kg}$$
$$\text{CO}_2/\text{kg} = 43.48 \times 0.044 = 1.913 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

### Step 3: Weighted CO₂ for E10 (mass fractions)

$$y_{\text{eth}}^{E10} = \dfrac{0.10 \times 0.789}{0.10 \times 0.789 + 0.90 \times 0.740} = \dfrac{0.0789}{0.7449} = 0.1059$$

$$y_{\text{gas}}^{E10} = 0.8941$$

### Step 4: Tailpipe CO₂ per kg of E10

$$\text{CO}_2^{E10} = (0.1059)(1.913) + (0.8941)(3.169)$$

$$= 0.2026 + 2.834 = 3.037 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kg E10}$$

### Step 5: Reduction Percentage

$$\%\text{Reduction}_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{3.169 - 3.037}{3.169} \times 100\% \approx 4.16\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{E10 reduces tailpipe CO}_2 \text{ by } \approx \mathbf{4\text{–}5\%} \text{ per kg of fuel burned}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **tailpipe only**. The **well-to-wheel reduction is 20–30%** when sugarcane ethanol's agricultural carbon uptake is credited.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

### Table 3.1 — Gasohol vs E85: Engineering Trade-off Matrix

| Parameter | Gasohol (E10) | E85 |
|-----------|---------------|-----|
| Ethanol content (vol%) | 5–10% | 70–85% |
| Engine modification | None (drop-in) | FFV required |
| Octane number (RON) | ~91–94 | ~100–105 |
| Energy density (MJ/L) | ~32.5 | ~22.9 |
| Cold-start capability | Excellent | Marginal (needs gasoline injector) |
| Material compatibility | Safe with standard elastomers | Requires fluorocarbon seals |
| Volumetric fuel economy penalty | ~2–3% | ~25–30% |
| Tailpipe CO reduction | ~20–25% | ~30–40% |
| CO₂ reduction (tailpipe) | ~4–5% | ~10–12% |
| Production cost vs gasoline | Slightly higher | 1.5–2× higher |
| Vehicle availability | All modern cars | FFV only (~20% of US fleet) |
| Refueling infrastructure | Universal | Limited (USA ~4,000 stations) |
| Hygroscopic risk | Low | High (phase separation at ~0.5% water) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Alcohol-Gasoline Blend Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Alcohol-Gasoline Blends]:::root --> B[Low-Percentage Blends]
    A --> C[Mid-Range Blends]
    A --> D[High-Percentage Blends]

    B --> B1[E5 - 5 percent ethanol]
    B --> B2[E10 Gasohol - 10 percent ethanol]
    B --> B3[E20 - Brazil Standard]

    C --> C1[E25 - Brazilian Premium]
    C --> C2[E30 - Experimental Markets]

    D --> D1[E70 - Winter Grade E85]
    D --> D2[E85 - Summer Grade FFV]

    B1 --> Z[Drop-in Fuel - No Engine Modification]
    B2 --> Z
    B3 --> Z
    C1 --> Y[Mid-FFV Calibration Required]
    C2 --> Y
    D1 --> X[Dedicated FFV System Required]
    D2 --> X

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef default fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Flexible Fuel Vehicle (FFV) System Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FUEL[Ethanol-Gasoline Blend Tank]:::tank --> FUELPUMP[High Pressure Fuel Pump]
    FUELPUMP --> FUELR[Stainless Fuel Rail]
    FUELR --> ACS[Alcohol Composition Sensor]
    ACS --> ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    ECU --> INJ[Multiport Fuel Injectors]
    ECU --> IGN[Ignition Coil Pack]

    ECU -->|Pulse Width Modulation| INJ
    ECU -->|Timing Advance Control| IGN

    INJ --> CYL[Combustion Chamber]
    IGN --> CYL
    CYL --> EXH[Exhaust Manifold]
    EXH --> CAT[Three-Way Catalytic Converter]
    CAT --> TAIL[Tailpipe]

    LAM[Lambda Oxygen Sensor] -->|Feedback| ECU
    CYL -->|Exhaust Gas| LAM

    COLD[Secondary Cold-Start Gasoline Injector]:::cold --> CYL
    CYL --> CRANK[Crank Position Sensor] --> ECU
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[Ethanol Hygroscopic Behaviour]:::warn --> W[Water Absorption Threshold]
    W --> W1[Below 0.5 percent water: Miscible]
    W --> W2[Above 0.5 percent water: Phase Separation]

    W2 --> W2A[Aqueous Phase Ethanol-Rich Bottom Layer]
    W2A --> W2A1[Engine Corrosion Risk]
    W2A1 --> W2A1a[Cylinder Wall Pitting]

    W2 --> W2B[Gasoline Phase Top Layer]
    W2B --> W2B1[Reduced Octane Number]

    M --> M2[Aluminium Fuel Rails - Susceptible]
```
