# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Define the term "gasohol". State its typical composition and the role of ethanol in the blend.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**Definition (1 mark):** Gasohol is a low-percentage alcohol-gasoline blend, originally developed in the 1970s, in which anhydrous ethanol is mixed with unleaded gasoline to enhance octane rating, improve combustion cleanliness, and provide renewable carbon content.

**Composition (1 mark):** The most common commercial formulation is **E10**, comprising **10% ethanol (by volume) + 90% unleaded gasoline**. Other variants include E5 (Europe, India) and E7.7 (Brazil legacy).

**Role of ethanol (1 mark):** Ethanol acts as an **octane booster** (raises RON by 2–3 points), an **oxygenated additive** (provides bound oxygen for cleaner combustion, reducing CO), and a **renewable component** that displaces petroleum-derived hydrocarbons.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Why is E85 not recommended for use in conventional gasoline vehicles? List three reasons.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):

1. **Energy density penalty:** E85 has ~30% lower volumetric energy content, demanding substantially more frequent refueling and incompatible injector sizing.

2. **Material incompatibility:** Ethanol attacks conventional elastomers (neoprene, Buna-N), plastic fuel rails, and certain aluminium alloys, causing seal failure, corrosion, and fuel leaks.

3. **Cold-start failure:** Ethanol's low vapor pressure at sub-zero temperatures (vapor pressure ≈ 17 kPa at 20°C vs 50–90 kPa for gasoline) prevents adequate fuel-air atomization, causing hard starting or no-start conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module ESE pattern requires **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. Both must be attempted; the higher score is retained.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** With the help of relevant chemical equations, derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for **ethanol**. Compare it with that of gasoline and explain why FFV engines require larger fuel injectors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A driver fills his FFV car with **E85 (85% ethanol + 15% gasoline by volume)**. Given:
- $\rho_{\text{ethanol}} = 0.789$ kg/L, $\rho_{\text{gasoline}} = 0.740$ kg/L
- $\text{LHV}_{\text{ethanol}} = 26.8$ MJ/kg, $\text{LHV}_{\text{gasoline}} = 44.0$ MJ/kg
- Stoichiometric AFR: ethanol = 9.0, gasoline = 14.7

Calculate: (i) the mass fractions, (ii) the **stoichiometric AFR of the E85 blend**, (iii) the **volumetric fuel consumption penalty** if the engine delivers 100 MJ of brake energy per km. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Derivation of Stoichiometric AFR for Ethanol (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Combustion equation (2 marks):**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Verification of atom balance:
- C: 2 = 2 ✓
- H: 6 = 6 ✓
- O: 1 + 6 = 7; RHS: 4 + 3 = 7 ✓

**Step 2 — Moles of air (2 marks):**

$$n_{\text{O}_2} = 3 \text{ mol}, \quad n_{\text{air}} = 3 \times \dfrac{100}{21} = 14.286 \text{ mol air}$$

**Step 3 — Mass calculation (2 marks):**

$$m_{\text{fuel}} = (2)(12) + (6)(1) + (1)(16) = 46 \text{ g}$$

$$m_{\text{air}} = 14.286 \times 29 = 414.29 \text{ g}$$

$$\boxed{\text{AFR}_{\text{ethanol}} = \dfrac{414.29}{46} = 9.0}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison & Injector Sizing (1 mark):**

Gasoline AFR ≈ 14.7 vs ethanol ≈ 9.0. The **FFV must inject ~63% more fuel mass** to maintain stoichiometry when running on E85. Consequently, **injector orifice area is enlarged** (typically by 30–40%) and **fuel pump flow capacity is increased** to deliver the higher mass flow.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Analysis of E85 Blend (7 marks)

**(i) Mass fractions (2 marks):**

For 1 L of E85:
- $m_{\text{eth}} = 0.85 \times 0.789 = 0.6707$ kg
- $m_{\text{gas}} = 0.15 \times 0.740 = 0.1110$ kg
- $m_{\text{total}} = 0.7817$ kg

$$y_{\text{eth}} = \dfrac{0.6707}{0.7817} = 0.8581, \quad y_{\text{gas}} = \dfrac{0.1110}{0.7817} = 0.1419$$

**(ii) Stoichiometric AFR of E85 (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{1}{\text{AFR}_{\text{E85}}} = \dfrac{0.8581}{9.0} + \dfrac{0.1419}{14.7} = 0.09534 + 0.009653 = 0.10499$$

$$\boxed{\text{AFR}_{\text{E85}} = 9.52 : 1}$$

**(iii) Volumetric fuel consumption penalty (3 marks):**

First, compute blend energy density:

$$\rho_{\text{blend}} = (0.85)(0.789) + (0.15)(0.740) = 0.782 \text{ kg/L}$$

$$\text{LHV}_{\text{blend}} = (0.8581)(26.8) + (0.1419)(44.0) = 23.00 + 6.24 = 29.24 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

$$\text{ED}_v^{\text{E85}} = 29.24 \times 0.782 = 22.86 \text{ MJ/L}$$

For pure gasoline: $\text{ED}_v^{\text{gas}} = 44.0 \times 0.740 = 32.56 \text{ MJ/L}$

Mass of E85 needed per km:
$$m_{\text{E85}} = \dfrac{100}{29.24} = 3.420 \text{ kg/km}$$

Volume of E85:
$$V_{\text{E85}} = \dfrac{3.420}{0.782} = 4.373 \text{ L/km}$$

Mass of gasoline needed per km:
$$m_{\text{gas}} = \dfrac{100}{44.0} = 2.273 \text{ kg/km}$$

Volume of gasoline:
$$V_{\text{gas}} = \dfrac{2.273}{0.740} = 3.072 \text{ L/km}$$

Penalty:
$$\%\text{Increase} = \dfrac{4.373 - 3.072}{3.072} \times 100\% = 42.3\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Volumetric fuel consumption on E85 is } \approx 42.3\% \text{ higher than gasoline per km}}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations of alcohol-gasoline blends** as automotive fuels, with particular reference to gasohol (E10) and E85. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain in detail the construction and working of a **Flexible Fuel Vehicle (FFV)** system. Discuss the role of the **alcohol composition sensor** and **ECU fuel-mapping strategy** in enabling multi-fuel operation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline (7 marks each):

### Part (a) — Advantages & Limitations (7 marks)

| Advantages (3 marks) | Limitations (4 marks) |
|---|---|
| Higher octane (RON 108 for ethanol) | Lower energy density (33% deficit) |
| Reduced CO & HC emissions | Cold-start difficulty |
| Renewable, bio-based feedstock | Material corrosion (rubbers, plastics) |
| Lower aromatic content (less carcinogenic) | Hygroscopic — phase separation risk |
| Reduces petroleum import dependence | Increased NOx at certain blends |
| Supports rural agricultural economy | Higher aldehyde & unburned alcohol emissions |
| Biodegradable; non-toxic | Distillation energy intensive |
| Allows higher CR in future engines | Limited refueling infrastructure |

### Part (b) — FFV System (7 marks — 2+2+2+1 allocation)

1. **Fuel tank & delivery system (2 marks):** Stainless-steel tank with fuel-grade ethanol-compatible lining; high-pressure pump rated for E85's lower lubricity.

2. **Alcohol composition sensor (2 marks):** Measures ethanol fraction in real time using **capacitive dielectric sensing** — ethanol has dielectric constant ~24 vs gasoline ~2. Sensor output (0–5 V) is fed to ECU.

3. **ECU fuel-mapping strategy (2 marks):** ECU references a multi-dimensional lookup table of injection pulse-widths vs (engine speed, load, ethanol fraction, coolant temp). On-the-fly recalculation adjusts **stoichiometric pulse width**, **cold-start enrichment**, and **ignition advance** for current ethanol content.

4. **Cold-start assist (1 mark):** Secondary gasoline injector or heated fuel rail vaporizes a volatile gasoline fraction during cranking to enable cold starts in winter conditions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (read carefully before writing your exam):**
>
> 1. **Confusing volumetric and mass fractions** — Many students use volume % directly as mass % in AFR calculations, costing **2–3 marks instantly**. Always convert using densities.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to verify combustion equation atom balance** — KTU examiners specifically check whether students balanced **C, H, and O** on both sides. An unbalanced equation = **−1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Using only one density (gasoline's) for the blend** — Energy density of blend MUST use the blended density, not pure gasoline density.
>
> 4. **Confusing LHV with HHV** — Use **Lower Heating Value** for IC engines (water leaves as vapor in exhaust). HHV is for boilers/furnaces.
>
> 5. **Skipping units in final answer** — Always annotate "MJ/kg", "kg CO₂/kg fuel", "L/km" — examiners deduct **0.5 mark per missing unit**.
>
> 6. **Linear octane blending assumption** — Octane blending is **non-linear** (especially for MON). State "approximate linear rule" if you use it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary — Pin This to Your Wall Before Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Gasohol** = generic term for low-ethanol blends (E5, E10, E20); **E10** is the global standard
- ✅ **E85** = 70–85% ethanol + 15–30% gasoline; **only for FFVs**
- ✅ Ethanol chemical formula: **C₂H₅OH** — renewable, oxygenated, high-octane
- ✅ Ethanol RON: **~108** (vs gasoline 87–98) → superior antiknock quality
- ✅ Ethanol LHV: **26.8 MJ/kg** (~33% less than gasoline's 44 MJ/kg)
- ✅ Stoichiometric AFR: ethanol = **9.0:1**, gasoline = **14.7:1**, E85 ≈ **9.52:1**
- ✅ Density: ethanol = **0.789 kg/L**, gasoline = **0.74 kg/L**
- ✅ E10 → ~4–5% tailpipe CO₂ reduction; **20–30% well-to-wheel** with biomass credit
- ✅ E85 → ~25–30% volumetric fuel-economy **penalty** (real-world penalty ≈ 25–35%)
- ✅ **Advantages:** high octane, renewable, cleaner exhaust, supports agriculture
- ✅ **Limitations:** hygroscopic, corrosive to materials, cold-start issues, lower energy density
- ✅ **FFV essentials:** alcohol composition sensor + ECU fuel-mapping + cold-start gasoline injector + fluorocarbon seals
- ✅ **Brazil** uses E20–E25 (since 1976 ProÁlcool); **USA** uses E10 standard + E85 FFV; **India** launched **E20** in 2023 (target)
- ✅ Material compatibility: use **Viton, Teflon, stainless steel** — never neoprene/Buna-N with E85
- ✅ **Phase separation** occurs at **>0.5% water** in gasoline-ethanol blend → catastrophic engine damage
- ✅ **Cold-start solution:** winter-grade E70 (30% gasoline) or secondary gasoline injector
- ✅ Memorize the combustion equation: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- ✅ AFR harmonic blending rule is **mass-fraction weighted** — never use volume fraction directly

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressed natural gas (CNG), liquefied natural gas (LNG) and biogas: availability, properties and engine modifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compressed natural gas (CNG), liquefied natural gas (LNG) and biogas: availability, properties and engine modifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)** is natural gas that has been compressed to a high pressure (typically **200–250 bar / 20–25 MPa**) and stored in high-pressure cylindrical tanks at ambient temperature for use as a clean-burning automotive fuel. It is primarily composed of **methane (CH₄)** with traces of ethane, propane, CO₂, N₂, and H₂S.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Canteen Cylinder" Analogy**
Think of CNG like a pressurized water cooler bottle. A 20-litre water bottle holds only ~20 L of air at atmospheric pressure, but if you compress that same air into a tiny diving tank, you can store a much larger *mass* of gas in a smaller *volume*. CNG works on the exact same principle — natural gas is squashed into thick-walled steel/composite cylinders so that a meaningful quantity of fuel fits on a vehicle.

---

### 1.2 Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG)** is natural gas (primarily methane) that has been cooled down to its condensation point of approximately **−162 °C (111 K)** at near-atmospheric pressure, converting it to a cryogenic liquid. LNG occupies only **1/600th** the volume of natural gas in its gaseous state at standard conditions, making it ideal for long-distance transport and storage.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Freezer Trick"**
Imagine blowing up a balloon and then putting it in a deep freezer at −162 °C. The gas molecules slow down so much that they "fall" onto each other and become a liquid. LNG is the same idea, but the "freezer" is a huge industrial cryogenic plant and the "balloon" is an insulated cryogenic tank.

---

### 1.3 Biogas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Biogas** is a **renewable gaseous fuel** produced by the **anaerobic digestion (bacterial fermentation in absence of oxygen)** of organic matter such as agricultural waste, animal dung, municipal solid waste, sewage sludge, and food waste. Its typical composition is **50–70 % CH₄**, **30–40 % CO₂**, with trace H₂S, water vapour, and N₂.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen Compost Jar"**
Think of the way food scraps rot in a sealed compost bin and produce bubbles of smelly gas. Biogas is exactly that — controlled rotting. The bacteria act like tiny "cooks" breaking down organic matter in a sealed oxygen-free digester, and the resulting methane-rich gas is captured as fuel.

---

### 1.4 Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | CNG | LNG | Biogas |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary State** | High-pressure gas | Cryogenic liquid | Low-pressure gas |
| **Storage Pressure** | **200–250 bar** | ~1 bar (gauge) | ~1 mbar to 1 bar |
| **Storage Temperature** | Ambient (~25 °C) | **−162 °C** | Ambient |
| **Main Constituent** | CH₄ (85–95 %) | CH₄ (>90 %) | CH₄ (50–70 %) |
| **Source** | Fossil (wells) | Fossil (wells, liquefied) | Renewable (biomass) |
| **Renewable?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Availability of Each Fuel

#### 2.1.1 CNG Availability
- Found in **natural gas reservoirs** (associated and non-associated gas fields).
- Major producers: Russia, USA, Iran, Qatar, India (KG Basin, Assam).
- Distributed via **pipelines** to city gas stations or **transported as CNG cascades** by trucks.

#### 2.1.2 LNG Availability
- Same source as CNG, but used when **long-distance transport** (sea) is required.
- Used in heavy-duty trucks and ships (bunkering fuel).
- Major exporters: Qatar, Australia, USA, Malaysia.

#### 2.1.3 Biogas Availability
- Sources: **agricultural residues** (rice straw, sugarcane bagasse), **animal manure**, **municipal waste**, **sewage**, **food waste**.
- Captured in anaerobic **digesters** (covered lagoons, plug-flow, CSTR).
- Especially abundant in Kerala — coconut husk, coir pith, kitchen waste, dairy manure.

---

### 2.2 Properties — KTU Formula & Data Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All properties below are **high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE**. Memorize the bold figures.

| Property | CNG | LNG | Biogas (typical) | Petrol (comparison) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Molecular weight** (kg/kmol) | 16–19 | ~16 | 18–22 | ~110 |
| **Density at storage** (kg/m³) | ~180–200 (at 200 bar) | **~410–450** (liquid) | ~1.2 (gas) | ~740 (liquid) |
| **Lower Calorific Value, LHV** (MJ/kg) | **~50** | **~50** | ~20 | 44 |
| **LHV (MJ/m³ at NTP)** | ~32 | ~22,000 (liquid per m³) | ~17–22 | 31,000 (liquid) |
| **Methane Number / Octane Number** | **120–130** | **120–130** | ~110 | 87–98 (RON) |
| **Stoichiometric AFR** | **17.2 : 1** | **17.2 : 1** | ~10–12 : 1 (with CO₂) | 14.7 : 1 |
| **Auto-ignition Temp.** (°C) | **540** | 540 | 650–750 | 228 |
| **Flammability Limits** (% vol) | 5–15 | 5–15 | 8.5–20.5 | 1.4–7.6 |
| **Laminar flame speed** (cm/s) | ~35 | ~35 | ~25 | ~37 |

#### KTU-Important Equations

**1. Ideal Gas Law for CNG Tank Sizing**

$$
PV = nRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{PV \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T}
$$

where $P$ = pressure (Pa), $V$ = volume (m³), $M$ = molar mass (kg/kmol), $R_u$ = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K**, $T$ = absolute temperature (K), $m$ = mass of gas stored (kg).

**2. Energy Stored in a CNG Cylinder**

$$
E_{\text{CNG}} = m \cdot \text{LHV} = \frac{P \cdot V \cdot M \cdot \text{LHV}}{R_u \cdot T}
$$

**3. Compression Work for CNG (Isothermal, ideal)**

$$
W_{\text{comp}} = P_1 V_1 \ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad \text{[Joules]}
$$

For multi-stage with intercooling, multiplied by stage count efficiency factor.

**4. LNG Volumetric Reduction Ratio**

$$
\text{Volume Ratio} = \frac{V_{\text{gas @ STP}}}{V_{\text{liquid}}} \approx \mathbf{600 : 1}
$$

Because at STP (1 atm, 15 °C) 1 m³ methane ≈ 0.717 kg, while 1 m³ LNG ≈ 410 kg.

**5. Density of Methane at LNG Conditions**

$$
\rho_{\text{CH}_4}^{\text{LNG}} \approx 422 \text{ kg/m}^3 \text{ at } -162\,^\circ\text{C, 1 atm}
$$

**6. Calorific Value of Biogas**

$$
\text{CV}_{\text{biogas}} = x_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{CH}_4} + x_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot \text{CV}_{\text{CO}_2}
$$

CO₂ contributes nothing (0), so:
$$
\text{CV}_{\text{biogas}} \approx 0.6 \times 50 = 30 \text{ MJ/kg methane fraction basis}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility** — These formulas are used by **fuel tank designers** (CNG cylinder sizing), **gas station engineers** (compressor work calculations), **cryogenic plant designers** (LNG storage), and **biogas plant operators** (digester yield estimation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation: Mass of CNG Stored in a 50 L Tank at 200 bar

**Given:**
- Tank volume, $V = 50 \text{ L} = 0.050 \text{ m}^3$
- Charging pressure, $P_2 = 200 \text{ bar} = 20 \text{ MPa} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Ambient temperature, $T = 298 \text{ K}$
- Assume methane, $M = 16 \text{ kg/kmol}$
- Universal gas constant, $R_u = 8314 \text{ J/(kmol·K)}$

**Step 1 — Apply ideal gas equation in mass form:**

$$
P V = \frac{m}{M} R_u T
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $m$:**

$$
m = \frac{P V M}{R_u T}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
m = \frac{(20 \times 10^6) \times (0.050) \times (16)}{(8314) \times (298)}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$
(20 \times 10^6) \times (0.050) = 1.0 \times 10^6
$$
$$
1.0 \times 10^6 \times 16 = 1.6 \times 10^7 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator evaluation:**

$$
8314 \times 298 = 2{,}477{,}572 \text{ J/kmol}
$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$
m = \frac{1.6 \times 10^7}{2.477 \times 10^6} \approx 6.46 \text{ kg of CH}_4
$$

**Step 7 — Energy stored (using LHV = 50 MJ/kg):**

$$
E = m \times \text{LHV} = 6.46 \times 50 = 323 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Comparison:** This is roughly equivalent to **~9 litres of petrol** in energy content — explaining why CNG tanks are physically large for the energy they store.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Biogas Calorific Value from Composition

**Given composition by volume:**
- CH₄ = 65 %, CO₂ = 33 %, H₂S = 0.5 %, N₂ = 1.5 %
- LHV of CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg, molecular weight = 16 kg/kmol

**Step 1 — Effective LHV of mixture per kg of gas:**
Only CH₄ contributes fuel value (CO₂, N₂ are inert; H₂S negligible).

**Step 2 — Mass fraction of CH₄ in mixture:**

$$
y_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{0.65 \times 16}{0.65(16) + 0.33(44) + 0.005(34) + 0.015(28)}
$$

Numerator: $0.65 \times 16 = 10.4$

Denominator: $10.4 + 14.52 + 0.17 + 0.42 = 25.51$

$$
y_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{10.4}{25.51} = 0.4077
$$

**Step 3 — Calorific value per kg of biogas:**

$$
\text{CV}_{\text{biogas}} = 0.4077 \times 50 = 20.39 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric basis at NTP (1 m³ ≈ 1.25 kg for this mixture):**

$$
\text{CV}_{\text{biogas}} \approx 25.5 \text{ MJ/m}^3 \text{ at NTP}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why Engine Modifications Are Needed

| Original Fuel | Property Limitation | Required Engine Modification |
|---|---|---|
| Petrol | High volatility, high energy density | — (baseline) |
| CNG | **Gaseous at all conditions** (no carburation) | Pressurized cylinder, pressure regulator, gas mixer / sequential injectors |
| LNG | **Cryogenic**, evaporates continuously | Cryogenic tank, vaporizer (heat exchanger), pressure regulator |
| Biogas | **Low CV, high CO₂ dilutes combustion** | H₂S scrubber, CO₂ removal (water scrubber / PSA), moisture trap |
| Diesel | Auto-ignition only | Dual-fuel mode: diesel pilot injection + gas induction (or full conversion with spark ignition & raised compression ratio) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examiners** — Gaseous fuels have **higher octane rating**, which means **higher compression ratios can be used** → improved thermal efficiency. This is a favourite ESE question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CNG Vehicle Fuel System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CNG Storage Cylinder 200 bar] --> B[Manual Shut-off Valve]
    B --> C[High-pressure Filter]
    C --> D[Pressure Regulator Stage 1 High to Med pressure]
    D --> E[Solenoid Valve]
    E --> F[Pressure Regulator Stage 2 Med to Low pressure]
    F --> G[Gas Mixer / Sequential Gas Injector]
    G --> H[Intake Manifold]
    H --> I[Engine Combustion Chamber]
    I --> J[Exhaust]
    K[Fuel Level Sensor] --> A
    L[ECU Engine Control Unit] --> G
    L --> E
```

**Description of Block Flow:**
1. CNG exits the high-pressure cylinder.
2. Passes through a **manual shut-off** (safety) and **filter** (removes particulates).
3. **Two-stage regulator** drops pressure from 200 bar → ~7 bar → ~1.05 bar.
4. ECU actuates the **solenoid valve** for sequenced injection into the **mixer/injector**.
5. Gas-air mixture enters the **intake manifold** for SI combustion.

---

### 4.2 LNG Vehicle Fuel System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[LNG Cryogenic Tank -162 C] --> B1[Economizer Heat Exchanger uses engine coolant]
    B1 --> C1[Regulator Low pressure gas]
    C1 --> D1[Solenoid Injector]
    D1 --> E1[Intake Manifold]
    E1 --> F1[Engine SI Mode]
    G1[Tank Pressure Build-up Vaporizer uses exhaust heat] --> A1
    H1[Vent Valve safety relief] --> A1
    I1[ECU] --> D1
    I1 --> G1
```

**Key Components:**
- **Cryogenic tank** — double-walled vacuum insulation.
- **Economizer** — uses engine coolant to gasify LNG.
- **Pressure build-up vaporizer** — uses exhaust heat to maintain tank pressure at ~7–9 bar.
- **Vent valve** — releases boil-off gas during long parking.

---

### 4.3 Biogas-to-Engine Supply System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Anaerobic Digester] --> B2[Primary Gas Holder]
    B2 --> C2[H2S Scrubber Iron Oxide or biological]
    C2 --> D2[Dehumidifier Moisture Trap]
    D2 --> E2[CO2 Removal Water Scrubber or PSA]
    E2 --> F2[Secondary Gas Holder Pressurized Buffer]
    F2 --> G2[Compressor 1 bar to 200 bar]
    G2 --> H2[CNG-style Regulator and Mixer]
    H2 --> I2[Engine]
    J2[Sludge Outlet] --> A2
```

**Description:**
Biogas from the digester undergoes **cleaning train**: H₂S removal (corrosion & pollution control) → moisture removal (prevents corrosion) → CO₂ removal (boosts CV) → compression to vehicle-grade pressure.

---

### 4.4 Engine Modification Summary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[Original SI Engine] --> P[CNG Conversion]
    O --> Q[LNG Conversion]
    O --> R[Biogas Conversion]
    O --> S[Diesel to Dual-Fuel]

    P --> P1[Gas mixer, regulator, cylinder]
    Q --> Q1[Cryo tank, vaporizer, gas mixer]
    R --> R1[Scrubber, dryer, CNG-like system]
    S --> S1[Diesel pilot injector, gas induction port]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages of CNG as an automotive fuel over petrol.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher octane number (~120)** allows higher compression ratio → better thermal efficiency.
2. **Lower emissions** — produces ~25 % less CO₂, negligible lead, particulate emissions are minimal.
3. **Safer** — lighter than air, so disperses upward in case of leakage (no pooling like petrol).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Why is LNG preferred over CNG for long-distance transportation of natural gas?*

**Model Answer:**
LNG occupies **1/600th** the volume of gaseous methane at standard conditions because it is cryogenically liquefied at **−162 °C**. This drastically reduces the volume to be shipped, making marine transport via cryogenic tankers economically viable for intercontinental distances, whereas CNG would require either very high-pressure cylinders or pipelines.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **availability and production of CNG** for automotive applications. Discuss its key physical and chemical properties relevant to engine design.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **engine modifications** required to convert a petrol (SI) engine to run on CNG, with a neat block diagram of the fuel supply system.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Availability:**
- Natural gas is extracted from underground reservoirs (associated with crude oil or as non-associated dry gas fields).
- It is processed at gas processing plants to remove water, H₂S, CO₂, and heavier hydrocarbons.
- Distributed to cities via **PNG (Pipeline Natural Gas)** network or transported as CNG cascades.
- **India context:** KG Basin (Andhra Pradesh), Rajasthan, Assam, Cauvery Basin.

**Properties:**
- **Composition:** 85–95 % CH₄, balance ethane, propane, CO₂, N₂.
- **Octane number:** 120–130 (very high → permits high CR).
- **Calorific value (LHV):** ~50 MJ/kg.
- **Storage density at 200 bar:** ~180 kg/m³.
- **Stoichiometric AFR:** 17.2:1 (very lean compared to petrol 14.7:1).
- **Flammability limits:** 5–15 % by volume (narrower than petrol — safer in open air).
- **Auto-ignition temperature:** 540 °C (high → resists knocking).

**Model Solution (b):**
The major engine modifications for SI-to-CNG conversion are:
1. **CNG cylinder** (200–250 bar) mounted in trunk or under chassis.
2. **Filling valve** with check valve (non-return).
3. **High-pressure filter** to trap particulates.
4. **Two-stage pressure regulator** (200 bar → 7 bar → 1.05 bar).
5. **Solenoid valve** controlled by ECU for gas shutoff during deceleration.
6. **Gas mixer or sequential gas injector** at intake manifold.
7. **MAP sensor** & **lambda sensor** for closed-loop AFR control.
8. **LPG/CNG switch** in cabin for fuel selection (in bi-fuel vehicles).
9. **Crankshaft position sensor calibration** for sequential injection timing.
10. Sometimes **increased compression ratio** to exploit higher octane rating.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention that the **ignition timing** must be re-mapped because CNG burns slower than petrol. Loss of 1–2 marks typical here.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Compare the **properties of CNG, LNG and Biogas** in a tabular form. Comment on their relative suitability as automotive fuels.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **engine modifications required for diesel-to-dual-fuel** operation using biogas. What are the limitations?

**Model Solution (a):**

| Property | CNG | LNG | Biogas |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storage state | High-pressure gas | Cryogenic liquid | Low-pressure gas |
| Energy density | Medium | High | Low |
| Refuelling time | Fast | Moderate (cryogenic) | Slow (needs cleaning) |
| Infrastructure | Pipelines & cascades | Cryogenic terminals | Local digesters |
| CV (MJ/m³ NTP) | ~32 | ~22,000 (liquid) | ~18–22 |
| Suitability | City buses, cars | Heavy trucks, ships | Rural stationary / farm vehicles |

**Comparative Comments:**
- **CNG** is best suited for **urban fleets** (buses, autos, cars) where pipelines exist.
- **LNG** is best for **long-haul heavy-duty** trucks and ships due to higher energy density.
- **Biogas** is best for **rural/agrarian stationary power** or local farm vehicles, but cleaning and compression limit its large-scale adoption.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Diesel-to-Dual-Fuel Biogas Conversion Modifications:**
1. **Gas induction port** in intake manifold for biogas entry.
2. **Biogas cleaning train** (H₂S scrubber, dehumidifier, CO₂ removal).
3. **Biogas compressor** + regulator (1 bar to ~4 bar for induction).
4. **Diesel pilot injector** retained for ignition source.
5. **ECU re-mapping** for dual-fuel mode (shorter diesel pilot, advanced gas timing).
6. **Gas mass flow sensor** added.
7. **Throttle body** to control gas flow.

**Limitations:**
- **Power derating** of 10–25 % because biogas dilutes combustion (CO₂ acts as ballast).
- **Knocking tendency** at high loads due to lower methane number (~110).
- **Cold-start issues** — needs diesel pilot for ignition until exhaust warms.
- **Emission trade-off** — high HC and CO at low loads; need oxidation catalyst.
- **Storage constraints** — bulky gas cylinders at high pressure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to mention **power derating** and **H₂S corrosion** in biogas dual-fuel systems. This is a frequently-lost 2-mark topic in KTU valuation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **CNG** = natural gas compressed to **200–250 bar**, mainly CH₄, stored at ambient T.
- ✅ **LNG** = natural gas liquefied at **−162 °C**, occupies 1/600th volume.
- ✅ **Biogas** = renewable, 50–70 % CH₄ + 30–40 % CO₂, from anaerobic digestion.
- ✅ All three gaseous fuels have **higher octane number** than petrol → allow higher CR.
- ✅ All three have **higher auto-ignition temperature** → safer in knock terms but require spark ignition.
- ✅ **Stoichiometric AFR** for methane = **17.2 : 1**; for biogas higher due to CO₂ ballast.
- ✅ **LHV of pure methane** ≈ **50 MJ/kg**; biogas ≈ **20 MJ/kg** mixture basis.
- ✅ CNG requires **two-stage regulator + gas mixer/injector**.
- ✅ LNG requires **cryogenic tank + vaporizer (engine coolant + exhaust heat)**.
- ✅ Biogas requires **H₂S scrubber + moisture trap + CO₂ removal + compression**.
- ✅ Diesel-to-biogas uses **dual-fuel mode**: diesel pilot injection + biogas induction.
- ✅ **Power derating** of 10–25 % in dual-fuel mode is a guaranteed KTU exam point.
- ✅ **LNG boil-off** is unavoidable — vent valves must be designed for safety.
- ✅ **Renewability:** Only biogas is renewable among the three.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biogas: production and application (single or dual fuel)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Biogas: production and application (single or dual fuel)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biogas** is a **renewable, colourless, and odourless (when purified) gaseous biofuel** produced by the **anaerobic digestion (fermentation)** of organic biomass — such as agricultural residue, animal dung, municipal solid waste, food waste, sewage sludge, and energy crops — in the **complete absence of oxygen**, through the metabolic action of a consortium of **specialized microorganisms (methanogenic archaea)**.

The **typical composition of raw biogas** is:

| Component | Chemical Formula | Volumetric % |
|---|---|---|
| **Methane** | CH₄ | **55 – 65 %** |
| **Carbon Dioxide** | CO₂ | **30 – 40 %** |
| **Hydrogen Sulphide** | H₂S | **0.1 – 0.5 %** |
| **Water Vapour** | H₂O | **1 – 5 %** |
| **Nitrogen** | N₂ | **0.5 – 2 %** |
| **Trace Gases** | NH₃, Siloxanes | **< 0.5 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **calorific value of pure methane = 55.5 MJ/kg ≈ 39.8 MJ/m³ at STP**. Because raw biogas is only ~60 % methane, its **net calorific value ≈ 20 – 26 MJ/m³**, which is roughly **half of CNG (~36 MJ/m³)** and **two-thirds of LPG (~46 MJ/m³)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of biogas as a cow's stomach working in a steel tank!**
> A cow digests grass **without oxygen** inside its four stomach chambers. Tiny microbes break down the grass, releasing **methane** (which the cow belches!). Now imagine we **capture that methane** in a sealed tank — that captured gas **IS biogas**.

**Real-world parallel — what happens in a biogas plant step-by-step:**

- 🍽️ **Feeding** — Wet organic waste (kitchen scraps, cow dung, rice straw) is fed into a sealed tank called a **digester**.
- 🔒 **Sealed environment** — The tank is **airtight**, so no oxygen enters. Without oxygen, "good" aerobic microbes die, and "tough" anaerobic microbes thrive.
- 🦠 **Microbial feast** — These microbes eat the waste in stages, and as a by-product they release **methane + CO₂**, which bubbles up into a gas holder.
- ⛽ **Collection** — The gas is piped out for cooking, electricity, or **running vehicles**.
- 🌱 **Bonus output** — The leftover slurry is **bio-slurry**, an excellent organic fertilizer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for automobiles?**
> Biogas can either **completely replace** petrol/diesel (**single-fuel mode**, mainly in modified CNG vehicles) or **partially substitute** diesel in a **dual-fuel engine**, where a small pilot dose of diesel ignites the air-biogas mixture. India and Germany run thousands of buses and trucks on upgraded biogas (≈ 95–98 % CH₄), often called **CBG — Compressed Biogas**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Density of methane at STP (0 °C, 1 atm):** **0.717 kg/m³**
- **Density of CO₂ at STP:** **1.977 kg/m³**
- **Methane ignition temperature:** **595 °C** (much higher than petrol 280 °C, so harder to ignite → low cetane number)
- **Methane octane number:** **RON ≈ 130** (excellent anti-knock, but no lubrication)
- **Flammability limits of CH₄ in air:** **5 – 15 %** by volume
- **Stoichiometric A/F ratio for CH₄:** **17.16 : 1** (very lean compared to petrol 14.7)
- **Standard calorific value of raw biogas:** **≈ 22 MJ/m³** (for design purposes in KTU problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Biological Stages of Anaerobic Digestion

The conversion of complex biomass into biogas is **NOT a single chemical reaction** — it is a **4-stage microbiological cascade**. Each stage is performed by a different group of microbes, and **if any stage fails, the whole system collapses**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favourite — Draw & label the 4 stages.** Marks are awarded for the **names of intermediates and the group of microbes** at each stage.

#### **Stage 1 — Hydrolysis** *(Duration: hours to days)*
- Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, fats) are broken down by **extracellular enzymes** secreted by hydrolytic bacteria (*Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*).
- Outputs: **Sugars, amino acids, long-chain fatty acids**.
- This is the **rate-limiting stage** for lignocellulosic biomass (e.g., rice straw, wood) because lignin is hard to hydrolyse.

$$\text{Polymers} \xrightarrow{\text{Hydrolytic enzymes}} \text{Monomers (sugars, AA, LCFA)}$$

#### **Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation)**
- Acidogenic bacteria (*Clostridium, Bacillus*) convert monomers into **Volatile Fatty Acids (VFAs)**, alcohols, CO₂, H₂, and ammonia.
- pH drops to **5 – 6**; if it drops below 5, the system becomes "sour" and methanogens die.
- Examples: Glucose → Acetic acid, Propionic acid, Butyric acid, Ethanol.

#### **Stage 3 — Acetogenesis**
- Acetogenic bacteria convert VFAs and alcohols into **acetic acid (CH₃COOH), H₂, and CO₂** — the **direct precursors of methane**.
- These bacteria are **strictly anaerobic** and grow very slowly.

$$\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2$$

#### **Stage 4 — Methanogenesis** *(Most critical stage)*
- Two pathways occur **in parallel** by two distinct groups of methanogens:
  - **Acetoclastic methanogens** (*Methanosarcina, Methanosaeta*) — split acetate into methane (~70 % of CH₄):
  
  $$\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + \text{CO}_2$$
  
  - **Hydrogenotrophic methanogens** (*Methanococcus, Methanobacterium*) — reduce CO₂ with H₂ (~30 % of CH₄):
  
  $$\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why methanogens are fussy:** They need **strict anaerobic conditions**, a **neutral pH of 6.8 – 7.5**, and a **long retention time (15 – 40 days)**. Disturb any of these, and biogas production **stops abruptly**.

---

### 2.2 Critical Process Parameters (Asked in KTU numericals)

| Parameter | Mesophilic Range | Thermophilic Range | KTU Remark |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating temperature** | **30 – 40 °C** (optimum 35 °C) | **50 – 60 °C** (optimum 55 °C) | Higher T → faster reaction, but less stable |
| **pH** | 6.5 – 7.5 | 6.5 – 7.5 | Below 6 → acid crash |
| **C : N ratio** | 20 : 1 to 30 : 1 | 20 : 1 to 30 : 1 | Cow dung ≈ 25:1 (ideal) |
| **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)** | 20 – 40 days | 10 – 20 days | Thermophilic = faster |
| **Total Solids (TS) in feed** | 8 – 12 % | 8 – 12 % | Too dilute = washout; too thick = clogging |
| **Volumetric biogas yield** | 0.3 – 0.5 m³/m³/day | 0.6 – 1.0 m³/m³/day | Industrial benchmark |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All the formulas below are exam-ready.** Memorise the boundary conditions and units — KTU examiners deduct marks if units are missing.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Buswell Equation:** $\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + (n - a/4 - b/2)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow (n/2 - a/8 + b/4)\text{CO}_2 + (n/2 + a/8 - b/4)\text{CH}_4$ | Stoichiometric yield of CH₄ from any organic compound |
| 2 | **Theoretical CH₄ yield from glucose:** $1 \text{ kg glucose} = 0.25 \text{ kg CH}_4 = 0.37 \text{ m}^3$ CH₄ (at STP) | Used to compute maximum theoretical production |
| 3 | **Volumetric Biogas Yield:** $Y_v = \dfrac{V_g}{V_d \cdot t}$ where $V_g$ = gas volume (m³), $V_d$ = digester volume (m³), $t$ = time (days) | m³/m³/day, standard plant efficiency indicator |
| 4 | **Specific Gas Yield:** $Y_s = \dfrac{V_g}{M_s}$ (m³ gas per kg VS added) | Substrate comparison index |
| 5 | **Calorific value of biogas:** $CV_{bg} = X_{CH_4} \times 39.8$ MJ/m³ | Where $X_{CH_4}$ = volume fraction of CH₄ |
| 6 | **Density of biogas:** $\rho_{bg} = X_{CH_4}\cdot\rho_{CH_4} + (1-X_{CH_4})\cdot\rho_{CO_2}$ | Used for mass flow / mass balance |
| 7 | **Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR) for dual fuel:** $ESR = \dfrac{m_{diesel,saved} \cdot CV_{diesel}}{V_{bg} \cdot CV_{bg}}$ | Dimensionless — compares energy replaced to energy added |
| 8 | **Pilot fuel energy share:** $\%E_{pilot} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{pilot}\cdot CV_{pilot}}{\dot{m}_{pilot}\cdot CV_{pilot} + \dot{V}_{bg}\cdot CV_{bg}}\times 100$ | For dual-fuel engine design |
| 9 | **Theoretical CH₄ from substrate mass (general):** $V_{CH_4,STP} = \dfrac{22.4 \times M_s \times (n/2+a/8-b/4)}{M_w}$ in m³ | From Buswell stoichiometry, $M_w$ = molar mass of substrate |
| 10 | **Compressor work for CBG (200 bar from 1 bar):** $W_c = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}RT \ln(P_2/P_1)$ (isothermal, ideal gas approx.) | Upgrading / compression of biogas |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Biogas in Automobile Engineering

- **CBG (Compressed Biogas)** — Purified (>95 % CH₄), compressed to 200–250 bar, used in factory-fitted CNG vehicles. India's **SATAT scheme (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** has mandated 5,000 CBG plants by 2025.
- **LBG (Liquefied Biogas)** — Cryogenically liquefied at –162 °C for heavy trucks; 3× energy density of CBG.
- **Dual-fuel engines** — Used in tractors, gensets, marine engines, and heavy trucks (Cummins, MAN, Volvo have production models).
- **Range-extender for EVs** — Biogas-fuelled microturbines or fuel cells (PEMFC running on reformed methane) charge EV batteries — fully carbon-neutral cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Methane Yield from Glucose Using the Buswell Equation

This is the **most repeated KTU derivation** for this topic. Let us derive it completely.

**Given:** Substrate = glucose, formula **C₆H₁₂O₆** (so n = 6, a = 12, b = 6).
**General Buswell Equation:**

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4$$

**Step 1 — Substitute n = 6, a = 12, b = 6 into the H₂O coefficient:**

$$n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} = 6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the CO₂ coefficient:**

$$\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} = \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the CH₄ coefficient:**

$$\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} = \frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3$$

**Step 4 — Write the balanced reaction for glucose:**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{CH}_4$$

**Step 5 — Molar mass of glucose = 180 g/mol = 0.180 kg/mol.**

**Step 6 — One mole of glucose (180 g) produces 3 moles of CH₄.**

**Step 7 — Volume of 3 moles of CH₄ at STP = 3 × 22.4 = 67.2 litres = 0.0672 m³.**

**Step 8 — Methane yield per kg of glucose:**

$$Y_{CH_4} = \frac{0.0672 \text{ m}^3}{0.180 \text{ kg}} = 0.373 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 9 — Mass of 3 moles of CH₄ = 3 × 16 = 48 g = 0.048 kg.**

**Step 10 — Mass yield of CH₄ per kg of glucose:**

$$Y_{CH_4, mass} = \frac{0.048 \text{ kg}}{0.180 \text{ kg}} = 0.267 \text{ kg CH}_4/\text{kg glucose}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Checkpoint:** Theoretical max CH₄ from 1 kg of any carbohydrate = **0.25 kg = 0.373 m³** at STP. Real digesters achieve only 60–80 % of this due to microbial inefficiencies.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical CH₄ Yield from Cow Dung (C/N basis)

**Given:** Substrate composition (typical cow dung on dry basis):

| Element | Mass % |
|---|---|
| C | 40 % |
| H | 6 % |
| O | 35 % |
| N | 2.5 % |
| Others | 16.5 % |

**Step 1 — Normalize to empirical formula (per 100 g of VS, divide by atomic mass):**

$$n_C = \frac{40}{12} = 3.333, \quad n_H = \frac{6}{1} = 6.0, \quad n_O = \frac{35}{16} = 2.1875$$

So empirical formula = **C₃.₃₃H₆O₂.₁₉**.

**Step 2 — Apply Buswell (n = 3.33, a = 6, b = 2.19):**

H₂O coefficient:

$$3.33 - \frac{6}{4} - \frac{2.19}{2} = 3.33 - 1.5 - 1.095 = 0.735$$

CH₄ coefficient:

$$\frac{3.33}{2} + \frac{6}{8} - \frac{2.19}{4} = 1.665 + 0.75 - 0.5475 = 1.8675 \text{ mol CH}_4$$

**Step 3 — Molar mass of empirical formula:**

$$M_w = 3.33(12) + 6(1) + 2.19(16) = 39.96 + 6 + 35.04 = 81.0 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — CH₄ yield per kg of cow dung VS:**

$$V_{CH_4} = \frac{22.4 \text{ L/mol} \times 1.8675 \text{ mol}}{81.0 \text{ g}} = \frac{41.83}{81.0} = 0.516 \text{ L/g} = 0.516 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg VS}$$

**Step 5 — Methane fraction in biogas (typical = 60 %):**

$$V_{biogas} = \frac{0.516}{0.60} = 0.860 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg VS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches the **real-world benchmark of 0.8 – 1.0 m³ biogas per kg VS** of cow dung — confirming Buswell is an accurate upper-bound predictor.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Calorific Value & Energy Density of a Given Biogas

**Given:** Biogas with 60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂.

**Step 1 — CV of pure methane at STP:**

$$CV_{CH_4} = 55.5 \text{ MJ/kg} = 39.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — CV of biogas (per m³):**

$$CV_{bg} = 0.60 \times 39.8 = 23.88 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Density of biogas (per m³):**

$$\rho_{bg} = 0.60 \times 0.717 + 0.40 \times 1.977 = 0.4302 + 0.7908 = 1.221 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — CV per kg of biogas:**

$$CV_{bg,mass} = \frac{23.88}{1.221} = 19.56 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with petrol (CV ≈ 44 MJ/kg):**

$$\text{Energy ratio} = \frac{19.56}{44} = 0.445 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Biogas carries } 44.5\% \text{ the energy of petrol per kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Because of the heavy CO₂ ballast, biogas has **~½ the energy density** of petrol and **~⅔ that of pure methane**. **Upgrading to CBG (>95 % CH₄)** is therefore essential for any **automotive application** to give acceptable range.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dual-Fuel Engine: Energy Substitution & Pilot Fuel Share

**Given:** Dual-fuel engine running on 80 % diesel + 20 % biogas (by energy). Pilot diesel mass flow = 2 kg/h. Biogas volume flow = 8 m³/h. CV diesel = 43 MJ/kg, CV biogas = 22 MJ/m³.

**Step 1 — Energy from pilot diesel:**

$$E_{diesel} = 2 \times 43 = 86 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 2 — Energy from biogas:**

$$E_{bg} = 8 \times 22 = 176 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy input:**

$$E_{total} = 86 + 176 = 262 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Pilot fuel energy share:**

$$\%E_{pilot} = \frac{86}{262} \times 100 = 32.8\%$$

**Step 5 — Energy substitution ratio (ESR):** *(how many MJ of diesel energy are replaced by 1 MJ of biogas)*

$$ESR = \frac{86}{176} = 0.489 \quad \text{(i.e., 1 MJ biogas replaces 0.489 MJ diesel)}$$

**Step 6 — % diesel displacement:**

$$\% \text{ displacement} = \frac{176}{262} \times 100 = 67.2\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** In a well-tuned dual-fuel engine, **biogas can substitute 70–80 % of diesel energy** at full load, but at **low loads (< 25 %)** the pilot diesel share rises sharply because the air–biogas mixture may not auto-ignite.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Single Fuel vs Dual Fuel

| Parameter | Single-Fuel (100 % Biogas / CBG) | Dual-Fuel (Biogas + Pilot Diesel) |
|---|---|---|
| **Fuel storage** | High-pressure cylinders (200–250 bar CBG) | Biogas tank + diesel tank (hybrid) |
| **Ignition method** | Spark ignition (SI engine modification) | Compression ignition (CI engine, pilot diesel) |
| **Engine modification** | Major — new ECU, spark plugs, injectors, regulator | Minor — gas mixer + regulator, retain diesel pump |
| **Power output** | 85 – 95 % of petrol baseline | 90 – 100 % of diesel baseline |
| **Thermal efficiency** | 25 – 32 % (SI) | 32 – 38 % (CI) |
| **CO₂ emissions** | **70 – 80 % lower** than petrol (carbon-neutral fuel) | **30 – 45 % lower** than pure diesel |
| **NOx emissions** | Low (lean burn), but high at full load | Moderate (homogeneous charge reduces NOx) |
| **Capital cost** | High (new CNG kit ≈ ₹50–80k) | Low (retrofit kit ≈ ₹15–25k) |
| **Range anxiety** | High (CBG pumps sparse) | Low (diesel back-up always available) |
| **Application** | Cars, LCVs, city buses | Tractors, gensets, heavy trucks, marine |
| **Cold start** | Easy (SI spark) | Difficult (low cetane biogas) |
| **Maintenance** | High (dry fuel, valve seat recession) | Moderate (combustion of two fuels) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Biogas Production & Utilization Chain for Automobiles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Feedstock] --> A1[Cow dung]
    A --> A2[Agricultural residue]
    A --> A3[Food waste]
    A --> A4[Sewage sludge]
    A1 --> B[Pre-treatment Tank]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    B --> C[Anaerobic Digester - Sealed Tank]
    C --> D1[Stage 1: Hydrolysis]
    C --> D2[Stage 2: Acidogenesis]
    C --> D3[Stage 3: Acetogenesis]
    C --> D4[Stage 4: Methanogenesis]
    D1 --> E[Raw Biogas CH4 + CO2 + H2S]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Gas Cleaning Unit H2S Removal]
    F --> G[CO2 Scrubber - Water/MEA]
    G --> H[Upgraded Biogas 95 to 98 percent CH4]
    H --> I[Compressor 200 to 250 bar]
    I --> J[CBG Storage Cylinders]
    J --> K1[Single Fuel Mode - SI Engine]
    J --> K2[Dual Fuel Mode - CI Engine with Pilot Diesel]
    K1 --> L1[CNG Vehicle Retrofit]
    K2 --> L2[Heavy Trucks / Tractors / Gensets]
    C --> M[Digestate Bio-slurry Fertilizer]
    M --> N[Agricultural Field Application]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the **left-side input** (biomass) downward through the **digester** (4 microbial stages), up to the **right-side output** (two parallel engine modes). The **bottom loop** shows the **zero-waste circular economy** — even the digestate is a sellable product.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vehicle Application Architecture (Single vs Dual Fuel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SingleFuel ["SINGLE FUEL MODE - SI Engine"]
        SF1[CBG Cylinder 200 bar] --> SF2[Pressure Regulator]
        SF2 --> SF3[Gas Mixer Venturi]
        SF4[Air Intake] --> SF3
        SF3 --> SF5[SI Engine Cylinder]
        SF5 --> SF6[Spark Plug Ignition]
        SF6 --> SF7[Power Output Shaft]
    end
    subgraph DualFuel ["DUAL FUEL MODE - CI Engine"]
        DF1[Biogas Cylinder Low Pressure] --> DF2[Gas Regulator]
        DF2 --> DF3[Intake Manifold Mixer]
        DF4[Air Intake] --> DF3
        DF3 --> DF5[CI Engine Cylinder]
        DF6[Diesel Tank] --> DF7[High Pressure Pump]
        DF7 --> DF8[Common Rail]
        DF8 --> DF5
        DF5 --> DF9[Compression Ignition of Pilot Diesel]
        DF9 --> DF10[Power Output Shaft]
    end
    SF7 --> VEHICLE[Vehicle Drivetrain]
    DF10 --> VEHICLE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key visual difference:** In **Single-Fuel** mode, **only biogas + air** enter the cylinder, ignited by a **spark plug**. In **Dual-Fuel** mode, **biogas + air** enter through the **manifold**, while a **small pilot dose of diesel** is injected by the **common rail** and auto-ignites due to compression heat — this flame then ignites the biogas-air mixture.

---

### 4.3 Process Flow — The 4 Microbial Stages (Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Complex Polymers Starch Cellulose Proteins] --> S1[Stage 1 HYDROLYSIS]
    S1 --> S1A[Output Sugars Amino Acids LCFA]
    S1A --> S2[Stage 2 ACIDOGENESIS]
    S2 --> S2A[Output VFAs Alcohols CO2 H2]
    S2A --> S3[Stage 3 ACETOGENESIS]
    S3 --> S3A[Output Acetic Acid H2 CO2]
    S3A --> S4[Stage 4 METHANOGENESIS]
    S4 --> S4A[Acetoclastic 70 percent]
    S4 --> S4B[Hydrogenotrophic 30 percent]
    S4A --> BG[Biogas CH4 + CO2]
    S4B --> BG
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always mention the **microbe group** (hydrolytic, acidogenic, acetogenic, methanogenic) and the **% contribution** of the two methanogenesis pathways. This earns full KTU marks for process questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **biogas**. State its typical composition and calorific value.

**Model Answer:**

> Biogas is a **renewable gaseous fuel** produced by the **anaerobic digestion** of organic biomass in the absence of oxygen, through the action of **methanogenic bacteria**.
>
> **Typical composition:** CH₄ = 55–65 %, CO₂ = 30–40 %, H₂S = 0.1–0.5 %, H₂O = 1–5 %, N₂ = traces.
>
> **Calorific value:** ≈ **20 – 26 MJ/m³** (average taken as **22 MJ/m³** for design purposes).

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Composition table (1) + CV (1).

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List the **four stages of anaerobic digestion** with the end-products of each stage.

**Model Answer:**

| Stage | Microbes | End Products |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Hydrolysis | Hydrolytic bacteria (*Clostridium*) | Sugars, amino acids, fatty acids |
| 2. Acidogenesis | Acidogenic bacteria | VFAs, alcohols, CO₂, H₂ |
| 3. Acetogenesis | Acetogenic bacteria | Acetic acid, H₂, CO₂ |
| 4. Methanogenesis | Methanogenic archaea | **CH₄ + CO₂ (biogas)** |

**Marks Distribution:** Each stage 0.5 mark + products 0.5 mark = 4 points compressed to 3 (any 3 stages needed for full marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of the **Buswell equation**, derive the **stoichiometric equation for the anaerobic digestion of glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆)** and calculate the **theoretical yield of methane per kg of glucose at STP**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **working of a continuous-type biogas plant (KVIC model)** suitable for Indian rural conditions. State any **four operating parameters** that affect biogas yield. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Step 1** — Write the **general Buswell equation** [1 mark]:

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_a\text{O}_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CO}_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)\text{CH}_4$$

**Step 2** — Identify coefficients for glucose: **n = 6, a = 12, b = 6** [0.5 mark].

**Step 3** — H₂O coefficient [1 mark]:

$$6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0 \text{ mol H}_2\text{O}$$

**Step 4** — CO₂ coefficient [1 mark]:

$$\frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 \text{ mol CO}_2$$

**Step 5** — CH₄ coefficient [1 mark]:

$$\frac{6}{2} + \frac{12}{8} - \frac{6}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3 \text{ mol CH}_4$$

**Step 6** — Final balanced equation [0.5 mark]:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 3\text{CH}_4 + 3\text{CO}_2$$

**Step 7** — Molar mass of glucose = 180 g/mol = 0.180 kg/mol; moles of CH₄ per mole of glucose = 3 [0.5 mark].

**Step 8** — Volume of CH₄ at STP per mole of glucose = 3 × 22.4 = 67.2 L = 0.0672 m³ [1 mark].

**Step 9** — Theoretical yield per kg of glucose [0.5 mark]:

$$Y_{CH_4} = \frac{0.0672 \text{ m}^3}{0.180 \text{ kg}} = \boxed{0.373 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4/\text{kg glucose at STP}}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**KVIC Biogas Plant — Working (7 marks):**

The **Khadi and Village Industries Commission (KVIC) model** is a **fixed-dome, continuous-type** biogas plant, the most common design in rural India.

**Components (4 marks):**
1. **Mixing tank** — Cow dung is mixed with water in 1:1 ratio and fed in.
2. **Inlet pipe** — Slurry flows downward into the digester.
3. **Digester chamber** — Sealed, underground, anaerobic environment. **Hydraulic retention time = 30 – 40 days** (mesophilic, 30 – 40 °C).
4. **Displacement chamber / gas holder** — Biogas accumulates in the upper dome and pushes slurry into the **outlet / compensation tank**.
5. **Gas outlet pipe** — Biogas is taken to the kitchen stove, lamp, or upgraded for vehicles.
6. **Outlet tank** — Overflow of digested slurry (= **bio-slurry**, a nutrient-rich organic fertilizer).

**Working principle (1 mark):** Slurry enters, displaces gas, gas pushes slurry out — a **hydraulic displacement** mechanism. This is **continuous-feed, continuous-discharge**.

**Four operating parameters (2 marks):**

| # | Parameter | Optimum Value |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Temperature | 30 – 40 °C (mesophilic) |
| 2 | pH | 6.8 – 7.5 |
| 3 | C/N ratio of feed | 20 : 1 to 30 : 1 |
| 4 | Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT) | 30 – 40 days |

---

### **Question B** [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *(Internal choice alternative)*

**(a)** Compare **single-fuel (100 % biogas)** and **dual-fuel (biogas + pilot diesel)** operation of an automobile engine with respect to: **engine modification, efficiency, emissions, cold-start, range, and applications**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A dual-fuel engine runs on **biogas (CV = 22 MJ/m³) + pilot diesel (CV = 43 MJ/kg)**. The diesel flow is **3 kg/h** and biogas flow is **10 m³/h**. Calculate: (i) total energy input per hour, (ii) percentage of pilot fuel energy, (iii) percentage diesel displacement, and (iv) energy substitution ratio. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

| Criterion | Single-Fuel (CBG) | Dual-Fuel (Biogas + Diesel) |
|---|---|---|
| **Engine type** | Modified SI (spark ignition) | Standard CI (compression ignition) |
| **Modification** | Major — spark plug, regulator, ECU mapping, CNG tank | Minor — gas mixer, regulator; diesel system retained |
| **Thermal efficiency** | 25 – 32 % | 32 – 38 % (higher) |
| **CO₂ emissions** | 70 – 80 % lower (carbon-neutral fuel) | 30 – 45 % lower |
| **NOx** | Low (lean burn); rises at full load | Moderate (homogeneous charge helps) |
| **Cold start** | Easy (spark) | Difficult (low cetane of biogas) |
| **Range** | Limited by CBG pump density | Unlimited (diesel back-up) |
| **Capital cost** | High | Low (easy retrofit) |
| **Best application** | City cars, LCVs, buses | Tractors, gensets, heavy trucks, marine |

**Marks:** 6 points × 1 mark each + 1 mark for neat table/heading = 7 marks.

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Given:** $\dot{m}_{diesel} = 3$ kg/h, $\dot{V}_{bg} = 10$ m³/h, $CV_{diesel} = 43$ MJ/kg, $CV_{bg} = 22$ MJ/m³.

**(i) Total energy input [2 marks]:**

$$E_{diesel} = 3 \times 43 = 129 \text{ MJ/h}$$
$$E_{bg} = 10 \times 22 = 220 \text{ MJ/h}$$
$$E_{total} = 129 + 220 = \boxed{349 \text{ MJ/h}}$$

**(ii) % pilot fuel energy [1.5 marks]:**

$$\%E_{pilot} = \frac{129}{349} \times 100 = \boxed{36.96\%}$$

**(iii) % diesel displacement [1.5 marks]:**

$$\% \text{ displacement} = \frac{E_{bg}}{E_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{220}{349} \times 100 = \boxed{63.04\%}$$

**(iv) Energy substitution ratio (ESR) [2 marks]:**

$$ESR = \frac{E_{diesel}}{E_{bg}} = \frac{129}{220} = \boxed{0.586}$$

**Interpretation:** 1 MJ of biogas replaces 0.586 MJ of diesel in this engine. The remaining energy of biogas (≈ 41 %) is used to augment the pilot flame and improve combustion.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Writing "biogas = natural gas"** — Biogas is a *bio-product*; natural gas is a fossil fuel. Always mention **"produced by anaerobic digestion"** in the definition. **[–1 mark]**
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in the Buswell derivation** — Examiners check for **m³/kg** or **L/g** in the final answer. Always state *“at STP”* or *“at NTP”*. **[–1 mark]**
>
> 3. **Conflating the four microbial stages** — Students often write *“bacteria digest the waste and produce methane”* without naming the **four stages and their intermediates**. **[–2 marks]**
>
> 4. **Ignoring the 70/30 split of methanogenesis pathways** — KTU specifically tests the **acetoclastic (70 %)** vs **hydrogenotrophic (30 %)** contribution. **[–1 mark]**
>
> 5. **In dual-fuel numericals, forgetting to convert CV units** — CV of diesel in **MJ/kg** vs CV of biogas in **MJ/m³** — unit mismatch is a classic trap. **[–2 marks]**
>
> 6. **Not drawing a labelled sketch** in KVIC/biogas-plant questions. A neat diagram with 6 components is worth **at least 3 marks** alone.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise This Block:**

- ✅ **Biogas** = Anaerobic digestion product; **55–65 % CH₄ + 30–40 % CO₂ + trace H₂S/H₂O/N₂**.
- ✅ **Calorific value of raw biogas ≈ 22 MJ/m³**; **CBG (upgraded) ≈ 36–38 MJ/m³**; **pure methane = 39.8 MJ/m³**.
- ✅ **Four stages:** **Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis** (mnemonic: **"H-A-A-M"**).
- ✅ **Methanogenesis = 70 % acetoclastic + 30 % hydrogenotrophic**.
- ✅ **Buswell Equation** — the universal tool to predict stoichiometric CH₄ yield from any biomass.
- ✅ **Theoretical CH₄ yield = 0.25 kg = 0.373 m³** per kg of glucose; **≈ 0.5 m³/kg VS** of cow dung.
- ✅ **Optimum pH = 6.8 – 7.5; C/N = 20:1 to 30:1; HRT = 30–40 d (mesophilic), 10–20 d (thermophilic).**
- ✅ **Single fuel = SI engine, spark ignition, ~30 % efficient, easy cold start, used in CBG cars/buses.**
- ✅ **Dual fuel = CI engine, pilot diesel ignition, ~35 % efficient, 70–80 % diesel displacement at full load, used in tractors/trucks/gensets.**
- ✅ **Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR) = E_diesel / E_biogas** (dimensionless).
- ✅ **Heavy CO₂ in raw biogas halves its energy density → upgrading (CO₂ scrubbing) is mandatory for automotive use.**
- ✅ **CBG stored at 200–250 bar; SATAT (India) and RED II (EU) are policy drivers for vehicular biogas.**
- ✅ **By-product = bio-slurry**, a high-value organic fertilizer — completing the **circular bio-economy**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vegetable oils as diesel fuels: straight vegetable oil, biodiesel properties and performance in engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vegetable oils as diesel fuels: straight vegetable oil, biodiesel properties and performance in engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Vegetable Oils — The Origin

Vegetable oils are **lipid-based bio-fuels** extracted from the seeds, fruits, or nuts of energy crops such as **soybean, sunflower, rapeseed (canola), palm, jatropha, karanja, and coconut**. Chemically, they are **triglycerides** — esters formed between one molecule of glycerol (a trihydric alcohol) and three molecules of long-chain fatty acids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):**
> "Vegetable oils, when used as automotive fuels, may be utilized either in their **crude/refined form (Straight Vegetable Oil — SVO)** or in their chemically modified form known as **Biodiesel (Fatty Acid Methyl Esters — FAME)**."

The general molecular structure of a triglyceride is:

$$\text{Triglyceride} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Glycerol backbone}}_{\text{C}_3\text{H}_5} \;-\; \underbrace{\text{3 Fatty Acid Chains}}_{\text{R}_1\text{COO},\, \text{R}_2\text{COO},\, \text{R}_3\text{COO}}$$

## 1.2 Straight Vegetable Oil (SVO) — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Straight Vegetable Oil (SVO)** is vegetable oil that is used in a diesel engine **without any chemical modification** — only filtered and sometimes pre-heated.

### Real-World Analogy
Think of SVO like **unrefined crude honey** versus processed sugar. Both are sweet, but one is thick, viscous, and difficult to handle. SVO is like crude honey — it works, but it gums up the engine (the "bees") just like honey gums up a spoon. **Biodiesel is the processed sugar** — clean, free-flowing, and engine-friendly.

### Key Properties of SVO (Board-Favorite Numbers)

| Property | Approximate Value |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Kinematic Viscosity (40 °C)** | **30 – 50 mm²/s** (diesel ≈ 2–4 mm²/s) |
| **Density** | **910 – 925 kg/m³** |
| **Flash Point** | **220 – 320 °C** |
| **Calorific Value** | **35 – 40 MJ/kg** (vs diesel ≈ 42–45 MJ/kg) |
| **Cetane Number** | **30 – 50** |
| **Cloud / Pour Point** | Very high (often > 10 °C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **single most damaging property of SVO is its high kinematic viscosity** — typically **10 to 15 times higher than petro-diesel**. This causes:
> 1. Poor atomization in the combustion chamber
> 2. Carbon deposits on injectors and piston rings
> 3. Lubricating oil contamination and coking
> 4. Cold-start failure due to high pour point

## 1.3 Biodiesel — The Engineered Solution

**Biodiesel** is the **mono-alkyl ester** of long-chain fatty acids, derived from a vegetable oil or animal fat through a chemical reaction called **transesterification**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (ASTM D6751 / EN 14214 standard):**
> Biodiesel is a fuel comprised of **mono-alkyl esters of long-chain fatty acids** derived from vegetable oils or animal fats, designated **B100**, meeting the registry specifications of ASTM D6751.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a tangled ball of yarn (the triglyceride). You cannot push it through a small needle (the fuel injector). **Transesterification is like carefully un-tangling the yarn into three neat, individual threads (the methyl esters)** — each thread is now small, flexible, and easy to push through the needle.

## 1.4 Why Vegetable Oils Matter for KTU Examinations

- India's **National Policy on Biofuels (2018)** targets **20 % ethanol blending** and **5 % biodiesel blending** by 2030.
- Kerala's **Karanja and Coconut oil** reserves are actively researched as second-generation feedstocks.
- KTU frequently asks about **transesterification stoichiometry**, **viscosity problems**, and **engine modifications** for these fuels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry Behind the Fuel — Transesterification

The **transesterification** (also called **alcoholysis**) reaction converts triglycerides into fatty acid methyl esters (FAMEs — the biodiesel) using an alcohol (usually **methanol**) in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH or KOH).

### Reaction Step Logic (Bulleted)

- **Step 1 — Mixing:** Triglyceride + 3 Methanol → reaction mass
- **Step 2 — Catalysis:** NaOH/KOH catalyst activates the methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)
- **Step 3 — Nucleophilic Attack:** Methoxide ion attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride
- **Step 4 — Tetrahedral Intermediate:** Forms unstable intermediate → breaks into diglyceride + methyl ester
- **Step 5 — Cascade:** Process repeats for diglyceride → monoglyceride → glycerol
- **Step 6 — Separation:** Glycerol (denser, bottom layer) is separated from biodiesel (top layer)
- **Step 7 — Washing:** Biodiesel is washed with warm water to remove residual catalyst and soap

### Stoichiometric Equation

$$\underbrace{\text{Triglyceride}}_{\text{oil}} \;+\; 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{catalyst}]{\text{NaOH / KOH}}\; \underbrace{3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3}_{\text{Biodiesel (FAME)}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3}_{\text{Glycerol}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometric molar ratio of alcohol to oil = 3 : 1** (theoretical). In practice, **6 : 1 to 12 : 1** is used to drive the reversible reaction forward.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Application | Typical Units |
|---|----------------------|-----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{biodiesel}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100$ | Biodiesel yield (mass basis) | % |
| 2 | $\text{CNV} = \dfrac{\eta_{\text{biodiesel}}}{\eta_{\text{fossil}}} \times 100$ | Comparative (normalized) viscosity reduction | % |
| 3 | $\rho_{\text{mix}} = \sum x_i \rho_i$ | Density of fuel blend (mass-averaged) | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $CV_{\text{blend}} = \sum x_i \, CV_i$ | Calorific value of blend (mass-averaged) | MJ/kg |
| 5 | $\nu_{\text{blend}} \approx \nu_{\text{diesel}}^{x_D} \cdot \nu_{\text{bio}}^{x_B}$ | Viscosity blending (exponential, Arrhenius) | mm²/s |
| 6 | $\text{BSFC}_{\text{corr}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{P_e \times t}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{P_e}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$ | Brake thermal efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\text{AFR}_{\text{bio}} \approx \dfrac{1}{0.228}$ | Stoichiometric A/F for biodiesel | – |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{\text{filter}} = T_{\text{cloud}} - T_{\text{pour}}$ | Cold-flow operability range | °C |
| 10 | $\text{Cetane Index} \approx 0.72 \times (\text{CN diesel}) - k \cdot \nu$ | Approximate CN estimation from viscosity | – |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU numericals, students frequently need to compute **biodiesel yield** (Formula 1) and **blended fuel calorific value** (Formula 4). Memorize these two — they carry full marks when applied correctly.

## 2.3 Comparison — SVO vs Biodiesel vs Petro-Diesel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This three-way comparison is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

| Property | Petro-Diesel | Straight Vegetable Oil (SVO) | Biodiesel (B100) |
|----------|--------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| **Viscosity (40 °C, mm²/s)** | 2 – 4 | 30 – 50 | 3.5 – 5.0 |
| **Density (kg/m³)** | 830 – 860 | 910 – 925 | 870 – 890 |
| **Calorific Value (MJ/kg)** | 42 – 45 | 35 – 40 | 37 – 40 |
| **Cetane Number** | 45 – 55 | 30 – 50 | 48 – 65 |
| **Flash Point (°C)** | 60 – 80 | 220 – 320 | 100 – 170 |
| **Oxygen Content (%)** | 0 | 10 – 11 | 10 – 12 |
| **Pour Point (°C)** | -15 to -25 | 0 to +15 | -5 to +15 |
| **Sulphur Content** | < 10 ppm | 0 | 0 |

## 2.4 Engine Performance — Real-World Behaviour

When biodiesel (B20 or B100) is used in a **Compression Ignition (CI) engine**:

### 2.4.1 Power & Torque
- **Slight reduction** in brake power (typically **2 – 8 %**) due to lower calorific value per kg.
- Since biodiesel is denser, **volumetric fuel flow increases**, partially compensating.

### 2.4.2 Brake Thermal Efficiency
- Usually **equal or slightly higher** than diesel because of:
  - Better combustion (in-cylinder oxygen content ≈ 10–12 %)
  - Higher cetane number → shorter ignition delay
  - More complete combustion

### 2.4.3 Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)
- **BSFC increases 8 – 12 %** because of lower heating value.
- **BSEC (Brake Specific Energy Consumption)** remains nearly constant → **true thermodynamic metric**.

### 2.4.4 Emissions Profile

| Pollutant | % Change vs Diesel |
|-----------|--------------------|
| **CO (Carbon Monoxide)** | ↓ 30 – 50 % |
| **HC (Unburnt Hydrocarbon)** | ↓ 20 – 40 % |
| **PM (Particulate Matter)** | ↓ 40 – 60 % |
| **NOₓ (Oxides of Nitrogen)** | ↑ 5 – 15 % (problematic) |
| **SO₂** | Negligible (no sulphur) |
| **CO₂ (Net Carbon)** | ↓ 60 – 80 % (carbon-neutral cycle) |

> [!WARNING]
> **NOₓ increase** is the **biggest environmental drawback** of biodiesel. Higher in-cylinder oxygen + higher cetane (earlier combustion, higher peak temperatures) both promote thermal NOₓ.

## 2.5 Practical Engineering Utility

1. **Direct Injection Engines** — B20 and B100 can be used with no modification (up to 6 months warranty).
2. **Indirect Injection (IDI) engines** — Need fuel-line heating; otherwise coking of injectors.
3. **Cold-start** — Pure biodiesel (B100) has cold-start issues below 0 °C; tropical conditions in Kerala (avg. 25 °C) make this less critical.
4. **Material Compatibility** — Biodiesel attacks natural rubber and certain plastics; **Viton and Teflon seals** are required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Biodiesel Yield from Transesterification

Let us derive the **theoretical mass yield of biodiesel** from a given mass of vegetable oil.

### Given
- Mass of oil processed: $m_{\text{oil}}$
- Molar mass of oil (triglyceride): $M_{\text{oil}}$ (depends on fatty acid profile)
- Stoichiometric alcohol:oil ratio = 3 : 1 (theoretical)

### Step 1 — Calculate moles of triglyceride
$$n_{\text{oil}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}}$$

### Step 2 — Apply stoichiometry (1 mole oil → 3 moles FAME)
$$n_{\text{FAME}} \;=\; 3 \times n_{\text{oil}} \;=\; \frac{3 \, m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}}$$

### Step 3 — Average molar mass of FAME
For a typical oil, the average molar mass of one FAME molecule is approximately $M_{\text{FAME}} \approx \dfrac{M_{\text{oil}} - 92}{3}$ (subtracting 3 × glycerol mass difference).

$$\boxed{\,M_{\text{FAME}} \;\approx\; \frac{M_{\text{oil}} - 92}{3}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Theoretical mass of biodiesel
$$m_{\text{FAME, theo}} \;=\; n_{\text{FAME}} \times M_{\text{FAME}} \;=\; \frac{3 \, m_{\text{oil}}}{M_{\text{oil}}} \times \frac{M_{\text{oil}} - 92}{3}$$

$$m_{\text{FAME, theo}} \;=\; m_{\text{oil}} \times \frac{M_{\text{oil}} - 92}{M_{\text{oil}}}$$

### Step 5 — Percentage yield
$$\eta_{\text{biodiesel}} \;=\; \frac{m_{\text{FAME, actual}}}{m_{\text{FAME, theo}}} \times 100 \, \%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
> 1000 kg of soybean oil (M_oil ≈ 885 g/mol) is transesterified. Theoretical yield of FAME?
>
> $m_{\text{FAME, theo}} = 1000 \times \dfrac{885 - 92}{885} = 1000 \times 0.896 = 896 \text{ kg}$
>
> This means about **89.6 % biodiesel yield**, with **~ 100 kg glycerol** as byproduct.

## 3.2 Derivation — Calorific Value of B20 Blend

**B20 = 20 % Biodiesel + 80 % Petro-Diesel** (by volume, commonly; by mass in Indian standards).

Given:
- $CV_{\text{bio}} = 37.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $CV_{\text{d}} = 43.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- $\rho_{\text{bio}} = 880 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{\text{d}} = 840 \text{ kg/m}^3$

### Step 1 — Convert volume fractions to mass fractions
$$x_{\text{bio}} \;=\; \frac{0.20 \times 880}{0.20 \times 880 + 0.80 \times 840} \;=\; \frac{176}{176 + 672} \;=\; \frac{176}{848}$$

$$x_{\text{bio}} = 0.2075$$

$$x_{\text{d}} = 1 - 0.2075 = 0.7925$$

### Step 2 — Apply mass-averaged calorific value
$$CV_{\text{blend}} \;=\; x_{\text{bio}} \cdot CV_{\text{bio}} + x_{\text{d}} \cdot CV_{\text{d}}$$

$$CV_{\text{blend}} = (0.2075)(37.5) + (0.7925)(43.0)$$

$$CV_{\text{blend}} = 7.78 + 34.08 \;=\; \boxed{41.86 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

### Step 3 — Energy penalty vs pure diesel
$$\Delta CV \% = \frac{43.0 - 41.86}{43.0} \times 100 = 2.65\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical B20 blend suffers only **2–4 % energy penalty** while delivering **20–25 % of the biodiesel's emission benefits** — a popular compromise.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Vegetable Oil Route

| Parameter | SVO Route | Biodiesel Route | Conclusion |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|------------|
| Engine Modification | Major (heated lines, twin tank) | Minimal (B20) / None (B20 long-term) | **Biodiesel wins** |
| Cost of Conversion | Low (mechanical) | High (chemical plant) | SVO wins short-term |
| Cold-start Performance | Poor | Moderate | Biodiesel wins |
| Carbon Deposit | High | Low | Biodiesel wins |
| Lubricating Oil Life | Reduced 30–50 % | Reduced 5–10 % | Biodiesel wins |
| Sustainability | High | High | Tie |
| Process Complexity | Filter & dewater | Transesterification | SVO wins |

## 3.4 Derivation — Material Balance for Glycerol Byproduct

For every **1 mole of triglyceride** reacted:

$$1 \text{ mol oil} + 3 \text{ mol methanol} \rightarrow 3 \text{ mol FAME} + 1 \text{ mol glycerol}$$

For 1 tonne (1000 kg) of oil:
- Moles of oil ≈ 1000 / 0.885 ≈ **1130 mol**
- Theoretical glycerol produced = 1130 mol × 92 g/mol = **104 kg**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Glycerol has substantial commercial value (pharma, cosmetics, explosives industry) — improves **overall economics** of the biodiesel plant by 8–12 %.
