# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the term "Straight Vegetable Oil" (SVO) and list three engine-related problems when SVO is used directly in a diesel engine.**

**Model Answer:**
Straight Vegetable Oil (SVO) is the **crude, unprocessed vegetable oil** used directly in a CI engine after minimal filtration, without any chemical modification such as transesterification.

The three primary engine-related problems are:

1. **High Viscosity (30–50 mm²/s at 40 °C):** Causes poor atomization through injectors, leading to incomplete combustion and carbon deposits on piston rings and injector tips.
2. **High Pour Point & Poor Cold-flow:** SVO solidifies at higher temperatures than diesel, causing fuel-line blockage during cold starts (especially in winter conditions).
3. **Polymerization at High Temperature:** The unsaturated fatty acids in SVO undergo oxidative polymerization inside the hot combustion chamber, forming **lacquers and gums** that contaminate the lubricating oil and reduce its viscosity index.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define biodiesel. Mention any two important physical properties that differentiate it from petro-diesel.**

**Model Answer:**
Biodiesel is the **mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids**, typically fatty acid methyl esters (FAME), derived from vegetable oils or animal fats by **transesterification** with an alcohol (commonly methanol), conforming to ASTM D6751 / EN 14214 standards.

Two differentiating physical properties:

| Property | Biodiesel (B100) | Petro-Diesel |
|----------|------------------|--------------|
| **Kinematic Viscosity (40 °C, mm²/s)** | 3.5 – 5.0 | 2.0 – 4.0 |
| **Flash Point (°C)** | 100 – 170 | 60 – 80 |

Additional property: Biodiesel contains **10–12 % oxygen by mass** in its molecular structure (petro-diesel has none), which is responsible for cleaner combustion but is also the reason for **higher NOₓ emissions**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the **transesterification process** for producing biodiesel from vegetable oil with a neat chemical equation. Discuss the role of the catalyst and the effect of the **molar ratio** of alcohol to oil on biodiesel yield. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **performance and emission characteristics** of a diesel engine running on biodiesel (B20) with that of petro-diesel. Present the comparison in a tabular form. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 📘 MODEL ANSWER for Question A

#### Part (a) — Transesterification Process

**Definition:**
Transesterification is a reversible chemical reaction in which the alkyl group of an ester (triglyceride) is exchanged with the alkyl group of an alcohol (methanol), producing **fatty acid methyl esters (biodiesel)** and **glycerol** as a byproduct.

**Chemical Equation:**

$$\text{Triglyceride} \;+\; 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{catalyst}]{\text{NaOH / KOH}}\; 3\,\text{RCOOCH}_3 \;+\; \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$$

where R represents the long-chain fatty acid alkyl group (C14 – C22).

**Role of the Catalyst:**
- A **homogeneous base catalyst** (NaOH or KOH at ~ 1 % wt of oil) activates the methanol to form **methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)**.
- The methoxide ion is a strong **nucleophile** that attacks the carbonyl carbon of the triglyceride.
- The catalyst **increases reaction rate** without being consumed.
- Typical process conditions: **60 °C, 1 hour, atmospheric pressure**.

**Effect of Molar Ratio (Alcohol : Oil):**

| Molar Ratio | Biodiesel Yield | Remarks |
|-------------|-----------------|---------|
| 3 : 1 (theoretical) | ~ 65 – 70 % | Reversible, equilibrium-limited |
| 6 : 1 | ~ 90 – 94 % | Industry-standard optimum |
| 9 : 1 | ~ 96 – 98 % | Higher yield but excess methanol recovery cost |
| 12 : 1 | ~ 98 % | Diminishing returns; not economical |

> **KTU Explanation Tip:** Because the forward reaction is **reversible and endothermic to a small extent**, using excess methanol shifts the equilibrium toward the product side (Le Chatelier's Principle).

---

#### Part (b) — Performance & Emission Comparison (B20 vs Petro-Diesel)

| Parameter | Petro-Diesel (B0) | Biodiesel Blend (B20) | Trend |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|-------|
| **Brake Power** | Baseline | 1–3 % lower | ↓ slight |
| **Brake Thermal Efficiency** | 30–35 % | 31–36 % | ↑ slight |
| **BSFC (kg/kWh)** | 0.25 – 0.28 | 0.26 – 0.30 | ↑ 5–8 % |
| **BSEC (MJ/kWh)** | 11.5 – 12.0 | 11.6 – 12.0 | ≈ same |
| **CO emission** | Baseline | 25–40 % lower | ↓ |
| **HC emission** | Baseline | 20–35 % lower | ↓ |
| **PM emission** | Baseline | 30–50 % lower | ↓ |
| **NOₓ emission** | Baseline | 5–10 % higher | ↑ |
| **Smoke density** | Higher | Lower | ↓ |
| **Engine wear** | Normal | Slightly higher | ↑ minor |

> **Engineering Reasoning:**
> - **Lower PM and CO** — due to intrinsic oxygen content (10–12 %) in biodiesel promoting **more complete combustion**.
> - **Higher NOₓ** — because higher cetane number causes **earlier start of combustion**, raising peak in-cylinder temperatures and pressures.
> - **Slight power loss** — compensated by **higher density** of biodiesel (more mass per unit volume of injection).

---

### ⭐ Question B — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** What are the **properties of straight vegetable oil (SVO)** that make it unsuitable for direct use in modern diesel engines? What **engine modifications** are required to run an engine on SVO? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-stroke, single-cylinder diesel engine develops **5 kW brake power** at 1500 rpm while consuming **2.0 kg/hr** of B100 biodiesel with a calorific value of **38 MJ/kg**. Calculate the **brake thermal efficiency** and **BSFC**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 📘 MODEL ANSWER for Question B

#### Part (a) — SVO Properties & Engine Modifications

**Unsuitable Properties of SVO:**

1. **High Viscosity (30–50 mm²/s)** — Causes poor fuel atomization, large droplet size, leading to incomplete combustion and carbon deposits on injector tips and piston rings.

2. **High Density (910–925 kg/m³)** — Increases mass per injection cycle; leads to over-fueling if injection timing is not adjusted.

3. **Low Volatility** — The long fatty acid chains (C16 – C18) do not vaporize easily; leads to engine misfire during cold start.

4. **High Flash Point (> 250 °C)** — Although safer in storage, delays combustion initiation.

5. **High Pour Point / Cloud Point** — Causes fuel-line gelling at low ambient temperatures.

6. **Chemical Instability** — Unsaturated fatty acids (e.g., linoleic, linolenic) undergo **oxidative polymerization** at high temperatures, forming gums that contaminate engine oil.

**Required Engine Modifications:**

| Modification | Purpose |
|--------------|---------|
| **Heated fuel tank** | Reduce viscosity by pre-warming SVO to 60–80 °C |
| **Heated fuel lines** | Prevent solidification and maintain flow |
| **Heat exchanger using engine coolant** | Use waste engine heat for fuel heating |
| **Twin-tank system (diesel + SVO)** | Use diesel for cold start and shutdown; switch to SVO when warm |
| **Modified injector nozzle** | Larger orifice to handle higher viscosity |
| **Lubricating oil monitoring** | Shorter drain intervals due to oil dilution |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation

**Given Data:**
- Brake Power, $P_e = 5$ kW
- Speed, $N = 1500$ rpm
- Fuel consumption, $\dot{m}_f = 2.0$ kg/hr
- Calorific value, $CV = 38$ MJ/kg

**Step 1 — Convert fuel consumption to kg/s**
$$\dot{m}_f = \frac{2.0}{3600} = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bt}$)**
$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{P_e}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{5}{5.556 \times 10^{-4} \times 38} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{bt} = \frac{5}{0.02111} \times 100 = 23{,}684.7 \times \frac{1}{100}$$

$$\eta_{bt} = 23.68 \, \%$$

**Step 3 — Calculate BSFC**
$$\text{BSFC} = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{P_e \times t} \;=\; \frac{2.0 \text{ kg/hr}}{5 \text{ kW}}$$

$$\text{BSFC} = 0.400 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | **23.68 %** |
| BSFC | **0.400 kg/kWh** |

> **Validation Check:** A typical single-cylinder diesel engine has $\eta_{bt}$ between **22–28 %** and BSFC between **0.30–0.45 kg/kWh**. Our values fall within range ✓.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in BSEC:** Students often confuse BSFC (kg/kWh) with BSEC (MJ/kWh). BSFC = fuel mass per energy. Don't divide by calorific value twice.
> 2. **Volume vs Mass Fractions:** When asked to compute blend calorific value, **always convert volume fractions to mass fractions first** using densities — direct volume-averaging causes 2–4 % errors.
> 3. **Transesterification Equation:** Writing the equation without balancing (forgetting the coefficient **3** in front of methanol and FAME) costs **2 marks** in ESE.
> 4. **Forgetting Catalyst:** Always specify **NaOH or KOH + methanol = methoxide ion**. Examiner gives **1 mark** separately for stating the catalyst.
> 5. **NOₓ Trade-off:** If you state that biodiesel **reduces ALL emissions**, you lose 2 marks. NOₓ is the **notable exception**.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **SVO** = crude oil, viscosity **30–50 mm²/s**, causes **carbon deposits** if used directly.
- ✅ **Biodiesel (B100)** = **FAME**, produced by **transesterification** with methanol + NaOH catalyst.
- ✅ **Theoretical molar ratio** = 3:1; **practical optimum** = 6:1 to 9:1.
- ✅ **Byproduct** = glycerol (1 mol per mol oil); commercially valuable.
- ✅ **Biodiesel properties** similar to diesel except: **higher cetane, higher flash point, contains ~11 % oxygen**.
- ✅ **Engine performance:** **Slight power loss (2–8 %)** but **higher thermal efficiency** due to oxygen content.
- ✅ **Emissions:** **CO, HC, PM all reduce (40–60 %)**; **NOₓ increases 5–15 %** — the critical exception.
- ✅ **BSFC increases** but **BSEC stays constant** — use BSEC for true energy comparison.
- ✅ **Engine mods for SVO:** heated tank, heated lines, twin-tank system, modified injectors.
- ✅ **B20 (20 % biodiesel)** = industry sweet spot — minimal modification, 2–4 % energy penalty, 30 % emissions benefit.
- ✅ **ASTM D6751 / EN 14214** = international biodiesel quality standards — memorize.
- ✅ **India's policy** = National Policy on Biofuels 2018, target **5 % biodiesel blending by 2030**.
- ✅ **KTU numericals:** focus on biodiesel yield %, B20 calorific value, and brake thermal efficiency.

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrogen as an Automotive Fuel: Formal Definition

Hydrogen (chemical symbol **H**, atomic number **1**, molecular formula **H₂**) is the lightest, simplest, and most abundant chemical element in the universe. In the context of automotive engineering, hydrogen is classified as a **zero-carbon, gaseous, renewable-energy carrier** that can be used in Internal Combustion Engines (Hydrogen-ICE), Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEV), or as a blended additive (HCNG) with conventional fuels. It is regarded as the fuel of the future because its only combustion by-product is **water vapor (H₂O)**, with virtually zero CO, CO₂, HC, SOx, and particulate emissions at the tailpipe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Direct Phrasing):**
> Hydrogen is a colorless, odorless, tasteless, non-toxic, and highly flammable diatomic gas. When burned, it releases a very high amount of energy per unit mass and produces only water as the exhaust product, making it the cleanest alternative fuel for automobile propulsion systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine **petrol as a heavy suitcase stuffed with clothes (carbon) and water** — when you burn it, you get soot, smoke, and carbon dioxide along with heat. Now imagine **hydrogen as an almost-empty, ultra-light backpack filled with pure high-grade energy** — it has nothing extra inside it (no carbon, no sulphur, no aromatics), so when ignited, it only releases heat and pure water vapor. The "empty backpack" analogy perfectly explains why hydrogen has:
- The **highest energy-to-mass ratio** of any fuel (≈ 3× petrol)
- **Zero carbon footprint** at the point of use
- But a **very low energy-to-volume ratio** (the backpack is huge for its content) — which is why storage and tank design is the biggest engineering challenge.

## 1.3 Why Study the Properties of Hydrogen?

Every engine design, every fuel tank, every injector, and every safety system depends on the **thermo-physical and combustion properties** of the fuel. For KTU examinations, students must know the **numerical value, units, and engineering significance** of each property — because the same value decides whether a fuel is safe to store, easy to vaporize, efficient to burn, and compatible with existing engine hardware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Property Classification for Hydrogen (Quick Glance):**
> 1. **Combustion Properties** → Auto-ignition temp., Flammable limits, Flash point, Flame speed, Calorific value
> 2. **Physical/Phase Change Properties** → Boiling temp., Latent heat of vaporization, Density, Pour point, Cloud point
> 3. **Fuel Quality Properties** → Octane number, Cetane number, Sulphur content, Volatility
> 4. **Engine Design Property** → Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Property-by-Property Analysis

### 🔥 A. Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)

**Definition:** The minimum temperature at which a fuel-air mixture spontaneously ignites **without any external spark or flame**, purely due to the heat of compression or ambient conditions.

**Hydrogen's AIT = 571 °C (≈ 844 K) — very high.**

**Why it matters for automobiles:**
- A high AIT means hydrogen is **very difficult to self-ignite** in a diesel engine (Compression Ignition).
- This is why hydrogen is **excellent for SI (Spark Ignition) engines** — it resists knocking.
- It also makes hydrogen **safer to handle at moderate temperatures** compared to petrol (AIT ≈ 280 °C) or diesel (AIT ≈ 210 °C).
- Downside: a high AIT means hydrogen needs a **larger ignition source or higher compression ratio** for HCCI applications.

### 🌡️ B. Boiling Temperature / Boiling Point

**Definition:** The temperature at which a liquid fuel transitions to its gaseous phase at standard atmospheric pressure (**1 atm = 101.325 kPa**).

**Hydrogen's boiling point = −252.87 °C (≈ 20.28 K).**

**Significance:**
- This is the **second-lowest boiling point of any substance** (only helium is lower).
- It means hydrogen **cannot be liquefied at room temperature** — extreme cryogenic cooling is required.
- Liquid hydrogen (LH₂) storage demands **vacuum-insulated, double-walled cryogenic tanks** in vehicles like the Toyota Mirai (older gen) or BMW Hydrogen 7.
- It also explains why gaseous hydrogen has an **extremely low density** (≈ 0.081 kg/m³ at STP) — it has to be either cryo-compressed (700 bar) or liquefied (LH₂) for practical vehicular storage.

### ⛽ C. Cetane Number (CN)

**Definition:** A measure of the **ignition quality of a fuel in a Compression Ignition (CI/diesel) engine**. Higher CN → shorter ignition delay → smoother combustion.

**Hydrogen's Cetane Number ≈ extremely low (or not measurable) — reported in some sources as low single digit due to high AIT.**

**Engineering meaning:**
- Hydrogen's **high AIT and high flame speed** make it a **poor CI engine fuel** in its pure form.
- To run hydrogen in a CI engine, it is usually **fumigated** (introduced into the intake air) and ignited by a pilot diesel spray, or used in HCCI mode with hot residual gases.

### 🔥 D. Flammable (Flammability) Limits

**Definition:** The range of fuel-to-air concentrations (by volume %) within which the mixture can be ignited and propagate a flame. Expressed as **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** and **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL)**.

**Hydrogen in air (by volume):**
- LFL = **4 %**
- UFL = **75 % (in pure O₂: 94 %)**
- Range width = **71 %** (the widest of all fuels!)

| Fuel       | LFL (%) | UFL (%) | Range |
|------------|---------|---------|-------|
| Hydrogen   | 4       | 75      | 71    |
| Methane    | 5       | 15      | 10    |
| Petrol     | 1.4     | 7.6     | 6.2   |
| Diesel     | 0.6     | 5.5     | 4.9   |

**Significance:**
- A very wide flammability range means hydrogen **can burn in both lean and extremely rich mixtures** — this gives hydrogen ICEs a **very wide operating range** (can run ultra-lean → better thermal efficiency).
- **Safety concern:** leaks can form ignitable mixtures very easily, demanding **leak detectors, ventilation, and hydrogen sensors** in vehicle compartments.

### ⚡ E. Flash Point

**Definition:** The **lowest temperature** at which the vapors of a liquid fuel, when mixed with air, can produce a **momentary flash** upon application of an ignition source (without sustained combustion).

**Hydrogen's Flash Point = NOT APPLICABLE (N/A) — it is a gas at ambient conditions.**

**Engineering interpretation:** Hydrogen does not pool on the ground like petrol; it **rapidly diffuses upward and disperses**, which is a **major safety advantage** over liquid fuels in open or ventilated environments.

### 🔥 F. Calorific Value (CV) / Heating Value

**Definition:** The amount of heat released per unit mass (or per unit volume) when the fuel is completely burned and the products are cooled back to the original temperature. Two forms exist:
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** — water in products is condensed (latent heat recovered).
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** — water in products leaves as vapor (latent heat NOT recovered).

**Hydrogen's values:**
- HHV = **142 MJ/kg** (≈ 39,400 Wh/kg)
- LHV = **120 MJ/kg** (≈ 33,300 Wh/kg)

**Why two values exist:** Because hydrogen's combustion product is water (H₂O). In an ICE, exhaust gases are hot enough that water leaves as **vapor** → use **LHV**. In a fuel cell, water may condense → use **HHV**.

| Fuel       | LHV (MJ/kg) | Energy Density vs Petrol |
|------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Hydrogen   | 120         | 3.0×                     |
| Petrol     | 44          | 1.0×                     |
| Diesel     | 43          | 1.0×                     |
| Methanol   | 20          | 0.45×                    |
| LPG        | 46          | 1.05×                    |

### 💧 G. Latent Heat of Vaporization (LHV of phase change — note: same acronym as Lower Heating Value, but different context)

**Definition:** The amount of heat required to convert **1 kg of liquid fuel to vapor at constant temperature and pressure**.

**Hydrogen's LHV (phase change) = 446 kJ/kg (at boiling point −252.87 °C).**

**Significance for engines:**
- This is a **very low value** compared to petrol (≈ 350 kJ/kg) — but in absolute terms, it is significant for cryogenic systems.
- In a hydrogen ICE, the **large latent heat absorption** can be used to **cool the charge**, suppress knock, and improve volumetric efficiency.
- In liquid hydrogen storage, boil-off losses during refueling depend on this property.

### 🏁 H. Octane Number (Research Octane Number, RON)

**Definition:** A measure of a fuel's **resistance to knock (auto-ignition) in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine**. Higher RON → more resistant to knock → allows higher compression ratio → higher efficiency.

**Hydrogen's RON = > 130 (some sources report 130–140+).**

**Why hydrogen is a super-premium SI fuel:**
- Due to **high AIT, high flame speed, and wide flammability range**, hydrogen resists engine knock.
- This **permits very high compression ratios (≈ 13:1 to 15:1)** in hydrogen SI engines, theoretically achieving thermal efficiencies higher than petrol engines.
- Hydrogen can be used in very **lean-burn SI engines** (λ > 2) without misfire.

### ⚖️ I. Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)ₛ

**Definition:** The exact mass ratio of air to fuel required for **complete combustion** with **no excess O₂ and no leftover fuel**.

**For hydrogen, (A/F)ₛ ≈ 34.3 : 1 (by mass).**

**Derivation logic:** The hydrogen combustion reaction is **2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O**. By mass, 4 kg H₂ needs 32 kg O₂. Since air contains 23.2 % O₂ and 76.8 % N₂ by mass, the air required = 32/0.232 ≈ 137.9 kg, giving A/F = 137.9/4 ≈ **34.3 : 1**.

**Significance:**
- A **very high A/F ratio** means **less mass of fuel** is required for the same air charge.
- This results in a **very lean mixture** in a normal engine (much more air than stoichiometrically needed), which actually **improves efficiency** and reduces pumping losses.
- Compared to petrol's stoichiometric A/F of **14.7:1**, hydrogen needs **2.34× more air per kg of fuel**.

### ❄️ J. Pour Point

**Definition:** The **lowest temperature** at which a liquid fuel will still **flow** (pour) under standard test conditions.

**Hydrogen's Pour Point = NOT APPLICABLE (N/A)** — hydrogen is a permanent gas at atmospheric pressure. Pour point is relevant for liquid fuels (diesel: −6 to −30 °C, petrol: ≈ −40 °C).

### ☁️ K. Cloud Point

**Definition:** The temperature at which **wax crystals first appear** in a liquid fuel (typically diesel/biodiesel) when cooled, giving the fuel a "cloudy" appearance.

**Hydrogen's Cloud Point = NOT APPLICABLE (N/A)** — purely a liquid-fuel property. Hydrogen has no wax-forming paraffins.

### 🌫️ L. Volatility

**Definition:** The tendency of a fuel to **evaporate/vaporize** at given conditions. Measured by Reid Vapour Pressure (RVP) or distillation curve.

**Hydrogen's Volatility = EXTREMELY HIGH** — it is a permanent gas with very low molecular weight (2 g/mol). This means:
- It mixes with air almost instantaneously → very **uniform mixture formation**.
- Eliminates **cold-start problems** related to fuel atomization/vaporization (a major issue in petrol/diesel cold starts).
- For cryogenic LH₂, volatility must be controlled via **insulated, pressure-regulated tanks** to prevent boil-off.

### 🟡 M. Sulphur Content

**Definition:** The amount of elemental sulphur and sulphur compounds (SOx) present in the fuel, measured in mg/kg or % mass.

**Hydrogen's Sulphur Content = 0 % (essentially NIL).**

**Significance:**
- This eliminates **SO₂/SO₃ emissions** that cause acid rain and poison catalytic converters.
- Hydrogen requires **no sulphur-tolerant materials** in the fuel system, and produces no sulphuric acid corrosion in engine oil.

### ⚖️ N. Density

**Definition:** Mass per unit volume (kg/m³ or g/L).

**Hydrogen Density values:**
- At STP (0 °C, 1 atm): **0.0899 kg/m³** (≈ 1/8 that of air)
- Gas at 200 bar (≈ 2900 psi): **≈ 17 kg/m³**
- Liquid hydrogen at −253 °C: **≈ 71 kg/m³**
- Solid hydrogen: **≈ 88 kg/m³**

**Significance:**
- Very low gas density → **huge storage volumes** required → 4× larger tank than petrol for same energy (by volume).
- Even **liquid hydrogen is only 1/10 the density of water** — that is why hydrogen vehicles need either **high-pressure (700 bar) composite tanks** or **liquid hydrogen cryo-tanks**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Hydrogen

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation for complete combustion of hydrogen.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Reactants} \rightarrow \text{Products} \\
&2\,\text{H}_2 \;+\; \text{O}_2 \;\rightarrow\; 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the molar quantities to mass quantities using atomic/molecular masses.**
- Molecular mass of H₂ = 2 × 1 = **2 kg/kmol**
- Molecular mass of O₂ = 2 × 16 = **32 kg/kmol**
- Molecular mass of H₂O = 2 × 1 + 16 = **18 kg/kmol**

For 2 kmol H₂ reacting with 1 kmol O₂:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Mass of H}_2 = 2 \times 2 = 4 \text{ kg} \\
&\text{Mass of O}_2 = 1 \times 32 = 32 \text{ kg} \\
&\text{Mass of H}_2\text{O} = 2 \times 18 = 36 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate mass of theoretical (stoichiometric) air required.**

By mass, air contains approximately 23.2 % O₂ and 76.8 % N₂ (i.e., N₂/O₂ mass ratio = 76.8/23.2 = 3.31; equivalently, mole ratio is 3.76).

For every kg of O₂, the air required is:

$$
m_{\text{air per kg O}_2} = 1 + 3.31 = 4.31 \text{ kg air / kg O}_2
$$

For 32 kg O₂:

$$
m_{\text{air}} = 32 \times 4.31 = 137.92 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mass-based stoichiometric A/F ratio.**

$$
(A/F)_s = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{137.92 \text{ kg air}}{4 \text{ kg H}_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{(A/F)_s = 34.48 : 1 \text{ (by mass)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU textbooks often use the value **34.3 : 1** (rounded) or **34.48 : 1** (precise). Either is acceptable in the exam.

**Step 5 — Convert to mole/volume basis (for completeness).**

By volume, 2 volumes of H₂ react with 1 volume of O₂. Air is 21 % O₂ by volume, so air needed = 1/0.21 = 4.76 volumes.

$$
(A/F)_{\text{vol}} = \frac{4.76 \text{ volumes of air}}{2 \text{ volumes of H}_2} = 2.38 : 1
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with petrol for context.**

For petrol (approx. CH₂):
- Stoichiometric A/F by mass = **14.7 : 1**
- Hydrogen needs **2.34× more air per kg of fuel**, meaning engines need to handle **much larger air mass flow** for the same power output (or deliver less power with the same air charge).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Higher and Lower Heating Value Relationship for Hydrogen

**Step 1 — Write the HHV of H₂ from standard tables.**

$$
\text{HHV of H}_2 = 142 \, \text{MJ/kg}
$$

This is the heat released when 1 kg of H₂ burns and the water vapor in the products is **condensed back to liquid**, releasing its latent heat.

**Step 2 — Calculate the mass of water produced per kg of hydrogen.**

From 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O:
- 4 kg H₂ produces 36 kg H₂O
- Per kg H₂ → 36/4 = 9 kg H₂O

**Step 3 — Apply the energy balance.**

The difference between HHV and LHV equals the **latent heat of vaporization of the water produced** (h_fg of water at 25 °C ≈ 2442 kJ/kg):

$$
\text{HHV} = \text{LHV} + (m_{\text{water}} \times h_{fg,\text{water}})
$$

$$
142 = \text{LHV} + (9 \times 2.442)
$$

$$
142 = \text{LHV} + 21.978
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LHV} = 120.02 \, \text{MJ/kg}}
$$

This matches the standard textbook value of **120 MJ/kg**.

**Step 4 — Engineering conclusion.**

In an **ICE**, exhaust temperature is high → water leaves as **vapor** → use **LHV (120 MJ/kg)** for energy analysis.
In a **Fuel Cell** (PEMFC), water may be condensed at the cathode → use **HHV (142 MJ/kg)** for efficiency calculation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydrogen Storage Volume Comparison (KTU Favorite Numerical)

**Problem:** A vehicle requires 5 kg of hydrogen for a 500 km range.
(a) Find the volume of gaseous hydrogen at 1 atm, 25 °C.
(b) Find the volume of hydrogen compressed at 700 bar, 25 °C.
(c) Find the volume of liquid hydrogen at its boiling point.
(d) Compare with petrol required for the same range (assume petrol consumption = 6 L/100 km).

**Given:**
- Mass of H₂ = m = 5 kg
- Molar mass of H₂ = M = 2 kg/kmol
- Universal gas constant = R_u = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K
- T = 25 °C = 298 K, P₁ = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa
- Liquid H₂ density = 71 kg/m³
- Petrol density = 750 kg/m³

**Solution:**

**(a) Volume at 1 atm, 25 °C — Using Ideal Gas Law PV = mRT**

First compute the specific gas constant for H₂:

$$
R_{H_2} = \frac{R_u}{M} = \frac{8.314}{2} = 4.157 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}
$$

Volume:

$$
V_1 = \frac{mRT}{P_1} = \frac{5 \times 4.157 \times 298}{101.325}
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{6193.93}{101.325} = 61.13 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**(b) Volume at 700 bar (≈ 700 × 100 = 70,000 kPa):**

Using Boyle's Law (isothermal):

$$
P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2} = \frac{1 \times 61.13}{700} = 0.0873 \, \text{m}^3 = 87.3 \, \text{liters}
$$

**(c) Volume as liquid hydrogen:**

$$
V_{LH_2} = \frac{m}{\rho_{LH_2}} = \frac{5}{71} = 0.0704 \, \text{m}^3 = 70.4 \, \text{liters}
$$

**(d) Petrol volume for same range:**

Distance = 500 km → petrol consumption = (6/100) × 500 = 30 L = 0.030 m³

**Comparison Table:**

| Form | Volume (m³) | Volume (Liters) | × Petrol Volume |
|------|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| H₂ gas at 1 atm | 61.13 | 61,130 | 2037× |
| H₂ at 700 bar | 0.0873 | 87.3 | 2.91× |
| H₂ liquid (LH₂) | 0.0704 | 70.4 | 2.35× |
| Petrol (liquid) | 0.030 | 30 | 1.00× |

**Conclusion:** Hydrogen needs **~3× more volume** than petrol even when compressed to 700 bar or liquefied — this is the **central storage challenge** for hydrogen vehicles.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: List any six important properties of hydrogen as an automotive fuel and state their values.**

**Model Answer (6 key properties):**

1. **Calorific Value (LHV)** = **120 MJ/kg** — highest among all common fuels.
2. **Auto-Ignition Temperature** = **571 °C** — very high, excellent knock resistance.
3. **Boiling Point** = **−252.87 °C** — requires cryogenic storage as liquid.
4. **Flammable Limits** = **4 % to 75 %** (in air) — widest range, supports lean burn.
5. **Octane Number (RON)** = **> 130** — allows high compression ratios in SI engines.
6. **Stoichiometric A/F Ratio** = **34.3 : 1** — needs much more air per kg than petrol (14.7:1).
7. **Density (STP gas)** = **0.0899 kg/m³** — very low, demanding high-pressure storage.
8. **Sulphur Content** = **≈ 0 %** — zero SOx emissions.

(Any 6 properties with values = 3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Why is hydrogen considered a "clean fuel"? Justify with reference to its emissions and combustion products.**

**Model Answer:**

Hydrogen is termed a "clean fuel" because its **complete combustion produces only water (H₂O) as the exhaust product**, with no carbon-based emissions.

- **Zero CO₂** → no greenhouse gas contribution at tailpipe.
- **Zero CO** → no carbon-monoxide poisoning risk.
- **Zero unburnt HC** → no hydrocarbon smog formation.
- **Zero SOx** → sulphur content is essentially **0 %**, so no SO₂/SO₃ → no acid rain.
- **Zero Particulate Matter (PM)** → no soot, no black smoke.
- **NOx** can be formed (due to high flame temperature) but is much lower than petrol/diesel and can be controlled with exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) or lean burn operation.

Hence, hydrogen is the cleanest practical fuel for IC engines and fuel cell vehicles.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — KTU December 2023 Pattern

**Q: (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the different methods of on-board hydrogen storage in automobiles. Discuss their advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**Q: (b)** Calculate the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of hydrogen by mass. The composition of air by mass is 23.2% O₂ and 76.8% N₂. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution (a) — Hydrogen Storage Methods

The major on-board hydrogen storage methods for automobiles are:

**1. Compressed Gaseous Hydrogen (CGH₂):**
- Stored at **350 bar or 700 bar** in **carbon-fiber reinforced composite tanks**.
- Most commercially mature; used in **Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo**.
- Volume per kg H₂ ≈ 25 L (at 700 bar) → still 3× petrol volume.
- Pros: fast refueling (3–5 min), established standards.
- Cons: tank cost high, energy for compression (≈ 10–15% of LHV).

**2. Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂):**
- Stored at **−253 °C** in **vacuum-insulated cryogenic tanks**.
- Density 71 kg/m³ → 4× denser than 700 bar gas.
- Pros: highest volumetric energy density.
- Cons: **boil-off losses** (~1–2 %/day), expensive cryogenic tanks, liquefaction energy = 30–40% of LHV.

**3. Metal Hydride Storage:**
- Hydrogen absorbed into metal alloys (e.g., LaNi₅, MgH₂, TiFe).
- Released by heating.
- Pros: safe, low-pressure operation.
- Cons: heavy (low gravimetric density), slow kinetics, high-temperature release.

**4. Carbon Nanotube / Adsorbent Storage:**
- H₂ adsorbed on **activated carbon, MOFs (Metal-Organic Frameworks), CNTs**.
- Still largely in R&D phase.
- Theoretical capacity 5–10 wt% achievable at cryo-temperatures.

**Conclusion:** CGH₂ at 700 bar is currently the **industry standard** for FCEVs.

#### Model Solution (b) — Stoichiometric A/F of Hydrogen

**Combustion reaction:** 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O

By mass:
- Mass of H₂ reacting = 2 × 2 = **4 kg**
- Mass of O₂ required = 1 × 32 = **32 kg**

Mass of air needed (since air is 23.2% O₂ by mass):

$$
m_{\text{air}} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{32}{0.232} = 137.93 \text{ kg}
$$

Therefore, stoichiometric A/F ratio (by mass):

$$
(A/F)_s = \frac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{137.93}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{(A/F)_s = 34.48 : 1}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students write **34.3:1** without showing derivation — you'll still get full credit if the value is correct, but the working carries **3 of the 7 marks**.
> - **Common mistake:** Forgetting to convert O₂ to air (using 32 instead of 137.9). Always include the "÷ 0.232" step.
> - Do NOT confuse stoichiometric A/F (34.3:1) with volumetric A/F (~2.38:1).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a)** Compare the properties of hydrogen with petrol (gasoline) as an automotive fuel. Mention at least 8 properties. **[7 Marks]**

**Q: (b)** A hydrogen-fuelled vehicle carries 4 kg of liquid hydrogen in a cryogenic tank. Calculate:
- (i) The volume of liquid hydrogen (density = 71 kg/m³).
- (ii) The volume this hydrogen would occupy as a gas at 1 atm and 25 °C.
- (iii) The energy stored (use LHV = 120 MJ/kg). **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution (a) — Hydrogen vs. Petrol Comparison

| # | Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Petrol (Gasoline) | Better For Engine? |
|---|----------|---------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | Calorific Value (LHV) | 120 MJ/kg | 44 MJ/kg | H₂ (3× better) |
| 2 | Density (liquid) | 71 kg/m³ | 740 kg/m³ | Petrol (10× denser) |
| 3 | Boiling Point | −252.87 °C | 30–220 °C | Petrol (easy storage) |
| 4 | Auto-Ignition Temp | 571 °C | 280 °C | H₂ (knock resistant) |
| 5 | Octane Number (RON) | > 130 | 92–98 | H₂ (high CR possible) |
| 6 | Flammable Limits | 4–75 % | 1.4–7.6 % | H₂ (wider range) |
| 7 | Stoichiometric A/F | 34.3:1 | 14.7:1 | Petrol (easier mixing) |
| 8 | Sulphur Content | ~0 % | 0.05–0.1 % | H₂ (zero emissions) |
| 9 | Storage Volume (5 kg) | ~70 L (LH₂) | ~6.7 L | Petrol (compact) |
| 10 | CO₂ Emissions | 0 g/MJ | 73 g/MJ | H₂ (clean) |

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical on Liquid Hydrogen

**Given:** m = 4 kg, ρ_LH₂ = 71 kg/m³, LHV = 120 MJ/kg, P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa, T = 25 °C = 298 K, R (H₂) = 4.157 kJ/kg·K.

**(i) Volume of liquid hydrogen:**

$$
V_{LH_2} = \frac{m}{\rho_{LH_2}} = \frac{4}{71} = 0.0563 \, \text{m}^3 = 56.3 \, \text{liters}
$$

**(ii) Volume as gas at 1 atm, 25 °C (Ideal Gas Law):**

$$
V_{\text{gas}} = \frac{mRT}{P} = \frac{4 \times 4.157 \times 298}{101.325}
$$

$$
V_{\text{gas}} = \frac{4955.14}{101.325} = 48.90 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**(iii) Energy stored:**

$$
E = m \times \text{LHV} = 4 \times 120 = 480 \, \text{MJ}
$$

**Final Interpretation:**
- 4 kg LH₂ = **56.3 L volume** but expands to **48,900 L (≈ 49 m³)** as gas at 1 atm — a **868× expansion ratio** when boiled off.
- 480 MJ stored energy ≈ **133 kWh** — enough to drive an FCEV ~500–600 km.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.B):**
> - **Common error:** Forgetting to convert m³ to L (1 m³ = 1000 L). Always quote the final answer in clear units.
> - **Common error:** Using R = 8.314 (universal) instead of R = 4.157 (specific for H₂). For **m = mass, not moles**, always use the specific gas constant.
> - **Common error:** Confusing LHV (120 MJ/kg) with HHV (142 MJ/kg) when the question says "ICE application" or doesn't specify. The KTU convention: if not specified, use **LHV**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> ### 🧠 KTU 2024 — Hydrogen Properties Quick-Recall Bullets
>
> ✅ **Hydrogen = simplest, lightest (M = 2 kg/kmol), colorless, odorless, non-toxic, clean fuel.**
>
> ✅ **HHV = 142 MJ/kg, LHV = 120 MJ/kg** — highest energy per kg of any fuel.
>
> ✅ **Auto-Ignition Temperature = 571 °C** — very high → excellent for SI engines (no knock).
>
> ✅ **Boiling Point = −252.87 °C** — needs cryogenic tanks for liquid storage; gaseous storage is 350/700 bar.
>
> ✅ **Cetane Number ≈ very low** — poor CI fuel; needs pilot injection or HCCI mode.
>
> ✅ **Octane Number (RON) > 130** — allows high compression ratios in H₂-ICE.
>
> ✅ **Flammable Limits in air = 4 % to 75 %** — widest of all fuels → supports ultra-lean burn.
>
> ✅ **Flash Point / Pour Point / Cloud Point = NOT APPLICABLE** (hydrogen is a permanent gas at atmospheric conditions).
>
> ✅ **Stoichiometric A/F ratio = 34.3:1 (mass) or 2.38:1 (volume)** — derive from 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O.
>
> ✅ **Latent Heat of Vaporization = 446 kJ/kg** — cools intake charge, suppresses knock in ICE.
>
> ✅ **Volatility = extremely high** — mixes instantly with air, no cold-start problem.
>
> ✅ **Sulphur content ≈ 0** — zero SOx emissions, no acid rain, no catalyst poisoning.
>
> ✅ **Density = 0.0899 kg/m³ (gas STP), 71 kg/m³ (liquid), ~40 kg/m³ (700 bar gas)** — 3–4× larger storage volume than petrol.
>
> ✅ **Universal gas law: PV = mRT** with R_H₂ = 4.157 kJ/kg·K.
>
> ✅ **HHV − LHV = 9 × 2.442 = 22 MJ/kg** (latent heat of water produced per kg H₂).
>
> ✅ **Engineering trade-off:** Hydrogen wins on **mass energy density & emissions**; loses on **volume energy density & storage cost**.
>
> ✅ **Applications:** Hydrogen ICE (H₂-SI), Fuel Cell EV (FCEV), HCNG (H₂ + CNG blend), HCCI research engines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sources and production of hydrogen: electrolysis of water, thermal decomposition and biological processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Hydrogen as a Future Automotive Fuel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Hydrogen Economy Context):**
> Hydrogen (H₂) is the lightest, most abundant chemical element in the universe and is regarded as the cleanest energy carrier for automotive applications. When combusted or used in a fuel cell, it releases energy while producing only **water (H₂O)** as the primary by-product, making it a zero-carbon-emission alternative to petroleum fuels. For KTU 2024 Scheme purposes, the *production* of hydrogen is as critical as its *use*, because hydrogen is not a primary fuel — it must be extracted from hydrogen-rich compounds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Molecular Weight of H₂ = 2.016 g/mol**
> **Lower Heating Value (LHV) = 119.96 MJ/kg**
> **Higher Heating Value (HHV) = 141.86 MJ/kg**
> For comparison, gasoline has LHV ≈ **44 MJ/kg**, meaning hydrogen carries **~2.7×** more energy per kilogram.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Battery of Fuels"

Think of hydrogen as **cash in an envelope**. You cannot just pick it up from the ground (unlike coal or crude oil). The cash (hydrogen) is always stored *inside* something — usually a water molecule, a hydrocarbon, or biomass. To get the cash out, you must "break open the envelope" using energy. The three production routes discussed in this module are simply three different "tools" used to break that envelope:

1. **Electricity** (electrolysis) — like cutting the envelope with a precise, clean electric blade.
2. **Heat** (thermal decomposition) — like melting the envelope with intense fire.
3. **Biology** (biological processes) — like letting friendly microbes gently eat the envelope from the inside.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Terminology:**
> The colour-coding of hydrogen is increasingly important for KTU answers:
> - **Grey Hydrogen** — produced from natural gas (fossil, with CO₂ emissions).
> - **Blue Hydrogen** — grey hydrogen + carbon capture and storage (CCS).
> - **Green Hydrogen** — produced by **electrolysis of water using renewable electricity** (zero-emission, the KTU-favoured answer).
> - **Pink Hydrogen** — produced by electrolysis using nuclear electricity.
> - **White Hydrogen** — naturally occurring geologic hydrogen.

## 1.3 Primary Sources of Hydrogen

Hydrogen does not exist in free molecular form on Earth in large quantities. The principal **hydrogen carriers** (sources) include:

| Source | Chemical Form | Typical Production Route |
|---|---|---|
| Water | H₂O | Electrolysis, Thermolysis, Photolysis |
| Natural Gas / Methane | CH₄ | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) |
| Biomass | C₆H₁₂O₆ etc. | Gasification, Fermentation |
| Fossil Fuels (Coal, Oil) | Hydrocarbons | Partial Oxidation, Gasification |
| Organic Wastes | Carbohydrates/Lipids | Anaerobic Digestion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Module 3**, the syllabus focuses on **water-based hydrogen production** through:
> 1. **Electrolysis of water** (electrical route),
> 2. **Thermal decomposition of water** (thermochemical route),
> 3. **Biological processes** (biophotolysis, fermentation, microbial electrolysis).

## 1.4 Why Focus on Water as a Source?

Water covers **~71%** of Earth's surface and is effectively an inexhaustible feedstock. The challenge is the **high bond energy** of the O–H bond in water, which requires a minimum theoretical energy input of **237.2 kJ/mol** (Gibbs free energy) to split. This is why hydrogen production is energy-intensive and routes must be carefully chosen.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 3 — SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 METHOD 1 — ELECTROLYSIS OF WATER

### 2.1.1 Principle
Electrolysis uses **direct electrical current (DC)** to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction — the splitting of water into hydrogen and oxygen. The overall reaction is:

$$2H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow 2H_2{_{(g)}} + O_2{_{(g)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Decomposition Voltage (E°cell) = 1.229 V at 25 °C and 1 atm.**
> This is the *minimum* voltage required; practical cells operate between **1.8 V to 2.5 V** due to overpotentials and ohmic losses.

### 2.1.2 Half-Reactions
- **Cathode (Reduction):** $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$ (alkaline) or $2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$ (acidic/PEM)
- **Anode (Oxidation):** $2H_2O \rightarrow O_2 + 4H^+ + 4e^-$ (acidic) or $4OH^- \rightarrow O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^-$ (alkaline)

### 2.1.3 Why It Works — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Power Supply** provides DC electricity across two electrodes immersed in an electrolyte.
2. **Electrolyte** is a conductive liquid (KOH solution, H₂SO₄, or a polymer membrane) that allows ion flow.
3. At the **cathode (−)**, water molecules are **reduced** to hydrogen gas.
4. At the **anode (+)**, water molecules are **oxidized** to oxygen gas.
5. The **diaphragm/membrane** physically separates the gases to prevent recombination.
6. Hydrogen is collected at the cathode, oxygen at the anode.

### 2.1.4 Types of Electrolyzers (KTU High-Yield)

| Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp | Efficiency | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Alkaline Electrolyzer (AEL)** | 25–30% KOH liquid | 60–90 °C | 60–70% | Most mature, cheapest |
| **PEM Electrolyzer** | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | 50–80 °C | 70–85% | High current density, fast response |
| **Solid Oxide Electrolyzer (SOEL)** | YSZ ceramic | 700–900 °C | 75–85% | Steam input, high efficiency |
| **Microbial Electrolysis Cell (MEC)** | Biofilm + solution | 20–40 °C | Low (research) | Uses bacteria on electrodes |

### 2.1.5 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis — The Governing Physics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Faraday's First Law:**
> The mass (m) of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge (Q) passed through the electrolyte.
> 
> **Faraday's Second Law:**
> The mass of substance liberated is proportional to its equivalent weight (M/n).

Combined equation:

$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of substance liberated (grams)
- $Q$ = total charge in Coulombs (C)
- $I$ = current in Amperes (A)
- $t$ = time in seconds (s)
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)
- $n$ = valency (number of electrons transferred)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96,500 C/mol)

### 2.1.6 Energy and Efficiency Relations

The energy required to electrolyze water is governed by **thermodynamics**:

$$\Delta H = \Delta G + T \Delta S$$

At standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm):
- $\Delta H$ = **+285.8 kJ/mol** (enthalpy change)
- $\Delta G$ = **+237.2 kJ/mol** (Gibbs free energy — minimum electrical work)
- $T \Delta S$ = **+48.6 kJ/mol** (heat energy)

The minimum theoretical cell voltage (reversible voltage):

$$E_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G}{nF} = \frac{237{,}200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

The thermoneutral voltage (using $\Delta H$):

$$E_{tn} = \frac{\Delta H}{nF} = \frac{285{,}800}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.481 \text{ V}$$

Faraday Efficiency (Current Efficiency):

$$\eta_F = \frac{\text{Actual } H_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{Theoretical } H_2 \text{ (from charge passed)}} \times 100\%$$

Energy Efficiency of Electrolyzer:

$$\eta_{energy} = \frac{\text{HHV of } H_2 \text{ produced}}{\text{Electrical energy input}} \times 100\%$$

## 2.2 METHOD 2 — THERMAL DECOMPOSITION (THERMOLYSIS)

### 2.2.1 Principle
Direct thermal decomposition (thermolysis) splits water at very high temperatures:

$$2H_2O \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2H_2 + O_2$$

### 2.2.2 The Problem — Why Direct Thermolysis Is Hard
Water is **extremely stable**. To get even 5% dissociation, temperatures above **2,000 °C** are required; for 50% dissociation, temperatures approach **3,000 °C**. No commercial material can withstand this continuously, and gas separation at such temperatures is impractical.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **thermochemical cycles** — they perform the same splitting using a series of reactions at moderate temperatures (500–900 °C) and achieve the same net result.

### 2.2.3 Thermochemical Cycles — Engineering Workaround

A thermochemical cycle breaks the single high-temperature step into 2–4 reactions whose net effect is the splitting of water, but each step happens at a manageable temperature.

#### **A. Sulfur–Iodine (S–I) Cycle (General Atomics Cycle)**

Three reactions:
1. **Bunsen Reaction (120 °C):** $I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2HI + H_2SO_4$
2. **Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (830 °C):** $H_2SO_4 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$
3. **Hydriodic Acid Decomposition (320 °C):** $2HI \rightarrow I_2 + H_2$

**Net Reaction:** $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The S–I cycle has a demonstrated efficiency of ~50%** and is the most mature thermochemical cycle globally. It was selected by Japan's JAEA for the HTTR nuclear hydrogen project.

#### **B. Copper–Chlorine (Cu–Cl) Cycle**
Operates at lower temperatures (≤ 530 °C) and is favoured when using lower-grade heat sources (e.g., SMRs, solar thermal). It involves 4–5 steps with intermediate compounds Cu, CuCl₂, Cu₂OCl₂, and HCl.

#### **C. Zinc–Zinc Oxide Cycle**
1. $ZnO \rightarrow Zn + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$ (solar thermal, 1,700–2,000 °C)
2. $Zn + H_2O \rightarrow ZnO + H_2$ (400 °C)

This is a **two-step** cycle, simple in concept but requires a solar concentrator to reach step 1's temperature.

### 2.2.4 Solar Thermal Hydrogen Production
Concentrated solar power (CSP) systems use parabolic mirrors or solar towers to focus sunlight, achieving temperatures of 1,500–2,500 °C. Two main paths:
- **Direct water splitting** (thermolysis at solar temperatures)
- **Thermochemical cycles powered by solar heat** (e.g., Zn/ZnO, S–I)

## 2.3 METHOD 3 — BIOLOGICAL HYDROGEN PRODUCTION

### 2.3.1 Why Biology?
Biological processes operate at **ambient temperature and pressure**, use **renewable feedstocks** (water, biomass, organic waste), and require **no precious metals**. They are ideal for distributed, small-scale, low-cost hydrogen production.

### 2.3.2 Main Biological Pathways

#### **A. Direct Biophotolysis**
Uses **green algae** (e.g., *Chlamydomonas reinhardtii*) or **cyanobacteria**. The algae's enzyme **hydrogenase** (or **nitrogenase**) splits water using sunlight.

$$2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{sunlight + hydrogenase}} 2H_2 + O_2$$

- **Inputs:** Sunlight + water + algae.
- **Problem:** O₂ inhibits hydrogenase → low yield.

#### **B. Indirect Biophotolysis**
Algae first fix CO₂ into starch (via photosynthesis); the starch is then fermented or metabolized anaerobically to release H₂. This separates O₂ evolution from H₂ evolution, increasing yield.

#### **C. Photo-Fermentation**
**Purple non-sulfur bacteria** (e.g., *Rhodobacter sphaeroides*) use sunlight and organic acids (acetate, lactate) to produce H₂ via **nitrogenase**.

$$CH_3COOH + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{light + bacteria}} 2CO_2 + 4H_2$$

#### **D. Dark Fermentation**
**Anaerobic bacteria** (e.g., *Clostridium* species) break down carbohydrates in the **absence of light**:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2$$

Maximum theoretical yield = **4 mol H₂ per mol glucose** (only ~33% of total H₂ available).

#### **E. Microbial Electrolysis Cell (MEC)**
Bacteria on the **anode** oxidize organic matter, releasing electrons and protons. A small applied voltage (~0.2–0.8 V) drives the protons to the cathode where they are reduced to H₂.

$$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$$

### 2.3.3 Comparison of Biological Routes

| Process | Microbe | Light Required? | Substrate | Yield |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Biophotolysis | Green algae | Yes | Water | Low |
| Indirect Biophotolysis | Cyanobacteria | Yes | Water → Starch | Medium |
| Photo-Fermentation | Purple bacteria | Yes | Organic acids | Medium |
| Dark Fermentation | Clostridia | No | Carbohydrates | High rate, low yield |
| MEC | Mixed cultures | No | Organic waste | Medium |

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they are 90% of the numerical problems in KTU Module 3.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^\circ_{rev} = \dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | Theoretical electrolysis voltage | Volts |
| 2 | $E_{tn} = \dfrac{\Delta H}{nF}$ | Thermoneutral voltage | Volts |
| 3 | $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law (mass liberated) | grams |
| 4 | $V_{H_2} = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot R \cdot T}{n \cdot F \cdot P}$ | Volume of H₂ at STP | m³ |
| 5 | $V_{H_2 \text{ (theor.)}} = 0.418 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg H}_2\text{O}$ | Stoichiometric volume of H₂ | m³ |
| 6 | $\eta_F = \dfrac{\text{Actual H}_2}{\text{Theoretical H}_2} \times 100\%$ | Faraday efficiency | % |
| 7 | $\eta_{energy} = \dfrac{\text{HHV(H}_2\text{)}}{\text{Electrical input}} \times 100\%$ | Energy efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ | Gibbs–Helmholtz relation | J/mol |
| 9 | $P_{electrolyzer} = V_{cell} \times I$ | Electrical power input | Watts |
| 10 | $E_{cell} = E_{rev} + \eta_{anode} + \eta_{cathode} + IR$ | Actual cell voltage | Volts |

> **NOTE on notation:** For absolute values, write $\vert x \vert$ in your exam papers. In this table, vertical bars were intentionally avoided to preserve the table structure — KTU answers should still display them clearly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 3 — SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Electrolysis Voltage from Thermodynamics

### 3.1.1 Starting Point
The electrolysis reaction: $2H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow 2H_2{_{(g)}} + O_2{_{(g)}}$

The Gibbs free energy change for this reaction at 25 °C, 1 atm:
$\Delta G^\circ = +237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (per mol of H₂, equivalently +474.4 kJ for 2 mol of H₂O → 2 mol H₂).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

The relation between Gibbs free energy and the maximum electrical work (reversible work) is:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$$

The negative sign in the Nernst equation arises because the cell does work *spontaneously*; in electrolysis, the external supply does work *into* the cell, so we take the magnitude:

$$E^\circ_{cell} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$$

Step 1 — Substitute the known values (per mole of H₂, $n = 2$ electrons transferred):

$$E^\circ_{cell} = -\frac{(+237{,}200 \text{ J/mol})}{(2)(96{,}485 \text{ C/mol})}$$

Step 2 — Compute the numerator sign and magnitude:
- $\Delta G^\circ = +237{,}200$ J/mol (positive → non-spontaneous).
- The negative sign cancels the positive value, yielding a positive voltage.

$$E^\circ_{cell} = \frac{237{,}200}{192{,}970}$$

Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:

$$E^\circ_{cell} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The minimum voltage to electrolyze water is **1.229 V**. Below this, the reaction will not proceed. KTU students should remember this number along with $\Delta H$ and $\Delta G$ values.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Thermoneutral Voltage

If we use $\Delta H$ instead of $\Delta G$ (i.e., the cell needs to supply the *total* energy including heat loss):

$$E_{tn} = \frac{\Delta H}{nF} = \frac{285{,}800}{192{,}970} = 1.481 \text{ V}$$

This is the voltage at which the electrolyzer is *thermally self-sustaining* — the heat produced by inefficiencies is exactly used to maintain temperature.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Faraday's Law Applied to Hydrogen Production

### 3.2.1 Setting Up the Problem

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
An alkaline water electrolyzer operates at a current of **500 A** for **8 hours**. Calculate:
(a) The mass of hydrogen produced, assuming 100% Faraday efficiency.
(Atomic mass: H = 1.008 g/mol; F = 96,485 C/mol.)

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the half-reaction at the cathode:**
$$2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$$
Here, $n = 2$ electrons per molecule of H₂, and molar mass $M = 2.016$ g/mol.

**Step 2 — Calculate the total charge passed:**
$$Q = I \cdot t = 500 \text{ A} \times (8 \times 3600) \text{ s} = 500 \times 28{,}800 = 14{,}400{,}000 \text{ C} = 1.44 \times 10^7 \text{ C}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's First Law:**
$$m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$m = \frac{(1.44 \times 10^7) \times (2.016)}{(2) \times (96{,}485)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator:**
$$1.44 \times 10^7 \times 2.016 = 2.903 \times 10^7 \text{ g·C/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Compute denominator:**
$$2 \times 96{,}485 = 1.9297 \times 10^5 \text{ C/mol}$$

**Step 7 — Final division:**

$$m = \frac{2.903 \times 10^7}{1.9297 \times 10^5} \approx 150.4 \text{ g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Mass of H₂ produced = **150.4 grams (≈ 0.150 kg)** in 8 hours at 500 A.

### 3.2.3 Derivation 3 — Volume of H₂ at STP

Using the ideal gas law, the volume of H₂ at STP (0 °C, 1 atm) is:

$$V = \frac{n \cdot R \cdot T}{P}$$

The *number of moles* of H₂ produced equals $Q/(nF)$ where $n=2$:

$$n_{H_2} = \frac{Q}{nF} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^7}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 74.63 \text{ mol}$$

Then:

$$V = \frac{74.63 \times 0.0821 \times 273}{1} = 1{,}672.9 \text{ L} = 1.673 \text{ m}^3$$

**Alternative quick formula** (specific to H₂ at STP):

$$V_{H_2} = 0.0105 \times Q \text{ (in litres, with Q in C, 100% efficiency)}$$

Check: $0.0105 \times 1.44 \times 10^7 = 1.51 \times 10^5$ L — this uses 100% efficiency at slightly different conditions; the precise value above is the rigorous derivation.

## 3.3 Derivation 4 — Energy Efficiency of an Electrolyzer

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement
A PEM electrolyzer draws **2.0 V** at **300 A** for **10 hours** and produces **0.280 kg** of hydrogen. Calculate the energy efficiency based on the HHV of hydrogen (141.86 MJ/kg).

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Energy input (electrical):**

$$E_{in} = V \cdot I \cdot t = 2.0 \times 300 \times (10 \times 3600) = 2.0 \times 300 \times 36{,}000$$

$$E_{in} = 21{,}600{,}000 \text{ J} = 21.6 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 2 — Energy output (chemical, from HHV):**

$$E_{out} = m \cdot HHV = 0.280 \times 141.86 = 39.72 \text{ MJ}$$

Wait — this gives efficiency > 100%, which is impossible. **Re-check the problem statement** (KTU examiners may use any value, but logically the input must exceed the output):

For real electrolysis: 21.6 MJ input vs. 39.72 MJ chemical output is *thermodynamically impossible*. The numbers were illustrative; the correct relation is:

$$\eta = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \frac{m \cdot HHV}{V \cdot I \cdot t}$$

**Step 3 — Rigorous example (corrected numbers):**

A *real* electrolyzer at 1.8 V and 300 A for 10 hours producing 0.100 kg of H₂:

$$E_{in} = 1.8 \times 300 \times 36{,}000 = 1.944 \times 10^7 \text{ J} = 19.44 \text{ MJ}$$

$$E_{out} = 0.100 \times 141.86 = 14.186 \text{ MJ}$$

$$\eta = \frac{14.186}{19.44} \times 100\% = 72.98\% \approx 73\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical PEM electrolyzer efficiency = 70–85% (HHV basis).**
> This is *electrical-to-chemical* conversion. If the *source* of electricity is also considered, then *well-to-tank* efficiency drops to 25–35% (for grid electricity) and is much higher only when paired with renewables.

## 3.4 Derivation 5 — Stoichiometric Water Requirement

### 3.4.1 Problem
How much water is theoretically required to produce **1 kg of hydrogen** by electrolysis?

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Moles of H₂ in 1 kg:**

$$n_{H_2} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{2.016 \text{ g/mol}} = 496.0 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — From stoichiometry** $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$, the molar ratio of H₂O : H₂ is **1 : 1**.

$$n_{H_2O} = n_{H_2} = 496.0 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water:**

$$m_{H_2O} = 496.0 \times 18.015 = 8935 \text{ g} \approx 8.94 \text{ kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approximately 9 kg of water produces 1 kg of hydrogen.**
> At 100% efficiency, this is the minimum. In practice, **~10–12 kg** of water is needed per kg of H₂.

## 3.5 Derivation 6 — Energy Required for Thermal Decomposition (Thermodynamic Check)

For direct thermolysis, at 2,500 K, $\Delta G \approx 0$ — but $\Delta H$ is still positive (~250 kJ/mol). The minimum heat required is:

$$Q_{min} = \Delta H = 250 \text{ kJ/mol of H}_2$$

For 1 kg H₂ (496 mol):

$$Q_{min} = 496 \times 250 = 124{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 124 \text{ MJ}$$

By the **Carnot limit**, a heat source at 2,500 K rejecting to 300 K has maximum efficiency:

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{300}{2500} = 0.88 = 88\%$$

This shows why direct thermolysis is *thermodynamically possible* but practically prohibitive: it requires a 2,500 K heat source that itself must be produced (e.g., solar concentrator at 70% efficiency → overall efficiency < 30%).

## 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Method

| Criterion | Electrolysis | Thermal (Direct) | Thermochemical Cycle | Biological |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy input** | Electricity | Heat (>2500 °C) | Heat (500–900 °C) | Sunlight + biomass |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | 9 (commercial) | 3 (lab scale) | 5–7 (pilot) | 3–4 (research) |
| **Efficiency** | 60–85% | <30% | 40–55% | <10% (often) |
| **Cost (USD/kg H₂)** | 4–8 (green) | N/A | 5–10 (projected) | 2–5 (projected) |
| **Purity of H₂** | 99.99% | 95% | 99.9% | Mixed with CO₂/CH₄ |
| **Suitability for autos** | High (Fuel cell EVs) | Low (impurity) | High (after purification) | Low (R&D stage) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to *compare* methods, structure your answer in this matrix format. Examiners reward tabulated comparisons with 2–3 extra marks.

# MODULE 3 — SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define electrolysis of water. Write the overall reaction and the theoretical decomposition voltage at standard conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Electrolysis of water is the process of splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen using **direct electric current (DC)** passed through an aqueous electrolyte.

**Overall reaction:**

$$2H_2O_{(l)} \xrightarrow{DC} 2H_2{_{(g)}} + O_2{_{(g)}}$$

**Theoretical decomposition voltage (E°rev) at 25 °C, 1 atm:**

$$E^\circ_{rev} = \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF} = \frac{237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

> **Mark split:** Definition (1) + Reaction (1) + Voltage with derivation (1).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List any three biological methods of hydrogen production and state the microorganism involved in each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Method | Microorganism |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct Biophotolysis | Green algae (*Chlamydomonas reinhardtii*) |
| 2 | Photo-Fermentation | Purple non-sulphur bacteria (*Rhodobacter sphaeroides*) |
| 3 | Dark Fermentation | Anaerobic bacteria (*Clostridium butyricum*) |

Each method's process needs to be stated in 1 line (1 mark each).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 3 Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **alkaline water electrolyzer** for hydrogen production. State the half-reactions at both electrodes. (7 marks)

**(b)** An **alkaline electrolyzer** operates at a current of **1,000 A** and a cell voltage of **2.1 V** for **24 hours**. Calculate:
(i) The mass of H₂ produced at 95% Faraday efficiency.
(ii) The energy efficiency based on the HHV of hydrogen. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Working of Alkaline Water Electrolyzer (Block Diagram Description, 3 marks):**

```
   [DC Power Supply]
         |
   + ----|---- -
   |    ANODE (+)    |   DIAPHRAGM    |    CATHODE (-)   |
   |  (Ni-coated)    |  (Asbestos/    |  (Ni-coated)     |
   |                 |  Polymer mem.) |                  |
   |   Electrolyte: 30% KOH solution                       |
   +-----------------+----------------+
         |                  |
       [O₂ outlet]      [H₂ outlet]
```

**Half-reactions (2 marks each, 1 for each electrode):**

- **At Cathode (Reduction):** $2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$
- **At Anode (Oxidation):** $4OH^- \rightarrow O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^-$

**Net reaction:** $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$

**Working principle (2 marks):** DC current from the power supply drives the non-spontaneous splitting of water. KOH electrolyte conducts OH⁻ ions from cathode to anode. The diaphragm prevents the H₂ and O₂ gases from mixing, ensuring high-purity hydrogen output. The system operates typically at 60–90 °C with ~70% efficiency.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given data:**
- $I = 1000$ A
- $V_{cell} = 2.1$ V
- $t = 24 \text{ h} = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400$ s
- $\eta_F = 95\% = 0.95$
- $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol
- $n = 2$
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $HHV = 141.86$ MJ/kg

**Step 1 — Total charge passed (0.5 marks):**

$$Q = I \cdot t = 1000 \times 86{,}400 = 8.64 \times 10^7 \text{ C}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical mass of H₂ from Faraday's law (1 mark):**

$$m_{theor} = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{8.64 \times 10^7 \times 2.016}{2 \times 96{,}485} = \frac{1.7418 \times 10^8}{1.9297 \times 10^5} = 902.7 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Actual mass of H₂ at 95% efficiency (1 mark):**

$$m_{actual} = 0.95 \times 902.7 = 857.6 \text{ g} \approx 0.858 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Energy input (1 mark):**

$$E_{in} = V \cdot I \cdot t = 2.1 \times 1000 \times 86{,}400 = 1.8144 \times 10^8 \text{ J} = 181.44 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 5 — Energy output (1 mark):**

$$E_{out} = m \cdot HHV = 0.858 \times 141.86 = 121.7 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 6 — Energy efficiency (0.5 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{121.7}{181.44} \times 100\% = 67.08\%$$

> **Final Answers:**
> (i) Mass of H₂ produced = **0.858 kg**
> (ii) Energy efficiency = **67.08%**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Sulfur–Iodine (S–I) thermochemical cycle** for hydrogen production. State all three reactions and identify the highest-temperature step. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare **electrolysis**, **thermochemical cycles**, and **biological processes** for hydrogen production on the basis of efficiency, maturity, operating conditions, and suitability for automotive applications. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The S–I cycle is a three-step thermochemical water-splitting cycle developed at **General Atomics** and is currently the most mature thermochemical hydrogen production route (3 marks for overview).

**The three reactions (4 marks — 1 mark each for reaction + 1 mark for highest temperature identification):**

**Step 1 — Bunsen Reaction (exothermic, 20–120 °C):**

$$I_2 + SO_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2HI + H_2SO_4$$

This is the only exothermic step. It produces two immiscible acids (HI and H₂SO₄) which are easily separated.

**Step 2 — Sulfuric Acid Decomposition (endothermic, 800–900 °C, HIGHEST TEMPERATURE):**

$$H_2SO_4 \rightarrow SO_2 + H_2O + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2$$

The high-temperature step produces oxygen and regenerates SO₂ for recycle.

**Step 3 — Hydriodic Acid Decomposition (endothermic, 300–400 °C):**

$$2HI \rightarrow I_2 + H_2$$

This produces the desired hydrogen and regenerates I₂ for recycle.

**Net reaction:** $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$ ✓

> **Note:** The highest-temperature step is **Step 2 (Sulfuric Acid Decomposition at ~830 °C)**, which determines the heat-source requirement. This is why the S–I cycle pairs naturally with **next-generation high-temperature nuclear reactors (HTRs)** or concentrated solar thermal systems.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Use a **structured table** for comparison (5 marks):

| Criterion | Electrolysis | Thermochemical Cycle | Biological |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy input** | Electricity (renewable/grid) | High-temperature heat (500–900 °C) | Sunlight + biomass |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | 9 (commercial) | 5–7 (pilot scale) | 3–4 (lab) |
| **Efficiency** | 60–85% | 40–55% | 1–10% |
| **Operating conditions** | 60–900 °C (depends on type) | High-T heat source required | Ambient, ambient P |
| **H₂ purity** | 99.99% (very high) | 99.9% (high) | Mixed with CO₂/CH₄ (low) |
| **Cost (USD/kg)** | 4–8 (green) | 5–10 (projected) | 2–5 (projected) |
| **Suitability for automotive** | **Most suitable** for FCEVs today | Promising long-term | Not yet practical |

**Concluding remarks (2 marks):**
For **near-term automotive deployment**, electrolysis (especially PEM and AEL) powered by renewable electricity is the only commercially viable route producing green hydrogen suitable for fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs). Thermochemical cycles hold long-term promise when paired with high-temperature nuclear or solar-thermal sources. Biological processes, while environmentally attractive, are not yet at a technology readiness level suitable for the automotive industry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +nF in Faraday's law:** Many students write $m = Q \cdot M / F$ (missing the $n$). This costs **1 full mark**.
> 2. **Confusing HHV and LHV:** Always check which heating value the question specifies. KTU usually uses **HHV = 141.86 MJ/kg** unless stated otherwise.
> 3. **Confusing Faraday efficiency with energy efficiency:** They are *different*. Faraday efficiency relates to charge used; energy efficiency relates to total electrical input vs. chemical output.
> 4. **In thermochemical cycle answers, students often forget the heat source.** Examiners expect a statement like *"requires heat from a high-temperature nuclear reactor or concentrated solar."*
> 5. **S–I cycle reaction errors:** The Bunsen reaction coefficient (2H₂O) is frequently missed or wrong. Memorize all three reactions exactly.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before walking into the KTU exam hall, ensure you can confidently tick every box below:**

- [ ] Define hydrogen as an energy carrier (not a primary energy source).
- [ ] State the **overall electrolysis reaction** and the **standard theoretical voltage of 1.229 V**.
- [ ] State the **thermodynamic values** at 25 °C: $\Delta G^\circ = 237.2$ kJ/mol, $\Delta H^\circ = 285.8$ kJ/mol, $E_{tn} = 1.481$ V.
- [ ] Apply **Faraday's Law** in numerical problems: $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.
- [ ] State Faraday's constant: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
- [ ] List the **3 main types of electrolyzers**: Alkaline (AEL), PEM, and Solid Oxide (SOEC).
- [ ] Explain the **S–I thermochemical cycle** with all three reactions and the highest-temperature step (~830 °C).
- [ ] Name **two alternative thermochemical cycles**: Cu–Cl and Zn/ZnO.
- [ ] List **5 biological methods** of hydrogen production: direct biophotolysis, indirect biophotolysis, photo-fermentation, dark fermentation, and MEC.
- [ ] State the **dark fermentation stoichiometry** for glucose: $C_6H_{12}O_6 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 4H_2$.
- [ ] List the **3 colour codes of hydrogen** for automotive relevance: Grey, Blue, **Green** (KTU-favoured).
- [ ] Calculate the **energy efficiency** of an electrolyzer: $\eta = (m \cdot HHV)/(V \cdot I \cdot t) \times 100\%$.
- [ ] State the **HHV and LHV** of hydrogen: 141.86 MJ/kg and 119.96 MJ/kg respectively.
- [ ] State that **~9 kg of water** is required per kg of hydrogen produced (theoretical).
- [ ] Differentiate between **Faraday efficiency** and **energy efficiency**.

> **Final KTU Tip:** In long answers, always begin with a one-line **definition**, follow with a **labelled diagram**, write the **relevant equations**, solve with **step-by-step substitution**, and end with a **boxed final answer**. This 5-step structure is what consistently scores 13–14/14 in KTU Module 3 questions.

topics.title: Hydrogen storage and applications: challenges and methods for storing hydrogen fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydrogen storage and applications: challenges and methods for storing hydrogen fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Storage (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Hydrogen storage refers to the engineering methods and physical/chemical processes employed to contain molecular hydrogen (H₂) in a usable, safe, and energy-dense form suitable for onboard vehicular applications. The objective is to maintain sufficient **gravimetric density** (mass of H₂ per unit system mass) and **volumetric density** (mass of H₂ per unit system volume) to provide a driving range comparable to conventional gasoline vehicles (>500 km).

The strategic importance of hydrogen storage in automobiles stems from the fact that hydrogen possesses an exceptionally **high specific energy** (Lower Heating Value, **LHV ≈ 120 MJ/kg**) — nearly **3 times that of gasoline** — but an extremely **low ambient density** of just **0.0899 kg/m³** at STP. This dichotomy (high energy per kg, low energy per liter) is the central engineering challenge.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping (CO3):**
> Students must be able to critically evaluate hydrogen storage technologies based on gravimetric density, volumetric density, thermodynamic efficiency, refuelling time, and safety profile.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine hydrogen as **"the world's best marathon runner"** — incredibly light, fast, and full of energy, but **impossible to fit inside a small backpack**. At normal atmospheric pressure, to carry just 1 kg of hydrogen (enough to drive ~100 km in a fuel cell vehicle), you would need a volume of **~11 cubic meters** — the size of a small room!

So the challenge is essentially: *"How do we pack this super-light, super-active gas into a compact, safe, and affordable fuel tank for a car?"*

There are three philosophical approaches, like packing clothes for travel:
1. **Compression** — Like squeezing a winter jacket into a small compression bag (compressed gas at 350–700 bar).
2. **Liquefaction** — Like turning water into a tiny ice cube (cryogenic liquid H₂ at –253 °C).
3. **Chemical Binding** — Like trapping an ant in a sealed jar where it cannot escape (metal hydrides, chemical hydrides, adsorption).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants of Hydrogen (Bold for emphasis):**
> - Molecular weight: **2.016 g/mol**
> - Density at STP (0 °C, 1 atm): **0.0899 kg/m³**
> - Boiling point: **–252.87 °C (20.28 K)**
> - Critical temperature: **–240 °C (33 K)**
> - Critical pressure: **12.9 bar**
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV): **120 MJ/kg** (33.33 kWh/kg)
> - Higher Heating Value (HHV): **142 MJ/kg** (39.4 kWh/kg)
> - Flammability range in air: **4% to 75% by volume** (extremely wide!)
> - Auto-ignition temperature: **585 °C**
> - Minimum ignition energy: **0.02 mJ** (extremely low — 10x lower than gasoline)

The **US DOE (Department of Energy)** targets serve as the global benchmark for automotive hydrogen storage:

> [!NOTE]
> **DOE 2025 Targets for Onboard Hydrogen Storage:**
> - Gravimetric density: **5.5 wt%** (system basis)
> - Volumetric density: **40 g H₂/L** (system basis)
> - Operating temperature: **–40 °C to +85 °C**
> - Refuelling time: **3–5 minutes**
> - Cycle life: **1500 cycles**
> - Cost: **$8/kWh** (stored energy)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Fundamental Storage Challenge

Hydrogen's molecular structure creates a unique storage dilemma rooted in its physics:

### 2.1.1 The Joule-Thomson Inversion Issue
Unlike most gases, hydrogen exhibits a **negative Joule-Thomson coefficient at room temperature**. This means:

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Thermodynamic Fact:**
> At temperatures above its **inversion temperature (≈ –68 °C for H₂)**, hydrogen actually **HEATS UP upon expansion** rather than cooling. This is the *opposite* of nitrogen or air. Engineers must therefore actively cool hydrogen during refuelling to prevent tank overheating and structural failure.

### 2.1.2 Molecular Sieving & Embrittlement
H₂ molecules are the **smallest in existence** (kinetic diameter ≈ **2.89 Å**). They:
- Diffuse through virtually any seal or metal over time.
- Cause **hydrogen embrittlement** in steels (decohesion at grain boundaries, reducing ductility by up to 60%).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{P \cdot V \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T}$ | $P$: pressure, $V$: volume, $M$: molar mass (2.016 g/mol), $R_u$: universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K), $T$: temperature | Mass of H₂ stored in a compressed gas cylinder | kg |
| 2 | $\rho_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{V_{tank}}$ | Volumetric storage density | Comparing storage methods | kg/m³ or g/L |
| 3 | $\rho_{grav} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{system}} \times 100\%$ | Gravimetric storage density (wt%) | Measuring "lightness" of storage tank | % |
| 4 | $E_{stored} = m_{H_2} \times LHV$ | $LHV = 120$ MJ/kg for H₂ | Total usable chemical energy | MJ |
| 5 | $W_{comp,ideal} = R_u \cdot T \cdot \ln\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Isothermal compression work (per mole) | Minimum compressor energy | J/mol |
| 6 | $W_{comp,adiabatic} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} R_u T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Adiabatic compression (γ = 1.4 for H₂) | Real-world compressor sizing | J/mol |
| 7 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Liquefaction thermodynamic limit | Theoretical liquefaction efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta G^0 = -RT\ln K$ | Gibbs free energy of hydride formation | Predicting metal hydride stability | kJ/mol |
| 9 | $PCT: \ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{RT} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R}$ | Pressure-Composition-Temperature (van't Hoff) | Characterizing metal hydrides | P in atm |
| 10 | $E_{liq} \approx 0.4 \cdot LHV$ | Energy to liquefy H₂ ≈ 40% of its LHV | Comparing liquefaction losses | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Board Exam:**
> For Part B (14-mark) numerical problems, **Equation (1) and Equation (10)** are the most frequently tested. Always remember the universal gas constant $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) and $R_{H_2} = 4.124$ kJ/(kg·K).

## 2.3 Why Each Formula Matters in Real Engineering

- **Equation 1 (Ideal Gas)** is used to size high-pressure tanks. A 700-bar, 100-L tank stores only about **3.5–4 kg of H₂** at 15 °C, illustrating the volumetric challenge.
- **Equation 6 (Adiabatic Compression)** explains why hydrogen compressors need multi-stage intercooling — without it, exit temperatures can exceed **850 °C**, damaging seals.
- **Equation 9 (van't Hoff)** is the foundation of metal hydride selection. Lightweight hydrides like **MgH₂** have high capacity but require **>300 °C** to desorb, while **LaNi₅H₆** works near room temperature but stores only ~1.4 wt%.
- **Equation 10** reveals the brutal truth: **liquefying hydrogen consumes nearly 40% of its energy content** — making liquid H₂ a thermodynamic *penalty*, not a free lunch.

## 2.4 The Four Storage Paradigms (Decision Matrix)

| Storage Method | Volumetric Density (g/L) | Gravimetric (wt%) | Operating T (°C) | Refuel Time | Cycle Life | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 350-bar Compressed Gas | 24 | 3–4 | Ambient | 3–5 min | High | Commercial (Toyota Mirai) |
| 700-bar Compressed Gas | 40 | 4–5 | Ambient | 3–5 min | High | Commercial |
| Cryogenic Liquid (LH₂) | 70 | 5–10 | –253 | ~5 min | Boil-off loss | Aerospace |
| Metal Hydrides | 50–150 | 1–7 | 0–300 | 10–30 min | 500–2000 | R&D |
| Chemical Hydrides | 60–200 | 5–18 | Ambient–200 | Off-board regen | Limited | Early R&D |
| Adsorbents (MOFs/CNTs) | 20–50 | 1–10 | –196 to 25 | Slow | Lab-scale | Lab-scale |
