# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass of Hydrogen in a Compressed Gas Tank (Ideal Gas)

### Problem Context
A Toyota Mirai-class vehicle uses a Type IV hydrogen tank rated at **700 bar** and **122 L** internal volume. At the end of refuelling, the tank temperature rises to **55 °C (328 K)** due to rapid compression. Calculate the mass of H₂ stored.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify governing equation.** For a real high-pressure gas, the ideal gas law introduces <5% error up to ~100 bar but ~10–15% at 700 bar. Use the compressibility-corrected form:

$$m_{H_2} = \dfrac{P \cdot V \cdot M}{Z \cdot R_u \cdot T}$$

**Step 2 — Insert compressibility factor Z.** For H₂ at 700 bar and 328 K, NIST data gives **Z ≈ 1.45**.

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 700 \text{ bar} = 700 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 7.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \\
V &= 122 \text{ L} = 0.122 \text{ m}^3 \\
M &= 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol} \\
R_u &= 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \\
T &= 328 \text{ K} \\
Z &= 1.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the arithmetic.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \dfrac{(7.0 \times 10^7)(0.122)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.45)(8.314)(328)} \\
&= \dfrac{(7.0 \times 10^7)(0.122)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{3952.5} \\
&= \dfrac{17216.6}{3952.5} \\
&\approx 4.36 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.** This **4.36 kg** of H₂ yields approximately:

$$E_{stored} = 4.36 \times 120 \text{ MJ/kg} = 523 \text{ MJ} = 145.3 \text{ kWh}$$

In a fuel cell vehicle with **~50% efficiency**, this delivers ~72.6 kWh at the wheels — equivalent to a **500–550 km range**, matching the Toyota Mirai's EPA-rated **647 km range**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away:** At 700 bar, even a 122-L tank barely holds 4–5 kg H₂. This *volumetric limit* is precisely why research continues on metal hydrides and MOFs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Minimum Energy to Compress Hydrogen from 1 bar to 700 bar

### Problem Context
Compare the **ideal (isothermal)** vs. **real (adiabatic)** compression work per kg of H₂ for an isothermal reference at T = 288 K.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Isothermal (reversible, minimum work):**

$$W_{iso} = \dfrac{R_u \cdot T}{M} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Substituting $P_2/P_1 = 700$, $T = 288$ K, $M = 2.016 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{iso} &= \dfrac{(8.314)(288)}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \times \ln(700) \\
&= \dfrac{2394.4}{2.016 \times 10^{-3}} \times 6.551 \\
&= 1.1876 \times 10^6 \times 6.551 \\
&\approx 7.78 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg} = 7.78 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Adiabatic (real, no cooling, single stage):**

$$
W_{adiabatic} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot \dfrac{R_u T_1}{M} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]
$$

For H₂ (diatomic), $\gamma = 1.41$, so $(\gamma-1)/\gamma = 0.291$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{adiabatic} &= \dfrac{1.41}{0.41} \times 7.78 \times 10^6 \times \left[(700)^{0.291} - 1\right] \\
&= 3.439 \times 7.78 \times 10^6 \times [5.726 - 1] \\
&= 3.439 \times 7.78 \times 10^6 \times 4.726 \\
&\approx 1.265 \times 10^8 \text{ J/kg} = 126.5 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Surprising result:** The single-stage adiabatic compression work (**126.5 MJ/kg**) is actually **greater than the LHV of hydrogen (120 MJ/kg)!** This means without intercooling and multi-stage compression, you would expend *more* energy compressing the gas than you would later extract from it. This is why real compressors use 3–5 stages with intercooling — bringing total work down to ~10–13 MJ/kg.

**Step 3 — Realistic multi-stage isothermal approximation:**

$$W_{multi} \approx 1.3 \times W_{iso} = 1.3 \times 7.78 \approx 10.1 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

This is ~**8.4%** of the LHV — a real, non-trivial "compression tax."

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Liquefaction Energy Penalty

### Problem Context
Determine the theoretical minimum work to liquefy 1 kg of H₂ starting from 300 K, 1 bar.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Cool from 300 K to 20.28 K (the boiling point).** Using an average specific heat of $c_p \approx 14.3$ kJ/(kg·K) for H₂:

$$Q_{cool} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1 \times 14.3 \times (300 - 20.28) = 3996 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat of vaporization** of H₂ at 20.28 K:

$$Q_{latent} = 1 \times 445 \text{ kJ/kg} = 445 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Carnot limit** of a refrigeration cycle operating between 300 K and 20.28 K:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{Carnot} &= \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot} - T_{cold}} = \dfrac{20.28}{300 - 20.28} = 0.0725 \\
&\text{(i.e., coefficient of performance for refrigeration)} \\
W_{min,liquefy} &= \dfrac{Q_{cool} + Q_{latent}}{\eta_{Carnot}} \\
&= \dfrac{3996 + 445}{0.0725} = \dfrac{4441}{0.0725} \\
&\approx 61,255 \text{ kJ} = 61.3 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with LHV:**

$$\dfrac{W_{min,liquefy}}{LHV} = \dfrac{61.3}{120} = 51\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Even with a *perfect* Carnot refrigerator, liquefying hydrogen requires **51% of its energy content**. Real liquefiers achieve **35–45%** of LHV. This is why liquid H₂ storage is used primarily in aerospace (where boil-off can be vented) and not in mass-market automobiles.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Method Comparison

| Criterion | Compressed (700 bar) | Liquid LH₂ | Metal Hydride | MOF Adsorbent |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy penalty | ~10 MJ/kg | ~50 MJ/kg | ~5–8 MJ/kg | ~3–5 MJ/kg |
| Volumetric density | **40 g/L** | **70 g/L** | 50–150 g/L | 20–50 g/L |
| Gravimetric density | 4–5% | 5–10% | 1–7% | 1–10% |
| Boil-off/loss | Slow leak | **0.1–1%/day** | Self-discharge | None |
| Refuel time | **3 min** ✓ | 5 min | 15–30 min | 10+ min |
| Safety | Burst risk | Cryogenic burn | Pyrophoric risk | Generally safe |
| Commercial status | **Production** ✓ | Aerospace only | Prototype | Lab-scale |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q: List three major challenges in onboard hydrogen storage for automotive applications.**

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. **Low volumetric density** — At STP, hydrogen occupies ~3000× the volume of gasoline for the same energy, making the tank physically large.
2. **Hydrogen embrittlement** — H₂ molecules diffuse into metal lattices, causing cracking and fatigue failure of conventional steel tanks.
3. **High energy input for storage** — Compression to 700 bar or liquefaction at –253 °C consumes 10–40% of the stored energy.
4. *(Optional 4th point)* Wide flammability range (4–75%) and low ignition energy (0.02 mJ) create safety hazards.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between physisorption and chemisorption in hydrogen storage.**

**Model Answer (3 points):**

| Aspect | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| Binding | Weak van der Waals (5–10 kJ/mol) | Strong chemical bond (50–200 kJ/mol) |
| Operating T | Cryogenic (–196 °C) | Ambient to 300 °C |
| Mechanism | Surface adhesion | Bulk absorption + dissociation |
| Examples | MOFs, CNTs, activated carbon | Metal hydrides (MgH₂, LaNi₅H₆) |
| Reversibility | Easy | Requires heat + kinetics |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **compressed gas hydrogen storage system** used in modern fuel cell vehicles. Discuss the construction of Type IV tanks, pressure levels (350 bar vs 700 bar), and the role of the Pressure Regulating System (PRS).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vehicle uses two **Type IV hydrogen tanks**, each with internal volume **60 L**, pressurized to **700 bar** at **25 °C** after refuelling. Using the ideal gas law, calculate:
- (i) The mass of H₂ in one tank. [3 marks]
- (ii) The total energy stored (LHV = 120 MJ/kg). [2 marks]
- (iii) The driving range if the fuel cell drivetrain has an overall efficiency of 50% and the vehicle consumes 1 kWh per 6 km. [2 marks]

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Compressed Gas Storage (7 marks)**

Compressed gas storage is the **only commercially mature method** for automotive hydrogen. Modern FCVs (Toyota Mirai 2, Hyundai Nexo, Honda Clarity) use this technology.

**Construction of Type IV Tank:**
1. **Liner (inner shell):** High-density polyethylene (HDPE) — provides hydrogen permeation barrier and seals the gas.
2. **Carbon fiber overwrap:** Wound at high tension around the liner; provides ~95% of the burst strength (>700 bar × 2.25 safety factor ≈ 1575 bar).
3. **Glass fiber outer layer:** Adds impact protection and abrasion resistance.
4. **Metal boss:** Aluminum or steel end-fitting with integrated valve.

**Pressure Levels:**
- **350 bar (5000 psi):** Older generation (Honda Clarity 2008), gives ~3 kg H₂ in 100 L → ~300 km range.
- **700 bar (10,000 psi):** Modern standard (Mirai 2), gives ~5–6 kg H₂ in 120 L → ~650 km range. Stored density: **40 g/L**, gravimetric: 4–5 wt%.

**Pressure Regulating System (PRS):**
- **1st stage regulator:** Drops pressure from 700 bar to ~50–100 bar.
- **2nd stage regulator:** Drops to ~10 bar for the fuel cell stack inlet.
- **Heat exchangers:** Cool compressed gas to prevent overheating from throttling.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Mass of H₂ in one tank [3 marks]:**

Using ideal gas law (Z ≈ 1.45 at 700 bar):

$$m = \dfrac{PVM}{Z R_u T}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 700 \text{ bar} = 7.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \\
V &= 60 \text{ L} = 0.060 \text{ m}^3 \\
M &= 2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol} \\
R_u &= 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \\
T &= 298 \text{ K} \\
Z &= 1.45
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \dfrac{(7.0 \times 10^7)(0.060)(2.016 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.45)(8.314)(298)} \\
&= \dfrac{8467.2}{3591.0} \\
&\approx 2.36 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total energy stored (both tanks) [2 marks]:**

$$E = 2 \times m \times LHV = 2 \times 2.36 \times 120 = 566.4 \text{ MJ} = 157.3 \text{ kWh}$$

**(iii) Driving range [2 marks]:**

Usable energy at wheels = 157.3 kWh × 0.50 = **78.66 kWh**

$$\text{Range} = 78.66 \text{ kWh} \times 6 \text{ km/kWh} = 472 \text{ km}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 3 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **metal hydride method** of hydrogen storage in detail. Explain the working principle, examples of hydrides (MgH₂, LaNi₅H₆, NaAlH₄), the Pressure-Composition-Temperature (PCT) diagram, and the van't Hoff equation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A researcher proposes using **MgH₂** for onboard storage. The standard enthalpy of hydride formation is **ΔH = –74.5 kJ/mol H₂** and entropy change is **ΔS = –135 J/(mol·K)**.
- (i) Using the van't Hoff equation, calculate the desorption temperature at 1 bar. [4 marks]
- (ii) If the tank contains 5 kg of MgH₂ (molar mass = 26.3 g/mol), calculate the theoretical mass of H₂ released and the gravimetric capacity. [3 marks]

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Metal Hydride Storage (7 marks)**

**Working Principle:**
Metal hydrides store hydrogen via **reversible chemisorption**. Hydrogen molecules dissociate at the metal surface, and the H atoms diffuse into the metal lattice, forming a metallic hydride phase. The reaction is reversible with heat/pressure swing.

$$M + \dfrac{x}{2} H_2 \rightleftharpoons MH_x + \text{Heat}$$

**Examples of Hydrides:**

| Hydride | Capacity (wt%) | Operating T (°C) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| MgH₂ | 7.6 | 300–400 | Cheap, high capacity, slow kinetics |
| LaNi₅H₆ | 1.4 | 25–80 | Mature, used in submarines |
| NaAlH₄ | 5.6 | 150–250 | Needs Ti catalyst |
| PdH₀.₇ | 0.7 | 25 | Reference material, expensive |
| TiFeH₂ | 1.9 | 25 | Cheap, requires activation |

**PCT Diagram:**
- At constant T, as H₂ is added, pressure rises (α-phase, solid solution).
- At a plateau pressure (P_eq), H is absorbed into β-phase (hydride).
- Plateau length = storage capacity.
- At higher T, plateau pressure rises exponentially (van't Hoff).

**Van't Hoff Equation:**

$$\ln P_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R_u T} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R_u}$$

This equation governs the equilibrium between metal, hydrogen, and hydride.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Desorption temperature at 1 bar [4 marks]:**

At 1 bar, $P_{eq} = 1$ atm, so $\ln P_{eq} = 0$. The van't Hoff equation simplifies to:

$$0 = -\dfrac{\Delta H}{R_u T} + \dfrac{\Delta S}{R_u}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{\Delta S}{\Delta H}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S} \\
&= \dfrac{-74,500 \text{ J/mol}}{-135 \text{ J/(mol·K)}} \\
&\approx 552 \text{ K} = 279 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the *sign convention*. Both ΔH and ΔS are **negative** for an exothermic absorption reaction, but the **ratio is positive**, giving a valid absolute temperature. Always verify T > 0 K.

**(ii) Theoretical H₂ mass and capacity [3 marks]:**

Reaction: $MgH_2 \rightarrow Mg + H_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{MgH_2} &= \dfrac{5000 \text{ g}}{26.3 \text{ g/mol}} = 190.1 \text{ mol} \\
n_{H_2} &= n_{MgH_2} \times 1 = 190.1 \text{ mol} \\
m_{H_2} &= 190.1 \times 2.016 \text{ g} = 383.2 \text{ g} = 0.383 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Gravimetric capacity:

$$\rho_{grav} = \dfrac{m_{H_2}}{m_{MgH_2}} = \dfrac{0.383}{5.0} = 7.66\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This confirms the textbook figure of 7.6 wt% for MgH₂** — the highest among practical hydrides. However, real-world capacity is only ~4–5 wt% due to tank mass and unreacted material.

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that COST MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Z (compressibility factor):** At 700 bar, ideal gas law overestimates mass by **30–45%**. Always include Z for high-pressure hydrogen.
>
> 2. **Wrong unit for $R_u$:** The trap is mixing $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) with $R_{specific} = 4.124$ kJ/(kg·K). Using the wrong one gives 2000× errors.
>
> 3. **Sign error in van't Hoff:** ΔH for absorption is negative. Students write "T = ΔH/ΔS = positive" but forget that BOTH ΔH and ΔS are negative.
>
> 4. **Confusing wt% (gravimetric) with g/L (volumetric):** A storage method can have high wt% but low g/L (e.g., MgH₂ at 7.6 wt% but only ~50 g/L if packed densely).
>
> 5. **Ignoring boil-off losses for liquid H₂:** A 100 L LH₂ tank loses ~1–2%/day; over a 7-day idle period, you could lose 10% of fuel.
>
> 6. **Mixing LHV (120 MJ/kg) and HHV (142 MJ/kg):** Automotive FCVs use LHV. Always quote the right number unless specifically asked.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Hydrogen Storage**
>
> ✅ **The 4 storage paradigms:** Compressed gas, Liquid LH₂, Metal Hydrides, Adsorbents (MOFs/CNTs)
>
> ✅ **Commercial standard today:** 700-bar Type IV composite tank (Mirai, Nexo)
>
> ✅ **DOE 2025 targets:** 5.5 wt% gravimetric, 40 g/L volumetric, 3-min refuel
>
> ✅ **Key constants:** $\rho_{H_2, STP} = 0.0899$ kg/m³; LHV = 120 MJ/kg; T_boil = 20.28 K
>
> ✅ **Master equations:** Ideal gas with Z correction, van't Hoff, Carnot for liquefaction
>
> ✅ **Specific energy ranking:** H₂ (120) > Gasoline (44) > Methanol (20) > Li-ion battery (0.6 MJ/kg)
>
> ✅ **Volumetric ranking (system):** Liquid LH₂ (70) > MgH₂ (50–100) > 700-bar (40) > MOF (20–50) > 350-bar (24)
>
> ✅ **Key disadvantages:** Embrittlement, boil-off, low ignition energy (0.02 mJ), wide flammability (4–75%)
>
> ✅ **Future trends:** Cryo-compressed (700 bar + LH₂), MgH₂ nanocomposites, Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC)
>
> ✅ **KTU-specific must-knows:** Toyota Mirai architecture, US DOE targets, PCT diagram interpretation, van't Hoff calculation

---
