topics.title: Applications of hydrogen and engine modifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Applications of hydrogen and engine modifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Applications in Automobiles:** Hydrogen is the lightest and most energy-dense fuel by mass (LHV = **120 MJ/kg**) used in two primary automotive powertrains: (a) the **Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engine (H2-ICE)** — a near-drop-in modification of the conventional SI engine burning H2 in air, and (b) the **Hydrogen Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)** — which electrochemically combines H2 with O2 in a Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell stack to produce electricity for an electric drivetrain.

**Engine Modifications for Hydrogen:** The set of mechanical, thermodynamic, and material-design changes required to adapt a conventional gasoline/diesel spark-ignition engine to safely and efficiently burn gaseous hydrogen, addressing its unique combustion properties — wide flammability limits, high laminar flame speed, low minimum ignition energy, and susceptibility to pre-ignition / backfire.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Tricky-Fuel Analogy" 🚗💨**
> Think of a gasoline engine as a calm campfire — easy to light, burns slowly and predictably. Now imagine swapping that wood for **hydrogen** — a fuel that is more like a bunch of dry fireworks. It:
> - Ignites from the tiniest spark (a *static zap* can light it),
> - Burns **8× faster** (flame races through the cylinder),
> - Has a *huge* range of air-fuel mixtures that will burn (4% to 75% by volume in air!),
> - And is so light it leaks through any microscopic gap.

So if you simply pour H2 into a gasoline engine, you get violent pre-ignition, backfire through the intake, super-knock, and damaged pistons. **Engine modifications for hydrogen** are essentially the "taming" steps — changing the combustion chamber geometry, the fuel injection point, the compression ratio, and the materials — so that this enthusiastic fuel behaves in a controlled, efficient, and safe way inside the engine.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline (Reference) |
|----------|---------------|----------------------|
| Molecular weight | **2.016 g/mol** | ~100–105 g/mol |
| Density (STP) | **0.0899 kg/m³** | ~750 kg/m³ |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **119.96 MJ/kg** | ~44 MJ/kg |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **141.88 MJ/kg** | ~46 MJ/kg |
| Stoichiometric A/F (mass) | **34.3 : 1** | 14.7 : 1 |
| Flammability in air (vol %) | **4 – 75 %** | 1.0 – 7.6 % |
| Laminar flame speed | **2.5 – 3.5 m/s** | 0.4 m/s |
| Auto-ignition temperature | **858 K (585 °C)** | ~500 K |
| Min. ignition energy | **0.02 mJ** | 0.25 mJ |
| Quenching distance | **0.6 mm** | 2.0 mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** A single comparison table question fetches **3–5 marks**. Memorize the bold values above — they appear almost every cycle.

---

## 1.4 Why Hydrogen for Automobiles? — The Engineering Motivation

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Zero Carbon Emissions** at tailpipe → only H₂O exits the exhaust.
> 2. **Highest mass-energy density** of any fuel (~3× gasoline).
> 3. Can be produced from **renewable electricity** (green H₂ via electrolysis).
> 4. Can be used in **existing SI engine architecture** with modifications → lower transition cost than BEV.
> 5. Fast refuelling (~5 min) vs BEV charging (~30–60 min).

---

## 1.5 Categories of Hydrogen Use in Automobiles (Roadmap Figure)

| Pathway | Form | Conversion | Engine Type |
|---------|------|------------|-------------|
| H₂-ICE | Gaseous H₂ | Thermochemical combustion | Modified SI engine |
| HFCV (FCEV) | Gaseous H₂ | Electrochemical | PEM fuel cell + e-motor |
| HCNG | H₂ + CNG blend (15–30%) | Combustion | Standard CNG engine |
| Micro-gas-turbine | Gaseous H₂ | Continuous combustion | Turbo-shaft unit |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engine (H2-ICE) — Concept Decoded

> [!NOTE]
> **What it really is:** A conventional 4-stroke spark-ignition engine with a **modified cylinder head, fuel rail, ECU map, and lubrication system**, burning pressurised gaseous H₂ instead of petrol.

### 2.1.1 Why must the engine be modified? (The Five Drivers)

1. **High Flame Speed → Pre-ignition / Backfire**
   - H₂ flame moves 8× faster than gasoline → can race back into the intake manifold.
   - *Fix:* Direct Injection (DI) into cylinder near TDC, after IVC.

2. **Knock Resistance is HIGH (Octane ≈ 130+ RON equivalent)**
   - Allows *higher compression ratios* — but we actually *reduce* CR to control abnormal combustion.
   - *Fix:* Reduce CR from ~10.5:1 to **8.5:1 – 10:1**.

3. **Very Low Ignition Energy (0.02 mJ)**
   - Even hot deposits act as glow plugs.
   - *Fix:* Cooled exhaust valves, no hot-spot deposits, shielded spark plug.

4. **Wide Flammability Range (4 – 75 %)**
   - Engine tolerates ultra-lean operation (λ = 2 – 10), giving high efficiency + low NOₓ.
   - *Fix:* Calibrate ECU for lean-burn maps.

5. **Material Embrittlement by Hydrogen**
   - H₂ diffuses into steel grain boundaries → cracking.
   - *Fix:* Austenitic stainless steel valves, PTFE-coated seals, nickel-alloy pipes.

### 2.1.2 The Two Major H₂-ICE Configurations

| Type | Injection Point | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------|----------------|------------|---------------|
| **Port Injection (PFI)** | Intake port (before IVC) | Simple retrofit, lower cost, proven in BMW 7-series (H₂) | Prone to backfire, pre-ignition; pumping loss at part-load |
| **Direct Injection (DI)** | Inside cylinder, near TDC | No backfire, higher efficiency, more power density | High-pressure tank (350–700 bar), injector durability, more complex |

---

## 2.2 Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles (FCEV) — Concept Decoded

> [!NOTE]
> **What it really is:** A *battery-electric vehicle* whose "battery" is a hydrogen tank + fuel cell stack instead of Li-ion. The fuel cell electrochemically combines H₂ and O₂ to generate DC electricity that drives a traction motor.

### 2.2.1 Core Reaction in PEM Fuel Cell

$$ 2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat} $$

The system block:

$$\text{H}_2 \text{ tank} \rightarrow \text{Regulator} \rightarrow \text{Anode} \;\;\|\;\;\text{PEM} \;\;|\;\; \text{Cathode} \leftarrow \text{Air blower} \rightarrow \text{DC-DC} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Traction motor}$$

### 2.2.2 Fuel Cell vs H2-ICE — Why the world moved toward FCEV

| Parameter | H2-ICE | FCEV |
|-----------|--------|------|
| Net efficiency (tank-to-wheel) | 20–25 % | 50–60 % |
| NOₓ emission | Low (lean burn) | Near-zero |
| Water emission | Yes | Yes (pure, drinkable in some designs) |
| Vibration / noise | High | Whisper-quiet |
| Cost | Low retrofit | High (Pt catalyst) |
| Cold start | Easy | Harder (membrane hydration) |

---

## 2.3 Hydrogen Storage Methods (Integral to Application)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Three primary on-board storage methods — examiners ask this as a 3-mark definition or 7-mark essay.

1. **Compressed Gas (CGH₂)** — 350 bar (Type III) or **700 bar (Type IV)** carbon-fibre tanks. Energy density ~ 4.7 MJ/L.
2. **Liquid Hydrogen (LH₂)** — cryogenic at **−253 °C** (20 K). Energy density ~ 8.5 MJ/L, but boil-off losses.
3. **Metal Hydrides / LOHCs (Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers)** — chemical absorption in MgH₂, LaNi₅, or toluene/methylcyclohexane. Safer but heavier.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 10 equations for a 14-mark Part B question.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|----------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$ | Stoichiometric combustion reaction | mol basis |
| 2 | $(A/F)_{stoich,H_2} = \dfrac{8 \times M_{O_2}}{2 \times M_{H_2}} = \dfrac{8 \times 32}{2 \times 2.016} \approx 34.3$ | Mass A/F ratio | kg air / kg fuel |
| 3 | $(A/F)_{vol,H_2} = \dfrac{V_{air}}{V_{H_2}} \approx 2.4$ | Volumetric A/F | m³ air / m³ fuel |
| 4 | $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96 \; MJ/kg$ | Lower Heating Value | MJ/kg |
| 5 | $HHV_{H_2} = 141.88 \; MJ/kg$ | Higher Heating Value (water liquid) | MJ/kg |
| 6 | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto efficiency (ideal) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{im} = \dfrac{\eta_{th} \cdot LHV \cdot m_f \cdot N}{n_r \cdot 60}$ | Indicated mean power (per cylinder) | kW |
| 8 | $S_L \; (H_2) \approx 2.5-3.5 \; m/s$ | Laminar flame speed | m/s |
| 9 | $E_{min} \; (H_2) = 0.02 \; mJ$ | Minimum ignition energy | mJ |
| 10 | $r_{opt} \approx 8.5 - 10 : 1$ | Compression ratio (H2-ICE) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for A/F Ratio:** "Hydrogen is so *light* it needs **34 kg of air** to burn just 1 kg of itself." This explains why hydrogen engines are bulky in terms of intake.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **BMW Hydrogen 7 (2005–2008):** Modified V12 PFI H₂-ICE; 700 bar tank.
> 2. **Toyota Mirai (2014 – present):** FCEV, 5 kg H₂, 650 km range.
> 3. **Hyundai Nexo XCIENT:** Commercial FCEV trucks and SUVs.
> 4. **Mazda Skyactiv-H:** Hydrogen rotary engine (Wankel) — a niche KTU-favourite topic.
> 5. **Bharat Hydrogen Mission (India, 2023):** Targets 5 MMT green H₂ by 2030; FCEV buses plying in Delhi, Kochi, Bhubaneswar.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Hydrogen Combustion

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Prove that 1 kg of H₂ needs 34.3 kg of air to burn completely.

### Step 1 — Write the Balanced Combustion Equation

$$ 2 H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2 H_2O $$

**Explanation:** 4 H atoms + 2 O atoms on the left give 4 H + 2 O on the right. The equation is already balanced.

### Step 2 — Convert to Mass Basis

$$ \text{Mass of } 2 H_2 = 2 \times 2.016 = 4.032 \; g $$

$$ \text{Mass of } O_2 = 1 \times 32 = 32 \; g $$

**Explanation:** Molar mass of H₂ is 2.016 g/mol, of O₂ is 32 g/mol.

### Step 3 — Compute Mass Ratio of O₂ to H₂

$$ \frac{m_{O_2}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{32}{4.032} = 7.937 $$

**Explanation:** 1 g of H₂ needs 7.937 g of pure O₂.

### Step 4 — Account for Air Composition (O₂ + 3.76 N₂ by mole)

$$ \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{m_{H_2}} \times \left( 1 + 3.76 \times \frac{M_{N_2}}{M_{O_2}} \right) $$

$$ \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = 7.937 \times \left( 1 + 3.76 \times \frac{28.014}{32} \right) $$

$$ \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = 7.937 \times (1 + 3.2917) $$

$$ \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = 7.937 \times 4.2917 $$

$$ \frac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = 34.06 \approx 34.3 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $\boxed{(A/F)_{stoich, H_2} \approx 34.3 : 1}$ — a "textbook-verified" classical result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Released per kg of Hydrogen

### Step 1 — Enthalpy of Formation of H₂O (g, vapour) at 298 K

$$ \Delta H_{f}^\circ (H_2O,g) = -241.82 \; kJ/mol $$

### Step 2 — Per kg of H₂

$$ \text{Moles of } H_2 \; \text{in 1 kg} = \frac{1000 \; g}{2.016 \; g/mol} = 496.03 \; mol $$

### Step 3 — Combustion Releases ½ mol H₂O per ½ mol H₂

$$ \text{Moles of } H_2O \; \text{formed} = 496.03 \; mol $$

### Step 4 — Total Energy Released

$$ E = 496.03 \; mol \times 241.82 \; kJ/mol = 119.96 \times 10^3 \; kJ $$

$$ E = 119.96 \; MJ \; \text{per kg of } H_2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \boxed{LHV_{H_2} \approx 120 \; MJ/kg}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Why Compression Ratio is Reduced in H2-ICE

### Step 1 — Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency

$$ \eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} $$

### Step 2 — For Gasoline, r ≈ 10.5, γ = 1.4

$$ \eta_{Otto,gasoline} = 1 - \frac{1}{10.5^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.607} = 1 - 0.3835 = 0.6165 $$

**Result:** $\eta \approx 61.65 \%$

### Step 3 — For Hydrogen ICE, r ≈ 9.0, γ effective ≈ 1.40

$$ \eta_{Otto,H_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.408} = 1 - 0.4153 = 0.5847 $$

**Result:** $\eta \approx 58.47 \%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why reduce CR?** Although H₂'s high octane rating *allows* high CR, real engines use a moderate CR (~9:1) because:
> - The very low ignition energy of H₂ causes **pre-ignition** from hot spots at high CR.
> - Reducing CR also reduces **peak cylinder temperature**, which suppresses NOₓ.
> - Power density is restored by high-energy ignition and direct injection.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Why Hydrogen Has 3× the Energy per kg vs Gasoline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Side-by-side numerical proof** — a favourite 7-mark question.

| Fuel | LHV (MJ/kg) | Energy in 1 kg fuel | Litres needed for 100 km (typical ICE) |
|------|-------------|---------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Gasoline | 44 | 44 MJ | 7 L |
| Hydrogen | 120 | 120 MJ | ~3.4 kg (at 35% engine efficiency) |

$$ \text{Energy ratio} = \frac{LHV_{H_2}}{LHV_{gasoline}} = \frac{120}{44} = 2.727 \approx 2.7 $$

**Hydrogen delivers ~2.7× the energy per kg**, BUT it occupies ~3× the volume of a gasoline tank at 700 bar — a critical packaging problem.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Component-wise Engine Modifications for Hydrogen

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a high-yield "matrix" — examiners expect it as a 7-mark short essay.**

| Subsystem | Original (Petrol) | Modified (Hydrogen) | Reason |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|--------|
| Compression ratio | 9.5 – 11.5 : 1 | 8.5 – 10 : 1 | Avoid pre-ignition from hot spots |
| Combustion chamber | Hemispherical or bathtub | **Pent-roof with centrally located spark plug** | Shorter flame travel for H₂'s high flame speed |
| Spark plug | Standard | **High-energy (60–80 mJ) + iridium tip** | Reliable lean-burn ignition |
| Fuel injector | Port (liquid) | **Port (PFI) or Direct (DI)** | Prevent backfire with DI |
| Intake manifold | Aluminium | **Heated manifold to vaporise any H₂ pockets** | Avoid pre-ignition in pipes |
| Valves / seats | Hardened steel | **Austenitic SS / Inconel** | Hydrogen embrittlement resistance |
| Lubricant | Standard | **Low-sulphur synthetic (ash-less)** | H₂ attacks oil additives |
| ECU map | Stoich calibration | **Wide λ range (1 → 10)** maps | Lean burn operating |
| Exhaust | Std catalytic conv. | **NOₓ trap (3-way) + lambda sensor × 2** | Lean burn → high NOₓ if not controlled |
| Cooling | Water | **Enhanced water + oil cooler** | Higher heat rejection in H₂ flame |
| Safety | Std | **H₂ sensors, leak detectors, TPRD** | Hydrogen leakage detection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engine (H2-ICE) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[H2 Tank 350 or 700 bar] --> R1[Pressure Regulator]
    R1 --> I1[Pressure Regulator Stage 2 10 bar]
    I1 --> IJ[H2 Injector PFI or DI]
    IJ --> CC[Combustion Chamber with Pent Roof Geometry]
    SP1[High Energy Ignition Coil 60 to 80 mJ] --> SP2[Spark Plug Iridium Tip]
    SP2 --> CC
    CC --> EX[Exhaust Manifold with NOx Trap]
    EX --> CAT[Three Way Catalyst]
    CAT --> MUFF[Muffler with H2 Sensor]
    AIR[Air Intake] --> TB[Throttle Body Electronic]
    TB --> IM[Heated Intake Manifold]
    IM --> CC
    CC --> CRANK[Crankshaft Power Output]
    ECU[Engine Control Unit with Lambda Map] -.-> IJ
    ECU -.-> SP1
    ECU -.-> TB
    SENSE[H2 Leak Sensor + TPRD] -.-> ECU
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCEV) Powertrain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HT[H2 Tank 700 bar Type IV] --> R2[Pressure Reducer to 20 bar]
    R2 --> ANO[Anode of PEM Fuel Cell Stack]
    AIR2[Atmospheric Air] --> CMP[Air Compressor]
    CMP --> HUM[Humidifier]
    HUM --> CAT[Cathode of PEM Fuel Cell Stack]
    ANO --> STACK[PEM Fuel Cell Stack 300 to 400 V DC]
    CAT --> STACK
    STACK --> H2OPUR[Water Vapor Exhaust Pure H2O]
    STACK --> INV[DC to DC Converter then Inverter]
    INV --> MOT[Three Phase Traction Motor]
    MOT --> WH[Wheels via Single Speed Gearbox]
    BAT[Li ion Buffer Battery] --> INV
    INV -.charges.-> BAT
    STACK -.charges.-> BAT
    CTRL[VCU Vehicle Control Unit] -.-> INV
    CTRL -.-> MOT
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing H2-ICE vs FCEV for an Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Need H2 Powertrain] --> B{Priority?}
    B -->|Low cost, simple retrofit| C[Hydrogen ICE H2-ICE]
    B -->|High efficiency, zero NOX| D[Fuel Cell EV FCEV]
    C --> C1{Power density required?}
    C1 -->|High, gt 100 kW| C2[Direct Injection DI H2-ICE]
    C1 -->|Moderate, lt 100 kW| C3[Port Injection PFI H2-ICE]
    D --> D1{Range required?}
    D1 -->|gt 500 km, frequent cycle| D2[700 bar Type IV Tank + PEMFC]
    D1 -->|lt 500 km, city bus| D3[350 bar Type III Tank + PEMFC]
    C2 --> END1[Selected H2-ICE DI Architecture]
    C3 --> END2[Selected H2-ICE PFI Architecture]
    D2 --> END3[Selected FCEV Long Range]
    D3 --> END4[Selected FCEV Short Range]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic — Hydrogen Storage Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Compressed Gas CGH2] --> S1A[350 bar Type III]
    S1A --> S1A1[Aluminium Liner Carbon Wrap]
    S1 --> S1B[700 bar Type IV]
    S1B --> S1B1[Polymer Liner Carbon Wrap]
    S2[Liquid Hydrogen LH2] --> S2A[Cryogenic at minus 253 C]
    S2A --> S2A1[Vacuum Insulated Tank]
    S3[Metal Hydride] --> S3A[LaNi5 or MgH2]
    S3A --> S3A1[Adsorption in Metal Lattice]
    S4[LOHC] --> S4A[Toluene to Methylcyclohexane]
    S4A --> S4A1[Liquid at Ambient Conditions]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "draw a schematic", a clear block-flow diagram (as above) carrying component names + key temperatures/pressures fetches full marks — even if you cannot draw cylinders and pistons perfectly.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A (3 marks each):** Direct conceptual / definition / short-derivation.
> - **Part B (14 marks each, internal choice):** Two-part essay with (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Q:** List any three advantages and any three disadvantages of using hydrogen as an automotive fuel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|-----------|---------------|
| 1. Highest mass-energy density (LHV ≈ 120 MJ/kg) — ~2.7× gasoline. | 1. On-board storage is bulky and heavy (350/700 bar tanks). |
| 2. Zero CO₂ / CO / HC tailpipe emissions — only H₂O. | 2. Hydrogen embrittlement damages metals over time. |
| 3. Wide flammability range (4–75 % in air) — supports ultra-lean burn. | 3. Very low ignition energy (0.02 mJ) → risk of pre-ignition / backfire. |

> Scoring: 1 mark per pair of points (max 3); one each from each column for completeness.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Q:** Define "Lower Heating Value" of hydrogen. Why is LHV used in engine analysis instead of HHV?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The Lower Heating Value (LHV) of hydrogen is the heat released when 1 kg of H₂ is completely combusted with stoichiometric air at 25 °C and the **water product is in the vapour phase**. $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96 \; MJ/kg$.

- **Reason (2 marks):** In an internal combustion engine, the cylinder temperature is always above 100 °C, so the water produced exits as steam (latent heat is *not* recovered). Therefore the *useful* energy per kg is the LHV, not the HHV (141.88 MJ/kg). Using HHV would over-estimate the energy available for work.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> **Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **Hydrogen Internal Combustion Engine (H2-ICE)** using **port fuel injection**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **five major engine modifications** required when converting a conventional petrol SI engine to run on hydrogen, with reasons. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Working of PFI H₂-ICE:**

1. **Pressurised H₂ storage (1 mark):** Hydrogen is stored in a 350 bar (or 700 bar) carbon-fibre-wrapped tank. A first-stage regulator reduces pressure to ~10 bar.

2. **Port injection (1 mark):** The ECU actuates a solenoid/piezo injector that injects gaseous H₂ into the **intake port**, just upstream of the **intake valve**. The H₂ mixes with intake air before the intake valve opens.

3. **Induction (1 mark):** The intake valve opens (IVO before TDC), drawing the H₂–air mixture into the cylinder. Mixing during induction may cause pre-ignition if any hot spot exists, hence a **heated intake** is used to vaporise any H₂ pockets.

4. **Compression (1 mark):** The mixture is compressed to a moderate CR of **8.5–10 : 1** (lower than petrol to avoid knock and pre-ignition).

5. **Combustion (1 mark):** A high-energy spark (60–80 mJ) from an iridium-tipped spark plug initiates combustion. The wide flammability range allows ultra-lean operation (λ up to 4–5), boosting efficiency and reducing NOₓ.

6. **Power & Exhaust (1 mark):** Combustion raises pressure, pushing the piston down (power stroke). Exhaust valves open, expelling H₂O + trace NOₓ through a NOₓ trap and 3-way catalyst.

7. **Block diagram (1 mark):** (Show components: Tank → Regulator → Injector → Intake port → Cylinder → Spark plug → ECU feedback loop.)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Five major modifications (1 mark each + reason):**

1. **Compression ratio reduced to 8.5–10:1** — H₂ has very low ignition energy; high CR creates hot spots that cause pre-ignition and backfire.

2. **High-energy ignition system (60–80 mJ)** with iridium plug — H₂'s low min. ignition energy and need for reliable lean-burn ignition require energetic, durable sparks.

3. **Combustion chamber re-shaped to pent-roof with centrally located spark plug** — H₂ burns 8× faster; a compact, centrally-initiated flame reaches all corners before the cycle ends, avoiding wall-quenching losses.

4. **Direct injection (or heated PFI manifold) instead of standard PFI** — to eliminate backfire caused by H₂ mixture being ignited in the intake manifold during overlap period.

5. **Material upgrades — austenitic stainless steel valves, Inconel exhaust, PTFE seals, low-sulphur lubricant** — to prevent **hydrogen embrittlement**, a phenomenon where atomic H₂ diffuses into metals and causes micro-cracking.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls (Valuation Warning):**
> 1. Writing "modifications for LPG" instead of hydrogen — examiners deduct 2–3 marks.
> 2. Forgetting the **ECU/lambda map** modification — for H₂ the engine operates lean, so calibration must change.
> 3. Confusing **pre-ignition** with **knock** — H₂ engines suffer *pre-ignition*, not classical knock, because of low ignition energy, *not* auto-ignition delay.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> **Q: (a)** Describe the working principle of a **PEM fuel cell** for automotive use and list its main components. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **H₂-ICE** and **FCEV** in terms of efficiency, emissions, cost, cold-start ability and refuelling time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Working Principle of PEM Fuel Cell (4 marks):**
- Hydrogen gas is supplied to the **anode**; air (O₂) to the **cathode**.
- At the anode, a Pt catalyst splits H₂ into 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation, half-reaction: $H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$).
- **Protons (H⁺)** pass through the **Proton Exchange Membrane** (e.g., Nafion) to the cathode.
- **Electrons** cannot pass through the membrane → they take the **external circuit**, producing DC current.
- At the cathode, $4H^+ + 4e^- + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$ (reduction) — water + heat are the only by-products.

**Main Components (3 marks — 1 each):**
1. **PEM membrane** (Nafion) — proton conductor, electron insulator.
2. **Platinum catalyst layers** (anode + cathode) — accelerate reactions.
3. **Bipolar graphite/metal plates** — distribute gases, collect current, remove water.
4. **Air compressor + humidifier** — supply and moisten cathode air.
5. **Hydrogen recirculation pump** — reuses unreacted H₂.
6. **Cooling plate** — removes ~50 % waste heat.
7. **Stack** — series of 300–400 individual cells giving 300–400 V DC.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (5 marks — 1 mark per parameter):**

| Parameter | H₂-ICE | FCEV |
|-----------|--------|------|
| Tank-to-wheel efficiency | 20–25 % | 50–60 % |
| Tailpipe emissions | H₂O + low NOₓ | H₂O (pure) only |
| Vehicle cost | Lower (retrofit) | Higher (Pt + stack) |
| Cold start | Easy (mechanical) | Harder (membrane hydration) |
| Refuelling time | 3–5 min | 3–5 min (similar) |
| Range | 300–400 km | 500–650 km (Mirai) |
| Durability of system | Mature | Improving (Pt degradation) |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** FCEV is the long-term zero-emission solution for heavy duty / long-range applications; H₂-ICE is a useful bridging technology in the transition period.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing CR change direction:** Some students *increase* CR for H₂. Wrong — CR is **decreased** to control pre-ignition.
> 2. **Mixing up LHV and HHV values** for H₂: 120 vs 142 MJ/kg. Examiners notice immediately.
> 3. **Writing "H₂-ICE produces no emissions"** — *incorrect*. It produces NOₓ (due to high flame temperature) and is suppressed with a 3-way catalyst + lean burn. Only the *carbon* emissions are zero.
> 4. **Forgetting hydrogen embrittlement** as a *material* challenge — examiners expect at least one full line on this.
> 5. **Ignoring the safety dimension** — H₂ sensors, TPRD (Thermally-activated Pressure Relief Device), flame arrestors, vent lines. Listing at least two safety features fetches extra marks.
> 6. **Drawing only the H₂-ICE combustion chamber and missing the fuel system** — full marks require *system-level* answer, not just cylinder details.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision for the night before the exam:**

- [x] **Stoichiometric reaction:** $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$
- [x] **Stoichiometric A/F (mass) = 34.3 : 1** for H₂
- [x] **LHV = 120 MJ/kg**, HHV = 142 MJ/kg
- [x] **Flammability range 4 – 75 %** (vs gasoline 1 – 7.6 %)
- [x] **Laminar flame speed 2.5 – 3.5 m/s** (vs gasoline 0.4 m/s)
- [x] **Min. ignition energy 0.02 mJ** (vs gasoline 0.25 mJ)
- [x] **Auto-ignition temperature = 858 K (585 °C)**
- [x] **Compression ratio reduced to 8.5 – 10 : 1** in H₂-ICE
- [x] **Two configurations:** Port Injection (PFI) and Direct Injection (DI)
- [x] **FCEV** = PEM fuel cell + traction motor; efficiency **50–60 %**
- [x] **H₂-ICE** = Modified SI engine; efficiency **20–25 %**
- [x] **Storage:** CGH₂ (350/700 bar), LH₂ (−253 °C), Metal hydrides, LOHC
- [x] **Five major modifications:** reduced CR, high-energy ignition, pent-roof chamber, DI/PFI safety, upgraded materials (against embrittlement)
- [x] **Safety devices:** H₂ leak sensor, TPRD, flame arrestor, vent system
- [x] **Real-world examples:** BMW H₂ 7, Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo, Mazda Skyactiv-H (Wankel)
- [x] **Indian context:** National Green Hydrogen Mission (5 MMT by 2030), FCEV buses in Delhi/Kochi

> [!NOTE]
> **"If you memorise the A/F ratio (34.3), LHV (120), CR (9:1), and 5 engine modifications — you will fetch at least 60 % of marks on any question from this topic."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazards and safety systems: safety concerns and safety systems required
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hazards and safety systems: safety concerns and safety systems required ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Alternate Energy Sources for Automobiles**, *Hazards and Safety Systems* refers to the systematic identification, assessment, and mitigation of risks associated with non-conventional vehicular powertrains — including **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs)**, **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles (FCEVs)**, **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)** vehicles, **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)** vehicles, and **Biodiesel/Ethanol-fueled** vehicles. A hazard is defined as any inherent physical, chemical, electrical, or thermal source that has the potential to cause harm to humans, the vehicle, or the environment. The corresponding **safety systems** are the engineered hardware and software controls designed to detect, isolate, and neutralize these hazards in real time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 3):** Students must be able to identify the principal hazards of each alternate energy source (electrical shock, hydrogen leakage, gas cylinder rupture, battery thermal runaway, fire) and map them to their corresponding engineered safety systems (BMS, crash-disconnect, gas leak sensors, pressure relief devices, thermal management, and isolation monitoring). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker" Mental Model Imagine alternate-energy vehicles as a high-performance **pressure cooker** running through a busy kitchen (the road). Inside the cooker, you have an extremely energetic "ingredient" — it could be a charged lithium-ion cell (electrical pressure at 400 V), a hydrogen tank at **350 bar / 700 bar**, or a high-pressure CNG cylinder at **200 bar**. The "lid" of the cooker represents all the **safety systems**: a pressure-release valve (Pressure Relief Device), a temperature sensor (Thermal Management), an automatic gas shut-off (Crash Disconnect), and a smart controller (BMS or ECU). Just as a pressure cooker without a valve would explode, an EV battery pack without a Battery Management System would simply ignite. The hazards are the **stored energy**; the safety systems are the **controlled release pathways**. ### 1.3 Classification of Hazards in Alternate-Energy Automobiles | Hazard Category | Primary Energy Source | Typical Manifestation | |---|---|---| | **Electrical** | EV/HEV high-voltage bus | Shock, arc flash, short circuit | | **Electrochemical** | Li-ion battery | Thermal runaway, electrolyte leakage, off-gassing | | **Chemical** | Hydrogen, CNG, LPG, Biodiesel | Flammability, asphyxiation, toxicity | | **Mechanical** | Hydrogen/CNG tanks (350–700 bar) | Cylinder rupture, projectile risk | | **Thermal** | Battery, fuel cell stack, exhaust | Fire, burn injuries, component damage | | **Cryogenic** | Liquid H₂ vehicles | Frostbite, embrittlement of metals | | **Electromagnetic** | Wireless charging, HV cables | EMI to pacemakers/electronics | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Operating Voltage Hazard Threshold:** Any DC voltage above **60 V** or AC voltage above **30 V (rms)** is universally considered hazardous (per **IEC 60950 / IEC 61851** and **OSHA**). Most modern EVs operate at **400 V (Tesla Model 3, Nissan Leaf)** or **800 V (Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP)** architectures, which are therefore classified as **Class III hazardous voltage systems**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Detailed Hazard Analysis per Energy Source #### 2.1.1 Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) / Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) Hazards **1. Electrical Shock Hazard** - The traction battery (typically 300–800 V DC) remains energized even after the vehicle is turned off, because there is no natural "discharge" path. - Touching two points at different potentials, or one point and the chassis ground, can cause lethal current. - The human-body resistance model gives the let-go current as approximately **I = 10 mA** (AC) and **I = 30–40 mA** (DC). Currents above **100 mA** passing through the chest for > 0.2 s cause ventricular fibrillation. **2. Thermal Runaway** - Li-ion cells undergo an **exothermic chain reaction** once internal temperature exceeds approximately **80–120 °C** (depending on chemistry). - Reactions: SEI layer decomposition → anode-electrolyte reaction → cathode decomposition → electrolyte combustion. - Cell-to-cell propagation can engulf a full module within **seconds to minutes**, releasing toxic and flammable gases (HF, CO, CH₄, C₂H₄). **3. Post-Crash Re-energization** - After a collision, damaged HV cables can still carry full pack voltage, creating a "live wreck" scenario for first responders. **4. Stranded Energy** - A damaged battery still contains a huge amount of energy. Even a submerged EV battery can re-ignite days later. #### 2.1.2 Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle (FCEV) Hazards **1. Hydrogen Embrittlement & Leakage** - Hydrogen is the **smallest molecule**, so it leaks through the tiniest micro-cracks. - It is **14× lighter than air** → disperses upward quickly, but traps easily in enclosed garages, leading to deflagration risk. - **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) = 4 %** by volume in air; Upper Explosive Limit (UEL) = **75 %**. **2. High-Pressure Storage** - Type IV hydrogen tanks store H₂ at **700 bar (≈ 10,000 psi)**, with a service pressure of **875 bar** for the burst-safe design factor of **2.25**. **3. Cryogenic Burns (for LH₂ vehicles)** - Liquid hydrogen boils at **−253 °C (20 K)**. Skin contact causes instant frostbite; metals become brittle. **4. Hydrogen Flame — Nearly Invisible** - An H₂ flame radiates almost no visible light, making fire detection difficult. #### 2.1.3 CNG / LPG Vehicle Hazards - CNG stored at **200–250 bar**; LPG at **~16 bar** (or low-pressure 4–7 bar for automotive). - Gasoline replacement: the *octane rating* differs — CNG ≈ 120, LPG ≈ 110. - Major hazard: **uncontrolled release** → flame jet, BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion) for LPG. - LPG is **2× heavier than air** → pools at ground level in pits and garages (the opposite of hydrogen). #### 2.1.4 Biodiesel & Ethanol Hazards - Flash point: Biodiesel (B100) ≈ **130–170 °C** (safer than diesel); Ethanol ≈ **13 °C** (more volatile than gasoline). - Corrosion of natural rubber, certain plastics, and aluminium in older fuel systems. - Ethanol's **high latent heat of vaporization (≈ 904 kJ/kg)** makes cold-starting difficult and can cause fuel-line freezing. ### 2.2 Engineered Safety Systems (The "Defence-in-Depth" Layering) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Defence-in-Depth Principle:** KTU expects students to recognize that safety in alternate-energy vehicles is achieved through **multiple independent layers**: (1) Prevention → (2) Detection → (3) Isolation → (4) Mitigation → (5) Post-event protection. No single system is relied upon completely. **Layer 1 — Preventive Systems:** - High-quality cell manufacturing (defect screening) - Robust HV cable insulation (orange-coloured per ISO 6469) - Hydrogen tank composite overwrap (Type IV carbon-fibre wrapped polymer liner) - Positive Temperature Coefficient (PTC) heaters to prevent cold-soak damage **Layer 2 — Detection Systems:** - Battery Management System (BMS) with per-cell voltage and temperature monitoring - Hydrogen leak detectors (electrochemical or catalytic bead sensors, detection threshold ≈ 10–100 ppm) - Insulation Monitoring Devices (IMD) — detect leakage current to chassis - Smoke / gas sensors in battery enclosures **Layer 3 — Isolation Systems:** - Manual Service Disconnect (MSD) — orange, removable plug that physically breaks the HV loop - Pyrotechnic battery disconnect — fires a small charge to sever the HV cable in < 5 ms after crash signal - Contactors (high-voltage relays) controlled by BMS - Hydrogen tank **Thermal Pressure Relief Device (TPRD)** — vents gas safely in fire to prevent rupture **Layer 4 — Mitigation Systems:** - Active liquid-cooling/heating loops for batteries - Phase-Change Material (PCM) thermal buffers - Cell-level fuses / Positive Temperature Coefficient (PTC) current limiters - Hydrogen flame arrestors **Layer 5 — Post-Event Protection:** - First-Responder Information Stickers (per ISO 17840 / SAE J2990) - Residual energy discharge circuits (bleed resistors) for HV buses - Crash-structure "keep-out zones" for HV components - Crash-triggered automatic gas-shutoff valves ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Quantity | Typical Value | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Hazardous DC voltage threshold | $V_{DC} > 60\,\text{V}$ | **60 V** | Above this → Class III hazard (IEC 60950) | | Hazardous AC voltage threshold | $V_{AC,rms} > 30\,\text{V}$ | **30 V** | Sinusoidal AC limit (IEC 60950) | | Body current (let-go) DC | $I_{DC} \approx 30\text{–}40\,\text{mA}$ | 30 mA | Sustained muscle paralysis threshold | | Body current (let-go) AC | $I_{AC} \approx 10\,\text{mA}$ | 10 mA | AC is more dangerous at lower current | | Ventricular fibrillation threshold | $I_{VF} > 100\,\text{mA}$ for $t > 0.2\,\text{s}$ | 100 mA | Lethal current @ 50/60 Hz | | EV bus voltage (modern) | $V_{bus}$ | **400 V or 800 V** | Tesla, Hyundai, Porsche architectures | | Hydrogen storage pressure | $P_{H_2}$ | **350 bar / 700 bar** | SAE J2600 / ISO 14687 | | Hydrogen tank burst factor | $SF$ | **2.25** | Burst pressure = 2.25 × service pressure | | Hydrogen LEL / UEL | $\phi_{LEL}$, $\phi_{UEL}$ | **4 % / 75 %** | Flammability range in air | | CNG storage pressure | $P_{CNG}$ | **200 bar** | Standard automotive CNG | | LPG storage pressure | $P_{LPG}$ | **~16 bar** | Liquid phase at ambient T | | Biodiesel (B100) flash point | $T_{flash}$ | **130–170 °C** | Safer than mineral diesel (≈ 55 °C) | | Ethanol flash point | $T_{flash}$ | **13 °C** | Highly volatile | | Li-ion thermal-runaway onset | $T_{TR}$ | **80–120 °C** | Depends on cathode chemistry | | Insulation resistance (IMD) | $R_{iso} > 100\,\Omega/\text{V}$ | 100 Ω/V | Min. allowed HV-to-chassis resistance | | Pyrotechnic disconnect time | $t_{pyro}$ | **< 5 ms** | Time to clear HV fault after crash signal | > [!NOTE] > **Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD) Rule of Thumb:** For a 400 V EV pack, the IMD must trigger a fault when $R_{iso} < 100\,\Omega/\text{V} \times 400\,\text{V} = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$. This is the threshold where leakage current could become dangerous. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why a 400 V EV Bus is Electrically Lethal **Given:** - HV bus voltage: $V_{bus} = 400\,\text{V}$ (DC) - Human body resistance (wet skin, worst case): $R_{body} \approx 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ - Human body resistance (dry skin, typical): $R_{body} \approx 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ - Ventricular fibrillation threshold: $I_{VF} = 100\,\text{mA}$ for $t > 0.2\,\text{s}$ **To find:** Whether a person touching the live HV bus and chassis simultaneously will experience ventricular fibrillation. **Step 1 — Calculate the current through the body using Ohm's Law.** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{body} &= \frac{V_{bus}}{R_{body}} \\ I_{body, wet} &= \frac{400\,\text{V}}{1{,}000\,\Omega} = 0.400\,\text{A} = 400\,\text{mA} \\ I_{body, dry} &= \frac{400\,\text{V}}{10{,}000\,\Omega} = 0.040\,\text{A} = 40\,\text{mA} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compare with the fibrillation threshold.** For wet-skin contact, $I_{body, wet} = 400\,\text{mA} \gg 100\,\text{mA}$. This current is **4× above** the ventricular-fibrillation threshold, making the contact immediately lethal. For dry-skin contact, $I_{body, dry} = 40\,\text{mA}$ is at the let-go threshold. Although sub-fibrillation, it can still cause muscle tetanus (involuntary grip), burns at contact points, and secondary injuries from being thrown away from the vehicle. **Conclusion:** Even under the best dry-skin assumption, contact with a 400 V DC automotive bus is **always medically dangerous**. This is the engineering justification for orange HV cables, interlock loops, MSDs, and post-crash automatic disconnect systems. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydrogen Tank Wall Thickness Safety Margin **Given:** - Type IV hydrogen storage tank - Service pressure: $P_{svc} = 700\,\text{bar}$ - Burst design factor: $SF = 2.25$ - Allowable material stress: $\sigma_{allow} = 250\,\text{MPa}$ (typical for carbon-fibre composites) **To find:** The minimum theoretical burst pressure and safety margin. **Step 1 — Compute the minimum required burst pressure.** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{burst, min} &= SF \times P_{svc} \\ P_{burst, min} &= 2.25 \times 700\,\text{bar} \\ P_{burst, min} &= 1{,}575\,\text{bar} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the minimum required wall stress (thin-wall assumption).** Using the hoop-stress equation for a cylindrical pressure vessel: $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{hoop} &= \frac{P_{burst} \times r}{t_{wall}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for wall thickness (rearranged):** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{wall} &= \frac{P_{burst} \times r}{\sigma_{allow}} \\ t_{wall} &= \frac{1{,}575 \times 10^5\,\text{Pa} \times 0.15\,\text{m}}{250 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}} \\ t_{wall} &= 9.45 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \approx 9.5\,\text{mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** A typical Type IV 700-bar hydrogen tank requires a composite wall approximately **9–10 mm thick** to survive 2.25× over-pressure events, even at the material's nominal allowable stress. The actual manufactured thickness is larger to account for fatigue, impact damage, and chemical exposure. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD) Trip Threshold **Given:** - HV bus: $V_{bus} = 400\,\text{V}$ DC - Maximum tolerable body current: $I_{safe} = 2\,\text{mA}$ (per IEC 61851 for EV charging) - Body resistance: $R_{body} = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ (worst case wet) **To find:** The minimum insulation resistance that will keep leakage current below the safe limit. **Step 1 — Set up the leakage current equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{leak} &= \frac{V_{bus}}{R_{body} + R_{iso}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the safety condition $I_{leak} \leq I_{safe}$ and solve for $R_{iso}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{leak} &\leq I_{safe} \\ \frac{V_{bus}}{R_{body} + R_{iso}} &\leq I_{safe} \\ R_{body} + R_{iso} &\geq \frac{V_{bus}}{I_{safe}} \\ R_{iso} &\geq \frac{V_{bus}}{I_{safe}} - R_{body} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{iso} &\geq \frac{400\,\text{V}}{0.002\,\text{A}} - 1{,}000\,\Omega \\ R_{iso} &\geq 200{,}000\,\Omega - 1{,}000\,\Omega \\ R_{iso} &\geq 199{,}000\,\Omega \approx 200\,\text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Compare with the standard threshold:** Per IEC 61851, the IMD must alarm at $R_{iso} \leq 100\,\Omega/\text{V}$: $$ \begin{aligned} R_{iso, min} &= 100\,\Omega/\text{V} \times 400\,\text{V} = 40\,\text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The standard $R_{iso} \geq 40\,\text{k}\Omega$ threshold is **even stricter** than the $200\,\text{k}\Omega$ derived from the $2\,\text{mA}$ body-current limit, providing a **5× safety factor** for human protection. The IMD continuously injects a small AC test signal and measures the leakage impedance to trigger the contactor opening when $R_{iso}$ drops below this trip level. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Hazard vs. Required Safety System | Energy Source | Dominant Hazard | Detection System | Isolation / Mitigation System | Post-Event System | |---|---|---|---|---| | **BEV (Li-ion)** | Thermal runaway, electrical shock | BMS (per-cell V/T), IMD, smoke sensor | Pyrotechnic disconnect, contactors, MSD, cell fuses | Residual energy bleed-down, first-responder tags | | **Hydrogen (FCEV)** | Leakage, embrittlement, invisible flame | H₂ sensor (10 ppm threshold), TPRD | TPRD (thermal relief), auto-shutoff solenoid, flame arrestor | First-responder cut-off, vent paths | | **CNG / LPG** | High-pressure release, BLEVE | Pressure sensor, gas detector | Solenoid shutoff, excess-flow valve, TPRD | Crash-triggered gas cut-off, venting | | **Biodiesel** | Lower hazard (high flash pt.) | None mandatory | Material compatibility upgrades | Standard spill response | | **Ethanol** | High volatility, corrosion | Vapour sensor | Vapour recovery, alcohol-resistant seals | Standard fire response | | **Hybrid (HEV)** | Both HV + ICE hazards | BMS + OBD-II | Both EV and ICE safety combined | Specialized hybrid rescue sheets | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Defence-in-Depth Safety Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hazard Source
HV Battery or H2 Tank or CNG Cylinder] --> B[Layer 1 Prevention
Quality Insulation
Robust Tank Design] B --> C[Layer 2 Detection
BMS, IMD, H2 Sensor
Smoke Detector] C --> D{Fault Detected?} D -- No --> E[Normal Operation] D -- Yes --> F[Layer 3 Isolation
Pyrotechnic Disconnect
MSD, Contactor, TPRD] F --> G[Layer 4 Mitigation
Liquid Cooling Loop
PTC Fuses, Flame Arrestor] G --> H[Layer 5 Post Event
Bleed Resistor, First Responder Tag
Auto Gas Shutoff] H --> I[Vehicle Safe State] E --> J[Continuous Monitoring
Closed Loop Feedback to BMS] J --> C ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram of a Battery Management System (BMS) Safety Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR Pack[HV Battery Pack
400 V or 800 V] --> Vmon[Per Cell Voltage Monitor] Pack --> Tmon[Per Cell Temperature Monitor] Pack --> Cmon[Current Sensor Shunt] Vmon --> MCU[BMS Master MCU] Tmon --> MCU Cmon --> MCU MCU --> Logic{Safety Logic} Logic -- Overvoltage --> Alert1[Alert and Open Contactor] Logic -- Overtemp --> Alert2[Trigger Liquid Cooling Loop] Logic -- Overcurrent --> Alert3[Activate Pyrotechnic Fuse] Logic -- Isolation Fault --> Alert4[Trigger IMD Shutdown] Alert1 --> VehicleSafe[Vehicle Safe State] Alert2 --> VehicleSafe Alert3 --> VehicleSafe Alert4 --> VehicleSafe MCU --> CAN[CAN Bus to VCU
Driver Display] ``` ### 4.3 Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle Safety Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TD Tank[Type IV H2 Tank
700 bar] --> Valve1[Manual Tank Valve] Valve1 --> Valve2[Solenoid Shutoff Valve 1] Valve2 --> Valve3[Solenoid Shutoff Valve 2
Redundant] Valve3 --> Regulator[Pressure Regulator
700 bar to 10 bar] Regulator --> LeakDetect[H2 Leak Sensor
Threshold 10 ppm] LeakDetect --> ECU[FCEV Safety ECU] ECU --> TPRD[Thermal Pressure Relief Device] ECU --> FuelCell[Fuel Cell Stack] FuelCell --> Battery[HV Traction Battery] Battery --> Motor[Traction Motor] ECU --> Display[Driver HMI
Warning Lamp] ECU --> Telematics[Telematics Cloud
Emergency Dispatch] ``` ### 4.4 High-Voltage Interlock Loop (HVIL) Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[HV Battery Pack] --> B[Orange HV Cable] B --> C[Inverter] C --> D[HVIL Low Voltage Loop Wire] D --> E[MSD Manual Service Disconnect] E --> F[Onboard Charger] F --> G[HVIL Return Wire] G --> A Note1[HVIL: A low voltage signal flows through a separate wire loop
If any HV connector is unplugged, loop breaks
Contactor opens within milliseconds to de-energize HV bus] ``` ### 4.5 CNG / LPG Vehicle Safety System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD Cyl[CNG Cylinder
200 bar] --> Fill[Fill Valve with Check Valve] Fill --> AutoShut[Automatic Shutoff Solenoid] AutoShut --> PresReg[High Pressure Regulator
200 to 16 bar] PresReg --> Stage2[Secondary Regulator
16 to 4 bar] Stage2 --> Mixer[Gas Mixer Unit] Mixer --> Engine[Internal Combustion Engine] AutoShut -. Crash Signal .-> CrashSensor[Inertia Switch
5 to 8 g threshold] PresReg -. Over Pressure .-> TPRDCNG[Pressure Relief Device] CrashSensor -- Triggers --> AutoShut CrashSensor -- Triggers --> FuelCut[Fuel Pump Cutoff] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** *"List any three safety hazards associated with Battery Electric Vehicles and state the corresponding engineered safety system for each."* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per hazard-system pair):** 1. **Hazard:** High-voltage electrical shock from the 400 V DC traction bus. **Safety System:** Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD) plus Manual Service Disconnect (MSD). 2. **Hazard:** Thermal runaway of Li-ion cells above ≈ 80–120 °C. **Safety System:** Battery Management System (BMS) with active liquid-cooling loop and per-cell temperature monitoring. 3. **Hazard:** Post-crash re-energization and stranded energy. **Safety System:** Pyrotechnic battery disconnect, triggered by the airbag ECU within 5 ms of crash detection, plus residual energy bleed-down resistors. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** *"Explain why hydrogen vehicles require a Thermal Pressure Relief Device (TPRD) on each high-pressure tank."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Type IV hydrogen tank stores H₂ at **700 bar** (service pressure) with a burst design factor of **2.25**. In the event of an external fire, the heat absorbed by the tank wall rapidly increases the internal gas pressure. Without a controlled vent, the composite wrap would fail catastrophically and the tank could become a high-energy projectile. The **Thermal Pressure Relief Device (TPRD)** is a thermally-activated valve (typically set to activate between **110 °C and 150 °C**) that opens automatically and **vents the hydrogen safely away from the vehicle**. This controlled release prevents the tank from reaching its burst pressure, avoiding an explosive rupture. The TPRD must vent the gas in a direction that does not impinge on passengers, responders, or ignition sources — usually **upward and away from the cabin**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!** > In Part B questions on hazards & safety systems, students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** by: > (a) Listing hazards *without naming the corresponding safety device* (the question asks for a paired relationship). > (b) Confusing **MSD (Manual Service Disconnect)** with **MSD (Most Severe Discharge)** — they are different. > (c) Writing "hydrogen is dangerous" without quantifying the LEL (4 %) and UEL (75 %). > (d) Confusing **CNG (200 bar)** pressure with **LPG (16 bar)** pressure. > (e) Forgetting to mention **standards** (ISO 6469, IEC 61851, SAE J2990) which examiners reward with 1–2 marks. > (f) Not providing numerical examples when asked for "engineering justification". --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question, Module 3]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the function of the **Battery Management System (BMS)** in an Electric Vehicle. Explain how it provides four key safety functions: cell monitoring, thermal management, state-of-charge balancing, and fault isolation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 400 V EV battery pack supplies power to the traction motor. The Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD) must trip if the leakage current to the chassis exceeds 2 mA. The worst-case human body resistance is 1 kΩ. Calculate the minimum insulation resistance at which the IMD must trigger. Comment on the safety margin provided by the **IEC 61851** standard threshold of 100 Ω/V. **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** The **Battery Management System (BMS)** is the central electronic controller of the HV battery pack. Its four key safety functions are: 1. **Cell Monitoring [2 marks]:** The BMS uses an analog front-end (AFE) integrated circuit to measure the **voltage of every individual cell** (typically 96–108 cells in series for a 400 V pack) and the **temperature of multiple points per module**. Sampling rates are 10–100 Hz. Any cell drifting outside the safe window (e.g., 2.5–4.2 V for Li-NMC) generates an alert. 2. **Thermal Management [2 marks]:** The BMS drives the coolant pump and valves of the liquid-cooling loop, regulating battery temperature between 15 °C and 35 °C for optimal performance. It also activates PTC heaters in cold climates to prevent lithium plating during charging below 0 °C. 3. **State-of-Charge (SoC) Balancing [1.5 marks]:** The BMS equalizes cell voltages using **passive balancing** (bleed resistors) or **active balancing** (DC-DC converters). This prevents individual cells from over-charging or over-discharging, which are the leading triggers of thermal runaway. 4. **Fault Isolation [1.5 marks]:** On detecting over-voltage, under-voltage, over-temperature, over-current, or insulation failure, the BMS commands the **HV contactors to open**, severing the traction circuit. In a crash, it coordinates with the airbag ECU to fire the **pyrotechnic disconnect** within 5 ms. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** **Step 1 — Identify the safety condition.** The IMD must ensure that the leakage current through any person touching the live HV bus and chassis simultaneously does not exceed $I_{safe} = 2\,\text{mA}$. **Step 2 — Apply Ohm's law for the worst-case body resistance.** $$ \begin{aligned} I_{leak} &= \frac{V_{bus}}{R_{body} + R_{iso}} \leq I_{safe} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for the minimum required insulation resistance.** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{iso} &\geq \frac{V_{bus}}{I_{safe}} - R_{body} \\ R_{iso} &\geq \frac{400\,\text{V}}{0.002\,\text{A}} - 1{,}000\,\Omega \\ R_{iso} &\geq 200{,}000\,\Omega - 1{,}000\,\Omega \\ R_{iso} &\geq 199\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 200\,\text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ So the IMD must trip when $R_{iso}$ falls below approximately **200 kΩ**. **Step 4 — Compare with the IEC 61851 standard threshold.** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{iso, IEC} &= 100\,\Omega/\text{V} \times 400\,\text{V} = 40\,\text{k}\Omega \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Calculate the safety factor.** $$ \begin{aligned} SF &= \frac{R_{iso, calculated}}{R_{iso, IEC}} = \frac{200\,\text{k}\Omega}{40\,\text{k}\Omega} = 5 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion [1 mark]:** The IEC 61851 standard of 100 Ω/V provides a **5× safety margin** over the calculated minimum. The actual IMD trip is therefore set conservatively at 40 kΩ, ensuring the leakage current is always kept well below 2 mA. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question, Module 3]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the safety concerns of **Hydrogen (FCEV)** and **CNG** vehicles. Your answer must include storage pressure, leakage characteristics, flammability limits, and at least one safety system unique to each technology. **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **High-Voltage Interlock Loop (HVIL)** in an electric vehicle. Explain how it interacts with the **Manual Service Disconnect (MSD)** and the **pyrotechnic battery disconnect** to protect first responders. **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** | Parameter | Hydrogen (FCEV) | CNG | |---|---|---| | Storage pressure | **700 bar** (Type IV) | **200 bar** | | Density vs. air | 14× lighter → rises | 2× lighter → neutral buoyant | | LEL (Flammability) | **4 %** in air | **5 %** in air | | UEL | **75 %** | **15 %** | | Leak behaviour | Micro-leaks through cracks; invisible flame | Audible hiss; pools at fittings | | Special safety device | **Thermal Pressure Relief Device (TPRD)** | **Excess-Flow Valve + Inertia Crash Switch** | | Detection method | Electrochemical H₂ sensor (10 ppm) | Hydrocarbon / methane sniffer | | Standard | SAE J2600, ISO 14687 | ISO 11439 (CNG cylinders) | **Hydrogen-specific safety system [2 marks]:** The **TPRD** is a thermally-activated vent that opens at 110–150 °C to release H₂ safely upward during a fire, preventing tank rupture. **CNG-specific safety system [2 marks]:** The **Excess-Flow Valve** automatically closes when flow rate exceeds a safe threshold (indicating a major leak), and an **Inertia Crash Switch** (5–8 g threshold) cuts the solenoid valve on impact. **Conclusion [1 mark]:** Hydrogen has wider flammability range and higher storage pressure, demanding more rigorous composite tank design and thermal venting. CNG is lower-pressure but its methane content requires careful sealing and crash-triggered shutoff. **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** **High-Voltage Interlock Loop (HVIL) [3.5 marks]:** The HVIL is a **low-voltage (typically 5–12 V) signal loop** that runs through every HV connector in the vehicle. The loop begins at the BMS, threads through the battery connector, inverter connector, motor connector, on-board charger connector, and returns to the BMS. As long as the loop is closed, the BMS keeps the HV contactors closed and the high-voltage bus energized. If **any HV connector is unplugged**, or if the HVIL wire is cut, the loop opens. Within milliseconds, the BMS detects the open loop and **opens the HV contactors**, de-energizing the entire high-voltage bus. This prevents a technician or first responder from being exposed to 400 V or 800 V when opening service panels. **Manual Service Disconnect (MSD) [1.5 marks]:** The MSD is a **bright-orange, hand-removable plug** that physically breaks the HV battery's main series connection. When pulled out, the traction pack is split into two lower-voltage halves that are individually below the 60 V hazardous threshold. It is the primary tool for **first-responder de-energization** before extrication. **Pyrotechnic Battery Disconnect [2 marks]:** The pyrotechnic disconnect is a one-shot, explosive-fired cable cutter, similar in principle to an airbag inflator. It is triggered by the **airbag crash ECU** within **5 milliseconds** of a confirmed crash event (typically deceleration > 8 g and airbag deployment confirmed). The cutter **physically severs the HV cable** between the battery and the rest of the vehicle, eliminating the risk of post-crash arcing, re-energization, and stranded energy. **Integrated safety logic:** HVIL provides *preventive* day-to-day protection, MSD provides *manual service* protection, and the pyrotechnic disconnect provides *automatic crash* protection — together forming the **defence-in-depth** required by ISO 6469 and SAE J2990. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Revision Block — Memorize These Bullets Before the Exam** - ⚡ **Hazardous voltage threshold:** 60 V DC / 30 V AC (IEC 60950). Modern EVs (400 V / 800 V) are far above this. - 🔋 **Li-ion thermal runaway:** starts at **80–120 °C**; produces HF, CO, flammable gases; propagates cell-to-cell. - 🛡️ **Battery safety trio:** BMS + IMD + MSD. They are non-substitutable layers of defence. - 💥 **Pyrotechnic disconnect time:** **< 5 ms** after crash signal. - 🟠 **Orange HV cables:** ISO 6469 convention for all Class III wiring; never cut without disabling. - 💧 **Insulation resistance rule:** $R_{iso, min} = 100\,\Omega/\text{V}$ of bus voltage. - 🌀 **Hydrogen tanks:** **Type IV, 700 bar** service, **2.25×** burst safety factor, TPRD vents at **110–150 °C**. - 🔥 **Hydrogen flammability:** LEL **4 %**, UEL **75 %** — the widest range of any common fuel. - 👻 **Hydrogen flame:** nearly invisible in daylight — never use bare eyes to detect a leak. - 📐 **CNG vs LPG:** CNG ≈ **200 bar**, LPG ≈ **16 bar**; LPG pools (heavier than air), H₂ rises (lighter than air). - ⛽ **Biodiesel flash point:** **130–170 °C** (safer than mineral diesel at 55 °C). - 🍷 **Ethanol flash point:** **13 °C** (more volatile than petrol — high cold-start risk). - 🚨 **Inertia crash switch:** triggers at **5–8 g** deceleration to shut off CNG/LPG solenoid. - 🏷️ **First-responder information:** per **ISO 17840 / SAE J2990** — orange triangle, MSD location, battery cut-off. - 📏 **Human-body resistance:** 1 kΩ wet, 10 kΩ dry; fibrillation threshold = **100 mA for > 0.2 s**. - 📚 **Key standards to cite in answers:** **ISO 6469, IEC 61851, IEC 60950, SAE J2600, ISO 14687, SAE J2990, ISO 11439, ECE R134 (CNG/LPG)**. - 🧠 **Defence-in-Depth:** Prevention → Detection → Isolation → Mitigation → Post-Event. Always describe in this order for full marks. - 🔍 **HVIL rule:** any unplugged HV connector = contactors open automatically within milliseconds. - 🧊 **Liquid hydrogen:** boils at **−253 °C (20 K)**; cryogenic burn + metal embrittlement hazard. - ⚙️ **Active vs Passive balancing:** passive = resistor bleed (simpler, wastes energy); active = DC-DC transfer (efficient, complex). - 🧯 **EV submerged in water:** treat as still energized; do not touch HV components until fully discharged. --- **End of Notes — PEAUT411, Module 3, Topic: Hazards and Safety Systems** *Compiled per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*

topics.title: Performance and emissions: performance characteristics of hydrogen engines and emissions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance and emissions: performance characteristics of hydrogen engines and emissions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Characteristics of Hydrogen Engines** refer to the quantitative assessment of a hydrogen-fuelled Spark Ignition (SI) or Compression Ignition (CI) engine's output parameters — **Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)**, **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)**, **Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)**, and **Brake Power (BP)** — measured as functions of speed, load, and equivalence ratio, with simultaneous monitoring of regulated and unregulated exhaust emissions (NOx, unburnt H2, H2O, trace CO/HC).

Hydrogen (H₂) is the lightest, simplest, and most energy-dense fuel **per unit mass** (≈ **120 MJ/kg** Lower Heating Value), but it suffers from a poor **volumetric energy density** (≈ **8.5 MJ/m³** at NTP) — roughly one-third of gasoline. This dictates engine design strategies for hydrogen SI engines.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Skydiver and the Marathon Runner"

Imagine two fuels delivering the same total energy to an engine:

- **Gasoline** is like a **marathon runner** — packed with energy in a small, dense, slow-burning volume. It releases energy gradually over a long combustion duration.
- **Hydrogen** is like a **skydiver** — extremely light, occupies a huge volume, but burns almost instantly (laminar flame speed ≈ **2.5–3.5 m/s** for hydrogen vs. **0.4–0.5 m/s** for gasoline at stoichiometric conditions).

This "skydiver" nature gives hydrogen engines **superb thermal efficiency** (fast, near-constant-volume combustion) but creates two headaches: **abnormal combustion** (pre-ignition, backfire) because the fuel is so easily ignitable, and **NOx emissions** because the adiabatic flame temperature (≈ **2400 K**) is very high.

## 1.3 Why Hydrogen as an Engine Fuel? — Key Properties Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Favourite Table — Memorise These Numbers**

| Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline (C₈H₁₈) | Methane (CH₄) |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|----------------|
| Molecular Weight (kg/kmol) | **2.016** | ~114 | 16.04 |
| Lower Heating Value (MJ/kg) | **119.93** | 44 | 50 |
| Density at NTP (kg/m³) | **0.0838** | 740 | 0.72 |
| Flammability Limits (φ) | **0.14 – 10.0** | 0.4 – 1.4 | 0.5 – 1.7 |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature (K) | **858** | 530 | 813 |
| Min. Ignition Energy (mJ) | **0.02** | 0.25 | 0.29 |
| Laminar Flame Speed (m/s) | **2.5 – 3.5** | 0.4 – 0.5 | 0.4 |
| Quenching Distance (mm) | **0.6** | 2.0 | 2.1 |
| Octane Number | **130+** | 80–98 | 120 |
| Stoichiometric AFR (mass) | **34.3 : 1** | 14.7 : 1 | 17.2 : 1 |
| Adiabatic Flame Temp. (K) | **2400** | 2300 | 2225 |

## 1.4 Combustion Chemistry — Why Emissions Differ

The complete combustion equation for hydrogen is uniquely clean:

$$2H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Heat}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theoretical Consequence**: Because hydrogen contains **zero carbon atoms**, stoichiometric or rich hydrogen combustion produces **ZERO CO, ZERO CO₂, and ZERO unburnt hydrocarbons (HC)** in the ideal case. The only combustion products are **water vapour (H₂O)** and **heat**. This makes hydrogen the only fuel that can theoretically produce *zero carbon emissions* from the tailpipe.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydrogen Induction and Supply Methods

Hydrogen cannot be used in a conventional SI engine "as-is" because of its exceptionally wide flammability limits (φ = 0.14 – 10.0). A residual hot spot inside the combustion chamber can ignite the incoming charge *anywhere* in the suction stroke, causing **backfire** (combustion propagating back into the intake manifold).

To manage this, three hydrogen supply architectures exist:

### 2.1.1 (a) Carburetion / Port Injection (Most Common in SI Conversions)
- Hydrogen is metered through a pressure regulator → gas-mixer → intake manifold.
- **Advantage:** Simple, cheap retrofit.
- **Disadvantage:** Wide flammability range + high diffusivity (H₂ diffuses 3.8× faster than gasoline) → backfire risk high.

### 2.1.2 (b) Timed Port Injection (Liquid or Gaseous H₂)
- Injector fires hydrogen into the port **after the intake valve closes**, eliminating manifold residence.
- **Advantage:** Excellent backfire suppression; precise metering.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires cryogenic LH₂ or 350–700 bar GH₂ storage.

### 2.1.3 (c) Direct In-Cylinder Injection (High-Pressure DI)
- Hydrogen injected directly into the cylinder near TDC.
- **Advantage:** Best thermal efficiency, no backfire at all (inject only after IVC).
- **Disadvantage:** Requires very high pressure (~200 bar) and cryogenic-tolerant injectors; high cost.

### 2.1.4 (d) Hydrogen as a CI Engine Fuel — Dual-Fuel Mode
- Pure hydrogen CI is impractical (very high auto-ignition temperature, **858 K**).
- Used as a **pilot-ignited** supplemental fuel in a **diesel-hydrogen dual-fuel** engine.
- Diesel pilot (5–15 % energy) ignites → ignites hydrogen-air mixture.
- Hydrogen **substitutes** up to **70–80 %** of diesel energy.

## 2.2 Performance Characteristics — Qualitative Trends

### 2.2.1 Brake Thermal Efficiency (η_th)
- Hydrogen SI engines achieve **η_th ≈ 38–45 %** at best, vs. ~30 % for gasoline SI.
- **Why?** (i) Higher octane number permits higher compression ratios (CR = 13–15); (ii) faster flame speed → closer to Otto ideal cycle; (iii) no dissociation losses because no CO₂ dissociation equilibrium dominates.

### 2.2.2 Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) — Mass Basis vs. Energy Basis
- On a **mass basis**, hydrogen BSFC looks high (~75–100 g/kWh) because of low LHV per unit mass? No — actually because of low heating value density per kg requires *more* mass... wait — H₂ LHV = 120 MJ/kg, gasoline LHV = 44 MJ/kg. So H₂ has *higher* LHV per kg → BSFC in **g/kWh** is **2.5–3× lower** than gasoline.
- On a **volume basis**, hydrogen is at a severe disadvantage (energy density 1/3000 of gasoline at NTP), explaining why on-board storage is the primary engineering bottleneck.

### 2.2.3 Power Output Penalty
- A hydrogen-fuelled engine displacing the same volume as a gasoline engine typically produces **15–25 % less peak power** when inducted as a gas, because hydrogen displaces a large fraction of the intake air (volumetric efficiency drops).
- **Direct injection** or **supercharging** restores or exceeds gasoline power density.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations for KTU Numerical Problems**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}$ | dimensionless | $BP$ in kW, $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/s |
| 2 | BSFC | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP}$ | g/kWh | |
| 3 | BSEC (energy specific) | $BSEC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times LHV}{BP}$ | MJ/kWh | Comparative across fuels |
| 4 | Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $AFR_{st,H_2} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \mathbf{34.3:1}$ | – | Stoichiometric |
| 5 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = \dfrac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | – | φ > 1 rich, φ < 1 lean |
| 6 | Volumetric Efficiency (gasoline baseline) | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{act}}{\rho_{air} \times V_d \times N/2}$ | dimensionless | Drops ~20–25 % for port H₂ |
| 7 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | $IMEP = \dfrac{W_{cycle}}{V_d}$ | bar or kPa | |
| 8 | NOx Formation — Zeldovich | $\dfrac{d[NO]}{dt} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ [O][N₂] | – | Arrhenius, $E_a \approx 319$ kJ/mol |
| 9 | Adiabatic Flame Temp. | $T_{ad}$ from energy balance $\sum n_i h_i^{form} = \sum n_p (h_p - h_p^{ref})$ | K | Approx. **2400 K** stoich, **2270 K** lean |
| 10 | Energy Density Ratio (volumetric) | $\dfrac{LHV_{H_2}/\rho_{H_2}}{LHV_{gas}/\rho_{gas}} \approx 1/3000$ | – | At NTP gaseous H₂ |
| 11 | Hydrogen Diffusivity | $D_{H_2} \approx 3.8 \times D_{gasoline}$ | m²/s | Causes leakage/backfire |
| 12 | Hydrogen Lower Heating Value | $LHV = \mathbf{119.93\ MJ/kg}$ | – | Memorise! |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These performance equations are used in:
- **Engine Calibration Software** (AVL CRUISE, GT-SUITE) to model fleet emissions.
- **Hydrogen Refuelling Station** sizing — using BSFC and duty cycles.
- **Hybrid H₂/Battery Vehicle Design** — comparing hydrogen fuel cell vs. hydrogen ICE range and emissions.
- **Stationary Power Generation** — large H₂ engines (Wärtsilä, MAN) for grid balancing using renewable hydrogen.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio for Hydrogen

**Given:** Complete combustion of hydrogen in air:
$$2H_2 + O_2 + 3.76\,N_2 \longrightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76\,N_2$$

**Step 1 — Identify molecular masses**

$$M_{H_2} = 2 \times 1.008 = 2.016 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

$$M_{O_2} = 32 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

$$M_{N_2} = 28 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

$$M_{air} = 0.233 \times M_{O_2} + 0.767 \times M_{N_2} = 0.233(32) + 0.767(28)$$

$$M_{air} = 7.456 + 21.476 = 28.932 \text{ kg/kmol}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of fuel (H₂) per kmol of combustion**

From the stoichiometric equation, per kmol of O₂ we consume 2 kmol of H₂:

$$m_{fuel} = 2 \times M_{H_2} = 2 \times 2.016 = 4.032 \text{ kg of H}_2$$

**Step 3 — Mass of air per kmol of O₂**

$$m_{air} = 1 \times M_{O_2} + 3.76 \times M_{N_2} = 32 + 3.76(28)$$

$$m_{air} = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28 \text{ kg of air}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric AFR (mass basis)**

$$AFR_{st} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \dfrac{137.28}{4.032}$$

$$\boxed{AFR_{st,\,H_2} = 34.05 \approx \mathbf{34.3 : 1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** To burn 1 kg of hydrogen completely, you need 34.3 kg of air — more than **2.3× the air** required for gasoline (14.7:1). This is why hydrogen engines operate with very low equivalence ratios in practice.

## 3.2 Derivation: Brake Thermal Efficiency Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A hydrogen-fuelled 4-stroke SI engine delivers **BP = 45 kW** at 2400 rpm. It consumes **3.6 kg of hydrogen per hour**. Compute:
(i) Brake thermal efficiency (η_bth)
(ii) BSFC
(iii) BSEC (Brake Specific Energy Consumption)
(iv) Compare with gasoline (assume η_bth,gas = 30 % and BSFC = 280 g/kWh).

**Given:**
- $BP = 45$ kW
- $\dot{m}_f = 3.6 \text{ kg/hr} = 3.6 / 3600 = 0.001 \text{ kg/s}$
- $LHV_{H_2} = 119.93$ MJ/kg
- $LHV_{gas} = 44$ MJ/kg

### 3.2.1 (i) Brake Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \times LHV_{H_2}}$$

**Step A** — Compute fuel energy input rate:

$$\dot{m}_f \times LHV_{H_2} = 0.001 \text{ kg/s} \times 119.93 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg}$$

$$\dot{m}_f \times LHV_{H_2} = 119{,}930 \text{ W} = 119.93 \text{ kW}$$

**Step B** — Substitute:

$$\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{45}{119.93}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} = 0.3752 = 37.52\,\%}$$

### 3.2.2 (ii) BSFC

$$BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} \times 3600 \times 1000 \text{ (g/kWh)}$$

$$BSFC = \dfrac{3.6 \text{ kg/hr}}{45 \text{ kW}} = 0.08 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

$$\boxed{BSFC = 80 \text{ g/kWh}}$$

### 3.2.3 (iii) BSEC

$$BSEC = \dfrac{BSFC \times LHV_{H_2}}{1000} = \dfrac{80 \times 119.93}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{BSEC = 9.59 \text{ MJ/kWh}}$$

### 3.2.4 (iv) Comparison Verdict

| Parameter | Hydrogen | Gasoline (η=30 %) | Interpretation |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|----------------|
| η_bth | **37.5 %** | 30 % | H₂ is 25 % more efficient thermodynamically |
| BSFC | **80 g/kWh** | 280 g/kWh | H₂ is **3.5× lower** in mass terms |
| BSEC | **9.59 MJ/kWh** | 12.32 MJ/kWh | H₂ delivers same work with less chemical energy |

**Conclusion:** Despite needing a larger mass of fuel to deliver identical energy (because hydrogen mass-flow is actually *less* — 80 vs. 280 g), the **hydrogen engine uses its fuel energy 25 % more efficiently**. Combined with zero-carbon combustion, this is the dual driver of hydrogen engine research.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Emissions Profile of a Hydrogen Engine

| Pollutant | Source Mechanism | Magnitude vs. Gasoline | Mitigation Strategy |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **NOx** | Thermal Zeldovich (high $T_{ad}$ 2400 K) | **3–5× higher** at stoich; same/lower at very lean | Lean burn (φ = 0.5–0.7), EGR 10–20 %, water injection |
| **CO** | Dissociation at rich mixtures only | **0 at stoich/lean**; rises if rich | Stoichiometric calibration, lambda sensor |
| **HC (THC)** | Wall quenching, crevice volume | **Very low** (H₂ quenches at walls minimally but… ) | Reduce crevice; optimised spark timing |
| **CO₂** | None — carbon-free fuel | **0 g/km (TTW)** | Already solved by chemistry |
| **PM (Soot)** | No carbon in fuel | **0** | Already solved |
| **Unburnt H₂** | Crevices, valve overlap, blowby | 1–5 % of fuel input | Valve timing, optimised injection end |
| **H₂O (water vapour)** | Combustion product + greenhouse | Negligible GWP₁₀₀ = 0 | – |

## 3.4 Derivation: NOx Formation — The Zeldovich Mechanism

The extended Zeldovich mechanism (3 reactions) governs **thermal NOx**:

$$\text{(Z1)}\quad O + N_2 \longrightarrow NO + N$$

$$\text{(Z2)}\quad N + O_2 \longrightarrow NO + O$$

$$\text{(Z3)}\quad N + OH \longrightarrow NO + H$$

> [!NOTE]
> All three are endothermic, with **(Z1) being rate-limiting** requiring an activation energy of **$E_a \approx 319$ kJ/mol**. This is why NOx formation is **exponentially sensitive** to peak flame temperature.

The empirical correlation (Lavoie, Heywood):

$$\dot{m}_{NO} = A \cdot \phi^{\alpha} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT_{ad}}\right)$$

Where $\alpha$ is a fitted exponent (≈ 0.5–1.0 depending on engine).

**Quantitative Insight:** A **100 K increase** in adiabatic flame temperature **roughly doubles** the NOx emission rate. This is why even a 10 K drift in combustion quality matters — a key reason for **closed-loop lambda control** in hydrogen engines.

## 3.5 Reducing NOx: The Four-Lever Approach (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Lean Burn (φ = 0.4–0.6):** Reduces $T_{ad}$ from 2400 K → ~2100 K. NOx ↓ by 5–10×. **Power density** ↓ too.
2. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) of 10–20 %:** Inert gases dilute charge, lowering peak T. NOx ↓ 2–3×.
3. **Water Injection (direct or port):** Latent heat absorption drops $T_{ad}$. NOx ↓ 30–50 %.
4. **Retarded Ignition Timing + Miller/Atkinson Cycle:** Lower peak TDC pressures and temperatures. NOx ↓ 20–40 %.

## 3.6 Abnormal Combustion in Hydrogen Engines

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** You will be asked to differentiate these phenomena. Memorise the cause-effect:

| Phenomenon | Cause | Consequence | Solution |
|------------|-------|-------------|----------|
| **Backfire** | Inlet H₂ ignited by residual hot spot during intake stroke | Flame travels back through intake pipe → loud bang | Port or DI injection; cooler EGR; anti-reversion grooves |
| **Pre-ignition** | Hot deposits or glowing valve ignite charge *before* spark | Pressure rise before TDC → high P, loss of work | Eliminate hot spots; water injection; lean mix |
| **Knock (End-gas auto-ignition)** | End gas auto-ignites due to high T and pressure before flame arrival | Sudden sharp pressure oscillation; possible engine damage | Lean burn; high CR tolerated due to high octane; EGR |
| **Flash-back** | Flame kernel in port propagates into manifold | Pre-ignition-like damage, hard restart | Close-coupled injection; no upstream flame |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrogen SI Engine Functional Block Diagram (Port Injection Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2Tank["H2 STORAGE TANK 350 bar GH2"] --> Reg["PRESSURE REGULATOR"]
    Reg --> Mixer["GAS MIXER"]
    AirFilt["AIR FILTER AND SUPERCHARGER"] --> Mixer
    Mixer --> IntakeMan["INTAKE MANIFOLD"]
    IntakeMan --> IV["INTAKE VALVE"]
    IV --> Cyl["CYLINDER COMBUSTION"]
    Cyl --> EV["EXHAUST VALVE"]
    EV --> Catalyst["THREE WAY CATALYST NOx TRAP"]
    Catalyst --> Tail["TAILPIPE H2O VAPOUR"]
    ECU["ECU LAMBDA SENSOR"] -. feedback .-> Mixer
    Spark["SPARK PLUG HIGH ENERGY IGNITION"] --> Cyl
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the bold black arrows for the *energy and mass flow* (hydrogen + air + combustion). The dashed grey arrow from the **Lambda sensor** back to the **Mixer** indicates the **closed-loop air-fuel control** essential for managing NOx.

## 4.2 Hydrogen Combustion Pathway — Emission Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["H2 INJECTED INTO CHARGE"]
    Start --> Mix["AIR-FUEL MIXTURE FORMS IN CYLINDER"]
    Mix --> Compress["COMPRESSION STROKE T AND P RISE"]
    Compress --> Spark["SPARK IGNITION TAD APROX 2400 K"]
    Spark --> HotFlame["ADIABATIC FLAME TEMPERATURE ZONE"]
    HotFlame --> Zeldovich["ZELDOVICH MECHANISM N2 plus O EQUALS NO"]
    Zeldovich --> NOx["NOX EMISSION PRODUCTION"]
    HotFlame --> H2O["PRODUCTS H2O VAPOUR CLEAN"]
    H2O --> Tailpipe["TAILPIPE WATER VAPOUR ZERO CO2"]
    HotFlame --> Quench["WALL QUENCHING LAYER"]
    Quench --> UH2["UNBURNT H2 SLIP"]
    NOx --> Aftertreat["AFTERTREATMENT EGR WATER INJECTION LEAN BURN"]
    Aftertreat --> Clean["REDUCED NOX OUTPUT"]
```

## 4.3 Performance Curve Comparison — Hydrogen vs Gasoline vs Diesel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LoadCurve["BRAKE THERMAL EFFICIENCY VERSUS LOAD"]
        direction LR
        Idle["IDLE 5 percent LOAD"] --> L25["25 percent LOAD"]
        L25 --> L50["50 percent LOAD"]
        L50 --> L75["75 percent LOAD"]
        L75 --> Full["FULL LOAD 100 percent"]
    end
    H2Curve["H2 SI 38 to 45 percent ETA BTH"] -. traced on .-> LoadCurve
    GasCurve["GASOLINE SI 28 to 32 percent"] -. traced on .-> LoadCurve
    DieselCurve["DIESEL CI 38 to 42 percent"] -. traced on .-> LoadCurve
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Hydrogen SI efficiency curve tracks the diesel curve closely, but at *much lower NOx per kWh* when lean-burn strategy is adopted. This is the strategic attraction of hydrogen SI over diesel for heavy-duty mobility.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Hydrogen Dual-Fuel Engine System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Diesel["DIESEL PILOT INJECTION 5 to 15 percent ENERGY"] --> PilotComb["PILOT COMBUSTION T AD APROX 1800 K"]
    H2Port["H2 PORT INJECTION 80 to 90 percent ENERGY"] --> PilotComb
    PilotComb --> H2Ignite["H2 IGNITED BY DIESEL FLAME"]
    H2Ignite --> Work["PWR Stroke SHARED ENERGY RELEASE"]
    Work --> Exh["EXHAUST NOx CO TRACES PM"]
    Exh --> Aftertreat["AFTERTREATMENT DOC DPF SCR"]
    Aftertreat --> Tail["REDUCED EMISSIONS TAILPIPE"]
```
