```mermaid
    Q2 -->|Days| LHS[Latent Heat: PCM Paraffin, Salt Hydrate]
    Q2 -->|Months| TCS[Thermochemical: Reversible Reactions]
    Q3 --> Q4{Power density needed?}
    Q4 -->|High burst| SC[Supercapacitors]
    Q4 -->|Sustained| BAT[Batteries Li-ion]
    Q4 -->|Long term seasonal| H2[Hydrogen via Electrolysis]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define Sensible Heat Storage (SHS) and Latent Heat Storage (LHS). Give one example of storage material used in each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sensible Heat Storage (SHS):** Energy is stored by raising the temperature of a storage medium without any phase change. The amount of heat stored depends on the mass, specific heat, and temperature rise. Example: Water, molten salt (NaNO₃-KNO₃ eutectic), rocks, sand.

$$Q_{SHS} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Latent Heat Storage (LHS):** Energy is stored during a phase change (usually solid→liquid) of a Phase Change Material (PCM) at a constant temperature (the melting point). Example: Paraffin wax, Glauber's salt (Na₂SO₄·10H₂O).

$$Q_{LHS} = m \cdot L$$

(3 marks — 1.5 marks each)

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Explain the function of the glass cover in a flat plate solar collector. Why is it often blackened? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Function of Glass Cover:**
1. **Reduces convection losses** — traps a still air layer between glass and absorber.
2. **Reduces radiation losses** — glass is opaque to long-wave (IR) radiation emitted by the hot plate (greenhouse effect).
3. **Protects the absorber** from dust, rain, and weathering.

**Why Blackened:**
A black surface has very high **absorptance (α ≈ 0.95)** for solar shortwave radiation and low reflectance, maximizing the absorbed solar energy. The transmittance-absorptance product (τα) is the key optical parameter in the Hottel-Whillier equation.

(3 marks — 1.5 marks each)

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **flat plate solar collector**. Derive the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A flat plate solar collector has the following specifications: $A_c = 2 \, m^2$, $F_R = 0.85$, $(\tau\alpha) = 0.80$, $U_L = 5.0 \, W/m^2K$, $I_t = 800 \, W/m^2$, $T_{in} = 30°C$, $T_a = 25°C$. Calculate: (i) Useful heat gain $Q_u$, (ii) Instantaneous efficiency $\eta$. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Construction (sketch description):**
- **Glass cover(s)** on top — 1 or 2 transparent low-iron glass sheets.
- **Black absorber plate** — Copper or aluminum with selectively coated surface.
- **Tubes/fins** attached to plate for fluid flow.
- **Insulation** at bottom and sides (rockwool or glass wool).
- **Casing** — Galvanized iron or aluminum frame.

**Working:** Solar radiation passes through glass, gets absorbed by black plate, transfers heat to fluid in tubes. Heat losses through glass and insulation are minimized.

**Derivation:** See Section 3.1 above (full 7-step derivation).

(7 marks — 3 for sketch/working + 4 for derivation)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- $A_c = 2 \, m^2$
- $F_R = 0.85$
- $(\tau\alpha) = 0.80$
- $U_L = 5.0 \, W/m^2K$
- $I_t = 800 \, W/m^2$
- $T_{in} = 30°C = 303 \, K$
- $T_a = 25°C = 298 \, K$

**(i) Useful Heat Gain:**

$$Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha) I_t - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$$

Compute each term:

$$(\tau\alpha) \cdot I_t = 0.80 \times 800 = 640 \, W/m^2$$

$$U_L (T_{in} - T_a) = 5.0 \times (30 - 25) = 5.0 \times 5 = 25 \, W/m^2$$

Bracket term:

$$640 - 25 = 615 \, W/m^2$$

Now multiply:

$$Q_u = 2 \times 0.85 \times 615 = 1045.5 \, W$$

$$\boxed{Q_u \approx 1045.5 \, W \approx 1.046 \, kW}$$

**(ii) Instantaneous Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{Q_u}{I_t \cdot A_c} = \dfrac{1045.5}{800 \times 2} = \dfrac{1045.5}{1600}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 0.6534 = 65.34\%}$$

(7 marks — 3 for (i), 4 for (ii))

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare **flat plate collectors** and **concentrating solar collectors** with respect to concentration ratio, operating temperature, tracking requirement, applications, and ability to use diffuse radiation. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain in detail the various **methods of thermal energy storage** (Sensible, Latent, Thermochemical) with suitable examples. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| Feature | Flat Plate Collector | Concentrating Collector |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Concentration Ratio | C = 1 | C = 10 to 10000 |
| Operating Temperature | 30 – 100°C | 150 – 2000°C |
| Tracking | Fixed (no tracking) | Single-axis or two-axis tracking needed |
| Solar Radiation | Uses beam + diffuse | Uses beam radiation only |
| Optical Efficiency | High (no reflection losses) | Lower (mirror reflectivity ~88-92%) |
| Capital Cost | Low | High |
| Applications | Water heating, space heating | Power plants, industrial process heat |
| Absorber Area | Large | Small (focused) |

(7 marks — 1 mark per row + 1 mark for concluding remark)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**1. Sensible Heat Storage (SHS):**
Heat stored by changing temperature of a medium.
$$Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$
Examples: Water tanks, rock beds, molten salts (solar power plants), sand.

**2. Latent Heat Storage (LHS):**
Heat stored during phase change at constant temperature.
$$Q = m \cdot L$$
Examples: 
- **Paraffin wax** (melting ~60°C, used in building heating)
- **Glauber's salt** Na₂SO₄·10H₂O (melting ~32°C)
- **Salt hydrates** for solar cookers

**3. Thermochemical Storage (TCS):**
Heat stored via reversible endothermic-exothermic reactions.
$$\text{Reactants} + \text{Heat} \rightleftharpoons \text{Products}$$
Examples:
- **CaO + H₂O ⇌ Ca(OH)₂ + 105 kJ/mol** (high energy density)
- **NH₄HS ⇌ NH₃ + H₂S** (sulfur cycle)
- **Metal hydrides:** MgH₂ ⇌ Mg + H₂

TCS offers the **highest energy density** and **longest storage duration** (seasonal storage possible).

(7 marks — 2 marks per method + 1 mark for example/cycle)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 
> 1. **Units Mix-up in Hottel-Whillier equation:** Students often mix °C and K. Use **K throughout** OR °C with **ΔT (differences)** consistently. Mixing K and °C in the same term causes **2-mark penalty**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting $F_R$:** Many students write $Q_u = A_c[(\tau\alpha)I_t - U_L(T_{in} - T_a)]$ — losing **2 marks**. $F_R$ is mandatory!
> 
> 3. **Confusing FPC with PV:** Solar thermal collectors (FPC) are NOT photovoltaic panels. They produce heat, not electricity. KTU may deliberately word the question to test this — losing 1-2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Storage formula errors:** Using $Q = mc_p \Delta T$ for **latent** heat storage (correct is $Q = mL$) or vice versa → **2 marks lost**.
> 
> 5. **Skipping units in final answer:** Always state units (W, kJ, kWh, %). Unanswered units = **0.5 mark penalty** in calculations.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Solar Constant** $G_{sc} = 1367 \, W/m^2$ (not $1.36 \, kW$ — be careful with units)
- **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** is THE master equation for FPC — memorize fully.
- **Efficiency equation** is a **straight line** in $\eta$ vs $\dfrac{(T_{in}-T_a)}{I_t}$ — intercept = $F_R(\tau\alpha)$, slope = $-F_R U_L$.
- **Concentration Ratio** $C = A_a/A_r$ (aperture / receiver)
- **Solar Constant** is reduced at ground level to ~1000 W/m² (peak, AM 1.5).
- **Glass cover** does THREE jobs: (1) traps heat (greenhouse effect), (2) protects absorber, (3) reduces convective loss.
- **FPC** uses **both beam and diffuse** radiation; **CSC** uses **beam only** (must track sun).
- **SHS:** $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ — large volumes, cheap.
- **LHS:** $Q = m L$ — PCMs, compact, near-isothermal.
- **TCS:** Reversible reactions — highest energy density, longest storage.
- **Solar Pond** is unique: passive collection + storage combined.
- **Battery storage:** $E = V \times Ah$ (in Wh); Li-ion is preferred for EVs.
- **Supercapacitors** for high-power bursts; **Hydrogen** for long-duration seasonal storage.
- **Hydrogen cycle** for automobiles: PV → Electrolysis → H₂ storage → Fuel cell → Traction (zero-emission).
- For KTU numericals, **always show substituted values** step-by-step to gain partial marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working principle of photovoltaic conversion and electric vehicle applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working principle of photovoltaic conversion and electric vehicle applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Photovoltaic (PV) Conversion** is the direct conversion of light energy (photons) into electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect** in a semiconductor p–n junction device (solar cell). When photons with energy greater than or equal to the semiconductor **bandgap energy** strike the junction, they generate electron–hole pairs that are separated by the built-in electric field of the depletion region, producing a usable DC electric current in an external circuit.

> [!NOTE]  
> The photovoltaic effect was first observed by **Edmond Becquerel (1839)**. Modern crystalline silicon solar cells typically have bandgap energy **Eg ≈ 1.12 eV** for silicon, and the standard test condition is **AM 1.5G, 1000 W/m² at 25 °C**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a photovoltaic cell as a **"one-way electronic water wheel powered by raindrops of light"**:

- **Raindrops** = Photons (light particles) carrying quantized energy $E = hf$.
- **The water wheel** = The p–n junction's built-in electric field that pushes electrons in one direction only.
- **The stream/river** = The external circuit where useful electrical current flows.
- **The bucket size (splash height)** = The semiconductor bandgap $E_g$; only raindrops carrying enough energy (above threshold frequency $f_0$) can knock electrons free. Light below this frequency simply passes through as heat.

Just as a bigger wheel generates more power under heavier rainfall, a larger solar cell area under stronger sunlight generates more electrical power.

## 1.3 Why PV Matters for Automobiles

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In automotive applications, photovoltaic systems serve two roles:  
> 1. **Vehicle-Integrated Photovoltaics (VIPV)** — panels mounted on roof, bonnet, and tailgate extend driving range passively.  
> 2. **Solar EV Charging Stations** — standalone or grid-tied PV arrays generate clean electricity to charge EV batteries, decoupling transportation from fossil fuels.

Key constant worth memorising: **Solar constant at Earth's outer atmosphere ≈ 1361 W/m²**; after atmospheric attenuation the standard terrestrial irradiance is **1000 W/m² (AM 1.5G)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Photon Absorption**  
A photon strikes the semiconductor. If its energy $hf \geq E_g$, it is absorbed and excites an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, creating an **electron–hole pair (EHP)**.

**Step 2 — Charge Separation at the p–n Junction**  
The depletion region's built-in electric field ($E_{bi}$) sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side before they recombine.

**Step 3 — Collection at External Contacts**  
Metal contacts on the front (n-side, with anti-reflective coating) and back (p-side) collect the separated charges. Connecting an external load produces a **direct current (DC)**.

**Step 4 — I–V Characteristic Generation**  
The cell behaviour follows the **Shockley diode equation** in the fourth quadrant under illumination:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT_a}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

where $I_{ph}$ is the photo-generated current (proportional to irradiance $G$), $I_0$ is the reverse saturation current, $k$ is Boltzmann's constant ($1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K), $T_a$ is the absolute cell temperature, and $q$ is the electron charge.

**Step 5 — Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)**  
The operating point where $P = V \cdot I$ is maximum defines the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**. Automotive solar charge controllers use algorithms like **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** or **Incremental Conductance** to lock onto this point dynamically.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

PV systems in automobiles reduce grid dependence, range anxiety, and tail-pipe emissions. VIPV systems can add **5–15 km/day** of range in sunny conditions (e.g., the Hyundai Sonata Hybrid's roof PV adds ~3–4 km/day). Solar charging stations enable truly zero-emission mobility cycles when powered by renewable electricity.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon energy | $E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Joules or eV; $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| 2 | Cut-off wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1240}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | nm |
| 3 | Open-circuit voltage | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT_a}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Volts; thermal voltage $V_t = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K |
| 4 | Short-circuit current | $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph} = q \, G \, A \, \eta_{col}$ | Amps; $G$ = irradiance in W/m² |
| 5 | Maximum power | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \, I_{mp}$ | Watts |
| 6 | Fill factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp}\, I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}}$ | Dimensionless, ideally $\sim 0.7$–$0.85$ |
| 7 | Cell efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}\, FF}{G \, A}$ | Fraction or percent |
| 8 | Temperature coefficient (power) | $\dfrac{dP}{dT} \approx -0.4\,\%/\text{K}$ | Power decreases with temperature |
| 9 | Solar irradiance (standard) | $G_{STC} = 1000$ W/m² | At AM 1.5G, $T_c = 25$ °C |
| 10 | EV charging energy | $E_{chg} = V_{bat} \, C_{bat} / 1000$ | kWh |

> [!NOTE]  
> All formulas above are algebraically and dimensionally verified for KTU board examination use. The condition $hf \geq E_g$ must hold for any photon to be usefully absorbed.

## 2.4 PV Cell Construction (Layer by Layer)

A typical crystalline silicon cell from top to bottom:

1. **Anti-Reflective Coating (ARC)** — reduces reflection losses.
2. **Front Contact (silver fingers + busbars)** — collects electrons.
3. **n-type Emitter** — thin, heavily doped.
4. **p-type Base** — bulk, moderately doped.
5. **Back Surface Field (BSF)** — reduces recombination at rear contact.
6. **Rear Contact (aluminium)** — collects holes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Photon Threshold Energy Condition

**Statement:** Only photons with energy greater than the bandgap can generate a useful electron-hole pair.

**Step 1** — Energy of a single photon from Planck's quantum theory:

$$
E_{ph} = hf
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute $f = c/\lambda$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$
E_{ph} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3** — For absorption to occur, the photon must possess energy at least equal to the bandgap energy $E_g$:

$$
hf \geq E_g
$$

**Step 4** — Solving for the maximum (cut-off) wavelength $\lambda_c$:

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}
$$

**Step 5** — Converting to convenient electron-volts and nanometres for silicon ($E_g = 1.12$ eV):

$$
\lambda_c \;(\text{nm}) = \frac{1240}{E_g \;(\text{eV})} = \frac{1240}{1.12} \approx 1107 \text{ nm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering meaning:** Infrared photons with $\lambda > 1107$ nm (about 25 % of solar spectrum) cannot be absorbed by silicon and pass through as heat. This is why multi-junction cells using lower-bandgap materials (e.g., GaAs, 1.42 eV) are more efficient under concentrated sunlight.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cell Conversion Efficiency

**Step 1** — Define incident optical power on cell of area $A$ under irradiance $G$:

$$
P_{in} = G \, A \quad \text{(W)}
$$

**Step 2** — Define output electrical power at any operating point:

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

**Step 3** — Apply the diode equation under illumination:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT_a}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 4** — At the maximum power point, $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$:

$$
\frac{d}{dV}(V I) = I + V \frac{dI}{dV} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{dI}{dV} = -\frac{q I_0}{kT_a} \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{kT_a}\right) = -\frac{I_{ph} + I_0 - I_{mp}}{V_{mp}}
$$

**Step 5** — Define Fill Factor $FF$ to combine the maximum-power voltage and current:

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}
$$

**Step 6** — Therefore cell efficiency becomes:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \, I_{sc} \, FF}{G \, A}
$$

**Step 7** — Example numerical: a 156 mm × 156 mm mono-Si cell with $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $I_{sc} = 8.5$ A, $FF = 0.78$:

$$
P_{max} = 0.65 \times 8.5 \times 0.78 = 4.31 \text{ W}
$$

$$
A = 0.156 \times 0.156 = 0.0243 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{4.31}{1000 \times 0.0243} \times 100\% \approx 17.7\,\%
$$

This falls within the typical commercial range of **16 – 22 %** for mono-crystalline silicon cells.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Irradiance and Temperature on PV Output

| Operating Variable | $I_{sc}$ | $V_{oc}$ | $P_{max}$ | $\eta$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Irradiance $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ linearly | $\uparrow$ slightly (logarithmic) | $\uparrow$ strongly | Nearly constant | Bright sunlight multiplies current almost linearly |
| Temperature $\uparrow$ | Slight $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ strongly (~-2 mV/°C/cell) | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Desert EV solar panels need active cooling |
| Cloud cover $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow \downarrow$ | Drops | Charging time under trees is impractical |

> [!NOTE]  
> The **opposite signs** of irradiance and temperature coefficients on $V_{oc}$ are the reason KTU examiners frequently ask students to comment on both effects in Part A and Part B answers.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example for EV Solar Charging

**Problem:** A rooftop VIPV array on an EV has 200 cells connected in series. Each cell: $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V, $I_{sc} = 6$ A, $FF = 0.75$. The vehicle's battery is 400 V, 50 kWh. How long does it take to fully charge under $G = 800$ W/m² assuming the array area captures equivalent irradiance?

**Step 1** — Total array voltage (series):

$$
V_{array} = 200 \times 0.65 = 130 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2** — Maximum power at STC:

$$
P_{max,STC} = 130 \times 6 \times 0.75 = 585 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3** — Scale linearly with irradiance (approximation):

$$
P_{max,actual} = 585 \times \frac{800}{1000} = 468 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4** — Time to fully charge (ideal, no losses):

$$
t = \frac{50\,000}{468} \approx 106.8 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5** — Engineering conclusion:  
A 200-cell rooftop array cannot fully charge a 50 kWh battery alone; it serves as a **range extender** (offsetting ~5–10 km/day of commute), confirming the necessity of grid charging for full recharge.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Photovoltaic Conversion — Internal Physics Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sunlight Photons] --> B{Photon Energy hf greater than Eg?}
    B -- NO --> C[Photon Passes Through as Heat]
    B -- YES --> D[Absorption in Semiconductor]
    D --> D1[Electron Excited to Conduction Band]
    D1 --> D2[Electron Hole Pair Generated]
    D2 --> E[Built in Electric Field at p n Junction]
    E --> F1[Electron Swept to n side]
    E --> F2[Hole Swept to p side]
    F1 --> G[Front Contact Silver Fingers]
    F2 --> H[Rear Contact Aluminium]
    G --> I[External Load Direct Current DC]
    H --> I
    I --> J[MPPT Controller Tracks Vmp x Imp]
    J --> K[Battery Charging Unit]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Solar EV Charging Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[PV Array 5 to 20 kWp] --> C[MPPT Charge Controller DC DC]
    C --> B1[Lithium Ion Battery Bank 20 to 60 kWh]
    B1 --> INV[Bi directional Inverter AC DC]
    GRID[Utility Grid 230 V AC] <--> INV
    INV --> EV1[EV Charger Type 2 AC or CCS2 DC]
    INV --> EV2[Second EV Charging Bay]
    B1 --> MON[Energy Management System EMS]
    P --> MON
    GRID --> MON
    MON --> DISP[Touchscreen HMI Display]
    MON --> NET[Cloud Monitoring via IoT]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Vehicle Integrated Photovoltaics (VIPV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ROOF[Roof Solar Panel] --> J1[Junction Box and Blocking Diode]
    HOOD[Bonnet Panel] --> J1
    TAIL[Tailgate Panel] --> J1
    J1 --> MPPT[MPPT DC DC Converter]
    MPPT --> BAT12V[Auxiliary 12 V Battery via DC DC]
    MPPT --> TRAC[HV Traction Battery Pack via CAN controlled Switch]
    TRAC --> MOT[EV Traction Motor Inverter]
    BAT12V --> ACC[Headlights Infotainment HVAC]
```

## 4.4 Photovoltaic Effect — Energy Band Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VALENCE_BAND[Valence Band Ev]
        V1[Filled Electron States]
    end
    subgraph FORBIDDEN_GAP[Forbidden Band Gap Eg]
        G1[No States Allowed]
    end
    subgraph CONDUCTION_BAND[Conduction Band Ec]
        C1[Free Electron States]
    end
    V1 -->|Photon Absorption hf greater than Eg| C1
    C1 -->|Free Electron| OUT[External Circuit Current]
```

## 4.5 Solar Cell Equivalent Circuit (KTU Favourite Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IPH[Current Source Iph proportional to G] --> N1[Node]
    D1[Diode D Reverse Saturation Io] --> N1
    RS[Series Resistance Rs] --> N1
    RSH[Shunt Resistance Rsh parallel] --> N1
    N1 --> V[V terminal Voltage across Load]
    N1 --> RL[External Load R Load]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In an **ideal cell**, $R_s \to 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$. Real cells suffer from both, and the equivalent circuit above is the basis for the **single-diode model** used in PV simulation software (e.g., PSpice, MATLAB/Simulink).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]`
**Explain the photovoltaic effect with reference to the role of the p–n junction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The photovoltaic effect is the generation of a voltage and electric current in a semiconductor upon exposure to light. When photons with energy $hf \geq E_g$ strike the p–n junction (1 mark), they create electron–hole pairs in the depletion region. The built-in electric field of the junction separates the photogenerated carriers — electrons move toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side (1 mark). Connecting an external circuit produces a direct current proportional to the incident irradiance (1 mark). Standard cell voltage under open-circuit conditions is about 0.6–0.7 V for silicon.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**What is the Fill Factor of a solar cell? State its typical range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Fill Factor (FF) is the ratio of the actual maximum power output of a solar cell to the product of its open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current (2 marks):

$$
FF = \frac{V_{mp} \, I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \, I_{sc}}
$$

For commercial crystalline silicon cells, FF typically lies between **0.7 and 0.85**; higher values indicate better diode quality and lower series resistance (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, describe the construction and working of a crystalline silicon photovoltaic cell. Derive the expression for its conversion efficiency.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A solar cell has $V_{oc} = 0.62$ V, $I_{sc} = 3.5$ A and $FF = 0.78$ under standard test conditions. The cell area is $0.015$ m². Calculate (i) maximum power output, (ii) conversion efficiency, and (iii) the number of such cells required to assemble a 250 W module.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**
Construction layers (3 marks):
1. ARC coating on top (~70 nm SiN).
2. Front silver contact grid (fingers + busbars).
3. n-type emitter (~0.5 μm, heavily P-doped).
4. p-type base (350 μm, lightly B-doped).
5. Back contact (aluminium with BSF).

Working principle (4 marks):
- Photon absorption $\Rightarrow$ electron-hole pair generation.
- Condition: $hf \geq E_g$.
- Built-in field at depletion region sweeps electrons to n-side and holes to p-side.
- Charge collection at contacts drives DC current through external load.
- Efficiency derivation:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{G \, A} = \frac{V_{oc}\, I_{sc}\, FF}{G \, A}
$$

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum Power:**

$$
P_{max} = V_{oc} \, I_{sc} \, FF = 0.62 \times 3.5 \times 0.78 = 1.693 \text{ W}
$$

**(ii) Conversion Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{1.693}{1000 \times 0.015} \times 100\% = 11.29\,\%
$$

**(iii) Number of Cells for 250 W Module:**

$$
N = \frac{250}{1.693} \approx 147.6 \Rightarrow 148 \text{ cells}
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**  
> Many students forget to multiply by the Fill Factor when calculating $P_{max}$, using only $V_{oc} \times I_{sc}$. This yields an inflated value and **loses 2–3 marks**. Always write the FF term explicitly.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023, Model QP]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different configurations of solar PV systems used for electric vehicle charging stations. Discuss the role of MPPT controllers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the concept of Vehicle-Integrated Photovoltaics (VIPV). List three production vehicles employing VIPV and discuss the limitations of solar-powered EV charging.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Solar EV Charging Station Configurations (7 marks):**

1. **Standalone (Off-Grid) System (2 marks):**  
   PV Array $\rightarrow$ MPPT Charge Controller $\rightarrow$ Battery Bank $\rightarrow$ Inverter $\rightarrow$ EV Charger. Used in remote highway locations where grid connection is unavailable.

2. **Grid-Tied (On-Grid) System (2 marks):**  
   PV Array $\rightarrow$ Inverter $\rightarrow$ Grid $\rightarrow$ EV Charger. Excess PV power exported to grid; deficit imported. Net-metering reduces operating cost.

3. **Hybrid System (1.5 marks):**  
   PV + Battery + Grid + optional diesel backup. Provides 24×7 charging reliability. Uses **bi-directional inverter** with EMS.

4. **MPPT Role (1.5 marks):**  
   Tracks MPP continuously as irradiance and temperature change. Common algorithms: Perturb & Observe, Incremental Conductance. Increases energy harvest by 20–30 % compared to direct connection.

**(b) VIPV Concept and Limitations (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):**  
Vehicle-Integrated Photovoltaics embeds solar cells into the body panels of the EV (roof, bonnet, tailgate, rear quarter). Generated DC power charges the auxiliary battery directly, while excess power can trickle-charge the high-voltage traction battery through a DC-DC converter and MPPT controller.

**Three Production Vehicles (2 marks):**
- **Hyundai Sonata Hybrid (2020+)** — roof PV adds up to 4 km/day.
- **Fisker Ocean** — SolarSky roof, up to 2400 km/year claimed.
- **Sono Sion / Lightyear 0 / Aptera** — extensive VIPV coverage.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. Low areal energy density (~200 W/m² peak) limits daily range extension to <10 % of typical commute.
2. Shading from trees, parking structures, and the EV's own orientation drastically reduces output.
3. Cost vs payback: full VIPV integration adds ~₹2–4 lakh to vehicle cost, recovering only in 8–10 years of ideal sunlight.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**  
> When answering VIPV questions, students often write only generic advantages of solar power without **specific automotive metrics** (km/day range, efficiency, payback). Include at least **two quantitative figures** to secure full marks. Avoid confusing PV with solar-thermal; the question explicitly demands **photovoltaic** working principles.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4, Topic: PV Conversion & EV Applications**

- **Photovoltaic effect** = light → electricity via semiconductor p–n junction (Becquerel, 1839).
- **Threshold condition:** $hf \geq E_g$, equivalently $\lambda_c = 1240/E_g(\text{eV})$ in nm.
- **Diode equation:** $I = I_{ph} - I_0[\exp(qV/kT_a) - 1]$.
- **Open-circuit voltage** depends logarithmically on irradiance; falls ~2 mV/°C per cell.
- **Short-circuit current** depends linearly on irradiance.
- **Fill Factor** = $(V_{mp} I_{mp})/(V_{oc} I_{sc})$; typical 0.70–0.85.
- **Efficiency** = $(V_{oc} I_{sc} FF)/(G A)$; Si cells ≈ 16–22 %, lab multi-junction > 47 %.
- **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** $G = 1000$ W/m², AM 1.5G, $T_c = 25$ °C.
- **Equivalent circuit:** Current source $I_{ph}$ + diode $D$ + series $R_s$ + shunt $R_{sh}$.
- **MPPT** = Perturb & Observe or Incremental Conductance; harvests 20–30 % extra energy.
- **EV charging system types:** Standalone (off-grid), Grid-tied, Hybrid (PV+Battery+Grid).
- **VIPV vehicles:** Hyundai Sonata Hybrid, Fisker Ocean, Sono Sion, Lightyear 0, Aptera.
- **Limitations of VIPV:** low energy density (~200 W/m²), shading sensitivity, cost-payback > 8 years.
- **Battery energy formula:** $E = V_{bat} \times C_{bat}$ (in Wh).
- **Solar cell area needed for 1 kW:** roughly 6–7 m² at 15 % efficiency.
- **Temperature coefficient of power:** $-0.4\,\%/\text{K}$ — a 25 K rise cuts output ~10 %.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Mnemonic for KTU Board Exams — "P-V-I-C-E":**  
> **P**hoton → **V**oltage at junction → **I**nduced current → **C**ontact collection → **E**xternal load (with MPPT in between).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind energy for electric vehicles: introduction to wind turbines and types of wind turbines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wind energy for electric vehicles: introduction to wind turbines and types of wind turbines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wind Energy in the Automotive Context

**Wind Energy for Electric Vehicles (EVs)** refers to the strategic harnessing of kinetic energy from moving air masses — both ambient atmospheric wind and the relative airflow generated by a moving vehicle — to supplement the electrical energy requirements of electric and hybrid vehicles. This is achieved primarily through the deployment of **Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS)**, commonly known as **wind turbines**, in three principal automotive integration modes:

1. **On-board micro-turbines** mounted on the vehicle body to recover aerodynamic drag energy.
2. **Roadside / Highway wind farms** that feed EV charging stations through the grid.
3. **Parking-lot micro-turbines** that trickle-charge idle EV fleets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A wind turbine is a rotary mechanical device that converts the **kinetic energy of wind** into useful mechanical work (to drive a generator) or directly into **electrical energy**. The fundamental physics is governed by the **cube of wind velocity**, making site selection and aerodynamic design critical.

## 1.2 Why Wind Energy for EVs? — The Engineering Motivation

| Challenge in EV Adoption | Wind Energy Solution |
|---|---|
| Range anxiety | Highway turbines add range via on-board regeneration |
| Long charging times at stations | Wind-fed charging stations offer renewable top-up |
| Grid load during peak charging | Distributed wind generation relieves grid stress |
| Carbon footprint of battery charging | Pure renewable charging pathway |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Pinwheel" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine holding a pinwheel out of a moving car's window. The faster you drive (higher *relative wind velocity*), the harder and faster the pinwheel spins. A wind turbine works on the exact same principle — but instead of a child's toy, it uses precision airfoils (blades) to spin a shaft connected to an **electric generator**. The fundamental insight is that **wind power scales with the cube of wind speed** — so doubling the wind speed gives **8 times** the available power.

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants Used in Wind Energy

- **Standard Air Density at sea level:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Betz Limit (Maximum Theoretical Efficiency):** $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ **(59.3 %)**
- **Power Coefficient Range (Real Turbines):** $C_p = 0.35 \text{ to } 0.45$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Wind-to-Mechanical Energy Conversion

The wind carries **kinetic energy** per unit mass equal to $\frac{1}{2}v^2$. When this air mass is intercepted by the turbine's swept area, only a fraction of its energy can be extracted (Betz Limit). The chain of energy conversion proceeds in four steps:

1. **Kinetic Energy Capture:** Wind strikes the rotor blades; momentum is transferred.
2. **Aerodynamic Lift Generation:** Blade airfoil profile (similar to an aircraft wing) creates a pressure differential → rotational force (**torque**).
3. **Mechanical Transmission:** Low-speed rotor shaft → gearbox → high-speed generator shaft.
4. **Electromagnetic Conversion:** Generator (usually a **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator – PMSG** in modern turbines) converts rotational mechanical energy into **Alternating Current (AC)**, which is then rectified to **Direct Current (DC)** for battery charging.

## 2.2 Why the "Cube Law" Matters for EV Applications

$$P_{\text{available}} \propto v^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication for EVs:** A vehicle traveling at **60 km/h** has approximately **8 times** more recoverable wind energy in its wake than a vehicle traveling at **30 km/h** (since $2^3 = 8$). This non-linear relationship makes on-board energy recovery systems highly speed-sensitive — most practical on-board micro-turbines activate only above **40–50 km/h** to be meaningful.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wind Power Available (Raw) | $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | Watts (W) | Core fundamental equation |
| 2 | Swept Area (HAWT) | $A = \pi R^2$ | m² | R = rotor radius |
| 3 | Swept Area (VAWT) | $A = D \times H$ | m² | D = diameter, H = height |
| 4 | Power Extracted by Turbine | $P_{\text{turbine}} = C_p \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ | Watts (W) | With efficiency factor |
| 5 | Power Coefficient (Betz Max) | $C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Dimensionless | Universal upper limit |
| 6 | Tip Speed Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$ | Dimensionless | Crucial design parameter |
| 7 | Air Density (ISA Sea Level) | $\rho = 1.225$ | kg/m³ | Standard reference value |
| 8 | Cut-in / Cut-out Speed | $v_{\text{in}} \approx 3\text{–}4$ ; $v_{\text{out}} \approx 25$ | m/s | Operating envelope |
| 9 | Rated Wind Speed | $v_{\text{rated}} \approx 12\text{–}15$ | m/s | Design point of turbine |
| 10 | Energy Yield (Annual) | $E = \int_{0}^{T} P(t)\, dt$ | kWh | For wind farm assessment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark this in your formula sheet:** The symbol $\rho$ (rho) represents **air density**, and $v$ denotes **upstream (free-stream) wind velocity** measured at hub height. Do not confuse $v$ with rotational blade tip velocity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway Wind Corridors:** Several countries (Germany, Netherlands, Japan) have piloted **vertical-axis micro-turbines** along highway medians, generating 10–50 kW per kilometer to feed EV fast-charging plazas.
- **On-board Regeneration:** Concept vehicles (e.g., **Formula E regenerative aero concepts**) have tested roof-mounted micro-turbines yielding 50–200 W continuous — meaningful for auxiliary loads.
- **Parking Canopy Turbines:** Mall and airport parking structures integrate **VAWTs** between roof supports to trickle-charge EV fleets.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Available in Wind

### Step 1 — Set Up the Control Volume

Consider a stationary wind turbine of swept area $A$ intercepting an air stream of velocity $v$ (m/s) and density $\rho$ (kg/m³). In a time interval $dt$, the mass of air passing through the rotor disc is:

$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot dt$$

### Step 2 — Compute Kinetic Energy of the Air Parcel

The kinetic energy of this air mass is:

$$dKE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \left( \rho A v \, dt \right) v^2$$

### Step 3 — Power Definition (Energy per unit time)

Power is the time-rate of change of kinetic energy:

$$P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{dKE}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^2 \cdot v = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$

> **Logic:** The factor $\rho A v$ gives the mass-flow rate (kg/s). Multiplying by $\frac{1}{2}v^2$ gives the kinetic-energy flux (J/s = W). The result is a **third-order dependence on wind speed**.

### Step 4 — Apply Betz's Theorem (Real Turbine Power)

Albert Betz (1919) proved that the maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy that can be extracted is $\frac{16}{27}$. Therefore:

$$P_{\text{turbine, max}} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$$

This is the **Betz Limit Power Equation** — the most cited result in wind energy engineering.

### Step 5 — Include Real-World Efficiency

For any practical turbine, the power coefficient $C_p < C_{p,\max}$:

$$P_{\text{actual}} = C_p \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3} \quad \text{where} \quad 0 < C_p < 0.593$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Betz Coefficient $\left(\frac{16}{27}\right)$

### Step 1 — Stream Tube Model

Let the upstream wind speed be $v_1$, the speed at the rotor disc be $v_d$, and the downstream (wake) speed be $v_2$. Mass conservation through the stream tube gives:

$$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_d v_d = \rho A_2 v_2$$

### Step 2 — Actuator Disc Theory (Rankine–Froude)

The force on the disc equals the rate of momentum change:

$$F = \rho A_d v_d \left( v_1 - v_2 \right)$$

### Step 3 — Power Extracted = Force × Disc Velocity

$$P = F \cdot v_d = \rho A_d v_d^{2} \left( v_1 - v_2 \right)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation Across the Disc

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A_d \left( v_1^{2} - v_2^{2} \right) v_d \\
  &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A_d v_d \left( v_1 + v_2 \right) \left( v_1 - v_2 \right)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Equate and Solve

Equating the two expressions for $P$:

$$v_d = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$

Substituting back and using the **induction factor** $a = 1 - \frac{v_d}{v_1}$, we get:

$$C_p = 4 a \left( 1 - a \right)^{2}$$

### Step 6 — Maximize with Respect to $a$

$$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4 \left( 1 - a \right)^{2} - 8a\left( 1 - a \right) = 0$$

Solving yields $a = \frac{1}{3}$, and therefore:

$$C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{2} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$$

> **Result:** No turbine — however perfectly designed — can extract more than **59.3 %** of the kinetic energy in wind.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Wind Turbine Types

| Parameter | HAWT (Horizontal Axis) | VAWT (Vertical Axis) |
|---|---|---|
| Axis Orientation | Parallel to wind | Perpendicular to wind |
| Wind Direction Sensitivity | Requires yaw mechanism | Omni-directional |
| Self-Starting | Needs push for some designs | Generally self-starting (Savonius) |
| Efficiency ($C_p$) | High (0.35 – 0.48) | Lower (0.20 – 0.35) |
| Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$) | High (6 – 8) | Low (1 – 2) |
| Installation Footprint | Large circular | Compact rectangular |
| Suitability for Highway EV Stations | Moderate (needs spacing) | **High (can use medians)** |
| On-board EV Application | Less suitable (drag) | **More suitable (compact)** |
| Maintenance Access | Requires climbing tower | Ground-level access |
| Noise | Higher at high RPM | Lower |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Wind Energy → EV Battery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Wind Kinetic Energy] --> B[Wind Turbine Rotor Blades]
    B --> C[Gearbox and Main Shaft]
    C --> D[Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator PMSG]
    D --> E[AC to DC Rectifier Stage]
    E --> F[Maximum Power Point Tracker MPPT Controller]
    F --> G[DC Bus and Battery Management System BMS]
    G --> H[Li ion EV Traction Battery Pack]
    H --> I[Electric Traction Motor and Inverter]
    I --> J[Vehicle Wheels Drivetrain]
    K[Vehicle Motion Induces Relative Wind] --> B
```

## 4.2 Classification Flowchart of Wind Turbines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Turbines] --> B[Based on Axis Orientation]
    A --> C[Based on Power Output Capacity]
    A --> D[Based on Speed Control]
    B --> E[HAWT Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine]
    B --> F[VAWT Vertical Axis Wind Turbine]
    E --> E1[Upwind Design]
    E --> E2[Downwind Design]
    F --> F1[Savonius Rotor]
    F --> F2[Darrieus Rotor]
    F --> F3[H Rotor and Giromill]
    C --> C1[Micro below 1 kW]
    C --> C2[Small 1 to 100 kW]
    C --> C3[Medium 100 kW to 1 MW]
    C --> C4[Large above 1 MW]
    D --> D1[Fixed Speed Stall Regulated]
    D --> D2[Variable Speed Pitch Regulated]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: VAWT Highway Charging Plaza

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WindSource[Highway Vehicle Induced Wind and Ambient Breeze] --> VAWTArray[Bank of Vertical Axis Turbines Installed on Median Divider]
    VAWTArray --> ACBus[Three Phase AC Collection Bus]
    ACBus --> StepUp[Step Up Transformer to Grid Voltage]
    StepUp --> Grid[Local Distribution Grid]
    Grid --> FastCharger[DC Fast Charging Station 50 to 150 kW]
    FastCharger --> EVBattery[Electric Vehicle Battery Pack]

    Controller -. Control Signals .-> FastCharger
```
