# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Recap (per KTU 2024 ESE pattern for PEAUT411):**
> - Part A: Short answer (3 marks each, typically 2 questions asked out of 4).
> - Part B: Long answer with internal choice (14 marks: sub-part (a) = 7 marks, sub-part (b) = 7 marks).
> - Total per question: 20 marks. The questions below are calibrated to this exact pattern.

---

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a DC shunt motor and a DC series motor with respect to their speed-torque characteristics. State one automotive application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | DC Shunt Motor | DC Series Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Field Connection | Parallel to armature | Series with armature |
| Flux Behavior | $\Phi \approx$ constant (since $I_f = V/R_{sh}$) | $\Phi \propto I_a$ |
| Speed Regulation | **Good** (nearly constant speed) | **Poor** (speed varies inversely with load) |
| Starting Torque | Moderate | **Very High** |
| No-Load Risk | Safe | **Unsafe** (tends to overspeed) |
| Application (auto) | Auxiliary drives (fans, wipers) | **Traction (early EVs, forklifts)** |

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define slip in a three-phase induction motor. A 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor runs at 960 rpm. Find the slip percentage.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Slip $s$ is defined as the **ratio of the relative speed** between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor to the synchronous speed.

$$
s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}
$$

**Calculation:**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \; \text{rpm}
$$

$$
s = \frac{1000 - 960}{1000} = \frac{40}{1000} = 0.04 = 4\%
$$

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a **three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor**. Derive the expression for **synchronous speed**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a):**

**Construction (Sketch Description):**
- **Stator:** Three-phase distributed windings placed in the slots of a laminated silicon-steel core. The windings are spatially displaced by 120° electrical.
- **Rotor:** Cylindrical laminated core with **aluminum (or copper) bars** embedded in slots, short-circuited at both ends by conducting **end rings**. The rotor has no electrical connection to the external circuit — hence "squirrel cage."

**Working Principle:**
1. When balanced 3-phase AC supply is given to the stator, currents $I_a, I_b, I_c$ displaced 120° in time flow in the three-phase windings.
2. The combined effect of these spatially and temporally displaced windings produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** of constant magnitude rotating at synchronous speed $N_s$.
3. This RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing an EMF in them (Faraday's law).
4. Since the rotor bars are short-circuited, induced EMF drives a current through them.
5. By Lenz's law, the rotor current produces a magnetic field that **opposes the cause** (relative motion), forcing the rotor to rotate in the same direction as the RMF.
6. The rotor accelerates until it approaches $N_s$, but **never reaches** it (otherwise no relative motion, no induced EMF, no torque). The fractional shortfall is the **slip**.

**Derivation of Synchronous Speed (already in Section 3.3):**

$$
N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} \; \text{rpm}
$$

**(b)** A **3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor** has a full-load slip of 4%. Calculate: **(i)** Synchronous speed, **(ii)** Rotor speed at full load, **(iii)** Rotor frequency, **(iv)** Slip speed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b):**

Given: $P = 4$, $f = 50$ Hz, $s = 0.04$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Synchronous speed} \quad & N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \; \text{rpm} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(ii) Rotor speed} \quad & N_r = N_s(1 - s) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \; \text{rpm} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(iii) Rotor frequency} \quad & f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \; \text{Hz} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(iv) Slip speed} \quad & N_{slip} = N_s - N_r = 1500 - 1440 = 60 \; \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B** *(Alternative Choice)* [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)**. List its **advantages over an induction motor** for EV application. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a):**

**Construction:**
- **Stator:** Three-phase distributed AC winding on a laminated core — structurally identical to an induction motor stator.
- **Rotor:** High-energy **permanent magnets** (typically NdFeB) mounted on the rotor surface (Surface-Mounted PMSM) or embedded within the rotor body (Interior PMSM).
- **Position Sensor:** A **resolver or encoder** mounted on the shaft provides real-time rotor angular position to the controller.
- **No rotor winding, no slip rings, no brushes.**

**Working Principle:**
1. The permanent magnets on the rotor generate a **constant magnetic field** that rotates with the rotor.
2. The inverter supplies three-phase **sinusoidal currents** to the stator windings, producing a rotating magnetic field at synchronous speed $N_s = 120 f / P$.
3. The controller adjusts the **phase and frequency** of stator currents based on rotor position feedback such that the stator field "pulls" the rotor along.
4. The rotor **locks with the stator RMF** and rotates at exactly synchronous speed.
5. By varying frequency $f$, the speed is precisely controlled (V/f or field-oriented control).

**Advantages of PMSM over Induction Motor for EV:**

1. **Higher efficiency** (no rotor copper losses — only stator has electrical losses).
2. **Higher power and torque density** → smaller and lighter for the same output.
3. **Better dynamic response** (lower rotor inertia, no slip dynamics).
4. **Improved thermal management** (heat generated only in stator, which is easier to cool).
5. **Higher power factor** at rated load.
6. **No slip** → no rotor speed variation under load transients.
7. **Compact packaging** simplifies integration into vehicle floor or wheel hub (in-wheel motor application).

**(b)** A **DC shunt motor** operates at 220 V with armature resistance $R_a = 0.3 \; \Omega$ and field resistance $R_{sh} = 110 \; \Omega$. At no-load, armature current is 3 A and speed is 1100 rpm. Calculate: **(i)** No-load back EMF, **(ii)** Field current, **(iii)** Line current at no-load, **(iv)** Speed if flux is reduced to 75% of its original value (load unchanged). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b):**

Given: $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.3 \; \Omega$, $R_{sh} = 110 \; \Omega$, $I_{a0} = 3$ A, $N_0 = 1100$ rpm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) No-load back EMF} \quad & E_{b0} = V - I_{a0} R_a = 220 - (3 \times 0.3) = 220 - 0.9 = 219.1 \; \text{V} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(ii) Field current} \quad & I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \; \text{A} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(iii) Line current at no-load} \quad & I_L = I_{a0} + I_f = 3 + 2 = 5 \; \text{A} \\[4pt]
\textbf{(iv) New speed with reduced flux} \quad & \text{Since } E_b \approx V \text{ (negligible } I_a R_a \text{ at the new load):} \\
& N \propto \frac{1}{\Phi} \\
& \frac{N_{new}}{N_0} = \frac{\Phi_0}{\Phi_{new}} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3} \\
& N_{new} = \frac{4}{3} \times 1100 = 1466.67 \; \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating $N_s$ and $N_r$:** In induction motor questions, students often write $N_r = N_s$, which is physically impossible. Always state that rotor must run at $N_r < N_s$ to develop torque.
> 2. **Sign of $E_b$ in DC motors:** Back EMF always **opposes** the supply voltage: $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. Some students write $V = E_b - I_a R_a$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing lap vs wave winding:** In lap winding $A = P$; in wave winding $A = 2$ (always). Wrong $A$ value → wrong $E_b$ → entire 7-mark numerical becomes incorrect.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in $N_s$ formula:** $f$ must be in Hz, $P$ is the number of poles, and result is in rpm. Forgetting the constant 120 is a classic error.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** KTU evaluators explicitly award 1–2 marks for a "neat sketch." Always draw a labeled figure, even if rough.
> 6. **PM demagnetization risk (PMSM):** Students often overstate PMSM advantages. Always mention the **risk of irreversible demagnetization at high temperature or high current** as a disadvantage.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Electric motors** convert **electrical → mechanical energy** via Lorentz force.
- ✅ The **back EMF** $E_b$ in a DC machine is $E_b = \Phi N Z P / (60A)$.
- ✅ The **electromagnetic torque** is $T = P \Phi Z I_a / (2 \pi A)$.
- ✅ The **armature voltage equation** is $V = E_b + I_a R_a$.
- ✅ The **synchronous speed** (universal for AC machines) is $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- ✅ **Slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ is the operating principle of induction motors.
- ✅ **Rotor frequency** $f_r = s \cdot f$; **slip speed** $= N_s - N_r$.
- ✅ **DC Series** → high starting torque, used in older traction.
- ✅ **DC Shunt** → nearly constant speed, used in auxiliaries.
- ✅ **Induction motor** → robust, brushless; Tesla's choice.
- ✅ **PMSM** → highest efficiency, used in Prius and Leaf.
- ✅ **BLDC** → trapezoidal EMF, used in 2-wheelers and e-bikes.
- ✅ **SRM** → most robust and fault-tolerant, no permanent magnets.
- ✅ **Four-quadrant operation** (motoring + generating in both directions) is native to electric motors — the foundation of **regenerative braking**.
- ✅ **Traction motor requirements:** high torque density, wide CPSR, high efficiency, 4-quadrant operation, robustness.
- ✅ For KTU numericals: **always** state the formula, substitute, simplify, and **box the final answer with units**.
- ✅ Memorize the **Kloss equation** for induction motor torque analysis in dynamic loading.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Batteries: various battery types, capacities, limitations and future possibilities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Batteries: various battery types, capacities, limitations and future possibilities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔋 Module 4: Batteries for Automobile Applications

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *"A battery is an electrochemical energy storage device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy through spontaneous redox (oxidation-reduction) reactions. In automobiles, batteries serve as the primary energy buffer for cranking, lighting, ignition (SLI), traction propulsion in HEVs/EVs, and auxiliary loads."*

### 1.1 The Working Analogy — "The Reservoir of Electrical Water"

Imagine a water tank on a rooftop. The tank **stores** water at a height (potential energy). When you open the tap, water flows down and does work. A battery works exactly the same way:

- The **chemical reactants** inside the cell = water in the tank.
- The **electrode potential difference (V)** = height of the tank.
- The **charge capacity (Ah)** = volume/quantity of water stored.
- The **discharge** = water flowing out and doing useful work.

When all the chemical energy is spent, you cannot get more electricity unless you **recharge** it (pump water back up) — this is a *secondary* or *rechargeable* battery. If it cannot be recharged, it is a *primary* battery (single use).

### 1.2 Fundamental Battery Terminology

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | KTU Significance |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------------------|
| Electromotive Force | EMF (ε) | **Volts (V)** | Theoretical open-circuit voltage |
| Terminal Voltage | V | Volts (V) | Actual voltage under load |
| Capacity | Q | **Ampere-hour (Ah)** | Total charge stored |
| Energy | E | **Watt-hour (Wh)** or Joules | Total work available |
| Energy Density (Gravimetric) | ρ_E (mass) | **Wh/kg** | Range per kg of battery |
| Energy Density (Volumetric) | ρ_E (vol) | **Wh/L** | Packaging efficiency |
| Power Density | ρ_P | **W/kg** | Acceleration capability |
| C-Rate | C | dimensionless (h⁻¹) | Discharge/charge speed |
| State of Charge | SOC | **% (0-100)** | Usable energy remaining |
| Depth of Discharge | DOD | **% (0-100)** | Energy withdrawn |
| Cycle Life | N | **cycles** | Longevity indicator |
| Internal Resistance | R_int | **Ohms (Ω)** | Efficiency / heat loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The single most-asked numerical concept in this module is the **relationship between Capacity (Ah), Energy (Wh), and Voltage (V)**. Master it before anything else.

### 1.3 The Electrochemical Cell — Minimum Repeating Unit

A single galvanic cell consists of three core elements:

1. **Anode (−):** Site of **oxidation**; releases electrons to the external circuit.
2. **Cathode (+):** Site of **reduction**; accepts electrons from the external circuit.
3. **Electrolyte:** Ionic conductor (liquid, gel, or solid) that completes the internal circuit by transporting ions between electrodes.

A **battery** is simply **n cells** connected in **series** (for higher voltage) and/or **parallel** (for higher capacity).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Equations of Battery Operation

### 2.1.1 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis (Foundation of Capacity)

**Faraday's First Law:** The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed.

$$ m = \left(\frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}\right) $$

**Faraday's Second Law:** For the same quantity of charge, masses liberated are proportional to their equivalent weights.

$$ m = Z \cdot I \cdot t $$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of substance deposited (grams)
- $Q$ = total charge = $I \cdot t$ (Coulombs)
- $M$ = molar mass of substance (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per ion
- $F$ = **Faraday's constant = 96,485 C/mol (≈ 26.8 Ah/mol)**
- $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent (g/C)

### 2.1.2 Capacity Relationships

The **theoretical capacity** of an electrode is dictated purely by chemistry:

$$ Q_{th} = \frac{n \cdot F}{M} \quad \text{[Ah/kg]} $$

**Practical capacity** is always less due to inactive materials, side reactions, and inaccessible active mass:

$$ Q_{actual} = \eta_{utilization} \cdot Q_{th} $$

Where the **utilization factor** $\eta_{utilization}$ typically ranges from **0.25 to 0.90** depending on chemistry, design, and discharge rate.

### 2.1.3 Energy and Energy Density

$$ E = V_{nominal} \times Q \quad \text{[Wh]} $$

Gravimetric Energy Density:

$$ \rho_{E,grav} = \frac{E}{m_{cell}} = \frac{V \cdot Q}{m} \quad \text{[Wh/kg]} $$

Volumetric Energy Density:

$$ \rho_{E,vol} = \frac{E}{\text{Volume}_{cell}} \quad \text{[Wh/L]} $$

### 2.1.4 C-Rate — The Universal Discharge Speed Indicator

A **1C** rate discharges the entire battery in **1 hour**; **2C** discharges in **30 minutes**; **0.5C** (or C/2) in **2 hours**.

$$ I_{discharge} = C_{rate} \times Q_{rated} $$

For example, a **50 Ah battery discharged at 2C** delivers:

$$ I = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ A} $$

### 2.1.5 Peukert's Law — Capacity Loss at High Discharge Rates (Lead-Acid!)

Discharging faster than the rated rate yields **less usable capacity** — a critical KTU topic:

$$ C_{p} = I^{k} \cdot t $$

Rearranging for time:

$$ t = \frac{C_{p}}{I^{k}} $$

Where:
- $C_p$ = Peukert capacity (constant for a given battery)
- $I$ = discharge current (A)
- $t$ = discharge time (hours)
- $k$ = **Peukert exponent** (≈ 1.1–1.3 for Li-ion, 1.3–1.6 for lead-acid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Higher $k$ ⇒ greater capacity loss at high discharge rates. Lead-acid ($k \approx 1.3$) suffers significantly; Li-ion ($k \approx 1.05$) is far more robust — this is why **lithium chemistries dominate EV traction applications**.

### 2.1.6 State of Charge (SOC) and Depth of Discharge (DOD)

$$ SOC(\%) = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100 $$

$$ DOD(\%) = 100 - SOC(\%) $$

### 2.1.7 Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM) — Thevenin Battery Model

$$ V_{terminal} = V_{OCV} - I \cdot R_{int} - V_{polarization} $$

The internal resistance $R_{int}$ is itself a function of:
- **SOC** (rises sharply below 20% SOC)
- **Temperature** (rises in cold conditions)
- **Age/cycle count** (rises with degradation)

### 2.1.8 Battery Pack Scaling Equations

For $n_s$ cells in **series** and $n_p$ cells in **parallel**:

$$ V_{pack} = n_s \times V_{cell} $$

$$ Q_{pack} = n_p \times Q_{cell} $$

$$ E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot E_{cell} $$

> [!NOTE]
> Series connection multiplies **voltage**; parallel connection multiplies **capacity**. Both multiply energy. This is fundamental to EV pack architecture (e.g., Tesla Model 3: ~4,416 cells in 96s46p = 4,416 configuration).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $Q = I \times t$ | Capacity definition | All numericals |
| 2 | $E = V \times Q$ | Stored energy | Range calculation |
| 3 | $\rho_E = E/m$ | Gravimetric energy density | Battery selection |
| 4 | $\rho_P = P/m$ | Power density | Acceleration analysis |
| 5 | $C_{rate} = I/Q_{rated}$ | Discharge speed indicator | Thermal design |
| 6 | $t = C_p / I^{k}$ | Peukert's law | Lead-acid runtime |
| 7 | $V_t = V_{OCV} - IR_{int}$ | Terminal voltage under load | Loss estimation |
| 8 | $P_{loss} = I^2 R_{int}$ | Heat generation | BMS thermal design |
| 9 | $\eta = E_{out}/E_{in} \times 100$ | Coulombic/energy efficiency | Round-trip loss |
| 10 | $V_{pack} = n_s V_{cell}$ | Series voltage scaling | Pack architecture |
| 11 | $Q_{pack} = n_p Q_{cell}$ | Parallel capacity scaling | Pack architecture |
| 12 | $E = \int V \, dQ$ | Real energy (with profile) | Discharge curve integration |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Automotive Utility

- **EV Range Estimation:** Range (km) = $\frac{E_{pack}}{\text{Wh/km consumption}}$. A Tesla Model 3 LR has ~75 kWh pack, consumes ~150 Wh/km → range ≈ 500 km.
- **12V SLI Batteries:** Traditional lead-acid for ICE vehicles, still needed in EVs for auxiliary systems.
- **Hybrid Vehicles:** Use NiMH (Toyota Prius legacy) or Li-ion (modern HEVs/PHEVs) for braking energy recovery and torque assist.
- **Battery-as-a-Service (BaaS):** Future business model where the pack is swappable — NIO, Ather Energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Faraday's Law to Ampere-Hour Capacity

We start with Faraday's First Law:

$$ m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} $$

**Step 1:** Solve for charge $Q$ in terms of mass $m$:

$$ Q = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M} $$

**Step 2:** Express charge in Ampere-hours instead of Coulombs using $Q_{Ah} = Q_C / 3600$:

$$ Q_{Ah} = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{3600 \cdot M} $$

**Step 3:** Normalize to unit mass (1 kg = 1000 g) to obtain **theoretical specific capacity**:

$$ Q_{th,grav} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot 1000}{3600 \cdot M} = \frac{n \cdot F}{3.6 \cdot M} \quad \text{[Ah/kg]} $$

**Step 4:** Numerical illustration for **lithium (Li⁺/Li)** with $n=1$, $M = 6.941$ g/mol:

$$ Q_{th,grav} = \frac{1 \times 96485}{3.6 \times 6.941} = \frac{96485}{24.99} = 3861 \text{ Ah/kg} $$

> This is the **theoretical maximum** — no real battery ever achieves it. The graphitic anode in commercial Li-ion cells delivers only ~372 Ah/kg, and a full cell ~150-250 Wh/kg.

**Step 5:** Include Faraday's constant for **molar volume** to get volumetric capacity:

$$ Q_{th,vol} = \frac{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}{M \cdot 3600} \quad \text{[Ah/L]} $$

where $\rho$ is the active material density (kg/L).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Density from First Principles

**Step 1:** Start from the definition of electrical energy:

$$ E = \int_{0}^{Q} V(q) \, dq $$

**Step 2:** Assume constant nominal voltage $V_{nom}$ (a reasonable approximation for engineering):

$$ E \approx V_{nom} \times Q $$

**Step 3:** Substitute $Q$ in Ah and $V$ in volts to obtain Wh directly:

$$ E [\text{Wh}] = V [\text{V}] \times Q [\text{Ah}] $$

**Step 4:** Gravimetric energy density:

$$ \rho_{E,grav} = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{V \cdot Q}{m} = \frac{V}{m/Q} = \frac{V}{\text{specific mass per charge}} $$

**Step 5:** Worked example — **A NMC811 Li-ion cell (21700 format):**
- Nominal voltage: 3.7 V
- Capacity: 4.8 Ah
- Mass: 70 g
- Energy: $E = 3.7 \times 4.8 = 17.76$ Wh
- Gravimetric energy density: $\rho_E = 17.76 / 0.070 = 253.7$ Wh/kg

> **Compare with lead-acid:** 35-40 Wh/kg. Li-ion is **~6-7× better** — explaining the EV revolution.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Peukert's Law for a Lead-Acid Traction Battery

**Step 1:** A 100 Ah lead-acid battery rated at the 20-hour rate (C/20 = 5A) actually delivers LESS than 100 Ah when discharged faster.

**Step 2:** Peukert's law:

$$ t = \frac{C_p}{I^{k}} $$

**Step 3:** Determine $C_p$ from rated condition. For a battery rated $Q_n$ at current $I_n$ over time $t_n$:

$$ t_n = \frac{C_p}{I_n^{k}} \implies C_p = t_n \cdot I_n^{k} = Q_n \cdot I_n^{k-1} $$

**Step 4:** So capacity actually delivered at any other current $I$:

$$ Q_{delivered} = I \cdot t = I \cdot \frac{C_p}{I^{k}} = C_p \cdot I^{1-k} $$

$$ Q_{delivered} = Q_n \cdot \left(\frac{I}{I_n}\right)^{1-k} $$

**Step 5:** Worked numerical — **100 Ah battery, $k=1.3$, rated at 5A (20-hr rate):**

- **Scenario A:** Discharged at 5A (rated):
$$ Q_A = 100 \times (5/5)^{1-1.3} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ Ah} \checkmark $$

- **Scenario B:** Discharged at 50A (10× faster, ~1C):
$$ Q_B = 100 \times (50/5)^{1-1.3} = 100 \times 10^{-0.3} = 100 \times 0.501 = 50.1 \text{ Ah} $$

- **Scenario C:** Discharged at 100A (20× faster, 2C):
$$ Q_C = 100 \times (100/5)^{1-1.3} = 100 \times 20^{-0.3} = 100 \times 0.416 = 41.6 \text{ Ah} $$

> **Result:** Pulling current 20× faster than rated **loses ~58% of the rated capacity**. This is why EV traction demands Li-ion over lead-acid.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Battery Chemistries for Automobiles

| Parameter | Lead-Acid (VRLA) | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) | LiFePO₄ (LFP) | Solid-State (Future) |
|-----------|------------------|------|--------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Nominal Voltage (V) | 2.0 | 1.2 | 3.7 | 3.2 | 3.7+ |
| Energy Density (Wh/kg) | 30-50 | 60-90 | 180-250 | 90-160 | 300-500 |
| Power Density (W/kg) | 180 | 250-1000 | 500-2000 | 800-3000 | 800-3500 |
| Cycle Life | 300-800 | 500-2000 | 1000-3000 | 2000-6000 | 5000+ |
| Self-Discharge (%/mo) | 3-20 | 20-30 | 1-3 | 1-3 | <1 |
| Operating Temp (°C) | -20 to 50 | -20 to 45 | -20 to 60 | -20 to 60 | -30 to 80 |
| Cost ($/kWh) | 100-150 | 250-350 | 110-140 | 90-130 | 200-400 (est) |
| Safety | Moderate (sulfation) | Moderate (venting) | Moderate (thermal runaway) | **High** (stable) | **Very High** |
| Toxicity | High (Pb) | Low | Low | Low | Low |
| EV Application | 12V SLI only | Older HEVs (Prius) | **Mainstream EVs** | BYD Blade, entry EVs | 2027-2030+ |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Battery Pack Energy for an EV — Worked Example

**Problem:** An EV uses a 400V architecture pack with 96s46p configuration of 21700 cells. Each cell: 3.7V nominal, 4.8 Ah, 70 g.

**Step 1 — Pack voltage:**

$$ V_{pack} = 96 \times 3.7 = 355.2 \text{ V} \quad (\approx 400V \text{ class}) $$

**Step 2 — Pack capacity (parallel group):**

$$ Q_{pack} = 46 \times 4.8 = 220.8 \text{ Ah} $$

**Step 3 — Pack energy:**

$$ E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} = 355.2 \times 220.8 = 78,428 \text{ Wh} \approx 78.4 \text{ kWh} $$

**Step 4 — Pack mass:**

$$ m_{pack} = 96 \times 46 \times 70 \text{ g} = 309,120 \text{ g} = 309.1 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 5 — Pack-level energy density:**

$$ \rho_{E,pack} = \frac{78,400}{309.1} = 253.6 \text{ Wh/kg} $$

> This is **near the cell-level** because series-parallel doesn't degrade energy density — it scales.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📚 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Define the terms: (a) State of Charge (SOC), (b) C-rate, and (c) Energy Density. Why is energy density critical for EV range?**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) State of Charge (SOC):** The ratio of the remaining capacity in a battery to its rated maximum capacity, expressed as a percentage.

$$ SOC(\%) = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100 $$

**(b) C-Rate:** A dimensionless measure of the rate at which a battery is discharged (or charged) relative to its maximum capacity. A 1C rate means the battery will be fully discharged in 1 hour; a 2C rate in 30 minutes.

$$ C_{rate} = \frac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{rated}} $$

**(c) Energy Density:** The amount of energy stored per unit mass (Wh/kg) or volume (Wh/L) of a battery. It is the product of nominal voltage and specific capacity.

$$ \rho_E = \frac{V \times Q}{m} \quad \text{[Wh/kg]} $$

**Why critical for EV range:** Higher energy density means more energy per kg of battery → either longer range for the same battery mass, OR lighter vehicle for the same range. Improving energy density is the single biggest engineering driver of EV adoption. From lead-acid (~40 Wh/kg) to Li-ion (~250 Wh/kg) is a **6× improvement** that made modern EVs practical.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare Lead-Acid, NiMH, and Li-ion batteries with respect to: (i) Nominal voltage per cell, (ii) Energy density, (iii) Cycle life, and (iv) Self-discharge rate.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Lead-Acid (VRLA) | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) |
|----------|------------------|------|--------------|
| (i) Nominal Voltage | **2.0 V** | **1.2 V** | **3.7 V** |
| (ii) Energy Density | 30-50 Wh/kg | 60-90 Wh/kg | **180-250 Wh/kg** |
| (iii) Cycle Life | 300-800 | 500-2000 | **1000-3000** |
| (iv) Self-Discharge | 3-5 %/month | **20-30 %/month** | 1-3 %/month |

**Inferences:**
- Li-ion has the **highest nominal voltage** per cell, requiring fewer cells in series for a given pack voltage.
- Li-ion has the **highest energy density** by 5-8× over lead-acid.
- Li-ion has the **longest cycle life** in deep-cycle applications.
- NiMH suffers from the **worst self-discharge**, making it unsuitable for long-term storage.
- **Conclusion:** For modern EV traction, **Li-ion is the dominant chemistry**. Lead-acid survives only in 12V SLI roles; NiMH persists in older HEVs (e.g., Toyota Prius gen 1-3).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between battery capacity, current, and time. A 60 Ah lead-acid battery is discharged at 6 A for 8 hours. Using **Peukert's Law** with exponent $k=1.3$, determine the actual capacity delivered. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **Lithium-ion (LiCoO₂/graphite) cell** with appropriate reactions at both electrodes. List **four major advantages** and **two major limitations** of Li-ion batteries for EV applications. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### **Part (a) — Capacity, Peukert's Law Derivation and Calculation**

**Step 1 — Definition of Capacity:**

Capacity is the total charge a fully charged battery can deliver under specified conditions:

$$ Q = I \times t $$

Where:
- $I$ = discharge current (A)
- $t$ = discharge time (hours)
- $Q$ = capacity (Ah)

**Step 2 — Peukert's Law Statement (Empirical):**

Emil Peukert (1897) observed that a battery's deliverable capacity **decreases** as discharge current increases:

$$ t = \frac{C_p}{I^{k}} $$

**Step 3 — Derivation from Rated Conditions:**

At rated current $I_n$, the battery delivers rated capacity $Q_n$ over rated time $t_n = Q_n / I_n$:

$$ t_n = \frac{C_p}{I_n^{k}} \implies C_p = t_n \cdot I_n^{k} = \frac{Q_n \cdot I_n^{k}}{I_n} = Q_n \cdot I_n^{k-1} $$

So for any current $I$:

$$ t = \frac{Q_n \cdot I_n^{k-1}}{I^{k}} $$

**Capacity delivered:**

$$ Q_{del} = I \cdot t = I \cdot \frac{Q_n \cdot I_n^{k-1}}{I^{k}} = Q_n \cdot \left(\frac{I_n}{I}\right)^{k-1} $$

**Step 4 — Numerical Substitution:**

Given: $Q_n = 60$ Ah, $I_n = 3$ A (C/20 rate, the standard 20-hour rate), $k = 1.3$, discharge current $I = 6$ A.

$$ Q_{del} = 60 \times \left(\frac{3}{6}\right)^{1.3 - 1} = 60 \times (0.5)^{0.3} $$

$$ (0.5)^{0.3} = e^{0.3 \cdot \ln(0.5)} = e^{0.3 \times (-0.693)} = e^{-0.2079} = 0.8123 $$

$$ Q_{del} = 60 \times 0.8123 = 48.74 \text{ Ah} $$

**Step 5 — Time delivered:**

$$ t = \frac{Q_{del}}{I} = \frac{48.74}{6} = 8.12 \text{ hours} $$

**Step 6 — Comment:**

The battery delivers only **48.74 Ah** instead of the rated 60 Ah — a **loss of 18.8%** — when discharged at double the rated rate. This confirms the Peukert effect: **higher currents reduce available capacity**. For traction applications with high current pulses, this is unacceptable, motivating the use of Li-ion (where $k \approx 1.05-1.1$).

---

### **Part (b) — Li-ion Cell Construction, Working, and Merits/Demerits**

**Construction:**

A cylindrical Li-ion cell (e.g., 18650/21700) consists of:
- **Cathode:** Lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO₂) coated on Al foil
- **Anode:** Graphite coated on Cu foil
- **Separator:** Microporous polyethylene/polypropylene film (15-25 μm)
- **Electrolyte:** LiPF₆ salt in organic carbonate solvents (EC + DMC + EMC)
- **Housing:** Steel can with safety vent

**Working — During Discharge:**

At the **anode (oxidation):**

$$ \text{LiC}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{Li}^+ + e^- $$

At the **cathode (reduction):**

$$ \text{CoO}_2 + \text{Li}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2 $$

**Net reaction:**

$$ \text{LiC}_6 + \text{CoO}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{LiCoO}_2 \quad (E_0 = 3.7 \text{ V}) $$

**During charge:** The reactions are reversed by applying external voltage; Li⁺ ions migrate from cathode back to anode.

**Four Major Advantages of Li-ion for EVs:**

1. **High Energy Density (180-250 Wh/kg):** 5-6× lead-acid, enabling 400+ km EV ranges.
2. **Long Cycle Life (1000-3000 cycles):** Translates to 8-15 years of vehicle life with proper thermal management.
3. **Low Self-Discharge (1-3%/month):** EV can sit unused for weeks without significant range loss.
4. **High Nominal Voltage (3.7 V/cell):** Fewer cells in series to achieve 400V/800V architectures → simpler BMS, lower cost.

**Two Major Limitations:**

1. **Thermal Runaway Risk:** Internal short circuits can cause exothermic reactions leading to fire/explosion. Requires sophisticated BMS and liquid cooling.
2. **Cobalt Dependency (in NMC/NCA):** Cobalt is expensive, toxic, and mined in politically unstable regions. Drive toward LFP and NMC-811 reduces but doesn't eliminate this issue.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Retest]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a Lead-Acid battery**. Write the reactions during discharge and charge. State **three disadvantages** of lead-acid batteries. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **future possibilities** of battery technology for automobiles, including: (i) Solid-state batteries, (ii) Li-S batteries, (iii) Li-Air batteries, and (iv) Sodium-ion batteries. Compare their theoretical energy densities. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Lead-Acid Battery**

**Construction:**

A lead-acid cell consists of:
- **Positive plate:** Lead dioxide (PbO₂) pasted on Pb grid
- **Negative plate:** Sponge lead (Pb) pasted on Pb grid
- **Electrolyte:** Dilute sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) of specific gravity ~1.28
- **Separator:** Microporous PVC or polyethylene between plates
- **Container:** Hard rubber or polypropylene case with vent caps

Multiple cells (each 2V) are connected in series in a polypropylene monoblock to form a 12V SLI battery (6 cells).

**Working — During Discharge:**

**Anode (oxidation — negative plate):**

$$ \text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^- $$

**Cathode (reduction — positive plate):**

$$ \text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Net reaction:**

$$ \text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (E_0 = 2.05 \text{ V}) $$

During discharge: H₂SO₄ is consumed and water is produced → **specific gravity decreases** (used as SOC indicator).

**During charge** (reverse of above): PbSO₄ on both plates is converted back to Pb (anode) and PbO₂ (cathode); water is consumed and H₂SO₄ regenerated.

**Three Disadvantages of Lead-Acid Batteries:**

1. **Low Energy Density (30-50 Wh/kg):** Too heavy for EV traction; 6-8× lower than Li-ion.
2. **Sulfation:** Hard lead sulfate crystals form during deep discharge; if left for long, they are irreversible and permanently reduce capacity.
3. **Toxicity of Lead:** Environmental hazard in manufacturing, use, and disposal; requires strict recycling protocols.

(Other valid points: hydrogen evolution during overcharge → explosion risk, acid spillage, slow charging, low cycle life.)

---

### **Part (b) — Future Battery Technologies**

**(i) Solid-State Batteries (SSB):**

- Replace the liquid organic electrolyte with a **solid ceramic, sulfide, or polymer electrolyte**.
- **Anode:** Pure lithium metal (theoretical capacity 3861 Ah/kg).
- **Advantages:** Energy density 300-500 Wh/kg, eliminates fire risk, enables wider temperature range.
- **Challenges:** Solid-solid interface resistance, dendrite formation, manufacturing scale-up.
- **Status:** Toyota, QuantumScape, Solid Power targeting 2027-2030 mass production.

**(ii) Lithium-Sulfur (Li-S) Batteries:**

- **Cathode:** Sulfur (S₈); **Anode:** Li metal; **Electrolyte:** Organic liquid.
- Theoretical energy density: **2,500 Wh/kg** (single cell).
- **Advantages:** Sulfur is cheap and abundant; 5× energy density vs Li-ion theoretical.
- **Challenges:** "Polysulfide shuttle" — dissolved polysulfides migrate to anode causing rapid capacity fade (~100-200 cycles currently).

**(iii) Lithium-Air (Li-O₂) Batteries:**

- **Cathode:** Porous carbon (O₂ from air); **Anode:** Li metal.
- Theoretical energy density: **~11,400 Wh/kg** (approaching gasoline at ~13,000 Wh/kg).
- **Advantages:** Highest theoretical energy of any chemistry; uses ambient oxygen.
- **Challenges:** Poor cycle life (<100), slow kinetics, water contamination, cathode clogging. Still primarily lab-scale (IBM, MIT research).

**(iv) Sodium-Ion (Na-ion) Batteries:**

- **Cathode:** Prussian blue analogs, layered oxides (Na₃V₂(PO₄)₃); **Anode:** Hard carbon; **Electrolyte:** NaPF₆ in carbonates.
- Theoretical energy density: **~160 Wh/kg** (CATL first-gen).
- **Advantages:** Sodium is **1000× more abundant** than lithium; cost 30-40% lower; cold-weather performance.
- **Challenges:** Larger Na⁺ ion → slower kinetics, lower energy density than Li-ion.
- **Status:** CATL, BYD, Faradion deploying in entry-level EVs and 2/3-wheelers in India, China (2023-2025).

**Comparison Table — Theoretical Energy Densities:**

| Chemistry | Theoretical Energy Density (Wh/kg) | Practical Achievable (Wh/kg) | Maturity |
|-----------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------|----------|
| Li-ion (NMC) | ~580 (cell), ~250 (pack) | 250-300 | **Production (2024)** |
| Solid-State (Li-metal) | ~900-1,200 | 350-500 | Pilot (2027-2030) |
| Li-S | ~2,500 | 300-500 | Lab/pilot |
| Li-Air | ~11,400 | 200-400 (limited) | Research |
| Na-ion | ~300 | 120-160 | Early production (2024) |

**Conclusion:** The EV battery of the next decade is likely to be a **diversified portfolio** — LFP and Na-ion for budget/urban EVs, NMC for mid-range, solid-state for premium/long-range.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> - **Sign errors in cell reactions:** Oxidation occurs at the **anode** (negative in discharge) — students often swap anode/cathode. Lose **1-2 marks per error**.
> - **Forgetting to multiply Faraday constant by 1000 for kg units:** Capacity numericals often lose **1 mark** when unit conversion is missed.
> - **Peukert's law sign of exponent:** $Q_{del}$ formula is $Q_n \times (I_n/I)^{k-1}$, NOT $Q_n \times (I/I_n)^{k-1}$. Inverse of the ratio — students confuse this regularly.
> - **Confusing energy and power density:** Energy = range; Power = acceleration. Mixing them up in long answers costs **2-3 marks**.
> - **Sketching in theory exams:** Even crude pencil sketches of cell construction get **2-3 marks** in Part B (a). Don't omit them.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision)

### ✅ Quick-Fire Facts (MUST memorize)

- **1 Faraday (F) = 96,485 C/mol ≈ 26.8 Ah/mol**
- **1 Ah = 3600 Coulombs**
- **1 kWh = 1000 Wh = 3.6 MJ**
- **Peukert exponent $k$:** Lead-acid ≈ 1.3-1.6; Li-ion ≈ 1.05-1.1
- **Faraday's First Law:** $m = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$
- **Lead-acid cell voltage:** 2.0 V nominal, 6 cells → 12 V battery
- **NiMH cell voltage:** 1.2 V
- **Li-ion cell voltage:** 3.7 V (NMC), 3.2 V (LFP)
- **Lithium metal theoretical specific capacity:** 3,861 Ah/kg
- **Graphite anode practical capacity:** ~372 Ah/kg

### ✅ Battery Type Classification

- **Primary:** Non-rechargeable (Alkaline, Li-primary, Zn-air)
- **Secondary:** Rechargeable (Lead-acid, NiCd, NiMH, Li-ion, Solid-state, Na-ion, Flow)

### ✅ Lead-Acid Reactions (Discharge)

- Anode: $\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$
- Cathode: $\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Net: $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$

### ✅ Li-ion Reactions (Discharge)

- Anode: $\text{LiC}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{Li}^+ + e^-$
- Cathode: $\text{CoO}_2 + \text{Li}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2$
- Net: $\text{LiC}_6 + \text{CoO}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{LiCoO}_2$

### ✅ Advantages of Li-ion (4 key points)

1. High energy density (180-250 Wh/kg)
2. Long cycle life (1000-3000 cycles)
3. Low self-discharge (1-3%/month)
4. High nominal voltage (3.7 V/cell)

### ✅ Limitations of Li-ion (2 key points)

1. Thermal runaway → fire risk
2. Cobalt toxicity/supply chain

### ✅ Disadvantages of Lead-Acid (3 key points)

1. Low energy density (30-50 Wh/kg)
2. Sulfation (irreversible capacity loss)
3. Lead toxicity / environmental hazard

### ✅ Future Battery Hierarchy (Energy Density)

- Li-Air (11,400 Wh/kg) > Li-S (2,500 Wh/kg) > Solid-State (900 Wh/kg) > Li-ion NMC (580 Wh/kg) > Na-ion (300 Wh/kg) > Lead-Acid (170 Wh/kg)

### ✅ Peukert's Law Quick-Use

$$ Q_{del} = Q_n \times \left(\frac{I_n}{I}\right)^{k-1} $$

> **Rule of thumb:** Faster discharge → less delivered capacity. Lead-acid suffers more than Li-ion.

### ✅ EV Pack Scaling Rules

- **Series (nₛ):** Multiplies voltage
- **Parallel (nₚ):** Multiplies capacity (Ah)
- **Both:** Multiply energy
- **Total cells:** $n_s \times n_p$

### ✅ BMS Core Functions (MUST know)

1. **SOC/SOH estimation** (Coulomb counting, Kalman filter)
2. **Cell balancing** (passive resistive vs active capacitive/inductive)
3. **Thermal management** (liquid cooling/heating)
4. **Safety** (isolation monitoring, contactor control, HVIL)

### ✅ Key Terms Cross-Reference

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| SLI | Starting, Lighting, Ignition |
| HEV | Hybrid Electric Vehicle |
| PHEV | Plug-in Hybrid EV |
| BEV | Battery Electric Vehicle |
| BMS | Battery Management System |
| SOC | State of Charge |
| SOH | State of Health |
| DOD | Depth of Discharge |
| OCV | Open Circuit Voltage |
| VRLA | Valve Regulated Lead Acid |
| LFP | Lithium Iron Phosphate |
| NMC | Nickel Manganese Cobalt |
| NCA | Nickel Cobalt Aluminum |

> **🎓 Final KTU Tip:** When answering Part B, ALWAYS start with a labeled **neat sketch** (cell cross-section or block diagram) — this alone secures **2-3 marks** even before you write a word. Then state definitions, write balanced chemical equations with **correct phase/state symbols**, and end with a **comparison table**. This is the formula for full marks in KTU 2024 scheme B.Tech board examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric vehicle layout and hybrid vehicles: advantages, limitations and types of hybrid vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT411 - ALTERNATE ENERGY SOURCES FOR AUTOMOBILE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electric vehicle layout and hybrid vehicles: advantages, limitations and types of hybrid vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electric Vehicle (EV) — Formal Definition

An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is an automotive vehicle that is propelled by one or more electric motors, drawing electrical energy from a rechargeable on-board energy storage system (typically a battery pack), and may also receive energy from an off-board source (e.g., the electrical grid). The vehicle layout refers to the physical arrangement and interconnection of the **Energy Source**, **Power Converter**, **Electric Motor**, **Transmission**, and **Wheels**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition Anchor:**
> As per the PEAUT411 syllabus, an EV layout is the *systematic placement* of the battery pack, power electronics controller, motor, and final drive that replaces the conventional IC engine + fuel tank + gearbox assembly.

### 1.2 Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) — Formal Definition

A **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** is a vehicle that combines two distinct propulsion energy sources — typically an **Internal Combustion (IC) Engine** and one or more **Electric Machines (EM)** — with an energy storage system (battery + sometimes ultra-capacitor) to deliver improved fuel economy, lower emissions, and enhanced performance compared to a conventional IC engine vehicle.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Two-Friends Carrying a Load" Story:**
> Imagine two friends carrying a heavy sack of rice up a hill.
> - **Friend A (IC Engine):** Strong and powerful, but gets very tired quickly and drinks a lot of water (fuel).
> - **Friend B (Electric Motor):** Not as strong alone, but tireless and works silently (efficient).
>
> - In a **Pure EV**, only Friend B carries the load (no fuel at all — clean but limited range).
> - In a **Conventional Car**, only Friend A carries the load (powerful but polluting).
> - In a **Hybrid**, both friends share the load intelligently — Friend B helps during starts and slow movement; Friend A handles highway speeds; sometimes both work together for extra power. The result: **less water (fuel) consumed, less sweat (emissions) generated, longer journey (range) achieved.**

### 1.4 Standard EV System Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants)

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Battery Specific Energy | **150 – 250 Wh/kg** | Determines vehicle range |
| Battery Pack Voltage | **300 – 800 V (modern EVs)** | Affects motor power capability |
| Motor Peak Efficiency | **≥ 95%** | Much higher than IC engine (~35%) |
| EV Energy Efficiency | **~ 0.15 kWh/km** | Includes grid-to-wheel losses |
| ICE Thermal Efficiency | **~ 25–35%** | Baseline for comparison |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Electric Vehicle — Layout Architecture

A typical Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) layout consists of the following major subsystems arranged in series electrically and mechanically:

1. **Energy Storage System (ESS):** Lithium-ion battery pack (Battery Management System — BMS included).
2. **On-board Charger (OBC):** Converts AC grid supply to DC for charging.
3. **Power Electronics Converter (Inverter + DC-DC Converter):**
   - Inverter converts DC → 3-phase AC for the motor.
   - DC-DC converter steps voltage for auxiliary 12 V/24 V systems.
4. **Electric Traction Motor (AC induction / PMSM / SRM / BLDC):** Provides propulsive torque.
5. **Transmission (Single-speed reducer / Multi-speed gearbox):** Reduces motor RPM and increases torque.
6. **Final Drive & Wheels:** Mechanical delivery of torque to road.

**Logical Operation Flow:**
$$\text{Grid (AC)} \rightarrow \text{OBC} \rightarrow \text{Battery (DC)} \rightarrow \text{Inverter (DC→AC)} \rightarrow \text{Motor} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

### 2.2 Hybrid Electric Vehicle — General Architecture

A Hybrid Electric Vehicle integrates **two or more energy sources and converters**:

- **Primary Energy Source 1:** Liquid fuel (Petrol/Diesel) → IC Engine → Mechanical Output.
- **Primary Energy Source 2:** Battery (Electrical) → Electric Motor → Mechanical Output.
- **Energy Coupling Device:** Either mechanical (clutch, planetary gear) or electrical (generator-motor pair).
- **Regenerative Braking Path:** Kinetic energy → Motor acts as Generator → Battery charges.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy stored in battery | $E = V_{bat} \times C_{bat}$ | Wh | $V_{bat}$ in V, $C_{bat}$ in Ah |
| 2 | Vehicle range from battery | $R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{\text{Energy consumed per km}}$ | km | Pure EV metric |
| 3 | Motor output power | $P_{motor} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | W | $T$ in Nm, $N$ in rpm |
| 4 | Torque required for grade | $T = m \cdot g \cdot r \cdot \sin\theta$ | Nm | For hill climb angle $\theta$ |
| 5 | Acceleration force required | $F_a = m \cdot a$ | N | $m$ = vehicle mass, $a$ = acceleration |
| 6 | Rolling resistance force | $F_r = m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr}$ | N | $C_{rr}$ ≈ 0.015 for car tires |
| 7 | Aerodynamic drag force | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^{2}$ | N | $\rho \approx 1.225 \,\tfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^{3}}$ |
| 8 | Total tractive force | $F_{tr} = F_a + F_r + F_d + F_{grade}$ | N | Summed for design |
| 9 | IC Engine thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | — | Typically 0.25 – 0.35 |
| 10 | Overall HEV efficiency gain | $\eta_{HEV} \approx \dfrac{\eta_{th} \cdot \eta_{mot} \cdot \eta_{regen}}{1}$ | — | Approx. 1.3 – 1.5× ICE alone |
| 11 | Regenerative braking energy recovery | $E_{regen} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot (v_1^{2} - v_2^{2})$ | J | Up to **30 %** recovered in practice |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Memory Trick:** Memorize the four forces acting on a vehicle — **Acceleration, Rolling, Drag, Grade** — as **"AR-DG"** (ARREARS minus D Grade — the student must overcome all four to climb up!). Total tractive force = Sum of all four.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV layouts** are foundational to Tesla, BYD, Tata Nexon EV, Hyundai Kona, MG ZS EV designs.
- **HEV layouts** power Toyota Prius (the world's best-selling hybrid), Honda City Hybrid, Toyota Camry Hybrid — used widely in Indian automotive markets.
- Understanding these layouts enables engineers to design **Energy Management Strategies (EMS)** — the software logic that decides *when* to use the engine vs. motor.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Vehicle Range from a Given Battery Pack

**Problem Statement:** A small electric city car uses a 400 V, 100 Ah lithium-ion battery. The average energy consumption is **0.18 kWh/km**. Compute the theoretical all-electric range.

**Given:**
- $V_{bat} = 400 \,\text{V}$
- $C_{bat} = 100 \,\text{Ah}$
- Energy consumption $e = 0.18 \,\text{kWh/km}$

**Step 1 — Compute the stored energy in Watt-hours.**

$$E_{bat} = V_{bat} \times C_{bat} = 400 \,\text{V} \times 100 \,\text{Ah}$$

$$E_{bat} = 40{,}000 \,\text{Wh} = 40 \,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Convert stored energy into range by dividing by per-km consumption.**

$$R = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{e} = \dfrac{40 \,\text{kWh}}{0.18 \,\text{kWh/km}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$R = \dfrac{40}{0.18} = 222.22 \,\text{km}$$

**Step 4 — Apply a real-world Derating Factor (DoD ≈ 90 % for Li-ion to protect cycle life).**

$$R_{real} = 0.90 \times 222.22 \approx 200 \,\text{km}$$

**Conclusion:** The car can travel approximately **200 km** on a full charge under real-world conditions.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Motor Power Requirement for a Given Vehicle

**Problem Statement:** An EV of mass **1500 kg** (including battery) must accelerate from rest to **100 km/h** in **10 seconds** on a flat road. Compute the peak motor power required (neglect rolling, drag, and drivetrain losses for first-cut estimation).

**Given:**
- $m = 1500 \,\text{kg}$
- $v_f = 100 \,\text{km/h} = \dfrac{100 \times 1000}{3600} = 27.78 \,\text{m/s}$
- $t = 10 \,\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Compute average acceleration.**

$$a = \dfrac{v_f - v_i}{t} = \dfrac{27.78 - 0}{10} = 2.778 \,\text{m/s}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the tractive force needed for acceleration.**

$$F_a = m \cdot a = 1500 \times 2.778$$

$$F_a = 4166.67 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute average tractive power (assume linear velocity ramp).**

Average velocity during acceleration:

$$v_{avg} = \dfrac{v_i + v_f}{2} = \dfrac{0 + 27.78}{2} = 13.89 \,\text{m/s}$$

Average power:

$$P_{avg} = F_a \cdot v_{avg} = 4166.67 \times 13.89$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$P_{avg} = 57{,}876 \,\text{W} \approx 57.9 \,\text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Apply a 1.5× peak factor (motor peak power ≈ 1.5× average for transient loads).**

$$P_{peak} \approx 1.5 \times 57.9 = 86.8 \,\text{kW} \approx 87 \,\text{kW}$$

**Conclusion:** A motor of approximately **87 kW (~117 hp)** rating is required to meet this performance target.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of EV, HEV, and Conventional Vehicle

| Attribute | Conventional (ICE) | Battery EV (BEV) | Hybrid EV (HEV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Petrol/Diesel | Battery (Grid) | Fuel + Battery |
| Prime Mover | IC Engine | Electric Motor | IC Engine + Motor |
| Tailpipe Emissions | **High** | **Zero** | **Low** (≈ 30–50 % of ICE) |
| Energy Efficiency | 25–35 % | **80–90 %** (well-to-wheel) | **35–45 %** |
| Range | **Long (~700 km)** | Limited (~200–500 km) | **Long (~800–1000 km)** |
| Refueling Time | 3–5 min | **30 min – 8 h** | 3–5 min |
| Initial Cost | Moderate | **High** | High |
| Maintenance | Moderate | **Low** (no oil, fewer parts) | Moderate |
| Noise | High | **Very Low** | Low–Moderate |
| Power Density | High | Lower (battery-limited) | High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pure Electric Vehicle (BEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GRID[AC Grid Supply] --> OBC[On Board Charger]
    OBC --> BAT[Battery Pack with BMS]
    BAT --> INV[Inverter DC to AC]
    INV --> MTR[Electric Traction Motor]
    MTR --> TRX[Single Speed Transmission]
    TRX --> FD[Final Drive Differential]
    FD --> WH[Wheels]
    BAT --> DCDC[DC DC Converter 12V]
    DCDC --> AUX[Auxiliary Loads Lights Sensors]
    MTR -.-> REG[Regenerative Braking Generator Mode]
    REG -.-> BAT
```

**Description:** Energy flows from the grid → charger → battery → inverter → motor → transmission → wheels. Reverse energy flow (regenerative braking) recharges the battery during deceleration.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FUEL[Fuel Tank] --> ICE[IC Engine]
    ICE --> GEN[Generator]
    GEN --> REC[Rectifier]
    BAT[Battery Pack] --> INV[Inverter]
    REC --> INV
    BAT <--> INV
    INV --> MTR[Electric Traction Motor]
    MTR --> TRX[Transmission]
    TRX --> WH[Wheels]
```

**Description:** In a Series Hybrid, the ICE never directly drives the wheels. It only drives a generator to charge the battery. All wheel propulsion comes from the electric motor — like a range-extended EV.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FUEL[Fuel Tank] --> ICE[IC Engine]
    ICE --> CLUTCH[Clutch Coupler]
    BAT[Battery Pack] --> INV[Inverter]
    INV --> MTR[Electric Motor]
    MTR --> CLUTCH
    CLUTCH --> TRX[Transmission]
    TRX --> WH[Wheels]
```

**Description:** In a Parallel Hybrid, the ICE and the electric motor are mechanically coupled (via clutch) and both can propel the vehicle — either independently or simultaneously.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Series-Parallel (PSHEV / Full Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FUEL[Fuel Tank] --> ICE[IC Engine]
    ICE --> PSG[Planetary Gear Set Power Split Device]
    BAT[Battery] --> INV[Inverter]
    INV --> MG1[Motor Generator 1]
    MG1 --> PSG
    PSG --> MTR2[Motor Generator 2 Traction]
    PSG --> WH[Wheels]
    MTR2 --> WH
```

**Description:** This is the **Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS)** architecture. A planetary gear set acts as the energy coupling device — allowing ICE to send part of its power to wheels and part to MG1 (acting as generator). MG2 acts as the traction motor.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*List any three advantages of an Electric Vehicle over a Conventional IC engine vehicle.*

**Model Answer (3 points, 1 mark each):**

1. **Zero tailpipe emissions** — no CO₂, CO, HC, NOx, or particulate matter released during driving (improves urban air quality).
2. **Higher energy efficiency** — electric drivetrain efficiency is 80–90 % compared to 25–35 % for IC engines (well-to-wheel).
3. **Lower operating cost** — electricity per km is cheaper than petrol/diesel; fewer moving parts → lower maintenance cost (no oil change, spark plugs, timing belts, etc.).

**Additional accepted points (for partial credit):** Quieter operation, regenerative braking, single-speed transmission simplicity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State any three limitations of Electric Vehicles.*

**Model Answer (3 points, 1 mark each):**

1. **Limited driving range** — typically 200–500 km per charge compared to 600–800 km for ICE vehicles on a full tank.
2. **Long refueling (charging) time** — even with fast charging, 30–60 min is needed for 80 % charge; home charging may take 6–8 hours.
3. **High initial purchase cost** — battery pack is expensive (₹4–6 lakh for a 30 kWh pack), making EV upfront cost higher than equivalent ICE.

**Additional accepted points:** Battery degradation over time, charging infrastructure scarcity in rural areas, range anxiety.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the layout of a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). List the major components and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer:**

A Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) layout consists of the following subsystems arranged in a series energy flow path:

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Battery Pack (Li-ion)** | Stores electrical energy; managed by BMS |
| 2 | **On-board Charger (OBC)** | Converts AC grid supply to DC for battery |
| 3 | **DC-DC Converter** | Steps down high voltage (e.g., 400 V) to 12 V for auxiliary loads |
| 4 | **Inverter (Power Electronics)** | Converts DC battery voltage to 3-phase AC for the motor |
| 5 | **Traction Motor (PMSM / Induction)** | Converts electrical energy to mechanical torque |
| 6 | **Transmission (Single-speed reducer)** | Reduces motor RPM, increases torque |
| 7 | **Final Drive (Differential)** | Distributes torque to left and right wheels |
| 8 | **Regenerative Braking System** | Recovers kinetic energy during deceleration → recharges battery |

**Block Diagram (already shown in SECTION 4.1) carries 3 marks, component listing and function each carry 4 marks.**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Explain the three main types of Hybrid Electric Vehicles — Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel. State one example vehicle for each type.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV):**
- **Working:** IC Engine drives a **generator only**; the generator charges the battery / supplies the traction motor. Wheels are propelled **only by the electric motor**.
- **Coupling:** Electrical (no mechanical link between ICE and wheels).
- **Advantages:** Simple control, ICE always runs at optimum speed (high efficiency).
- **Disadvantages:** Multiple energy conversions → losses; large motor needed.
- **Example:** Chevrolet Volt (1st gen), BMW i3 with Range Extender.

**2. Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV):**
- **Working:** ICE and electric motor are **mechanically coupled** (via clutch or belt) and both can drive the wheels — either alone or together.
- **Coupling:** Mechanical.
- **Advantages:** Lower cost, smaller battery and motor sufficient.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot optimize ICE speed independent of wheel speed.
- **Example:** Honda Civic Hybrid, Honda City Hybrid (older generation).

**3. Series-Parallel Hybrid (Full Hybrid / PSHEV):**
- **Working:** Combines both architectures — uses a **planetary gear set** as the power-split device. ICE can drive wheels directly, charge battery via MG1, or both.
- **Coupling:** Both mechanical (via gear) and electrical.
- **Advantages:** Best of both worlds — high efficiency, flexibility.
- **Disadvantages:** Complex control system, higher cost.
- **Example:** Toyota Prius, Toyota Camry Hybrid, Toyota Innova Hycross.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the major advantages and limitations of Hybrid Electric Vehicles.*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of HEV (any 4 points):**

1. **Improved fuel economy** — typically 30–50 % better than ICE alone.
2. **Lower emissions** — CO₂, NOx, PM reduced significantly; some HEVs meet Euro 6 / BS-VI easily.
3. **Regenerative braking** — recovers kinetic energy that would otherwise be wasted as heat.
4. **Smaller IC Engine possible** — electric motor assists during peak loads (downsizing).
5. **No external charging required** — battery is self-charged by ICE and regen braking; no range anxiety like pure EV.
6. **Better performance** — instant torque from electric motor improves acceleration.

**Limitations of HEV (any 4 points):**

1. **Higher initial cost** — two powertrains (engine + motor + battery) increase vehicle cost by 20–40 %.
2. **Complex control system** — Energy Management Strategy (EMS) requires sophisticated software and sensors.
3. **Increased vehicle weight** — carrying both engine and battery adds mass (~100–200 kg extra).
4. **Still produces tailpipe emissions** — not a zero-emission vehicle like BEV.
5. **Battery replacement cost** — although rare, eventual battery pack replacement is expensive.
6. **Maintenance complexity** — both ICE and EV systems must be serviced.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare Series Hybrid and Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicles in a tabular form (any 7 parameters).*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Series Hybrid (SHEV) | Parallel Hybrid (PHEV) |
|---|---|---|
| Coupling between sources | **Electrical** (no mechanical link) | **Mechanical** (clutch/gear) |
| Role of ICE | Only drives generator → charges battery | Directly drives wheels |
| Role of Electric Motor | Sole traction provider | Assists ICE; can also drive alone |
| Transmission | Simple (no multi-speed needed) | Complex (multi-speed gearbox) |
| ICE Operating Point | Always at optimum efficiency | Variable (depends on driving mode) |
| Battery & Motor Size | Large motor + larger battery | Smaller motor + smaller battery |
| Overall Efficiency | Lower (multiple energy conversions) | Higher (fewer conversions) |
| Control Complexity | Simpler | More complex |
| Cost | Higher (large motor) | Lower |
| Examples | Chevrolet Volt, BMW i3 REx | Honda Civic Hybrid, Honda Insight |

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label arrows** in block diagrams — every arrow must indicate the direction of energy/power flow. Missing labels = -1 to -2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Series vs Parallel coupling** — many students write "Series Hybrid has mechanical coupling" — this is **WRONG**. Series = Electrical coupling only. Examiner deducts full marks for this.
> 3. **Not mentioning Regenerative Braking** in BEV/HEV layouts — this is a *favorite* KTU examiner question and carries 2–3 marks if asked as sub-part.
> 4. **Writing "electric vehicle has no emissions"** without specifying *"tailpipe / well-to-wheel"* — KTU 2024 expects precise terminology. Mark deduction of 1 mark for imprecision.
> 5. **Missing units** in numerical problems — always write units (kWh, km, V, Ah) — missing units = -0.5 to -1 mark per occurrence.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **EV Layout = 8 components:** Grid, OBC, Battery + BMS, Inverter, Motor, Transmission, Final Drive, Wheels. Energy flow: AC → DC → AC → Mechanical.
>
> ✅ **HEV = 2 Energy Sources:** IC Engine + Electric Motor + Battery. Always mention **Energy Management Strategy (EMS)**.
>
> ✅ **3 Types of HEVs:**
> - **Series** → ICE ↔ Generator ↔ Battery ↔ Motor ↔ Wheels (electrical coupling only).
> - **Parallel** → ICE + Motor mechanically coupled → Wheels (both propel).
> - **Series-Parallel** → Planetary gear + MG1 + MG2 (Toyota THS — best efficiency).
>
> ✅ **EV Advantages:** Zero tailpipe emission, high efficiency (80–90 %), low operating cost, regenerative braking, low noise.
>
> ✅ **EV Limitations:** Limited range (200–500 km), long charging time, high initial cost, battery degradation, charging infra scarcity.
>
> ✅ **Key Formulas to memorize:**
> - $E = V \times C$ (battery energy)
> - $R = E / e_{per-km}$ (range)
> - $F_{tr} = F_a + F_r + F_d + F_{grade}$
> - $P = T \cdot \omega$ (motor power)
>
> ✅ **Key Numerical:** $1 \,\text{kWh} = 1000 \,\text{Wh}$; $1 \,\text{Ah at 1 V} = 1 \,\text{Wh}$.
>
> ✅ **Toyota Prius is the gold standard example** for Full / Series-Parallel Hybrid.
>
> ✅ **Regenerative braking** recovers **up to 30 %** of kinetic energy.
>
> ✅ **Always include units, arrows, and labels** in diagrams — KTU valuation is strict.
